question: What densities have been reported for ZrB2 powder or ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramics in studies on spark-plasma-sintered UHTCs and electron-beam-processed UHTCs?
answer:
Densities reported for ZrB2 powder or ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramics in studies on spark-plasma-sintered UHTCs and electron-beam-processed UHTCs include:

- For spark-plasma-sintered ZrB2 with 20 vol.% SiC, a relative density of 99.6% was achieved [11].
- For spark-plasma-sintered ZrB2 with 30 vol.% SiC, a relative density of 99.7% was achieved [11].
- For spark-plasma-sintered ZrB2 with 40 vol.% SiC, a relative density of 99.7% was achieved [11].
- For ZrB2-based UHTCs with La2O3 addition, high densities (>99% of theoretical relative density) were achieved [14].
- For ZrB2-based UHTCs without La2O3 addition, a density of approximately 95% of theoretical relative density was achieved due to oxygen impurities [15].
- For electron-beam-processed ZrB2-ZrSi2 composites, while specific density values are not provided in the abstract, the study aimed to fabricate dense UHTCs using the EBM process [20]. 

These values reflect the relative densities achieved in the respective sintering processes.

Abstract
We have explored the feasibility of reducing the spark-plasma-sintering (SPS) temperature of additive-free ZrB2 ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) via crystal size refinement of the starting powder down to the low nanoscale. We found that under otherwise the same SPS conditions (75 MPa pressure, and 100 ◦ C/min heating ramp) nanoscale ZrB2 can be densified at temperatures about 450 ◦ C lower than for the typical micrometre and submicrometre ZrB2 powders, and at least 250 ◦ C below the ultra-fine powder temperature. Furthermore, the nanoscale crystal refinement also promotes the production of fine-grained ZrB2 UHTCs. We also found that elimination of the B2O3 impurities plays an important role in the complete densification. The unequalled sinterability of the nanoscale ZrB2 powders highlights the need to use high-energy ball-milling for the comminution of the typical commercially available ZrB2 powders. © 2012 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
Keywords: ZrB2; Ultra-high-temperature ceramics; Sintering; High-energy ball-milling

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  including  borides,  carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals [1 -3], have a high melting point  over  3000 ◦ C.  Due  to  their  excellent  thermal,  mechanical  and chemical stability, UHTCs have been considered as the most promising candidate materials for applications facing ultra-high temperature environments, such as the thermal protection for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, and the core components in propulsions and advanced nuclear fuels  [4 -9].  So  far,  many  methods  have  been  developed  to  fabricate UHTCs, among which the spark plasma sintering (SPS) is the most advantageous method, since it can realize full densification, reduce sintering  temperature  and  time,  and  improve  mechanical  and  thermal stability [10 -12]. As one type of UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been  proven to  be  highly  resistant  to  oxidation  [13 -15].  During  the sintering process of ZrB2, SiC is often introduced as a second phase, since it  inhibits  grain  growth,  improves  sinterability,  and  enhances  the oxidation  and  mechanical  properties  [16 -18].  Therefore,  ZrB2-SiC

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

1. Motivation and significance
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are refractory materials with unusual properties making them strong contenders for applications involving adverse and chemically aggressive environments. This paper presents an effort to process UHTCs using an additive manufacturing method, specifically Electron Beam Sintering (EBS) adoption of the Electron Beam powder-bed fusion (PBF) process. Such a process shows that the processing and consolidation phenomenon of UHTCs are different from what is observed in the traditional sintering process of UHTCs and Electron Beam Melting (EBM) of typical metals or alloys. In this article, the EBS process of UHTCs is studied. The differences between EBS and the two aforementioned traditional processing methods are analyzed. The scientific hypotheses have been backed by experimental results on a ZrB2 -30 vol% ZrSi2  UHTC mixture using an electron beam via Liquid Phase Sintering (LPS) theory. Efforts to develop processing conditions to fabricate dense and defect-free UHTC components are explored with the help of finite element (FE) simulations. Characterization of the EBS processed samples using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) coupled with Energy Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray Diffraction (XRD) revealed unique needle-like patterned grains of ZrB2  in a ZrSi2  matrix. Our findings demonstrate the feasibility of the EBS process to produce dense layers of UHTCs for coatings, potential three-dimensional, as well as complex-shaped applications.
adopted to create complex geometries using high-performance materials. Electron Beam Melting (EBM), a PBF process, has made its way to applications  ranging  from  custom  medical  implants  to  lightweight aerospace and automotive components [2,4,5].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) include borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting temperatures above 2700 ◦ C [1]. UHTCs have been investigated for high temperature applications, including thermal protection systems for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, high temperature electrodes, and molten metal crucibles [2-5]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramics have attracted much attention because of their unique combination of low density, high melting temperature and thermal shock resistance as well as excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [6-10].
Generally pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperature (>1900 ◦ C) are required to achieve complete densification of pure ZrB2 material due to its strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coefficient. Several studies have shown that the addition of SiC to ZrB2 powders has beneficial effects not only on sinterability, but also on mechanical properties and resistance to oxidation [9-12]. Although great efforts have been devoted to enhancing the mechanical properties and much progress has been made recently, the susceptibility to brittle fracture is still a major issue for the use of ZrB2based UHTCs. Whisker-reinforced ceramic matrix composites are of promising materials, especially for high temperature applications [13-15]. SiC whiskers, combining high strength, elastic modulus and chemical inertness at elevated temperature, are

4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In the case of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of ZrB2 ceramics, the effect of processing parameters (sintering time and sintering temperature) and powder particle size (nano- or micron- sized) on the densification has been shown in Fig. 7b [120]. It can be noted that the densification rate is extremely high for the smaller particle size (≤25 nm) and near-theoretical densification of ZrB2 is attained even at very low sintering temperatures (below 1700 °C) and shorter sintering times of 16 min or lesser. The spark plasma sintered di-borides are usually observed to possess fine microstructure and good combination of properties. In order to address some issues related to non-uniform densification (and properties) primarily due to fast heating rate and lesser holding times, recently we had explored the application of multi-stage spark plasma sintering (MSS-SPS) schedule, which involves holding at one or more intermediate temperatures for few minutes before reaching the final sintering temperatures [98,173-179]. Keeping the total holding time constant (i.e. lesser holding time at the final sintering temperature), more uniform densification and finer microstructures were achieved for ZrB2-SiC-TiSi2 [175] and TiB2-TiSi2 composites [98], resulting in still improved mechanical properties (as compared to similar compositions sintered via the usual single-step SPS route).

Spark plasma sintering of ZrO2 /uniFB01 ber toughened ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics
ZrO2f ceramics are obtained at 1950 1 C by hot pressing for 2 h; the /uniFB02 exural strength and fracture toughness are 633 7 63 MPa and 5.6 7 0.7 MPa m 1/2 , respectively. However, this high hotpressing temperature ( 4 1900 1 C) is harmful to the performance of ZrO2 /uniFB01 bers. Here, we investigate an alternative route for improving the properties of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics. The densi /uniFB01 -cation of the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics with rapid heating and short holding times at maximum temperature employed in spark plasma sintering is studied. Because the temperature is the key in /uniFB02 uencing factor, it is necessary to understand the effect of different holding temperatures on the microstructure and mechanical properties of the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics. The purpose of this work is to /uniFB01 nd the most suitable SPS condition for the ZrB2 -ZrO2f.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ =6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ =10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ =3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ =6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described previously. 10 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, as shown in previous work 10 and Fig. 1, are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density); (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

2. Experimental procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma-Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma-Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions; ZrB2, ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (ZS20), ZrB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC (HS20) and HfB2 + 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (HS20La). Ceramic powders were processed and then sintered using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) as described in a previous work. 22 The main microstructural features of SPS UHTCs, previously described, 22 are; (I) high density (>99% of theoretical relative density) except monolithic ZrB2 which has a density ∼ 95% as a consequence of O impurities which prevent its full densification; (II) ZrB2-based UHTCs contain a homogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the ZrB2 matrix with SiC grain sizes between ∼ 1 and 2 𝛍 m while HfB2-based UHTCs contain an inhomogeneous dispersion of SiC throughout the HfB2 matrix with SiC agglomerates as large as 20 𝛍 m; (III) La2O3 particles are often in close proximity to SiC particles.

1. Introduction
The insatiable demand for the development of hypersonic re-entry vehicle-structure thermal protection system mandate the usage of ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) [1,2] for hostile environmental conditions [3 -6]. Zirconium diboride (ZrB2), a potential UHTC, has a desirable combination of high melting point (3245 °C) [7], low density (6.09 g/cc), resistance to a chemical attack that makes it an attractive candidate for aforementioned applications [8 -11]. Nevertheless, the lower fracture toughness K IC = 2.3 -3.5 MPam 0.5 [12] and arduous sintering process has challenged the use of ZrB2 in wide applications. The sintering issues have been resolved using spark plasma sintering (SPS), a relatively novel method for consolidation of UHTC composites [3,4]. The mechanical and oxidation resistance properties of monolithic ZrB2 has signi /uniFB01 cantly been improved by reinforcing silicon carbide (SiC) as a second phase, making ZrB220SiC (20 vol%) as a base-line material [13,14]. However, this approach could not overcome the intrinsic brittleness, which limits their application in high damage tolerance structures. To overcome this meagreness, phases with high aspect ratio (such as graphite and carbon nanotubes, CNT) can be added in ZrB2-based UHTC [4,13,15 -17]. Carbon not only improves mechanical properties but, eliminates the oxide impurities at the surface in the starting materials (ZrO2, SiO2 etc.), resulting in an improved sintering [18]. The addition of CNT (10 vol%) has shown to enhance the fracture toughness of hot pressed

3.1. Materials selection and powder characterization
UHTC materials  are  mostly  sintered  using  pressure-assisted  technology -either  hot  pressing  or  spark  plasma  sintering -due  to  the general challenges when trying to sinter these materials to high density. However, pressureless sintering is required to sinter  UHTC materials with complex shapes enabled by additive manufacturing. We chose to evaluate ZrB2 based UHTC materials to demonstrate binder jet additive manufacturing  feasibility  because  ZrB2  is  arguably  the  most  studied UHTC material. We screened literature to identify pressureless sintered ZrB2  compositions that resulted in high relative densities and list examples in Table 1. The particles sizes from these studies are typical for conventional  UHTC  manufacturing  and  preferred  for  sintering,  but smaller than  what  is  typically  preferred for binder  jet additive manufacturing to ensure good flowability of the powder for spreading [24]. Of the materials evaluated, we selected the ZrB2-MoSi2  material system  to  evaluate  manufacturability  because  these  compositions improve the oxidation resistance of ZrB2, have a relatively low sintering temperature, and have other beneficial properties and characteristics.

2.3. Experimentation
This paper focuses on the fabrication of ZrB2-based ceramics with ZrSi2  additive using the electron beam melting (EBM) process. The experiments were conducted at the Center of Additive Manufacturing and Logistics (CAMAL) at NC State University. The processing of the powder particles is dependent on the electrical conductivity, the thermal properties, and the melting point of the starting powder mixture. 1D single distinct tracks were prepared and experimented to assess the feasibility of the fabrication of the ZrB2 -ZrSi2  mixture with EBM and to compare the results of the FE model. 6 pucks with 2.5 mm in diameter and 3.5 mm in depth were prepared by pressing the powders and then heating at 1000 ◦ C. Stainless steel base plate with corresponding holes was prepared to accommodate the pucks in the EBM machine. Six tracks were planned on four of the pucks, and square contour hatching was planned for two pucks to study the feasibility of the hatching process for the powders. 24 different sets of processing conditions were implemented for one-dimensional tracks under different electron beam energy inputs and the beam scanning speeds, as shown in Table 4. The chamber was initially flushed with inert gas multiple times before the experiment to remove any presence of oxygen. The chamber was then pumped down to ~10 GLYPH<0> 4 Torr. The vacuum environment prevents any undesired oxidation during the process and is required for electron beam travel. The initial heating sequence which includes heating the powder using a defocused electron beam was done by heating the center of the stainless steel base plate to 800 ◦ C. Then the programmed tracks were processed with the electron  beams  and  after  completion,  the  tracks  were  cooled  in

A B S T R A C T
Electron beam melting (EBM) is an additive manufacturing process based on the principle of heating powder in the powder bed using an electron beam as the high-intensity energy source [7]. Energy transfer takes  place  when  the  powder  particles  are  impinged  by  the  electron beam. The electrons travel along the direction of bombardment up to a couple of hundred microns and transfer the energy to the atoms on every collision  until  the  energy  decreases  below  the  threshold  energy  [7], These collisions increase the local surface temperature and result in the heat treatment for the affected area.
This  work  displays  the  feasibility  of  fabricating  UHTCs  using  the EBM process, which will open broad opportunities for advancing material applications in extreme environments. This study develops simulation tools for UHTC  fabrication using EBM,  enabling process Y. Jia et al.

question: What temperature thresholds around 1500-2000°C are described as critical operating or stability limits for ZrB2 ceramics, Hi-Nicalon SiC-fiber-reinforced borides, high-entropy pyrochlore thermistor ceramics, and hypersonic UHTC leading edges?
answer:
The temperature thresholds around 1500-2000°C described in the sources are as follows:

- For ZrB2 ceramics, a critical temperature threshold is 1127°C, above which pure ZrB2 exhibits rapid oxidation [2]. Additionally, the fracture toughness of ZrB2-based ceramics is significantly affected at 1500°C, with a notable reduction in flexure strength when tested at this temperature [19].
- For Hi-Nicalon SiC-fiber-reinforced borides, the effectiveness of the fibers as a reinforcing phase diminishes above 1700°C, where they start to degrade and can be considered just as a secondary phase [15]. 
- The information regarding high-entropy pyrochlore thermistor ceramics and hypersonic UHTC leading edges is not covered in the provided sources.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), a class of ceramic materials operating above 2000 ◦ C, primarily include borides, carbides, and nitrides of transition metals[1]. As critical materials for extreme environments, their oxidation resistance directly determines their service lifetime  and  reliability  under  high-temperature  oxidative  conditions. HfB ₂ -SiC is a representative UHTC system, widely applied in hypersonic vehicle nose cones, rocket engine nozzles, and other thermal protection components due to its ultra-high melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance[2]. In ultra-high-temperature oxidative  environments,  material  surfaces  undergo  intense  oxidation reactions,  forming  porous  or  volatile  oxide  layers  that  lead  to  rapid

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), integrated primarily by carbides, borides and some nitrides of early transition metals, are ultrarefractory materials that exhibit exceptional thermo-chemical stability and good mechanical strength even at high temperatures, which make them highly suitable for extreme environments [1 -3]. UHTCs find practical applications in aerospace [4], nuclear power, and hypersonic vehicles [5], where their excellent thermal and mechanical properties are convenient for the production of thermal shields, furnace linings, and structural components [6,7].
ZrB2 is the most studied UHTC, with a melting point of 3250 ◦ C [1], high strength and rigidity up to 2000 ◦ C [8], high hardness, remarkable chemical stability, and outstanding electrical and thermal conductivity [9 -13]. Despite its exceptional properties, pure ZrB2 faces several limitations for its practical industrial use. Due to its high melting point, it requires sintering temperatures ranging from 2100 to 2300 ◦ C, typically resulting in coarse microstructures with some residual porosity [14]. Additionally, pure ZrB2 exhibits brittle fracture and suffers from rapid oxidation at temperatures above 1127 ◦ C [15]. These weaknesses hinder its widespread adoption in high-temperature applications.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) include borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting temperatures above 2700 ◦ C [1]. UHTCs have been investigated for high temperature applications, including thermal protection systems for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, high temperature electrodes, and molten metal crucibles [2-5]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramics have attracted much attention because of their unique combination of low density, high melting temperature and thermal shock resistance as well as excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [6-10].
Generally pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperature (>1900 ◦ C) are required to achieve complete densification of pure ZrB2 material due to its strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coefficient. Several studies have shown that the addition of SiC to ZrB2 powders has beneficial effects not only on sinterability, but also on mechanical properties and resistance to oxidation [9-12]. Although great efforts have been devoted to enhancing the mechanical properties and much progress has been made recently, the susceptibility to brittle fracture is still a major issue for the use of ZrB2based UHTCs. Whisker-reinforced ceramic matrix composites are of promising materials, especially for high temperature applications [13-15]. SiC whiskers, combining high strength, elastic modulus and chemical inertness at elevated temperature, are

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are a class of refractory ceramics capable of operating in extreme environments (e.g. temperature above 2000 ◦ C, high heat fluxes, > 10 MW/m 2 ) [1]. They consist of borides, carbides and nitrides of early transition metals, which are characterized by a melting point above 3000 ◦ C [2]. Amongst ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), ZrB2  is the most interesting compound because it combines a relatively low density with high melting point, high strength and good electrical conductivity [3,4]. However, the oxidation resistance of pure ZrB2  is poor due to the formation of ZrO2  and evaporation of B2O3  above 1000 ◦ C [5]. To overcome  this  problem,  previous  studies  investigated  additives  such  as silicon  carbide (SiC) or other silicides that protect the material from oxidation up to 1600 ◦ C due to the formation of a glassy silica phase [6, 7]. Another main problem of ZrB2 based ceramics is their low fracture toughness [8], which limits their applicability when thermal shock and vibrations are present [9]. An effective method to improve their damage tolerance  has  become  the  addition  of  short  and  long  fibers,  such  as

1. Introduction
With the rapid development of hypersonic aircraft, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC), which are key thermal protection materials for their nose and leading edges, have received widespread attention [1 -3]. UHTC are a type of ceramic materials that can maintain physical and  chemical  stability  at  ultra-high  temperatures  ( ≥ 2000 ◦ C)  and extreme  service  environments.  They  mainly  include  transition  metal carbides, diborides and nitrides such as ZrB2, ZrC, and ZrN. However, their intrinsic brittleness, poor thermal shock resistance, and processing difficulties due to strong covalent bonding limit their application in the field of hypersonic aircraft [4,5]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop novel UHTC materials with high melting point, high damage tolerance, good mechanical properties and machinability.

Generally, the oxidation of ZrB2-SiC ceramics is negligible below 800 o C, as confirmed by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of SiC powder, ZrB2 powder, and ZrB2-SiC ceramics [46 -48]. When the temperature is between 800 o C and 1200 o C, ZrB2 oxidises faster than SiC [48 -50]. At an oxidation temperature of 800 o C, the base ZrB2-SiC ceramics oxidized slowly  with  no  obvious  changes  in  the  macrostructure,  as  shown  in Fig. 12(a), and the typical reaction for the ZrB2 oxidation is given below

1. Introduction
ZrB2 belongs  to  the  group  of  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics (UHTCs) and it has high melting point temperature of 3200 ◦ C and exhibits excellent properties such as moderate density (6.11 g/cc), high elastic modulus (489 GPa) and good electrical (10 7 S/m) and thermal conductivities (upto 140 W/mK) [1 -3]. Due to such good properties, ZrB2  can  be  used  in  thermal  protection  systems,  hypersonic  vehicle systems, solar concentrators and furnace elements [4]. Single phase ZrB2 is  difficult  to  densify  and  the  sintering  temperatures  of  more  than 1900 ◦ C would be required as it is characterized with strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion co-efficient [5]. Various ceramic additives such as SiC [6,7], MoSi2  [8 -10], Si3N4  [11 -14], AlN [15,16] and transition metals [17 -20] have been used to achieve fully dense ZrB2 ceramics. Recently, the addition of transition metals to ZrB2  ceramics achieved more attention due to its interesting microstructures, which improves  the  high-temperature  properties  [5].  The  transition  metals added in the form of silicides, carbides and even as in the form of pure metals [17 -20].
Recent studies  showed the improvement in densification of ZrB2based  composites  at  lower  sintering  temperatures  for  short  holding times using Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS). SPS is one of the advanced

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

5. Conclusions
During sintering of borides containing Hi-Nicalon fibers, the fibers evolve to a multi-layered structure: an almost unreacted core and a strongly modified rim, both in ZrB2 and in HfB2 matrices. The most important parameter affecting fiber transformation is the sintering temperature: the higher is the sintering temperature, the larger is the extent of fiber transformation. The fiber locally behaves as a sintering aid by cleaning borides from residual oxides and favoring densification in the closest proximity. Thus, the fiber/matrix interface is very strong and no bridging or pullout during crack propagation can be observed for any matrix or sintering additive. According to nanoindentation, the hardness and indentation modulus of the rim are higher than those of the core. This is mainly due to the elimination of turbostratic carbon and soft amorphous phases and to the crystallization of poorly organized Si-C phase as SiC platelets. These properties are instead independent of the type of sintering aid used. The toughening action of the Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers progressively decreases with increasing the sintering temperature owing to a drastic fiber change. Crack pinning was identified, and supported by data analysis, as the main toughening mechanism. At the end, this study enables to conclude that short SiC fibers can be considered a reinforcing phase until the majority of core is preserved, i.e. at around 1700 /C176 C, above this temperature they can be considered as a mere secondary phase.

a b s t r a c t
This paper deals with the effect of the addition of Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers to Zr- and Hf-borides. The main scope is to understand the fiber/matrix chemical interaction and correlate it to the fracture toughness. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used as key investigation tool to disclose the microstructural features at nanoscale level. Several sintering additives were used to enable densification in the temperature range 1600-1850 /C176 C. It was observed that the fiber strongly reacts with the matrix at the same temperature at which the sintering additive starts to be effective. At this point, the fibers themselves locally behave as sintering aid promoting a strong fiber/matrix bonding which prevents any possibility of fiber pullout. Fiber modification was correlated with the fracture toughness and it was at last deduced that these fibers exert a toughening action only when the sintering temperature is kept below 1700 /C176 C. Above this temperature fibers start to significantly degrade and can be considered just as a secondary phase.

1. Introduction
Zirconium and hafnium diboride (ZrB2 and HfB2) have been identified as two of the most promising materials for use as leading edges in hypersonic aerospace vehicles due to their potential performances over metal components in high-temperature environments, including low creep rate, high strength at elevated temperatures and high melting temperature [1-4]. Despite the well-known positive engineering attributes, these borides are brittle, characteristic which dramatically limits their use in those applications where failure of a small part would result catastrophic [1,3]. A well-known method for increasing the fracture toughness of brittle ceramics is the addition of a higher aspect ratio reinforcing phase [5]. Recent studies report on the development of self-reinforced microstructures [6], controlled growth of SiC platelets [7-9], and the use of discontinuous SiC whiskers [10-14]. The authors of the present work have extensively studied the effect of the addition of Hi-Nicalon SiC short fiber to borides matrices and have obtained fracture toughness values above 6 MPa m 1/2 [13-17]. Hyuga et al. [18] added short C fibers to Si3N4 ceramics and reported a toughness increase of about 50% accompanied by a 50% decrease of strength. Recently, chopped C fibers have been also introduced in a reaction bonded silicon carbide, again observing an increase in fracture toughness from 3 to 5 MPa m 1/2 [19], whilst other authors have used ZrO2 fibers to toughen borides

4.2. SiC continuous fiber reinforcement
Compared to Cf/UHTC materials, the literature available on continuous SiC reinforcements in UHTCMCs is severely limited. The architectures  exist  for  evaluation,  as  SiCf/SiC  composites  have  been  the subject of many studies since the mid-1900s. However, SiC fibers are known to become problematic at temperatures approaching 2000 ◦ C, as their mechanical properties tend to degrade at higher temperatures and the risk of active oxidation is significant. For that reason, the consensus is that SiCf materials will not have the capacity to operate effectively in applications  exceeding  2000 ◦ C,  which  is  why  the  high  temperature survivability of carbon fibers is preferred. Nevertheless, a handful of studies have conducted initial investigations into SiCf/UHTC materials. They will be reviewed here.
Lai [256] studied the compatibility of different SiC fibers and architectures in a ZrB2 -ZrSi2 matrix. Unidirectional Sylramic and M. Ammendola et al.
Fig. 24. SEM images of (a) fracture surface and (b -f) polished surfaces of ZrB2 composite containing long BN-coated Hi-Nicalon fiber and ZrSi2 as sintering additive [258]. Reproduced with permission, © 2015 Elsevier Ltd.

4.2. SiC continuous fiber reinforcement
Hi-Nicalon fibers were slurry coated in a tape casting method, followed by consolidation and densification via hot pressing. Significant reactions were observed between the matrix and fiber phases, which is consistent with the problems noted for short SiCf  composites. Microcracking was also  noted early in the study, attributed to the difference in thermal expansion  behavior  between  the  SiC  fibers  and  the  Zr-based  matrix phase. This was ameliorated by the inclusion of an inert BN interphase, which  reduced  the  fiber-matrix  interactions  and  improved  the  mechanical properties of the composites. Samples with uncoated Hi Nicalon fibers possessed a work of fracture of 1200 J/m 2 ; comparatively, the BN-coated fibers increased that value to 3500 kJ/m 2 . It should be noted that  these  work  values  were  determined  from  the  load-displacement curves of as-fabricated samples, without the inclusion of a notch per typical  fracture  characterization  techniques.  As  such,  these  metrics cannot be evaluated against other composites reported in literature, but they do offer a point of internal comparison within the individual study.

3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
It  should  be  noted  that  the  aforementioned  studies  on  short  SiCf reinforcement  have  exclusively  employed  Hi-Nicalon  fibers  in  UHTC matrices. In order to avoid the interfacial obstacles observed with this material, a second, more refractory SiC fiber has also been investigated in the literature: Tyranno SA3. Compared to the Hi-Nicalon materials, Tyranno fibers are manufactured at a higher temperature while using Al as a sintering additive. The more efficient pyrolysis merits greater refractory behavior in the material and fewer reactant species on the skin of the fiber. These differences are realized when hot pressing, as there are  cleaner  boundaries  between  the  Tyranno  fibers  and  the  matrix phase, contrasted to the jagged interfaces often observed for Hi-Nicalon SiCf  after sintering [143]. Even so, fractography imaging revealed that fiber  pullout  mechanisms  were  still  limited.  Regarding  mechanical properties,  Tyranno  composites  showed  marginally  higher  flexure strengths  (417 -457  MPa)  and  marginally  lower  fracture  toughness values (4.7 -5.1 MPa m ½ ) when compared to materials reinforced with Hi-Nicalon  fibers,  although  the  metrics  were  all-around  comparable. Composites  with  either  fiber  reinforcement  showed  good  retained bending  strength  up  to  1200 ◦ C  (347 -453  MPa),  but  significant  reductions were observed for tests conducted at 1500 ◦ C (80 -277 MPa). All things considered, the Tyranno fibers did not decisively outperform Hi-Nicalon  fibers,  as  the  desired  toughening  mechanisms  from  fiber pullout remained largely untapped [144].

1. Continuous SiC Fibers as the Reinforcement
Continuous SiC fibers contribute to the toughening of ceramics as the interface in  SiC f /SiC  composites  works  effectively.  Furthermore,  continuous  SiC  fibers are formed into the SiC fiber preform composed of multidimensional woven and braided structures (two-dimensional woven cloth, three-dimensional woven fabric, complex braids shaped as the desired component and so on). The shape of the SiC fiber preform mostly determines the final shape of the SiC f /SiC composites. question: What hardness values (in GPa) have been reported for Zr-Ta multiboride ceramic composites compared with the core and rim regions of Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers embedded in ZrB2 matrices?
answer: The sources do not contain specific hardness values for Zr-Ta multiboride ceramic composites. Additionally, the comparison of core and rim regions of Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers embedded in ZrB2 matrices in terms of hardness is not covered in the provided sources.

3.4. Nanoindentation measurements
The results of the nanoindentation tests are summarized in Table 1. As for ZrB2, the hardness and stiffness of the core are around 32 GPa and 330 GPa, respectively, and increase up to 38 and 410 GPa in the rim. For HfB2, the core hardness and stiffness are around 25 and 290 GPa, whilst in the rim they are 37 and 450 GPa, respectively. The experimental data reveal that the fiber core is always softer and less stiff than the fiber rim. The change of these properties was affected mainly by the change in the fiber morphology, as discussed later. However, some differences in the rim values seem to be related to the nature of the matrix. For instance, the indentation modulus of the rim is significantly higher for HfB2-based composites ( /C24 450 GPa) than for ZrB2 ( /C24 410 GPa). This is due to the fact that the indentation modulus is a long range property and for sure is influenced by the higher Young's modulus of the HfB2 matrix compared to that of ZrB2. On the other hand, hardness, which is a short range property, is basically dictated by the properties of the rim indented volume, which is mainly formed by crystalline SiC, independently of the matrix material. As for the core, hardness and stiffness of the fibers are significantly lower in HfB2 than in ZrB2. This is related to the fiber composition and to the mean grain size of SiC crystallites of the core: since the ZrB2-based composites were sintered at higher temperature and for longer time (hot-pressing instead of SPS-sintering as for HfB2), the fiber was purged of higher amount of soft phases, i.e. amorphous Si-C and turbostratic carbon, and, at the same time, a major crystallite coarsening occurred promoting an increase of the core hardness.
Finally, in the same family of composites, i.e. ZrB2- or HfB2based, values are nearly independent of the sintering aid used.

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
In the presence of the graphite nano-flakes, higher hardness (of ~16.5 GPa) and fracture toughness (of ~7.1 MPam 1/2 ) were achieved as compared to similar composites, but sans the graphite flakes. In another work, Guo et al. [262] reported that the addition amorphous carbon spheres (5 vol%) helped in improving the fracture toughness (to ~7.52 MPam 1/2 ) of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC, but upon compromising on the hardness (~16.6 GPa) of the ZrB2-SiC-Cspheres. The toughening was primarily due to weak grain boundary interfaces. On a different note, the use of BN-coated Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers aided achieving near theoretical sinter density (of ~99%ρth) in the case of ZrB2-10 vol% ZrSi2 ceramics at a considerably lower temperature of 1450 °C using hot press [263]. From Table 5, it can be realized that ZrB2 ceramics exhibit hardness, fracture toughness and flexural strength in the ranges of ~6.7-32.5 GPa, ~2-15.75 MPam 1/2 and ~168-1089 MPa, respectively (see Table 5 for more specific information). The lower values refers to mainly poor densification of ZrB2.

One-dimensional ZrB2 crystals with tube structure synthesized using a two-step heat treatment approach
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are commonly referred to as ceramics with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C and are desirable for structural applications at temperatures above 1500 ◦ C [1,2]. Owing to its excellent properties, like high melting point and lowest density in UHTCs, ZrB2 has been extensively studied [3,4]. Based on the difficulties in densification of monolithic ZrB2  due to strong covalent bonds, low diffusion coefficient, and limited fracture toughness, numerous studies focused on using sintering additives and reinforcing phases to fabricate ZrB2-based composite ceramics with comparatively improved properties [5,6]. Wang et al. [7] fabricated almost dense ZrB2-SiC composites using AlN and Si3N4  as sintering aids. The  fracture toughness of ZrB2  was increased from 5.46 MPa m 1/2 to 6.81 MPa m 1/2 by using 15 vol% SiC whiskers.  Demirskyi  et  al.  [8]  reported  a  Zr-Ta  multiboride  ceramic composite with highly reproducible repetitive superstructure via reaction-driven consolidation of ZrB2, Ta, and amorphous B powders, which led to extremely high hardness (28.6 GPa and 22.6 GPa using 98 N and 196 N loads).

3.2. Mechanical properties
Table 1 shows the nanoindentation results and it is also compared with  literature  [31,36 -39].  The  nano-indentation  hardness  of  16.81, 15.23 and 18.62 GPa was measured for ZS-2.5Ta, ZS-5Ta and ZS-10Ta samples, respectively. Additionally, the bulk hardness of ZrB2  composites was measured using the Vickers hardness and almost similar trend and hardness of 16.62, 15.59 and 17.75 GPa was measured for ZS-2.5Ta, ZS-5Ta and ZS-10Ta samples, respectively. The bulk hardness of samples was measured by Vickers hardness equipment (M/s. Shimadzu, HMV, Japan) by applying a load of 19.6 N for 15 s. It indicates that both the nanoindentation results and Vickers hardness values are more or less the same. The hardness values were relatively high for ZS-10Ta composition due to core-rim structure and presence of (Zr,Ta)C phase. On the other hand, ZS-5Ta composition achieved lower hardness due to the presence of  ZrO2 phase.  Dorner  et  al.  [31]  also  observed  that  ZrB2-3at.%  Ta composite achieved lower hardness due to the presence of ZrO2, which is having  lower  hardness  of  12  GPa.  Interestingly,  the  ZrB2-6at.%  Ta exhibited higher hardness than ZrB2-3at.% Ta. From Table 1, it can be realized that the hardness of ZrB2 -SiC slightly lowered (from 16.81 to 15.23 GPa) with 5%Ta and the hardness increased to 18.62 GPa with further addition of 10%Ta and it can be attributed to the absence of ZrO2 phase and the presence of (Zr,Ta)C phase. In case of Ni (upto 8 mol%) added  ZrB2-25mol%  SiC,  there  was  tendency  of  slight  decrease  in hardness [37].

1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.
enhanced relative density and fracture toughness have been reported for ZrB2-SiC composites by adding 10-30 vol% carbon fiber, the hardness and flexural strength have dropped [19,20]. Adding ZrC can be a good trick for compensating such a drop in hardness and flexural strength. Adding ZrC to ZrB2-SiC composites, individually, has also been investigated by several researchers [21-25]. Table 1 summarizes the average values of relative density and mechanical properties for some of ZrB2-based ceramics containing SiC, ZrC and/or Cf additives.

A B S T R A C T
In the recent years, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) based on transition metal carbides (TaC, ZrC, TiC) and diborides (ZrB2, TiB2) have emerged as promising materials for re-entry applications. A boride -carbide dualphase composite was designed in order to achieve good mechanical properties with a fine microstructure and strong interfacial bonding. Three compositions: (i) (Zr,Ti)B2, (ii) (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, and (iii) dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 were synthesized using spark plasma sintering at 1850 ◦ C, 40 MPa for 30 min, achieving ~99 % densification. Phase analysis confirmed single-phase solid solutions in (Zr,Ti)B2  and (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, while the dual-phase (Zr,Ti, Ta)C,B2  composite comprised ~42 % boride and ~58 % carbide. Nanoindentation analysis (8 mN) showed a uniform hardness (~36 GPa) for all the compositions, whereas microindentation revealed intermediate hardness of ~27 GPa for the dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2, as compared to that of ~29 GPa for (Zr,Ti,Ta)C and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ti)B2

3.3. Hardness
Despite all compositions containing four metals instead of the typical five for so-called high-entropy UHTCs, the hardness values were comparable  to  those  reported  for  dual  phase  boride-carbide  ceramics  in other studies. As an example, Huo et al. [15] achieved a hardness of 28.4 GPa using a 9.8 N load for the (Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr)B2-(Hf,Nb,Ta,Ti,Zr) C composition. Similarly, Smith et al. [16] obtained a HV0.2 of 27 GPa, while Naughton-Duszova et al. [24] found a HV1  of 29.4 GPa. For the same composition, Hassan et al.  [21] obtained a  hardness  of  HV0.05 = 47.5 ± 4 GPa and HV1 = 29.3 ± 1.4 GPa. For comparison, the dual phase ceramics in the present study ranged from 22.7 GPa to 27.4 GPa at a load of 9.8 N. The highest hardness for a dual phase ceramic was reported to be 48.6 ± 2.2 GPa at a load of 0.49 N and ~26.6 GPa at a load of 9.8 N, in a composition containing small amounts of Mo and W [4]. These differences in hardness among the HEDP ceramics indicate the importance of metal selection in optimizing properties of compositionally complex ceramics.

5. Conclusions
During sintering of borides containing Hi-Nicalon fibers, the fibers evolve to a multi-layered structure: an almost unreacted core and a strongly modified rim, both in ZrB2 and in HfB2 matrices. The most important parameter affecting fiber transformation is the sintering temperature: the higher is the sintering temperature, the larger is the extent of fiber transformation. The fiber locally behaves as a sintering aid by cleaning borides from residual oxides and favoring densification in the closest proximity. Thus, the fiber/matrix interface is very strong and no bridging or pullout during crack propagation can be observed for any matrix or sintering additive. According to nanoindentation, the hardness and indentation modulus of the rim are higher than those of the core. This is mainly due to the elimination of turbostratic carbon and soft amorphous phases and to the crystallization of poorly organized Si-C phase as SiC platelets. These properties are instead independent of the type of sintering aid used. The toughening action of the Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers progressively decreases with increasing the sintering temperature owing to a drastic fiber change. Crack pinning was identified, and supported by data analysis, as the main toughening mechanism. At the end, this study enables to conclude that short SiC fibers can be considered a reinforcing phase until the majority of core is preserved, i.e. at around 1700 /C176 C, above this temperature they can be considered as a mere secondary phase.

3.2. SiC short fiber reinforcement
Despite improvements in fracture toughness, the apparent deficiency in pullout activation is a significant concern for Hi-Nicalon SiC fibers. Toughening mechanisms are still present in the form of crack pinning and bowing, but the failure to engage in effective fiber pullout behavior severely undercuts the toughness and strain tolerance of the composites. 3.2. Mechanical properties

It is observed that with 10 wt% tantalum addition ZrB2-20 vol % SiC achieved highest hardness and elastic modulus due to core-rim structure  in  microstructure.  With  ZS-5Ta,  it  is  observed  that  lowest hardness  and  elastic  modulus  due  to  presence  residual  oxide  phase (ZrO2) and conversely even though there is presence of ZrO2 phase in ZS10Ta, the presence of additional (Zr,Ta)C phase in the microstructure resulted in highest hardness and elastic modulus values. It is to be noted that ZrO2 (12 GPa) [2] shows less hardness compared to (Zr,Ta)C (~26 GPa) [32] phases. The relative amount of (Zr,Ta)C phase in ZS-5Ta and ZS-10Ta samples were calculated using ImageJ software from the microstructures. It is observed that ~0.11 vol% and ~2.2 vol% amounts of (Zr,Ta)C phase was observed in ZS-5Ta and ZS-10Ta samples, respectively. Hence ZS-10Ta composite exhibited relatively high hardness and elastic modulus values due to the presence of hard (Zr,Ta)C phase.

4. Conclusions

Theoretical calculation of reinforcement factor (Rf) ~ 0.80 in dual  phase  (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2  suggests  good  interfacial  bonding  between carbide  and  boride  phases,  which,  along  with  crack  deflection  and bridging, resulted an increase in the fracture toughness of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 to 3.7 MPa.m 1/2 as compared to that of 2.4 MPa.m 1/2 for single phase diboride (Zr,Ti)B2 while being approximately similar (i.e. 3.8 MPa.m 1/2 ) to that of single phase carbide (Zr,Ti,Ta)C. Therefore, dual-phase (Zr,Ti, Ta)C,B2  composite, characterised by its remarkable mechanical properties, is a strong candidate for hypersonic application; however, thermal and oxidation behaviour of these composites needs to be evaluated further.

3.4.1. Hardness
At  the  nanoscale,  all  compositions  exhibit  comparable  hardness values. Consequently, bulk hardness was assessed using Vickers indentation, revealing lower values (24 -29 GPa) compared to that of nanoindentation. The microhardness of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 at 4.9 N is 27.25 ± 1.8 GPa, falling in between that of (Zr,Ti,Ta)C (29.25 ± 1.5 GPa) and (Zr,Ti) B2  (24.34 ± 1.9 GPa). Variations in hardness at different scales can be attributed to the indenter size effect [41]. Nanoindentation, with a ~150 nm indenter  tip  and  low  loads  (e.g.  8  mN),  confines  indents  within grains (2 -3 μ m), thereby measuring the intrinsic hardness of a specific phase by minimizing the influence of grain boundaries, microporosity, and  defects  [36].  In  contrast,  microindentation  employs  a  larger indenter and higher load (e.g., 4.9 N), encompassing both grain interiors and boundaries, which introduces contributions from these heterogeneities,  resulting  in  lower  hardness  values  [42].  Furthermore,  the dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 composite, having finer grains, lower porosity, and a 58 % carbide phase fraction, exhibits higher hardness than the boride (Zr,Ti)B2. Moreover, the Vickers hardness of all three specimens is more than that of their monolithic counterparts [43,44], because of the solid solution hardening. Even for dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, B2  composites, hardness values (~27 GPa) are higher than the previously reported many dual phase composites i.e., ~24 GPa for (Hf, Nb, Ta, Ti, Zr) boride-carbide dual phase [11], ~23 and ~24 GPa for (Zr,Ta) C and (Hf,Zr,Ta)C [44], respectively, as shown in Fig. 7b.

4. Conclusions
This study has synthesized a dense (~99 %) multi-component dualphase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2  composite via spark plasma sintering. Phase and microstructural analysis confirmed a dual-phase structure comprising 58 % cubic carbide (rock salt-type) and 42 % hexagonal boride (AlB2type) phases. The lattice strain in dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 composite, with values of ~1.35 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 for the boride phase and ~1.19 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 for the carbide phase, lies between those of their single-phase counterparts (~1.68 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 for (Zr,Ti)B2  and ~0.622 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 for (Zr,Ti,Ta)C), indicating optimized cation distribution. Significant grain refinement of the boride phase (~2.8 μ m) compared to that of single-phase (Zr,Ti)B2 (~12 μ m) was observed, driven by the pinning effect of the carbide phase. Grain refinement in dual phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2  resulted in an improved hardness  and  flexural  strength  (27.25 ± 1.8  GPa,  ~435  MPa)  as compared to that of the single phase boride (Zr,Ti)B2 (24.34 ± 1.9 GPa, ~212 MPa) and approach towards that of single phase carbide (Zr,Ti,Ta) C  (29.25 ± 1.5  GPa,  ~443  MPa),  indicating  that  in  the  dual-phase boride -carbide  composite,  the  carbide  matrix  strengthens  the  boride K.P. 3.3. Fracture toughness
The fracture toughness was measured only on selected materials according to [24] with relative density higher than 98% to avoid any effect of the residual porosity, Table 1. For the sake of comparison toughness values are reported also for unreinforced matrices, e.g. ZrB2 + 5 vol% Si3N4 (ZS, [13]), ZrB2 + 10 vol% ZrSi2 (ZZ, [15]), ZrB2 + 15 vol% MoSi2 (ZM, [15]), HfB2 + 8 vol% Si3N4 (HS, [19]). In most cases an increase of toughness of 40-50% is obtained upon addition of short SiC fibers [13-17]. However, for each matrix material, ZrB2 or HfB2, the fracture toughness values of the respective composites are quite scattered. In the case of ZMf, the fracture toughness of the composite is almost the same as the starting matrix. Considering the effect of the sintering aid, it can be seen that ZrSi2 is the most effective one for toughening both ZrB2 and HfB2 matrix, see Table 1. As for the matrix, the ZrB2 composites have a higher fracture toughness compared to the respective HfB2 composites. The obtained values are in the same range of other reported values for similar materials containing chopped fibers, despite the use of different techniques and different type of fibers [20,21].

3.2. Mechanical properties

However, the transition metal added ZrB2 -SiC composites reportedly exhibited hardness between 18.6 and 19.4 GPa and elastic modulus between 440 and 453 GPa. It infers that addition of additives significantly affecting the mechanical properties of ZrB2.

question: What range of porosity or relative density percentages has been reported for sintered UHTC composites such as ZrB2-SiC-TaB2 dual composites, Cf/C-UHTC carbon-fabric composites, and ZrB2/TiB2-based UHTCMCs?
answer:
The range of porosity and relative density percentages reported for sintered UHTC composites are as follows:

- For ZrB2-SiC-TaB2 dual composites, the relative density was reported to be 99.11% and 99.63% [13].
- For Cf/C-UHTC carbon-fabric composites, the open porosity was reported to be 17.59% [15], while another study reported a porosity of 8.44% for a ZrC-Cf/C-ZrC-SiC composite [16].
- For ZrB2/TiB2-based UHTCMCs, the relative density was reported to be greater than 95% [11].

These values indicate that the porosity and relative density can vary significantly depending on the composite composition and fabrication method.

[15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. Very recently, slurry infiltration followed by pressure assisted sintering (hot pressing and spark plasma sintering) was adopted for the development of UHTCMCs [19 -22]. Sciti et al. [19] fabricated carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 base UHTCMCs by vacuum-bag infiltration and hot pressing, using 1D fabric carbon preforms, having various fiber volume fractions  from  55  to  70  %.  The  reported  bulk  density,  porosity  and flexural strength of these composites are in the range of 2.4 -3.1 g/cm 3 , 16 -28%  and  40 -95  MPa  respectively  [19]. 1. Introduction
Characteristics of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) reinforced with ZrC and carbon fiber (Cf) were investigated in this article. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) process was utilized to fabricate the samples at 1800 °C for 5 min under 30 MPa punch pressure and vacuumed atmosphere. In all samples, the volume ratio of ZrB2: SiC was equal to 4:1, and the summation of ZrC and Cf reinforcements was 7.5 vol% with different ZrC: Cf ratios. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS), densitometry, flexural strength, and hardness measurements were employed for characterization of the prepared samples. Microstructural inspection revealed the formation of SiC sheath around the carbon fibers due to several reactions in the surface SiO2 layers existed on the SiC particles. Optimal flexural strength (628.4 MPa) and hardness (20.8 GPa) values were achieved for the sample co-reinforced with 6.5 vol% ZrC and 1 vol% Cf, with a relative density of 97.7%.
enhanced relative density and fracture toughness have been reported for ZrB2-SiC composites by adding 10-30 vol% carbon fiber, the hardness and flexural strength have dropped [19,20]. Adding ZrC can be a good trick for compensating such a drop in hardness and flexural strength. Adding ZrC to ZrB2-SiC composites, individually, has also been investigated by several researchers [21-25]. Table 1 summarizes the average values of relative density and mechanical properties for some of ZrB2-based ceramics containing SiC, ZrC and/or Cf additives.

Table 5
Zhang et al. [215] refined the approach and achieved exceptional mechanical properties in their fabricated Cf/ZrB2 -SiC material. Starting with a PyC-coated 3D carbon architecture, a slurry impregnation process was  carried  out  with  ZrB2 particles,  utilizing  vacuum  and  vibration assistance to enhance penetration through the weave. The slurry infiltration  step  was  proceeded  by  PIP  process,  in  which  liquid  polycarbosilane  was  introduced  into  the  composite  in  a  high-pressure chamber. After five pyrolysis cycles at 1300 ◦ C, crystalline SiC from the precursor effectively filled much of the porosity between ZrB2 particles. This  procedure  is  schematically  and  experimentally  represented  in Fig.  21.  The  final  composite  achieved  92  %  density  and  showed outstanding  mechanical  properties  compared  to  other  UHTCMCs  reported in literature. A high flexural strength (603 MPa) and high fracture toughness (18.1 MPa m ½ ) were recorded from mechanical testing, and the work of fracture (11.9 kJ/m 2 ) was superior to any other values reported in literature for UHTCMCs. These results are highly encouraging for the prospects of designing dense UHTC-based composites with high mechanical toughness and tolerance.
Continuous carbon fiber composites are currently the leading area of focus for the development of UHTCMCs. Unique processing approaches have made definitive progress in overcoming the relevant manufacturing challenges, where UHTC matrices have been adequately infiltrated  and  densified  in  complex  carbon  fiber  architectures. 3.1 Density of the composites and volume percentage of constituents
The densities and volume percentages of the constituents in the composites are reported in Table 1. The density of the composites has been found to remain consistent across all the prepared samples, as confirmed by a value of 2.4 g/cm 3 obtained for the UD-0/0 ◦ composite, which is comparable with 2.5 g/cm 3 measured for both UD-0/90 ◦ and 8H-SW composites. The volume percentages of the constituent phases have been measured to be similar for both UD-0/0 ◦ and UD-0/90 ◦ composites, having C f fabric with unidirectionally oriented fibers. In contrast, the 8H-SW composite has been found to contain a higher fiber volume percentage, surpassing the amounts in each of the composites madewithUDCf fabrics by approximately 14%. The higher amount of fibers in 8H-SW composite is attributed to its woven fiber architecture, which has resulted in higher C f content in the fabric.
The porosity of the composites made with UD Cf fabric is measured to be in a range of 18%-21%. On the other hand, the amount of porosity in the 8H-SW composite is found to be less by ∼ 50% compared to both UD-0/0 ◦ and UD-0/90 ◦ composites. Such a large difference in the amounts of porosity can be attributed to the existence of lesser intra-bundle as well as inter-bundle porosity in the 8H-SW Cf fabric owing to its processing method, which involves stretching and weaving operations. Despite these differences, the matrix content of the composites with all three different fiber architectures investigated in this study has been found to be consistently similar, that is, in the range of 25%-28%, which indicates that the lower porosity content of the 8H-SW composite is compensated by its higher fiber content compared to the composites having UDfiber orientations.
TABLE 1 Density and volume percentages of fiber ( V f ), matrix ( V m), and porosity ( V p ) of composites with different fiber architectures.

2.1. Hot-pressing
Hot-pressing (HP) is a conventional method for the fabrication of structural ceramics and composites [26]. HP of monolithic UHTCs is di /uniFB03 cult because their high melting points and low self-di /uniFB00 usion constants require a high sintering temperature ( ∼ 2000 °C) [16,27,28]. Various sintering aids and sintering methods such as HP, spark plasma sintering (SPS), and pressure-less sintering (PS) have been used, and high relative densities (98 -99%) have been achieved for UHTC compacts [16,20,24,29,30]. Guo [16] has reviewed the sintering behavior and basic properties of sintered UHTCs.
For the fabrication of C/UHTC composites by HP, carbon /uniFB01 bers are generally used as an additive. Carbon /uniFB01 bers having a length of ∼ 1 mm are known as ' milled carbon /uniFB01 bers ' (Cmil), while those having a length of 1 -6 mm are known as ' short carbon /uniFB01 bers ' (Csf). Yang et al. [31 -33] prepared a 20 vol% Csf-dispersed ZrB2 -20vol%SiC composite (Csf/ZS) by HP at 2000 °C and 30 MPa for 1 h. They achieved a relative density of ∼ 99% and used the same level of relative density as that used for preparing ZrB2-based composites without /uniFB01 ber [31 -33]. Guo et al. [34] also fabricated a 0 -50-vol% Cmil-dispersed ZrB2 -20vol%SiC composite (Cmil/ZS) with a relative density of 98 -99% by HP at 2000 °C under a pressure of 20 MPa for 1 h. They also prepared a 20 -50 vol% Cmil-dispersed HfB2 -20vol%SiC composite (Cmil/HS) with a relative density of 98 -99% by HP at 2100 °C under a pressure of 20 MPa for 1 h [35].

3.1. Relative density and microstructure
The variation of relative density of ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites with reinforcement content (SiC and CNT) is presented in Fig. 1. The relative density of monolithic ZrB2 is also indicated. For the given SPS processing parameters (sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C, soaking time of 15 min, and pressure of 70 MPa), the relative density of monolithic ZrB2 was about 85%. For the similar processing parameters, the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites exhibited much higher relative densities (>95%). In general, the relative density of the composites increased with the reinforcement content. The data on relative density showed relatively larger scatter at lower reinforcement levels (10% SiC and 2% CNT). For ZrB2-SiC composites, the samples reached near-full densification (relative density >99%) at all reinforcement levels (10-40% SiC). While similar trend of densification was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites, the near-full densification was achieved at reinforcement levels of 4-6% CNTs. The relative density of ZrB2-CNT composite reinforced with 2% CNTs was found to be about 95%. Clearly, very small amount of CNT reinforcement is sufficient to achieve full densification in these composites.

2. Materials and methods
The phase composition of the dual composite samples is represented by ZrB2, TaB2 and SiC, and the relative intensity of XRD peaks reflects the volume content of the corresponding component, Fig. 3. It can be seen from the figure that the XRD pattern of the HC composite practically does not differ from DC10, except for a lower level of background radiation, which may be due to the lower ' development ' of the surface and the density of interphase boundaries. In the given X-ray patterns, an increase in the width of X-ray reflections is noticeable. The broadening of peaks at low diffraction angles can be caused by an increase in the dispersity of blocks inside ZrB2 grains.
The  microstress  values  for  the  components  indicate  that  microstresses rapidly increase in TaB2  but gradually decrease in ZrB2 and SiC with increasing concentration of composite inclusions [28 -30], Fig. 4. The most probable reason for this dependence is the different CTE values of the phases. According to the literature data, the CTE of SiC is 7.9 ⋅ 10 GLYPH<0> 6 K GLYPH<0> 1 , and at 2000 ◦ C it can lie in the range of 5.1 -5.8 ⋅ 10 GLYPH<0> 6  K GLYPH<0> 1 [31], which is in good agreement with the range 5.3 -5.9 ⋅ 10 GLYPH<0> 6  K GLYPH<0> 1  demonstrated by ZrB2 [25,32]. The CTE of TaB2 is reported to be 8.2 ⋅ 10 GLYPH<0> 6  K GLYPH<0> 1 [33]. An increase in the concentration of TaB2 can lead to an increase in tensile stresses in ZrB2  and SiC, and to the formation of compressive stresses in TaB2 itself. The sign of stress can be determined using Hsueh ' s technique [34]. The higher microstresses in ZrB2  and SiC compared to TaB2 at a 10 vol % fraction of composite inclusions may be due to their insufficient concentration.

3. Results and discussion
Relative density values of the composite samples after the SPS process are presented in Fig. 4. With an average value of 99.58%, the highest relative density belongs to sample No. 3. The results of water absorption and open porosity measurements (not reported here), with the lowest values for sample No. 3 compared to other ones, are in good harmony with this outcome. Sample No. 1, as the baseline which is free of ZrC and Cf additives, reached a relative density of 93.66%. The low relative density of this sample can not only be related to the insu /uniFB03 cient sintering temperature, but can also to presence of oxide impurities on the surface of ZrB2 and SiC particles in the absence of carbon additive as a reductant/sintering aid. This is while; the relative density enhances (samples No. 2&3) by the addition of ZrC and Cf up to 5 vol% and 2.5 vol%, respectively. Such positive roles of ZrC and Cf additions on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -SiC composites were also reported in the literature [28,36]. Anyway, by adding higher amounts of these reinforcements, the negative role of extra Cf on densi /uniFB01 cation can be expected since the relative densities of samples No. 4 and No. 5 were dropped to 95.38% and 90.67%, respectively. Such a low relative density of sample No. 5 can be related to the agglomeration of carbon /uniFB01 bers in some areas.

3.1. Composition and microstructure
The density and open porosity of the prepared different Hf/Zr-based UHTCs modified C/C composites were shown in Fig. 1, revealing that all the  composites  were  dense  with  the  open  porosity  not  exceeding 6.25 vol%. The density of the prepared C/ZrC-SiC composites was the lowest at 2.57 ± 0.21 g/cm 3 . While with increasing HfSi2 content in the hybrid alloys for RMI, the density of the prepared C/HfC-ZrC-SiC composites increased. The prepared C/HfC-SiC composite had the highest density of 3.82 ± 0.44 g/cm 3 , since the density of infiltrated HfSi2  was higher than that of ZrSi2, as well as the density of the generated HfC in the matrix was higher than that of ZrC.
The X-ray diffraction patterns of the prepared different Hf/Zr-based UHTCs modified C/C composites were shown in Fig. 2(a), indicating that the composites were mainly composed of C, β -SiC, HfC/ZrC, HfSi2/ ZrSi2, and ZrSi. In HZC31, HZC11, and HZC13, HfC and ZrC formed a solid solution, as did HfSi2 and ZrSi2. Due to the very similar atomic radii of Hf and Zr [17 , 32], there was no obvious 2 θ shift in the diffraction peaks of the (Hf, Zr)C solid solutions compared with HfC and ZrC, as well as  the  (Zr,  Hf)Si2 solid  solutions  compared  with  HfSi2  and  ZrSi2,  as shown in Fig. 2(a). A B S T R A C T
Authors of the present work have focused their studies on the processing of UHTCMCs, adopting the method of slurry impregnation and sintering. This method allowed to achieve high density composites, with high fractions of UHTC phase and a quite strong fibre/matrix interface [1,16,17].  The  most  investigated  option  for  the  matrix  was  ZrB2, because  it  possesses  the  best  combination  of  refractoriness  (melting point  3250 ◦ C),  oxidation  resistance  and  low  specific  weight  (6.1 g/cm 3 ). Blends of ZrB2 and SiC powders were studied to understand the effect of SiC addition on mechanical properties and oxidation resistance of  UHTCMCs  at  intermediate  temperature  (1650 ◦ C)  [18].  In  other focused  studies,  the  matrices  were  based  on  ZrC,  TaC  and  HfC.  The bending strength of these composites was measured up to 2100 ◦ C [19, 20],  showing  very  promising  results.  As  for  the  consolidation  techniques, beside hot pressing [1,16,17], it has been recently demonstrated that spark plasma sintering is an effective method to achieve densification in a few minutes for these composites, provided that the thermal cycle is finely calibrated to avoid excessive reactions at the interface with the fibre [21]. Finally, for the high temperature characterization, high enthalpy arc-jet tests demonstrated the capability of Cf/ZrB2 -SiC composites to withstand temperatures as high as 2500 ◦ C, with limited erosion,  owing  to  the  partial  healing  of  surface  flaws  let  by  fiber burn-out by readily formed silica-based glass [22 -24].

A B S T R A C T
under  oxyacetylene  torch  flame  testing.  Paul  et  al.  [15]  fabricated Carbon fibre reinforced UHTC composites having various compositions including ZrB2, HfB2 base matrix through slurry impregnation and final densification with carbon through CVI technique. The final densities of the composites were in the range of 1.86 -2.11 ± 0.001 g/cm 3 and reported  that  Halfnium  (Hf)  based  UHTC  offered  superior  oxidation resistance [15]. Reactive melt infiltration (RMI) was also used in the development of UHTCMCs, in which carbon fibre performs were infiltrated wuth Zr or Hf based alloys and facilitated in-situ formation of UHTCM phases [16 -18]. Luo et al. [17] fabricated C -SiC-HfC composite by melt infiltration of HfSi2  alloy in to the porous preform of Cf-SiC composite at 1600 -1700 ◦ C. The reported density and porosity of the composite  were  2.60 ± 0.02  g/cm 3 and  17.4 ± 0.1%,  respectively [16 -18]. Very recently, slurry infiltration followed by pressure assisted sintering (hot pressing and spark plasma sintering) was adopted for the development of UHTCMCs [19 -22]. Sciti et al. [19] fabricated carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 base UHTCMCs by vacuum-bag infiltration and hot pressing, using 1D fabric carbon preforms, having various fiber volume fractions  from  55  to  70  %.  The  reported  bulk  density,  porosity  and flexural strength of these composites are in the range of 2.4 -3.1 g/cm 3 , 16 -28%  and  40 -95  MPa  respectively  [19].  Vinci  et  al.  studied  the oxidation behaviour of carbon fiber reinforced ZrB2 -SiC composites by thermogravimetric  analysis  up  to  1550 ◦ C  and  reported  that  of 800 -1000 ◦ C is  critical  temperature  range,  where  rapid  oxidation

Table 5
Zhang et al. [215] refined the approach and achieved exceptional mechanical properties in their fabricated Cf/ZrB2 -SiC material. Starting with a PyC-coated 3D carbon architecture, a slurry impregnation process was  carried  out  with  ZrB2 particles,  utilizing  vacuum  and  vibration assistance to enhance penetration through the weave. The slurry infiltration  step  was  proceeded  by  PIP  process,  in  which  liquid  polycarbosilane  was  introduced  into  the  composite  in  a  high-pressure chamber. After five pyrolysis cycles at 1300 ◦ C, crystalline SiC from the precursor effectively filled much of the porosity between ZrB2 particles. This  procedure  is  schematically  and  experimentally  represented  in Fig.  21.  The  final  composite  achieved  92  %  density  and  showed outstanding  mechanical  properties  compared  to  other  UHTCMCs  reported in literature. A high flexural strength (603 MPa) and high fracture toughness (18.1 MPa m ½ ) were recorded from mechanical testing, and the work of fracture (11.9 kJ/m 2 ) was superior to any other values reported in literature for UHTCMCs. These results are highly encouraging for the prospects of designing dense UHTC-based composites with high mechanical toughness and tolerance.
Continuous carbon fiber composites are currently the leading area of focus for the development of UHTCMCs. Unique processing approaches have made definitive progress in overcoming the relevant manufacturing challenges, where UHTC matrices have been adequately infiltrated  and  densified  in  complex  carbon  fiber  architectures. A B S T R A C T
Carbon fibre reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix composites were fabricated coupling water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration  and Pyrolysis  (PIP)  and  Hot  Pressing (HP)  techniques. This study aims to identify the best sequence of consolidation techniques to better integrate the carbon fibre cloths into an ultra-refractory sintered ceramic matrix of ZrB2-SiC. Infiltrated preforms with UHTC powder slurry were densified via: a) a pre-sintering step by HP followed by two PIP cycles with polycarbosilane, and vice versa, b) two PIP cycles followed by a cycle of HP. Flexural strengths at room temperature and at 1500 ◦ C (167 MPa and 592 MPa, respectively) were found to be significantly higher for composites obtained by the second route, suggesting that sintering of polymer-derived SiC during HP improves the structural properties of Cf/ZrB2SiC  composites. This  work  presents  an effective  method  for  UHTCMC  manufacturing in  a  shorter time  than traditional PIP process.
possessing a good strength even at 2100 ◦ C [13,14], a good fracture toughness (e.g. > 15 MPa m 1/2 ) [13,15,16], an excellent thermal shock resistance ( > 1500 ◦ C) [13], and an excellent ablation resistance above 1600 ◦ C [11,16 -21]. However, the choice of the manufacturing process remains  the  major  concern  for  these  materials.  Selection  of  the  best route is dictated by the need of fast processing/cost-effectiveness combined with quality of performance.
Several processing techniques have been used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs, re-adapted from the CMC manufacturing technologies:

of ceramics and makes them damage-tolerant. 5 Damagetolerant continuous fiber-reinforced ceramic matrix composites (CFCMCs), such as C/C, C/SiC, and SiC/SiC, have found applications as high-temperature structural components. 6-9 Continuous fiber-reinforced ZrB2 -based UHTC matrix composites (UHTCMCs) are being developed in the same line as CFCMCs. UHTCMCs have been also processed in the same way as conventional ceramic matrix composites (CMCs). 10,11 CMCsaremostlyproduced by using four different techniques: polymer infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), reactive melt infiltration (RMI), chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), and the ceramic route. 6 UHTCMC early development is mostly based on a hybrid process (a combination of two processes). For example, Tang et al. 12 and Wang et al. 13 developed Cf -ZrB 2-SiC composite using a hybrid process that included slurry infiltration followed by CVI. A Cf-ZrB2-SiC composite was also fabricated using a hybrid process that included PIP and slurry impregnation. 14-16 These are incremental advancements of a C f -SiC composite with ZrB 2 added via slurry impregnation. The RMI process is also wieldy used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs. 17-19 After adapting the ceramic route of processing of CMC, substantial progress in the processing of UHTCMCs has been made. The ceramic route consists of a slurry infiltration followed by densification through high-temperature sintering (both pressureless and pressure-assisted). The classic examples of using ceramic route processed composites are oxide-oxide CMCs 20 and nano-infiltration and transient eutectoid process for SiC-based CMCs. 21 Recently, huge developments for the processing of UHTCMCs have been carried out worldwide using a similar ceramic route. 22-27 However, in these studies, unidirectional carbon fabric were used.

2. Materials and methods
The  investigation  was  performed  on  ceramic  composite  samples obtained from ZrB2, SiC, and TaB2 powders. The samples were prepared by high-energy mechanical activation of ZrB2, SiC, and TaB2 powders in a planetary ball mill for 3 min at 1820 rpm with Y-TZP milling balls in a protective gas environment (argon). The average particle size of ZrB2 in the mixture was 2.33 ± 1.65 μ m, TaB2  1.7 ± 0.9 μ m, and SiC 4.51 ± 3.01 μ m.
The matrix phase of the dual composites consisted of ZrB2 and SiC in the ratio of 80/20 vol %. The inclusion phase consisted of TaB2 and SiC in similar proportions (dual composite). A composite made of 72 vol % of ZrB2, 20 vol % of SiC, and 8 vol % of TaB2  with a uniform volume distribution of components was used as a control sample (homogeneous composite, HC).
