The powder mixture hot pressed by Zhang et al. [22] at 1900 °C and 30 MPa for 60 min resulted in a ZrB2-SiC composite with a relative density of 98 %. A similar starting composition pressed for 60 min at 1800 °C and 20 MPa allowed Wu et al. [23] to achieve a density of 96.6 %. SPS of the same mixture by Orru et al. [24] at 1900 °C and 20 MPa led to dense material formed in 10 min, while Wu et al. [23] obtained 96.6 % density after 5 min SPS at 1800 °C and 50 MPa. Chamberlain et al. [25] obtained non-porous material by SPS of a 2Zr-B4C-Si powder mixture at 1800 °C and 40 MPa for 60 min. The replacement of Zr in reaction (1) by ZrH2 led to non-porous ceramics after 5 min at 1900 °C [26].
The above-mentioned data show that the reactive sintering approach can essentially simplify the ZrB2-SiC sintering process. On the other hand, reaction (1) does not allow to HP/SPS non-porous ZrB2-SiC composites under conditions where CNTs would not be degraded.
Our latest results, concerning the reactive hot pressing of a TiB2-SiCCNT composite [27] have demonstrated carbon nanotube survival in a TiB2-SiC matrix. SPS consolidation [34]. Stanfield et al. [29] provided additional evidence that final stage sintering of ZrB2 ceramics is predominantly controlled by dislocation motion, with activation energies ~ 168 ± 24 kJ/mol. Other studies on final stage sintering of TaB2 via SPS at 2050 ◦ C [36] and PS at 1800 -2275 ◦ C [22] reported high activation energy, Q ~ 1078 kJ/mol and ~1020 kJ/mol, respectively, although the actual mechanism was not specified. Collectively, these studies emphasize the complexity of densification  mechanisms  in  transition  metal  diboride  (TMB2)-based ceramics. Moreover, it suggests the intricate interplay of temperature, pressure, and compositional factors in determining the final material properties.
Although significant studies exist on the sintering kinetics of ZrB2 and TaB2, sintering kinetics for pure HfB2 are still less explored. Hence, the current work systematically investigates transition in sintering kinetics  during the intermediate stage and final stage (in the sintering temperatures of 1750 -1950 ◦ C)  on  the  densification,  microstructural evolution, and grain growth mechanisms of HfB2 ceramics during SPS. The study uses creep deformation models to explore grain boundary sliding and diffusion mechanisms at different sintering temperatures. It also estimates creep behaviour at these high temperatures.

6. Conclusions
UHTCs have shown their potential to perform under extreme environments in numerous researches, diborides being among promising candidates. Even though in terms of oxidation resistance a monolithic material has advantages, damage tolerance makes it difficult to handle. The main goals of development of UHTCMC therefore are improving damage tolerance with sustaining oxidation resistance as well as providing suitable substrates for monolithic UHTC coatings.
Highest boron contents inside the preforms are reached by using the foulard with PCS and two successive cycles. Especially boron content during the second cycle increases due to the lower contact angle of the pre-impregnated fiber sheets. The low contact angle of Zr2Cu on SiC, derived from polycarbosilane, improved infiltration depth.
Fig. 9. Cf after RMI (left) and Crack deflection of UHTCMC after bending test (right).
Formation of Zr-Si phases induced large volume expansion and must therefore be avoided during RMI. Dense UHTCMCs with small volume expansion were manufactured using Cf coated with TiB2 and using an infiltration temperature above 1400 ◦ C. Infiltration height was sufficient for production of thin, ∼ 10 mm in thickness, UHTCMC samples. Using different Zr alloys, as the tertiary system with Zr-Cu-B, further increased infiltration height, however this system is very sensitive to B content and is therefore subject to further investigations.
TiB2 fiber coating proved to protect Cf during Zr2Cu melt infiltration. Cf showed no critical degradation within the produced UHTCMC samples. Preliminary bending tests also showed the potential of mechanical properties using the TiB2 coating. Tested samples performed fiber bridging and crack deflection of the TiB2 coating which will be further investigated.

2.1.  Materials
To investigate the interaction between melt and ZrB2, TiB2 or SiC, the following fully dense ceramics were used as substrates: a fully dense ZrB2 ceramic provided by CNR-ISSMC (former ISTEC), Italy and produced according to the procedure described in [31], fully dense SiC  and  TiB2,  both  provided  by  FCT  Ingenieurkeramik  GmbH,  Germany. As melting phase, Zr2Cu was used (HMW Hauner GmbH & Co. KG). The substrate materials were cut into 15 mm × 15 mm square plates  with  thicknesses  between  2  and  3  mm  and  polished  using diamond suspensions on a range of polishing discs, with an average surface roughness of 0.1 /_181m.  Before beginning  the wetting experiments,  the  polished  surfaces  were  cleaned  using  ethanol  and acetone and then rinsed in an ultrasonic bath.
Subsequently,  a  UHTCMC  was  produced by RMI using Zr2Cu  as melting  phase  as  described  in  [15],  essentially comprising  the  following steps: deposition of TiB2 by chemical vapor deposition (CVD) on pitch-based carbon fibers sheets (XN80 Granoc, Nippon Graphite Corporation),  infiltration  of  the  TiB 2 coated  preform  with  a  polycarbosilane (Starfire SMP-877) enriched with 70 wt% amorphous B powder (Tradium GmbH 95/97); stacking of the coated sheets in 0/ 90°  configuration,  dry,  cure  and  pyrolysis  and  reactive  infiltration with the Zr2Cu melting phase at 1500 °C. This material was labeled as  "1".  The  size  of  the  composite  was  50  mm  ×  50  mm  with  a thickness of about 10 mm and the penetration height reached about 41 mm, as reported in [15].

A B S T R A C T
This research presents the influence of Al addition on microstructure and mechanical behavior of ZrB2-SiC ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composite (UHTCMC) fabricated by spark plasma sintering (SPS). A 2.5 wt% Al-doped ZrB2-20 vol% SiC UHTCMC was produced by SPS method at 1900 °C under a pressure of 40 MPa for 7 min. The microstructural and phase analysis of the composite showed that aluminum-containing compounds were formed in-situ during the SPS as a result of chemical reactions between Al and surface oxide films of the raw materials ( i.e . ZrO2 and SiO2 on the surfaces of ZrB2 and SiC particles, respectively). The Al dopant was completely consumed and converted to the intermetallic Al3Zr and Al4Si compounds as well as Al2O3 and Al2SiO5. A relative density of 99.8%, a hardness (HV5) of 21.5 GPa and a fracture toughness (indentation method) of 6.3 MPa m 1/2 were estimated for the Al-doped ZrB2-SiC composite. Crack bridging, branching, and deflection were identified as the main toughening mechanisms.
manufacturing UHTCs such as hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [30-37]. SPS is a latterly developed process that widely used to densify the UHTCs at lower temperatures, with higher heating, dwelling, and cooling rates [38-42].

2.1. Preparation
Commercially available raw materials were used to prepare the UHTCs in this work. The ZrB2 powder with a mean size of 2 𝛍 mwassupplied from the Northwest Institute for Non-Ferrous Metal Research, China. The SiC particles (1 𝛍 m, Weifang Kaihua Micro-Powder Co. Ltd., Shandong, China) were predominantly 𝛂 -SiC. The ZrO2 (1 𝛍 m, Fanmeiya Powders Co. Ltd, Jiangxi, China) used here was 3 mol% Y2O3 partially stabilized zirconia prepared by co-precipitation method.Fiveceramicswere considered as listed in Table 1.

5. Discussion
Fig. 7 shows the degradation of a SiC f during the Zr2Cu infiltration, the fiber reacts with the Zr and forms ZrC. In order to manufacture a UHTCMC by RMI a fiber coating which can sustain Zr2Cu infiltration environment is essential. From the fiber coatings tested, TiB2 is a suitable fiber coating for a UHTCMC manufactured by RMI. The TiB2 coated C fibers survived Zr2Cu infiltration and showed only little degradation in some cases, where they essentially formed ZrB2 with the Zr melt. The TiB2 even provided suitable mechanical properties in terms of damage tolerance. Fig. 9 shows a SEM image of a three point bending test sample, where crack deflection and fiber bridging of the crack starting at the bottom of the sample are visible. Further mechanical analysis to quantify damage tolerance will be performed in the upcoming work.

A B S T R A C T
Four different precursor powder mixtures of ZrC-B4C-Si, ZrC-B4C-ZrSi2, ZrC-B-ZrSi2, and ZrB2-SiC-C were hotpressed at 1850 ◦ C and 30 MPa for 3 min to produce ZrB2-SiC-C heteromodulus Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics  (UHTCs).  Pure  silicon,  as  one  of  the  precursors,  was  shown  to  cause  macroscopic  reaction-induced stratification  in  the  sintered  sample,  with  the  inner  part  enriched  in  oxygen  impurities  and  carbon.  Introducing silicon through ZrSi2 did not lead to notable stratification. The materials ' hardness decreased from 20 to 8 GPa, while the toughness rose from 4.2 to 5.4 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 with graphite content. The thermal conductivity of the reactively sintered samples increased from 38 to 73 W/mK with the increase in ZrB2 content. The conventionally hot-pressed materials exhibited an opposite trend in the thermal conductivity coefficient. The different behaviours were explained based on the composite structure and the interface contact thermal resistance. The thermal shock tolerance improved significantly with the increase in graphite content.

Additionally,  achieving  the  desired  microstructure and properties requires careful control of the composition [11] and manufacturing [6,12].

A B S T R A C T
ZrB2 based ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) is one of the most promising materials for thermal protection systems. This paper focuses on the oxidation behaviors of ZrB2 under compressive stress. ZrB2 UHTC is fabricated with nano-sized powders at 1500 °C, which is a relative low temperature. Nanoindentation tests are conducted to measure the basic mechanical properties of sintered samples. Results of nanoindentation show that ZrB2 is prepared with good mechanical properties. Oxidation tests are performed under di /uniFB00 erent stress states and temperatures, and oxidation mechanisms and microstructure evolution are discussed. As the oxidation proceeds, ZrO2 grains grow slowly at 1000 °C, but fast at 1200 °C. When oxidized over 1400 °C, ZrO2 grains fuse and microsized pores form on the surface due to the release of gaseous oxidation products. The thickness of oxide layer on the samples almost keeps unchanged under various compressive loads. Compared with the stress state, microstructure has a more obvious e /uniFB00 ect on the oxidation rate. The results in this paper provide a better understanding on the oxidation mechanism of ZrB2 based UHTCs.

question: What processing temperature was employed for densifying UHTC ceramics via infiltration and sintering methods?
answer: The processing temperatures for densifying UHTC ceramics via infiltration and sintering methods vary depending on the specific technique used. For example, hot pressing (HP) typically requires sintering temperatures between 1600 - 2300 ◦ C [15]. Pressureless sintering requires temperatures of at least 2000 ◦ C [1]. Spark plasma sintering (SPS) can achieve densification at temperatures above 1800 ◦ C [1], and ultrafast high-temperature sintering (UHS) can achieve densification at 2300 ◦ C for 120 seconds [5]. Additionally, reactive melt infiltration (RMI) can be conducted at temperatures ranging from 1100 - 1300 ◦ C [12].

chunks.text:
1 INTRODUCTION
Except for the mixture design, the sintering method also substantially influences the mechanical properties of UHTCs through the phase evolution and microstructure development. 2,6 The most common unreactive sintering methods for UHTC densification are hot pressing, pressureless sintering, and spark plasma sintering. The pressureless sintering, the simplest sintering method, is developed from the conventional sintering technique, which allows producing large, complex-geometry samples. 2,4,18 To be specific, the precursor powder with additives (enhancing densification) is cold pressed in a mold with a desired shape. Then, the green body is sintered in the furnace at least 2000 ◦ Cwithout pressure. Unlike pressureless sintering, pre-pressing is not needed for the hot pressing method. The samples are sintered in molds while applying 20-50-MPa pressure at > 1800 ◦ C. 2,4,19 The hot pressing method can achieve full densification with lower temperature, but it cannot fabricate complex shapes due to the uniaxial pressure. 18,20 The spark plasma sintering is a modified hot pressing, where the pulsed electric current is directly applied to punches of graphite dies in addition to uniaxial pressure. 21,22 Due to the direct heating, the spark plasma sintering method allows a high heating rate (up to 1000 ◦ C/min), thereby densifying UHTCs within a short period (no more than 10 min). 21,22 Parameters for the sintering methods mutually affect the properties of UHTCs, which increases the difficulty of discovering the optimal processing parameters. Generally, fully densified UHTCs require high sintering temperatures. However, a high pressing pressure applied to the sample can reduce the sintering temperature, and, moreover, a high heating rate enhances the densification and grain size of UHTCs. 1,2 To achieve the optimal grain size, density, and mechanical properties, researchers require to carefully control pressure, sintering time, sintering temperature, heating rate, and cooling rate.

KEYWORDS
As outlined in the review, 19 a major disadvantage of the industry-standard carbothermal and borothermal solid-state reactive synthesis methods is the presence of residual oxygen and carbon impurities-both amorphous and crystalline-along with trace of metallic content in the final product. Additionally, most reduction reactions require high temperatures: over 1500 ◦ C for carbothermal reduction and over 1200 ◦ C for borothermal reduction, often accompanied by lengthy processing times. The selfpropagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) or combustion synthesis (CS) method offers a way to overcome these limitations. CS is based on the concept of self-sustaining chemical reactions that generate extremely high reaction temperatures (up to 4000 ◦ C) without the need for external heating sources. 30,31 The characteristic reaction time is on the order of seconds. The high temperatures involved facilitate a self-cleaning effect, resulting in highly pure powders. 32-34 In our opinion, CS, being a clean, energyefficient, and time-saving method, is particularly attractive for the fabrication of UHTC powders. Main features of CS are briefly discussed in Section 1.2.
According to the aforementioned classification for fabricating bulk ceramics through the sintering of UHTC powders, various densification techniques are employed, including hot pressing (HP), field-assisted sintering techniques (FAST), additive manufacturing (3D Printing), high-temperature vacuum sintering, plasma sintering, laser sintering, and spark plasma sintering (SPS) 35-41 To densify UHTC powders with melting points exceeding 3000 ◦ C, other methods are employed, specifically electric arc sintering. 42 It should be noted here, that the modified methods of plasma arc melting and floating zone melting were applied to grow centimeter-scale single crystals of TiB2 , however these methods were not developed further. 43

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) such as TiC, ZrB2, TiB2, and HfN are a particular type of materials which are characterized by their high strength, excellent corrosion resistance, good wettability with some liquid metals, high hardness, machinability using electrical discharge machining, high melting temperature (above 3000 °C), and high electrical and thermal conductivity [1 -10]. These unique properties make these ceramics suitable candidates for high-tech and extremely hot conditions such as solar energy plants, gas turbine stator blades, heat exchangers, and cutting tools [11 -25]. Due to the high melting point, powerful covalent bonding, and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coef/uniFB01 cient of UHTCs, it is arduous to densify such materials without implementing high temperatures and pressures [26 -34]. In this regard, methods such as pressureless sintering [35,36], hot pressing (HP) [37 -40] or hot isostatic pressing [31,32,35,36,41 -46], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [47 -56] microwave sintering [57,58] and laser sintering [59,60] are used widely to manufacture the UHTCs.

∗∗ Corresponding author.
E-mail addresses: f_moghanlou@uma.ac.ir (F. Sadegh Moghanlou), shahedi@uma.ac.ir (M. Shahedi Asl).

4.1.6.  Ultrafast high-temperature sintering, UHS
Ultrafast high-temperature sintering (UHS) is a cutting-edge ceramic sintering technology that achieves rapid densification by applying high temperatures in an extremely short timeframe [87-90]. Chen et al. [91] used UHS to fabricate ZrC ceramics with the addition of B4C. The ZrC ceramic with 2.5 wt% B4C achieved a density of 99.8 % after sintering at  2300 °C  for  120 s  (as  shown  in  Fig.  12).  Compared  to  traditional pressureless  sintering,  which  requires  60 min  at  2400 °C,  the  ZrC
Fig. 12. (a) Schematic of the self-built UHS system, (b) typical temperature profiles of the UHS process for ZrC-based ceramics, (c) sample before and after sintering, (d) SEM images of fracture surfaces for ZrC-B4C by UHS at 2400 °C. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [91], © Elsevier 2024.
ceramic containing 2.5 wt% B4C prepared using UHS at 2400 °C for 30 s exhibited higher relative density, smaller grain size, and higher Vickers hardness due to its faster heating rate and shorter sintering duration.
In recent years, significant progress has been made in the hot press sintering techniques used for fabricating UHTCs and their composites. From traditional hot press sintering to advanced methods such as spark plasma  sintering,  reactive  hot  pressing,  pressureless  sintering,  nanoinfiltration transient eutectic, and ultrafast high-temperature sintering, each approach offers unique advantages and specific applications. As technologies continue to advance, the combination and optimization of these sintering processes will provide more flexible and efficient solutions for the fabrication of high-performance UHTCs and composites.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are classified as materials with melting points higher than 2500 ◦ C and include transition metal carbides and borides [1 -6]. These materials are expected to be used as components of re-entry and hypersonic vehicles because of their high thermal stability. Recently, the European Union-funded next-generation ceramic  composites  for  combustion  harsh  environments  and  space (C 3 HARME) projects has focused on the research and development of carbon  and  SiC  fiber-reinforced  UHTC  matrix  composites  (hereafter denoted as C/UHTCMCs and SiC/UHTCMCs, respectively) for application under harsh aerospace conditions,[7 -13] to improve manufacturing efficiency, reliability, and scalability [14 -23].
Numerous processing techniques for C/UHTCMCs and SiC/ UHTCMCs have been explored, including chemical vapor infiltration (CVI), sintering, and polymer impregnation and pyrolysis (PIP) [24]. For the conventional processing of ceramic matrix composites (CMCs), the melt  infiltration  (MI)  process  is  widely  used  because  it  is  more cost-effective than other processes, such as CVI and PIP. MI has been
* Corresponding author. E-mail address: inoue.ryo@rs.tus.ac.jp (R. Inoue).

Ceramics International
Ongoing efforts to overcome the above-mentioned limitations have focused on the development of ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs). This class of materials is defined by the combined descriptions for UHTCs and traditional CMCs, with fibrous phase reinforcements that include nanotubes, nanoflakes, whiskers, chopped (or short) fibers, and continuous fibers [51]. A more expansive assessment of relevant reinforcement types is offered in the follow-on section. Prevalent CMC processing approaches include hot pressing (HP) and spark plasma sintering (SPS)/field-assisted sintering (FAST) of ceramic slurry infiltrated preforms, as well as reactive melt infiltration (RMI), precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), and chemical vapor infiltration (CVI). A detailed account of the benefits and limitations for each of these  processing  methods  is  found  elsewhere  [52].  In  short,  dense composites  are  attained  using  sintering  approaches  (HP  and  SPS)  or RMI. However, the processing temperatures for sintering and molten infiltration  can  degrade  the  fibrous  phase  [53].  Additionally,  low melting residual alloy phases from RMI are typically found in the matrix, reducing  the  effective  application  temperature  of  the  composite. Comparatively, PIP and CVI render a high purity ceramic matrix without unreacted precursors. However, these processes generally fail to produce a fully dense matrix, and both demand considerable processing times. PIP requires multiple infiltration and pyrolysis cycles while CVI requires long extended runs with potential stoppage to compensate for canning effects. Overall, the mechanical and oxidation behaviors are found to vary based on the chemistry, volume fraction, and architecture of the fibrous reinforcement, in addition to the UHTC material selected as the matrix.

A B S T R A C T
Carbon fibre reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) matrix composites were fabricated coupling water-based powder slurry infiltration, Polymer Infiltration  and Pyrolysis  (PIP)  and  Hot  Pressing (HP)  techniques. This study aims to identify the best sequence of consolidation techniques to better integrate the carbon fibre cloths into an ultra-refractory sintered ceramic matrix of ZrB2-SiC. Infiltrated preforms with UHTC powder slurry were densified via: a) a pre-sintering step by HP followed by two PIP cycles with polycarbosilane, and vice versa, b) two PIP cycles followed by a cycle of HP. Flexural strengths at room temperature and at 1500 ◦ C (167 MPa and 592 MPa, respectively) were found to be significantly higher for composites obtained by the second route, suggesting that sintering of polymer-derived SiC during HP improves the structural properties of Cf/ZrB2SiC  composites. This  work  presents  an effective  method  for  UHTCMC  manufacturing in  a  shorter time  than traditional PIP process.
possessing a good strength even at 2100 ◦ C [13,14], a good fracture toughness (e.g. > 15 MPa m 1/2 ) [13,15,16], an excellent thermal shock resistance ( > 1500 ◦ C) [13], and an excellent ablation resistance above 1600 ◦ C [11,16 -21]. However, the choice of the manufacturing process remains  the  major  concern  for  these  materials.  Selection  of  the  best route is dictated by the need of fast processing/cost-effectiveness combined with quality of performance.
Several processing techniques have been used for the fabrication of UHTCMCs, re-adapted from the CMC manufacturing technologies:

4. Continuous reinforcement UHTCMCs
Finally, slurry infiltration (SI) uses aqueous or non-aqueous solvents as transport media to introduce solid ceramic particles into the fiber preform ahead of sintering. SI of UHTCs has received a lot of attention in literature due to the accessibility of materials (any commercially available  powder),  fast  processing  times,  and  the  relatively  low  cost compared to other techniques. That said, the high sintering temperatures needed for UHTC powder densification (~2000 ◦ C) can cause fiber degradation, whereas CVI and PIP processes can form matrices at more moderate  temperatures  ( < 1500 ◦ C).  Additionally,  solid  powders  can experience  blockage  effects  during  infiltration,  hindering particle transport  through  preforms  that  are  thick  or  complex.  Supplemental processes have been investigated to mitigate these effects (e.g. slurry injection,  vacuum  impregnation,  vibration  assistance,  electrophoretic deposition), which are addressed later in this text. As will be discussed below, many high-performing UHTCMC materials reported in literature have been fabricated with a combination of techniques, leveraging advantages from the different manufacturing processes.

Open Ceramics
It is difficult to process these materials, especially with fine-diameter continuous  ceramic  fibers,  without  damaging  them  or  causing  grain growth in the matrix, particularly with conventional sintering methods like  hot  pressing  (HP).  Recent  advancements  aim  to  merge  UHTC properties  with  ceramic  matrix  composite  design  principles,  forming UHTCMCs more conveniently and efficiently. The reinforcement type critically influences mechanical properties and processing challenges. Emerging  techniques  such  as  spark  plasma  sintering  (SPS)  and  nonsintering  methods,  including  chemical  vapor  infiltration  (CVI),  reactive melt infiltration (RMI), precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP), Sol-gel,  Slurry  Infiltration  are  under  investigation  to  address  these challenges. A variety of fabrication techniques is necessary due to the specific demands of different UHTCMC applications. The requirement of materials that can endure extreme thermal environments and mechanical loads necessitates methods that provide high precision and control over material properties. Additionally, each technique offers trade-offs between cost, processing time, material properties, and the complexity of  the  parts that can be produced. This diversity allows engineers to select the most appropriate method based on the application ' s specific requirements [26 -28].

4. Conclusion
A novel and simple method combining in-situ reaction and partial sintering process for preparing porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs with low thermal conductivity, high porosity and compressive strength is demonstrated in this work. In this method, the synthesis temperatures of ZrC and HfC are 1470 ◦ C and 1580 ◦ C, respectively, and their partial sintering temperature is fixed 1850 ◦ C. The as-prepared porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs have homogeneous porous structures with grain sizes in the range of 300-600 nm and 200-500 nm, respectively. They also possess low density of 2.06 and 2.82 g/cm 3 , high porosity of 68.74% and 77.82%, high compressive strength of
8.28 and 5.51 MPa, low room temperature thermal conductivity of 1.12 and 1.01 W · m -1 K -1 , respectively. Low thermal conductivity, which is only ca 1/20 of the dense materials, is due to small grain size and high porosity. The combination of these properties makes porous ZrC and HfC UHTCs promising for ultra-high temperature thermal insulation applications. This method can also be used to prepare other porous UHTCs such as NbC, TaC and TiC.

7. Summary and conclusions
- Approaches for lowering the infiltration temperature are based on adjusting  the  constituent  phases  of  the  infiltrating  material.  Four approaches can be used to make multiphase UHTC systems of at least two transition metals and/or including Si, while circumventing high infiltration temperature challenges:
- a.  Immiscible  metallic  systems  involve  RMI  at  temperature  range 1100 -1300 ◦ C by utilizing molten copper as a carrier of the solid phase of the refractory metal of which the later forms the carbide UHTC.  The  Cu-Ti  system  is  an  exception  to  the  immiscibility, however.
- b.  Immiscible metallic systems combine at least two refractory metals to leverage their  eutectic  composition  and  temperature.  This approach has been commonly used for M-Si and MI-MII alloys (M = refractory metal) as shown in
- c.  Metal silicides systems have lower melting points than UHTCs, hence provide a low RMI temperature and double as sintering aids.
- d.  Eutectic silicide systems involve combining at least two disilicides and/or penta-trisilicides to leverage their quasibinary eutectic point during RMI.
- Although RMI demands high processing temperatures, rapid heating techniques such as spark plasma sintering, microwave assisted sintering and electromagnetic induction heating sintering help to lower processing  times.  Heating  rates  of  up  to  700 ◦ C/min  have  been demonstrated.
- Temperature  gradients  can  occur  in  ceramic  matrix  composites during processing, leading to heterogeneous thermal spikes, thermal
stresses and volume expansion changes. Temperature gradients of up to ⁓550 ℃ have been reported in silicon carbide composites.

1. Introduction
Traditional  densification  of  UHTC  matrix  composites  normally  requires  towering  sintering  temperature  over 1800 � C and high pressure, or dozens of PIP circles around 1600 � C. Illuminated by realization of ferroconcrete, Cf/UHTC-SiC is prepared via a novel densification method and mechanism at 1300 � C without pressure, using liquid polycarbosilane to 50 nm SiC as sufficient supplementation to filling holes and ensuring a strong bonding between dispersive ZrC particles inside carbon fabric after slurry injection. The obtained Cf/ZrC-SiC not only achieves high density at 4.19 g/cm 3 , but also has robust strength and graceful toughness at 286 � 25 MPa and 11.12 � 1.25 MPa m 1/2 , meanwhile gains a level of ascension in work of fracture to 3046 J/m 2 .

3. Processing techniques for UHTCs
Effective sintering of UHTCs requires the initial powder to have high purity  (although  metallic  impurities  aid  in  the  densification  process, whereas oxide impurities promote grain growth and are detrimental for densification),  small  powder  particle  size,  narrow  particle-size  distribution, and low agglomeration factor [45]. The low self-diffusion coefficient and strong covalent bonds in UHTCs make sintering difficult [46]. Hot pressing (HP) has typically been employed to densify UHTCs [47 -53], while achieving 94 -97% densification in TaC-based composites sintered at 1800 -2300 ◦ C [50 -53], while 98 -100% densification is obtained in  ZrB2-  based  composites  upon  sintering  in  1600 -1870 ◦ C range [47,49]. To achieve full densification of ZrB2 -30ZrC -10SiC, the total sintering time is reduced from ~140 min in HP (with 3.7 vol % Si3N4 as a sintering aid) to ~23 min in SPS (without any sintering aid) [54].  With  HP  and  SPS,  complex  shapes  cannot  be  prepared,  which pushes utilization of pressureless sintering (with inherently poor mechanical properties attributed to poor densification). Pressureless sintering and hot pressing of ZrB2  have shown Vickers ' hardness of 14.5 GPa and 23 GPa, the elastic modulus of 454 GPa and 489 GPa, and flexural  strength  of  444  MPa  and  565  MPa, respectively  [55]. Other techniques to consolidate UHTCs are atmospheric plasma spraying [56], vacuum plasma spraying [8] and chemical vapor infiltration [57], etc. The pros and cons of these processing techniques towards enunciating microstructure  and  resulting  consolidation,  illustrated  in  Table

A B S T R A C T
ISTEC,  Italy,  and  Tecnalia,  Spain,  working  within  the  C 3 Harme programme [8 -10], it is not simple to process UHTCMCs, especially those that do not also contain SiC, using conventional sintering techniques such as hot pressing and spark plasma sintering since they require very high sintering temperatures ( > 2273 K) that can result in damage to the fibres  and/or  grain  growth  of  the  matrix  [7].  Since  we  aim  for  a maximum operating temperature of > 2000 ◦ C, the presence of SiC could lower significantly the ability to survive extreme conditions aimed for as SiC undergoes active oxidation and indeed can starts volatilizing above 1600 ◦ C. Hence the present work specifically focused on Cf-ZrB2 based composites without any SiC additions. Thus, an alternative approach was therefore to use non-sintering-based techniques such as chemical vapour  infiltration  (CVI)  [11 -13],  reactive  melt  infiltration  (RMI) [14,15] and precursor infiltration and pyrolysis (PIP) [16].
Chemical vapour infiltration is a leading technique for fabricating carbon / carbon (C/C) composites [17] and fibre-reinforced ceramic matrix composite materials, particularly silicon carbide / silicon carbide (SiC/SiC) composites [18,19]. The most common chemical reactions are the  dissociation  of  methane  and  methyl  trichlorosilane,  respectively, both typically at around 1273 K.

question: What sintering temperature was applied to consolidate the ultra-high temperature ceramic materials?
answer: The sintering temperature applied to consolidate the ultra-high temperature ceramic materials varied depending on the specific study and material. For instance, in one study, Ta0.8Hf0.2C was consolidated at 1650 ◦ C [1]. Another study reported that ZrB2-based ceramics were sintered at temperatures of 1800, 1900, and 2000 °C [4]. Additionally, a study on the entropystabilized (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ti0.25V0.25)B2-B4C composite used a sintering temperature of 1850 ◦ C [13]. Lastly, another study found that the sintering temperature could be as low as 1200 -1400 º C [14].

chunks.text:
Abstract
Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic has the highest melting point among the known materials (4000 ◦ C). Spark plasma sintering is a new route for consolidation of materials, specially ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), which are difficult to be sintered at temperatures lower than 2000 ◦ C.
The purpose of this study is to consolidate Ta0.8Hf0.2C by spark plasma sintering at low temperature using MoSi2 and TaSi2 as sintering aid. In this regard, effect of different amounts of sintering aids and carbides ratio on densification behavior and mechanical properties of Ta1 -x Hf x C were investigated.
Fully consolidation of Ta0.8Hf0.2C was achieved in presence of 12 vol.% sintering aid after sintering at 1650 ◦ C for 5 min under 30 MPa. The first stage of sintering was due to plastic deformation of sintering aids particles and consequent rearrangement. The second stage was occurred via Ta1 -x Hf x C solid solution and liquid phase formation.

Keywords: Spark plasma sintering; TaC; HfC; Disilicides; UHTC

6.1  INTRODUCTION
Ultra-High  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  commonly  referred  to  as  a  class  of highly refractory nonoxide compounds characterized by melting points above 3000°C [1].  Among  UHTCs,  transition  metal  diborides  MB 2 (M =  Ti,  Zr,  Hf,  Ta)  occupy  a prominent position of interest and, for that reason, will be treated as a focused case in the present chapter.
Sintering of UHTCs is difficult due to their strong covalent bonds and low selfdiffusion rates [2]. For instance, the most common transport mechanisms for solid-state sintering of MB 2 are lattice and grain-boundary diffusion, which involve boron and the transition metal atoms. Several sintering studies conducted on MB 2 have concluded that control of surface chemistry during processing is required to achieve dense ceramics with desirable microstructures: oxide impurities at the particle surfaces enhance coarsening at temperatures below those necessary for densification by the formation of fast diffusion paths at the grain boundaries. The real surface chemistry is sensitive to powder processing techniques and often very different from what can be predicted by fundamental
Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics: Materials for Extreme Environment Applications , First Edition.
Edited by William G. Fahrenholtz, Eric J. Wuchina, William E. Lee, and Yanchun Zhou.
© 2014 The American Ceramic Society. Published 2014 by John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
surface studies in ideal conditions. For instance, an increase in the oxide content in ZrB 2 powders was observed after conventional powder processing [3, 4]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy studies of MB 2 [5]  suggested that a number of B x O y ,  MO y ,  and  MB x O y stoichiometries may form within MB 2 ceramics. Extending the effects of these oxygenbearing  species  to  other  Ultra-High  refractory  ceramics  belonging  to  group  IV-VI transition metal carbides (MC), like ZrC, HfC, or TaC, the presence of surface oxides is the most important factor hindering densification.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) such as TiC, ZrB2, TiB2, and HfN are a particular type of materials which are characterized by their high strength, excellent corrosion resistance, good wettability with some liquid metals, high hardness, machinability using electrical discharge machining, high melting temperature (above 3000 °C), and high electrical and thermal conductivity [1 -10]. These unique properties make these ceramics suitable candidates for high-tech and extremely hot conditions such as solar energy plants, gas turbine stator blades, heat exchangers, and cutting tools [11 -25]. Due to the high melting point, powerful covalent bonding, and low self-di /uniFB00 usion coef/uniFB01 cient of UHTCs, it is arduous to densify such materials without implementing high temperatures and pressures [26 -34]. In this regard, methods such as pressureless sintering [35,36], hot pressing (HP) [37 -40] or hot isostatic pressing [31,32,35,36,41 -46], spark plasma sintering (SPS) [47 -56] microwave sintering [57,58] and laser sintering [59,60] are used widely to manufacture the UHTCs.

4. Conclusions
ZrB2-based ultrahigh temperature ceramics were prepared by the reactive spark plasma sintering using the vanadium powders and graphite nano/uniFB02 akes as sintering aids. The consolidation process was performed for 7 min under the pressure of 40 MPa at di /uniFB00 erent SPS temperatures of 1800, 1900 and 2000 °C. The relative density of all samples were > 99% as an evidence of approaching the theoretical density values. Compounds of VB2 and ZrC were in-situ synthesized during the reactive SPS at all temperatures. At the sintering temperature of 1800 °C, most vanadium and graphite additives were remained intact, while they completely were consumed by processing at 1900 °C. Beside the compounds of VB2 and ZrC, other complex phases of V1.54B50 and V3Zr3C were also in-situ formed at 2000 °C. Increasing the sintering temperature led to the signi /uniFB01 cant enhancement in the fracture toughness and slight drop in the hardness of ceramic matrix composites.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  including  borides,  carbides, and nitrides of early transition metals [1 -3], have a high melting point  over  3000 ◦ C.  Due  to  their  excellent  thermal,  mechanical  and chemical stability, UHTCs have been considered as the most promising candidate materials for applications facing ultra-high temperature environments, such as the thermal protection for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, and the core components in propulsions and advanced nuclear fuels  [4 -9].  So  far,  many  methods  have  been  developed  to  fabricate UHTCs, among which the spark plasma sintering (SPS) is the most advantageous method, since it can realize full densification, reduce sintering  temperature  and  time,  and  improve  mechanical  and  thermal stability [10 -12]. As one type of UHTCs, diborides, such as ZrB2, have been  proven to  be  highly  resistant  to  oxidation  [13 -15].  During  the sintering process of ZrB2, SiC is often introduced as a second phase, since it  inhibits  grain  growth,  improves  sinterability,  and  enhances  the oxidation  and  mechanical  properties  [16 -18].  Therefore,  ZrB2-SiC

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) include borides, carbides, and nitrides with melting temperatures above 2700 ◦ C [1]. UHTCs have been investigated for high temperature applications, including thermal protection systems for hypersonic aerospace vehicles, high temperature electrodes, and molten metal crucibles [2-5]. Recently, ZrB2-based ceramics have attracted much attention because of their unique combination of low density, high melting temperature and thermal shock resistance as well as excellent mechanical and chemical stability at high temperatures [6-10].
Generally pressure-assisted sintering techniques at very high temperature (>1900 ◦ C) are required to achieve complete densification of pure ZrB2 material due to its strong covalent bonding and low self-diffusion coefficient. Several studies have shown that the addition of SiC to ZrB2 powders has beneficial effects not only on sinterability, but also on mechanical properties and resistance to oxidation [9-12]. Although great efforts have been devoted to enhancing the mechanical properties and much progress has been made recently, the susceptibility to brittle fracture is still a major issue for the use of ZrB2based UHTCs. Whisker-reinforced ceramic matrix composites are of promising materials, especially for high temperature applications [13-15]. SiC whiskers, combining high strength, elastic modulus and chemical inertness at elevated temperature, are

1. Introduction
Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics (UHTC ' s) are a class of ceramic materials that are characterized by melting points that exceed 3000 ◦ C [1,2]. In addition, many of these materials exhibit exceptional mechanical properties at elevated temperatures making them well-suited to structural loading applications in extreme environments [3 -7]. While they, like many other ceramics, have high hardness and strength, these materials suffer from brittleness at lower temperatures leading to difficulties in the manufacturing of UHTC components [8,9]. One method of mitigating reliability issues is to incorporate a secondary phase creating a composite material whose interfaces provide toughening through crack bridging, deflection, or debonding [10 -12]. In many cases a secondary UHTC phase is added to retain the high-temperature performance of the composite while improving the low-temperature performance [13 -16]. These composites, known as ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs), have grown in popularity over the last two decades; however, their synthesis has remained difficult [17].
In recent publications the authors here have introduced an
A B S T R A C T

A B S T R A C T
Entropy-stabilized Ultra High-Temperature Ceramics (UHTC) offer a groundbreaking solution to the challenges of  extreme  environments,  showcasing  enhanced  mechanical  properties,  thermal  stability,  and  resistance  to oxidation  at  high  temperatures.  The  consolidation  of  UHTC  by  ultra-fast  high-temperature  sintering  (UHS) significantly reduces processing times and temperature and can produce dense high-performance ceramics with superior mechanical properties. This study reports the pressureless synthesis and consolidation of the entropystabilized  (Hf0.25Zr0.25Ti0.25V0.25)B2-B4C  composite  through  UHS  within  1  minute,  starting  from  transition metal diboride powders. B4C acts as an effective sintering aid, promoting the densification of the system and the formation of a nearly single-phase hexagonal diboride with a diboride-eutectic phase. Furthermore, a secondary minor hexagonal phase rich in V and Zr is formed close to the eutectic regions. Sintering currents of 40 A were necessary to reach densities higher than 90 % under pressureless conditions, achieving nano hardness higher than 27.3 GPa, comparable with high-entropy diborides produced by Spark Plasma Sintering. The study highlights the entropy-stabilized phase formation, diffusion, densification, and grain growth mechanisms involved during UHS. The work contributes to the understanding of entropy-stabilized ceramics produced by UHS as a faster and less energy-consuming process than conventional sintering methods.

1. Introduction
ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics have several unique properties such as high melting points (above 3000 1 C), hardness, thermal and electrical conductivity, but low volatility [1 -3]. These characteristics make them attractive candidates for high-temperature applications where resistance to corro[4 -6]. Owing to high melting points and low self-diffusion coef /uniFB01 cients, sintering of ZrB2 generally requires pressure-assisted procedures at temperature of 2000 1 C or even higher [7,8]. In earlier or recent studies, ZrB2-based ceramics are commonly prepared by conventional pressureless sintering (PS), hot pressing (HP), reactive hot pressing (RHP) and spark plasma sintering (SPS) [7 -10]. Compared with other sintering techniques, SPS has the advantage of eliminating the need for sintering aids, leading to improved material properties at high temperatures. SPS is a consolidation technique that uses a combination of pulsed electrical /uniFB01 eld application with high heating rates to density,

3.3 Spark Plasma Sintering
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) is a novel technique, which gives dense shapes of UHTCs at a lower temperature and in a shorter time compared with conventional techniques. In the SPS technique, a pulsed direct current passes through graphite plungers and dies simultaneously with a uniaxial pressure. Figure 3 presents the schematic of spark plasma sintering.
In SPS process, grain growth is suppressed by rapid heating and the densification is  accelerated  at  high  temperature.  UHTCs  densified  by  spark  plasma  sintering usually have higher densities, refined microstructures, clean grain boundaries as well as an overall improvement in the materials performance. In SPS, the temperature measured on the die surface is lower than the actual sample. The difference in temperature depends on the size of the die, level of vacuum and insulation.
Figure 3. Schematic of Spark plasma sintering process

2.4. Layered UHTC composite structure using spark plasma sintering (SPS)
The layered UHTC composite structure consists of an initial powder layer of HfC followed by a layer of HfC decorated Cnf  to obtain a 5layered system, schematically shown in Fig. 2. The component in each layer was poured into the graphite die (20 mm) lined with a graphite sheet  (as  per  the  stacking  mentioned  above  sequence)  and  manually pressed. Spark plasma sintering (SPS, Model 104, Thermal Technologies, Santa  Rosa,  CA)  was  used  to  consolidate  a  5  layered  structure.  The processing conditions for SPS were set at the maximum temperature of 1850 ◦ C and a pressure of 60 MPa with a dwelling of 10 min at a heating rate of 100 ◦ C/min under vacuum.

A B S T R A C T
Previously conducted studies determined that the reactive sintering of B4C and stoichiometric additions of TiSi2 or  Ti5Si3 intermetallics  created  a  dense  TiB2-SiC  double-phase  system  that  could  be  classified  as  ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC). In this work, a spark plasma sintering (SPS) technique was used to improve the sintering process and thus further reduce the obtaining temperature and improve the final microstructure and mechanical properties of the proposed, double-phase TiB2-SiC UHTC systems. To evaluate the effects of the experiment, a comprehensive study of phase composition, microstructure and mechanical properties was added. As a result, minimal sintering temperatures were further reduced from 1420 - 1650 º C to a range of 1200 -1400 º C. Among the prepared composites those sintered at 1400 º C achieved the best properties. The prepared composites  maintained  high  hardness  (up  to  24.85  GPa),  high  tensile  strength  (up  to  309  GPa),  relatively  high fracture toughness (up to 4.80 MPa · m 1/2 ) and relatively low density, all of which characterized TiB2-SiC UHTC systems.

1. Introduction
High temperature ceramics (HTC) and ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTC) are ceramic materials that typically have melting points above ~2500 -3000 °C. HTC and UHTC, in the form of powders, monolithic ceramics, or composites, /uniFB01 nd many important applications from grinding and cutting materials (e.g., WC, TiC, c-BN) in machinery and mining industries to thermal insulation tiles, missile nozzles, and hypersonic vehicle leading edges (e.g., SiC, TaC, ZrB2) in the aerospace industry [1,2]. Reducing the size for HTC and UHTC powders from microns to submicron and even nanometer range (i.e., <~100 nm) o /uniFB00 ers additional bene /uniFB01 ts such as simpli /uniFB01 ed post-synthesis powder processing by reducing the need for extensive grinding, lowering the sintering temperature and associated energy consumption for materials processing and densi /uniFB01 cation, and helping produce manufactured parts with sub-micron or even nano-sized grains, which often leads to further improved mechanical properties such as strength, hardness, and toughness for the ceramic materials [3,4] . In addition, nanopowders will o /uniFB00 er /uniFB02 exibility in subsequent processing to make either bulk ceramics via powder consolidation or coatings via techniques such as plasma spray.
Although synthesis of nano-sized HTC and UHTC powders have been studied for some time, most methods reported in the literature su /uniFB00 er from some drawbacks either in terms of cost, product quality, or

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are refractory transition-metal carbides, nitrides, and borides with the highest melting temperatures known materials, making them prime candidates for applications in aerospace and hypersonic  vehicles.  Of  the  UHTCs,  tantalum  carbide  (TaC)  and  hafnium  carbide  (HfC)  feature  the  highest melting  temperatures.  We  investigated  the  binderless  consolidation  of  HfC/TaC  powder  blends  using  Field Assisted  Sintering  Technology  (FAST).  Powders  consisting  of  90/10,  50/50,  and  10/90  vol%  HfC:TaC  were sintered to high densities ( > 94 %). Bulk and nanomechanical, chemical, and microstructural characterization revealed substantially greater strength, hardness, and stiffness for ternary alloys. Mechanical properties correlated  with  physiochemical  analysis  indicated  trace  oxygen  phases,  solid-solution  strengthening,  and  nonstoichiometric carbon were the key mechanisms driving the peak property enhancement of the 50 vol% solidsolution sample, despite lower densities. This study provides insight into optimizing the compositional design of HfC-TaC alloys by balancing influences from solid solution strengthening and the thermodynamic effects of oxygen/carbon stoichiometry.
the highest experimentally measured melting temperatures, exceeding 3900 ◦ C [15 -18]. Furthermore, it has been predicted that tailoring the stoichiometry of Ta0.8Hf0.2C can lead to the formation of an UHT-stable oxide phase (Hf6Ta2O17) that may reduce oxygen diffusion rates and thus the rate of subsequent oxidation [17,19 -21].

A.B. Peters et al.
consequently, high post-processing temperatures and pressure-assisted techniques are needed to produce dense components. These methods often  limit  geometric  complexity  to  simple  axially-symmetric  shapes (like  cylinders  or  tiles)  or  components  without  internal  features  [8]. When refractory ceramics are formed using AM techniques, high-temperature consolidation (sintering) of granular materials require a binder phase or organic additives (dispersants, binders, plasticizers, lubricants, etc.) to provide the desired rheological and cohesive properties  on  non-reactive  feedstocks.  For  AM  of  UHTC  materials,  slow atomic diffusion hinders consolidation and sintering of non-oxide particles:  high  temperatures  (in  excess  of  2000 ◦ C),  slow  heating  rates (0.1 -2 ◦ C/h) and hot isostatic pressing are used to prevent defects and achieve the desired mechanical integrity [10,11].

answer: The maximum operating temperature tested for ceramic systems includes up to 2000 ◦ C [2], and in some cases, the surface temperatures exceeded 2400 K [18]. Additionally, specific tests on ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been conducted at temperatures as high as 1800 ◦ C [14].

chunks.text:
1. Introduction
With the development of aerospace vehicles and atmospheric re -entry systems, increasing attention has been paid to ceramic matrix composites (CMCs) due to their excellent thermal, electrical, and mechanical properties such as high hardness, stability, and strength [1]. Meanwhile, the common CMCs not always meet the stringent requirements for modern industrial environments and instantaneous temperature changes. Particularly, the above goals could be mostly achieved by introducing ultra -high -temperature ceramics (UHTCs) with outstanding physicochemical properties and the ability to withstand harsh environments [2,3].
UHTCs belong to a class of refractory ceramics whose melting points can exceed 3000 /C14 C. These materials exhibit outstanding stability at temperatures above 2000 /C14 C, which can be attained within a few hours or even minutes. The impressive temperature characteristics make UHTCs attractive for the use in microelectronics, molten metal seals, high--temperature electrodes, and wear -resistant surfaces [4]. The phase composition of UHTCs includes transition metal borides, carbides, or nitrides of Zr, Hf, Ta, W, and Nb [5 -10]. However, the obstacles of using UHTCs is that they are preparation of UHTC by carbothermic reduction method is carried out using a ball -milled mixture of carbon black and oxide ceramic powder. However,  C/C  materials  start  to  oxidize significantly at 600 ◦ C and possess low COF in wet or cold conditions due to the inherent lubrication ability of graphite [35,36]. These disadvantages make them unsuitable for standard road vehicles. To overcome these problems, C/C-SiC composites, developed since the 1990 s, are manufactured by liquid silicon infiltration (LSI) of C/C and used in high performance cars [37]. These CMCs exhibit exceptional wear resistance, less  oxidation and a high COF in different weather conditions at the expense  of  weight  and  limited  operating  temperature  [36].  C/C-SiC disks  are  usually  paired  with  organic  or  metallic  pads  with  some research being carried out on ceramic pads for full-ceramic brake systems [38,39], but with C/C-SiC pads still having hard vibration harshness (NVH) and high thermal conductivity issues.

More recently, the development of suitable techniques to densify and machine UHTCs at reasonable cost triggered a considerable amount of testing. UHTC nozzle inserts were tested in small scale rocket engine motors [11] con /uniFB01 rming that they lacked the capability to withstand rapid thermal gradients without su /uniFB00 ering premature failure due to poor thermal shock resistance. However, these tests also con /uniFB01 rmed the outstanding erosion resistance displayed by this class of ceramics. TPS systems such as nose cones and leading edges entirely based on bulk, unreinforced UHTC ceramics were successfully tested in arc jet experiments [4].

1. Introduction
In recent years, with the rapid development of the aerospace field, higher requirements have been put forward for the key hot-end components of aero engines, aircraft and rocket engines, which must have the ability of long-term service in extreme environments such as high temperatures, high pressures and high-temperature water-oxygen corrosion [1 -3]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) can maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C, rendering them one of the most promising potential materials for high temperature thermal protection systems [4 -6]. Zr-based UHTCs have high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), low density, excellent high temperature mechanical properties, good thermal stability, good oxidation resistance and corrosion resistance, and are therefore widely used in

A B S T R A C T
Real-time  monitoring  of  surface  heat  flux  in  high-temperature  and  temperature-changing  environments  is gaining significant attention for various promising applications, including aero engines, gas turbines, and nuclear power plants. However, the survivability of most current thin-film heat flux sensors above 1200 ◦ C still faces challenges. Here, we designed and fabricated a ceramic thin-film thermopile heat flux sensor on an alumina ceramic substrate by physical vapor deposition (PVD). The ITO-In2O3 was chosen as the sensitive layer, while a multilayered YSZ-Al2O3/Al2O3  ceramic film was used as the protective layer to enhance high-temperature stability.  Meanwhile, the microstructure and electrical properties of the sensitive films were investigated under different  annealing conditions. The results indicate that the sensitive films have a stable structure and good electrical conductivity. The heat flux sensor reaches a sensitivity of 73.4 μ V/(kW/m 2 ) and can measure heat flux up to 1.06 MW/m 2 . Interestingly, the sensor has excellent high-temperature survivability, and can withstand temperatures  up  to  1250 ℃ in  the  ambient  atmosphere.  Moreover,  the  application  tests  were  conducted  to simulate engine environments, such as flame combustion, shock resistance. These results also indicate that the fabricated sensor shows great potential for actual working applications.

In our previous work, Wang et al. [11] chose Pt/Pt-13Rh as the thermopile material, then deposited SiO2 covering the whole thermopile to avoid oxidation and rapid failure at high temperatures. The sensitivity of sensor was 6.13 μ V(kW/m 2 ) with a temperature tolerance of 1000 ℃ . Although the design of an oxidation-resistant  layer  improves  the  sensor ' s  high-temperature  stability,  metal  thermopile  thin  films  are  unreliable  because  they  are oxidized at temperatures up to 1000 ℃ in air [12].

1. Introduction
Thermal protection systems of high-speed vehicles and spacecraft reentry require structural materials that are not only resistant to high temperatures,  but  also  have  long-term  mechanical  stability  [1,2]. Ultra-high-temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs),  especially  UHTC  carbides, are considered the most promising thermal protection materials due to their extremely high melting points, considerable hardness, and chemical stability [3 -6]. However, the high-melting-point oxides of ZrC and HfC (i.e., ZrO2 and HfO2) are porous and undergo phases transformation at the medium-high temperatures (1200 -1600 ◦ C), which severely restricts their practical applications [7 -9]. According to previous studies [6,10 -15], the main strategies to improve the oxidation resistance of the UHTCs at the medium-high temperatures are forming solid solutions with multi-elements or multicomponent ceramics. However, SiC/HfC composites  still  have  poor  oxidation  resistance  at  the  medium-high temperatures,  especially  at  lower  temperatures  such  as  1200 ◦ C  and 1300 ◦ C, because of the presence of severe cracking [16]. Although SiC has good oxidation resistance at 1600 ◦ C and below, the SiO2 formed at 1300 ◦ C and below is not enough to form a dense oxide scale with HfO2

Particularly, the (Ba0.85Ca0.15)(Zr0.10Ti0.90)O3 (BCZT) systems have been extensively studied due to its exhibited interesting ferroelectric, piezoelectric and dielectric characteristics at the morphotropic phase boundary (MPB)compared with those lead-based ceramic systems [17 e 19]. Nonetheless, in terms of energy storage properties, especially temperature stability, there are few studies on (Ba0.85Ca0.15) (Zr0.10Ti0.90)O3 (BCZT) ceramic systems. The  stress  values  along  the  diameter  of  the  specimen  in  Fig.  9d  are shown in Fig. 9g. Although WC has a high thermal conductivity, its high elastic modulus makes its surface stress larger, with the highest value of about 955 MPa, followed by the TiC system, both of which exhibit a larger thermal mismatch with the C/C composites in the plasma ablation environment. Which is consistent with the high d33 value  of  the  ceramic  at  such  high  temperature.  Moreover,  the leakage current density as a function of applied electric field measurements of pristine LTO and LLCTO:xMn (x = 0, 0.2) ceramics are presented  in  Fig.  S2,  which  demonstrate  the  consistency  with  the  I-E (Fig. 5a), impedance versus frequency (Fig. 5c), and resistivity versus temperature (Fig. 5d) of the compositions.
Finally, for the materials capable  of being  utilized in hightemperature piezoelectric devices, it is important for them to contain high Curie temperature ( TC ). Hence, the relative dielectric constant as a function of  temperature  plots  are  taken  for  the  pure  LTO  and LLCTO:0.2Mn ceramics (Fig. 6a). From the image, it is clearly evident that all the samples have demonstrated the little relaxed peaks at the maximum relative dielectric constant, confirming the better ferroelectric behavior of the ceramics. Pure LTO has exhibited the ε max at the TC of 1430 ◦ C; the value of TC has been reduced to 1424 ◦ C for the LLCTO ceramic. Similar decrement behavior in the TC values has been observed for  LLCTO:0.1Mn,  LLCTO:0.2Mn, and  LLCTO:0.3Mn  ceramics,  which are 1417 ◦ C, 1415 ◦ C, and 1405 ◦ C, respectively (Fig. 6b). Dielectric loss versus temperature plots are considered the defining parameters of the compactness, strength, and stability of the materials. Fig. 6c and d are presenting the dielectric loss values under varying temperature range 50 -1500 ◦ C, even at the high temperature of 1200 ◦ C, all samples have demonstrated the dielectric loss below 1. Hence, it can be said that the composition LLCTO:0.2Mn  ceramic is ideal candidate for hightemperature piezoelectric applications.

5. Conclusions
In conclusion, the present work provides CALPHAD models of lattice parameters and elastic constants in the NbC-TiC-VC-ZrC pseudo-quaternary  system,  which  gives  a  simple  but  effective  solution  for  high throughput exploring the enormous composition space in high-entropy carbides.  The  model  is  constructed  from  the  lattice  parameters  and elastic constants of all four pseudo-unary systems and six M1C-M2C (M1 ∕ = M2 = Nb, Ti, V or Zr) pseudo-binary monocarbide systems obtained from first-principles calculations. The accuracy of the models and the first-principles  calculation  are  confirmed  by  comparing  them  with available  experimental and theoretical works in the literature. Using these models, the lattice parameters, elastic constants, as well as the derived  properties  (including  the  ratio  of  bulk  modulus  to  shear modulus, anisotropy, hardness, fracture toughness, wear resistance, and critical depth of ductility-brittle transition in grinding) are obtained over the whole composition space, including their minimum and maximum values. The mechanical stability over the whole composition space is confirmed by the Born criterion.

ABSTRACT
This work deals with experimental characterisation of ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials for aerospace applications. Current research activities are focused on development and testing of new-class ultra-high-temperature ceramic matrix composites for near-zero ablation Thermal Protection Systems and near-zero erosion rocket nozzles. To investigate materials behaviour in atmospheric re-entry conditions, relevant tests are carried out on samples with di /uniFB00 erent shapes with a supersonic arc-jet facility, with speci /uniFB01 c total enthalpies higher than 20 MJ kg -1 and temperatures over 2000 K in a gas atmosphere with high concentration of atomic oxygen. The Aerospace Propulsion Laboratory allows investigating rocket components or subsystems manufactured in innovative materials, such as nozzles and nozzle inserts, but also small specimens exposed to the rocket exhaust plume, in highly relevant operating conditions. Computational models for numerical simulations of high-enthalpy /uniFB02 ows, in both arc-jet and propulsion environments, and thermal analyses of the material samples are developed to support the experimental activities.

2.2. Ultra-high temperature oxidation test
The  ultra-high  temperature  oxidation  test  of  the  as-fabricated HfC x N1x ceramics,  HfC0.5N0.5-20SiB6,  and  HfC0.5N0.5-20SiC  ceramics were conducted using the testing apparatus developed by our laboratory,  as  illustrated  in  Fig.  1.  A  two-color  ratio  pyrometer  (Marathon MR1SCSF, Raytek, U.S.A., accuracy: ± (0.5 % Tmeas + 2 ◦ C), Tmeas in ◦ C; measurement  range:  1000 -3000 ◦ C)  was  employed  to  measure  the surface temperature of the samples, while an electronic balance (BP211, Sartorius,  Germany)  with  an  accuracy  of  10 GLYPH<0> 5 g  was  utilized  for weighing.  Additional  details  can  be  found  in  Refs.  [35,36].  Samples were first cut into small blocks (8 mm × 8 mm × 2 mm). Following grinding and polishing, the ultra-high temperature oxidation tests were performed under low air pressures of 5, 10, and 20 kPa, corresponding to low oxygen partial pressures of 1, 2, and 4 kPa, respectively.
The  samples  were  heated  in  graphite  crucibles  to  1800 ◦ C  at  an average heating rate of 30 ◦ C/s. After maintaining this temperature for 30 min, the samples were allowed to cool naturally to room temperature. The rapid heating and cooling processes simulate the high aerodynamic  heating  environment  experienced  during  hypersonic  flight. The exposed surface area ( A ) of the tested samples was measured prior to oxidation. The weights before ( w0 ) and after ( w1 ) the oxidation tests were recorded, allowing for the calculation of weight change per unit area ( Δ wc ) using equation (1).

2.3. Phase compositions and microstructures

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) is a potential candidate material for advanced aerospace vehicles. In advanced aircraft, the terms supersonic and hypersonic are used to represent the speed of aircrafts. For supersonic aircraft, the speed exceeds the speed of sound (Mach 1) and hypersonic aircraft has 5 times and above the speed of sound. In reaching the supersonic speed, the jet engine components are subjected to a huge amount of hot gases ( > 2500 /C14 C) which are compressed and thrust forward in the ramjet. Performance improvements in high temperature mechanical systems have resulted in increasingly severe operating environment for high-temperature structure materials. This has sparked an increased demand for more reliable thermal barrier coatings (TBCs) that improve the performance of structural materials and extend the operating time and temperature in harsh environments. These TBCs systems should be compatible with the component, withstand the thermal expansion stress associated with heating and cooling and have a coef /uniFB01 cient of thermal expansion (CTE) close to the substrate [1,2].
Plasma-sprayed ZrO2-8 wt% Y2O3, and (Ba,Sr) Al2Si2O8 (BSAS)mullite coatings have been used by NASA as thermal barrier coatings for superalloy components and environmental barrier coatings for SiC/SiC ceramic matrix composite (CMC) systems, respectively

1. ADVANCES IN THE DEVELOPMENT AND IMPROVEMENT OF PROPERTIES OF HIGH-TEMPERATURE, CARBON BASED COMPOSITES
The  severe  operational  environment  of  airframes and  the  flow  paths  of  propulsion  systems  of  atmospheric,  high-speed  aircraft  and  reusable  aerospace vehicles result in a considerable demand for new materials  for  the  creation  of  high-performance  heat-protection  systems.  Alloys  based  on  refractory  metals (Nb, Mo, W), ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs),  and  carbon-based  refractory  composites (carbon-carbon  and  carbon-ceramic  composites) are available alternatives for use in extreme operating conditions. The use of metals is considerably limited by their high density, low heat resistance in oxygencontaining environments, and a lack of reliable antioxidant coatings with operating temperatures exceeding 1750-1800 ° C. The applicability of one-piece UHTCs is limited by their low crack resistance and resistance to thermal shocks. In addition, the production of onepiece,  especially  large  or  curved,  structural  ceramic elements is highly sophisticated and is impossible in most cases at the current stage of the development of production technologies. Carbon-based materials and carbon-carbon and carbon-ceramic composites are the only currently known  materials suitable for extreme operating conditions. However, their application in oxygen-containing environments is limited by carbon's tendency to oxidation starting from temperatures  of  400-450 ° C  and  the  insufficient  heat  resistance of ceramic matrices, which leads to the loss of mechanical properties by the materials. In high-speed, high-enthalpy flows, the composite structure degradation is significantly aggravated due to the simultaneously occurring thermochemical processes (oxidation and heterogeneous recombination), erosion,  and ablation. The expansion of the temperature and time intervals  of  the  working  capacity  of  carbon-based materials is an extremely urgent task of modern materials science.

A B S T R A C T
Two samples of Ultra-High-Temperature Ceramic Matrix Composites, with carbon /uniFB01 bers in a ZrB2-SiC matrix, were exposed to supersonic dissociated air /uniFB02 ow, simulating the atmospheric re-entry environment, in an archeated facility at speci /uniFB01 c total enthalpies up to 20 MJ/kg. Surface temperatures, exceeding 2400 K, were monitored by non-intrusive infrared equipment, which allowed detecting thermo-chemical surface instability phenomena. A zirconium oxide layer formed on the surface, below which the original material is perfectly preserved. Numerical simulations allowed describing the /uniFB02 ow /uniFB01 eld around the samples and characterizing the materials behavior, in terms of thermal conductivity, catalycity and oxidation e /uniFB00 ects at high enthalpies.
The capability of surviving exposure to harsh environments without signi /uniFB01 cant oxidation/ablation is one of the key requirements of ceramic materials for TPS applications, and relevant experimental characterization is needed. Initial evaluations of high temperature ablation-resistance can be performed relatively inexpensively utilizing high power lasers [10] and plasma [11,12] or oxyacetylene torches [13 -15], which can produce signi /uniFB01 cant heat /uniFB02 uxes. However, as suggested in [16], testing the material performance in an environment representative of hypersonic /uniFB02 ight conditions requires a realistic combination of heat /uniFB02 ux, gas composition, surface pressure, and /uniFB02 ow velocity. Therefore, the most useful facilities for the characterization of aerospace ceramic materials in relevant, simulated /uniFB02 ight environments are hypersonic arcjet wind tunnels [17 -19].

2.3.1. Random vibration test
UHTCMC samples tested in the random vibration mode and the corresponding nominal loads at which they have been tested, in terms of the level of the uniform ASD and the frequency range. It can be noticed that mainly short fiber material samples were tested in the random vibration mode, allowing the full characterization in this operating environment. The reason for this is that, as it will be shown in the following, long fiber material samples showed a more stable response and a corresponding longer fatigue life never reaching failure, not even at the system highest load capability.

2.2. Oxidation test
The non-isothermal oxidation behavior of (Zr1/3Hf1/3Ti1/3)C ceramics was analyzed by thermogravimetric differential scanning thermal analyzer (TG-DSC, NETZSCH STA 449F3, Germany) in air from room temperature to 1500 ℃ , and the heating rate was 10 ℃ /min. In a separate experiment, a high-temperature sintering process analyzer (ZC1600, industrial microscope, China) was employed to observe the macroscopic morphology and volume shrinkage of the ceramic samples during the non-isothermal oxidation process. The morphology was imaged using a CCD high-definition industrial microscope, with image capture initiated at 550 ◦ C and a ramp-up rate of 10 ◦ C/min. It is noteworthy that these two experiments were conducted independently and not simultaneously.
The isothermal oxidation behavior of the ceramics (bulk, 20 × 20 × 4 mm) was tested by a tube furnace (SK-G10163, China) in air. After heating to the setting temperature, samples were placed in the tube. The samples were taken out of the furnace after oxidation for a certain time and cooled to room temperature for weighting. The weight changes of the coated samples were measured by an electronic balance (TD20002A, China) with a sensitivity of ± 0.1 mg. The oxidation rate of the ceramics was evaluated by weight gain per unit area ( Δ M/A), where Δ M is the mass change after oxidation (mg); and A is the surface area of the ceramic (cm 2 ).

question: What is the hardness of ceramic materials used in extreme thermal environments?
answer: The hardness of ceramic materials used in extreme thermal environments varies depending on the specific material. For example, HfB2 exhibits a room temperature hardness of 38 GPa and retains a hardness of 8.5 GPa up to 1000°C [12]. YB4 has a Vickers hardness of approximately 28.5 GPa [2]. Additionally, the hardness of TiB2-based materials can be improved with ceramic additives, such as TiSi2 and MoSi2, reaching up to 8.9 GPa at 900°C [19].
