1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are an emerging class of materials that have the potential for use in extreme environments [1,2]. Most often, UHTCs are de /uniFB01 ned as compounds that have melting points above 3000 °C (Fig. 1) [3]. Several alternative de /uniFB01 nitions have been proposed with the most pragmatic being that UHTCs are ceramic materials that can be used for extended times at temperatures above 1650 °C [4]. However, none of these de /uniFB01 nitions captures the wide range of extreme conditions in which UHTCs may be used. Chemically, nearly all UHTCs are binary compounds in which boron, carbon, or nitrogen combine with one of the early transition metals (TMs) such as Zr, Hf, Ti, Nb, and Ta [5]. The strong covalent bonds between the TMs and B, C, or N result in compounds with high hardness, stiffness, and melting temperature [6]. These compounds also exhibit higher electrical and thermal conductivities than oxide ceramics due to varying degrees of metallic bond character. This intriguing combination of metal-like and ceramic-like properties allow UHTCs to survive extreme temperatures, heat /uniFB02 uxes, radiation levels, mechanical loads, chemical reactivities, and other conditions that are beyond the capabilities of existing structural materials.
Reports of the synthesis of UHTCs date back to the late 1800s [7,8], but technological interest in these materials grew dramatically during the space race that started in the late 1950s and extended through the 1960s. At that time, groups in the Soviet Union and the U.S. were searching for materials to be used in rocket motors, heat shields, and structural components for the /uniFB01 rst generation of spacecraft [9,10].
⁎ Corresponding author. E-mail address: billf@mst.edu (W.G. Fahrenholtz).

1. Introduction
With  the  development  of  next  generation  hypersonic  vehicles, ensuring their stable and repeated operation under extreme environmental conditions has imposed increasingly stringent demands on the properties of ultrahigh temperature materials [1 -5]. Particularly in hot end components such as the leading edges, engine combustion chambers, and nose sections these areas are frequently subjected to extreme thermal environments reaching temperatures as high as 2500 ◦ C. As a result, the materials are required to have excellent oxidation resistance, ablation resistance, and thermal shock resistance [6 -9]. Based on the above harsh work conditions, especially the necessity for an extremely high melting point (exceeding 3000 ◦ C), ultrahigh-temperature ceramics becomes the most promising candidate materials for the hot end structures of both current and future hypersonic aerospace systems [10 -12].
Among these ultrahigh-temperature ceramic, YB4  exhibits an high melting point of 2800 ◦ C and excellent thermal stability, making it one of the most promising candidate material for extreme environment applications [13]. Early studies have demonstrated that YB4 ceramics can be successfully synthesized under vacuum conditions with a temperature range of 1500 ◦ C ~ 1900 ◦ C [14]. The compound crystallizes in the tetragonal system (space group: P4/mbm ) [15], with lattice parameters of a = 7.107  Å  and c = 4.018  Å  [16].  Experimental  measurements indicate that the Vickers hardness of YB4  is measured to be approximately 28.5 GPa [16]. Furthermore, due to the existence of B6 octahedra

1. Introduction
Ceramics, in particular the ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs), have much higher melting points (over 3000 °C) [41-49]. UHTCs are a specific type of ceramics, which can be utilized in harsh environments containing extreme temperatures and chemical reactivity. Considering the melting temperature as a criterion, a handful of carbides [50-59], borides [60-103], and nitrides [104] of transition metals can be considered as UHTCs [66,105-107]. High strength, long fatigue life, good resistance to oxidation and corrosion at high temperatures are some of the most important features of UHTCs [108-125]. Borides such as TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 offer high thermal and electrical conductivities, good elastic modulus and excellent hardness [99-101]. They have widely used in oxidizing environments because of their superior oxidation resistance in comparison with the other materials [126-129].

1. Introduction
Ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) refer to thermal structural ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ Cthat can stably exist in high-temperature, oxygen-rich environments exceeding 2000 ◦ C [6]. These typically include carbides, borides, oxides, and nitrides of refractory metals (Figure 2) and their multicomponent composite ceramics [7]. Table 1 lists the physical and mechanical properties of several boride, carbide, and nitride-based UHTCs [5]. It is evident that UHTCs have extremely high melting points (>3000 ◦ C), high thermal conductivity, and very high strength and hardness, making them highly promising materials for ultrahigh-temperature thermal protection [8]. Figure 1f shows the historical perspective on research related to ultrahigh-temperature ceramics and composites [5]. The earliest research on UHTCs can be traced back to the 1950s, when HfB2 and ZrB2 were used as nuclear reactor materials because of their excellent high-temperature corrosion resistance [9]. Starting from the 1960s, the UHTCs were considered as thermal protection materials by the ManLabs laboratory funded by the Air Force Materials Laboratory of the USA [5,10]. At the end of the 1990s, the demonstration tests of the thermal protection structure of UHTCs carried out by Ames and Glenn Research Center together with Sandia National Laboratory and the Air Force Space Command proved their great potential in extreme environments from the viewpoint of ablation resistance, although they exposed the intrinsic brittleness [7]. Therefore, strengthening and toughening the UHTCs have become research hotspots in recent years. The fracture toughness of bulk UHTCs can be improved to a certain extent by introducing particulates, whiskers, or platelets as reinforcements. However, the intrinsic brittleness cannot be eliminated completely. In addition, due to ultrahigh processing temperatures of bulk UHTCs, it is difficult to prepare components with complex shapes on a large scale.

1. Introduction
Among ceramics that can be used above 2000 ◦ C, ultra-high temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  (carbide,  boride  and  nitride  of  transition group IVB elements) have promising application potential in extreme environment [1 -3]. These ceramic materials are featured by strong covalent bonds, showing the high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), high hardness, high strength and good ablation resistance, and high electrical and thermal conductivity in view of their metallic bond properties [4 -6]. Based on the above advantages, they are used in key thermal structural components  of  hypersonic  vehicles  and  thermal  protective  coatings [7 -10].
In  general,  when  UHTCs  are  used  as  high-temperature  protective coatings, they can protect the substrate materials to maintain their own structural performance advantages in ultra-high temperature environment [11,12]. C/C composites have been widely used in aerospace field due  to  their  low  density  (~2.0  g/cm 3 ),  high  strength  and  excellent high-temperature mechanical properties. Actually, C/C composites will be oxidized in oxygen-containing environments above 450 ◦ C, resulting in rapid degradation of their mechanical properties [13,14]. Therefore,

3. Applications of HEBs, HECbs and HENs in extreme environments
Generally,  High  entropy  ceramics  (HECs,  HEBs,  HENs)  work perfectly in aerospace functional parts as they are good at withstanding severe  stress  (mechanical,  thermal,  environmental).  They  provide unique hardness (over 20 GPa), fracture toughness (between 4 -6 MPa ⋅ m¹ ᐟ ²), low CTE, high oxidation resistance and excellent wear characteristics,  outdoing  traditional  ultra -high-temperature  ceramics operating at high velocity scenario and repeated thermal stress [47,49, 77,80,82,117]. Given their high melting temperatures (above 2000 ◦ C) and excellent thermal and wear properties, they protect the underlying substrates at atmospheric fluctuations. The high thermal conductivity and  low  thermal  expansion  alleviate  thermal  gradients  and  stresses, enhancing structural integrity in the wing leading edges, nose cones, and turbine  blades  (see  Fig.  12)  [36,77].  Entropy  effects  like  sluggish diffusion  and  sever  lattice  distortion  ensure  that  their  dimensional integrity  and  chemical  stability  are  maintained  notwithstanding  the level of cyclic stress.
Processing of Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics for Hostile Environments

1. Introduction
Another kind of material, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), has also extensively caused much attention as potential candidates for extreme environments associated with rocket propulsion and hypersonic flight [9,20,21] . The UHTCs refer to a family of refractory transition metal borides (e.g., HfB2, ZrB2), carbides (e.g., HfC, ZrC, TaC) and nitrides (e.g., HfN), whose melting points are over 3000 °C. Many investigations have been carried out on the mechanical, physical, thermal and ablative properties of UHTCs since 1950s [22] . The results have presented that the UHTCs have good oxidation resistance and ablation resistance at ultra-high temperature, high thermal conductivity (TC), the modest thermal expansion, and high hardness and retained strength at high temperature [7,8,23-26] . However, due to their intrinsic characteristics, such as low fracture toughness and poor thermal shock resistance and damage tolerance, the reliabilities of these materials must be improved for applications in extreme environments [27] .
Processing of Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics for Hostile Environments
Processing of Ultra-High Temperature Ceramics for Hostile Environments

Journal of Materials Science & Technology
The oxidation and ablation behaviors of UHTCs, C/SiC, C/C and graphite have been extensively studied respectively under a variety of different oxidizing environments. However, no experimental data exists for these materials under the same oxidation conditions. The relative researches are very few currently. The comparisons and relationships among these materials under extreme environments are unavailable up to now. It remains difficult to design and select the preferred candidate material in extreme environments. In order to develop an accurate evaluation of the thermal response, oxidation and ablation processes, it is necessary to thoroughly understand the underlying chemical and physical phenomena. The purpose of this paper is to investigate thermal response, oxidation and ablation of the most promising high temperature thermal protection materials such as UHTCs, C/SiC, C/C, graphite and graphite-ceramics, and relationships among them under extreme oxidizing environments, which provides a fundamental understanding of these materials under aerodynamic heating environments, a powerful strategy for the selection of preferred material and an effective design principle to enhance and optimize the overall performance of thermal protection materials according to special service environments. Furthermore, thermal response and oxidation behaviors of pre-oxidized material or repeated tests were performed to evaluate the reusability of thermal protection materials and the effect of pre-treatment on thermal response and oxidation and ablation of these materials.

Ultra High Temperature Ceramics
The hot hardness of various UHTCs is recorded in Table 2. A common observation is that the hardness of all the ceramics decreases with temperature, which should be true for most material classes. A decrease in hardness with temperature can be attributed to enhanced deformation of the materials during indentation at higher temperatures. Fundamentally, it is known that with increasing temperature the lattice spacing broadens, which in turn causes reduction in bonding strength and lowers the critical stress requirement for dislocation mobility. Among all the ceramics, HfB 2 exhibits probably the highest room temperature hardness of 38 GPa and retains hardness of 8.5 GPa up to 1000 o C (Koester, 1967). However, it is interesting to note that both SiC and Si 3 N4 show better hardness properties at elevated temperatures (see Table 2) (Wang, 1999; Lankford, 1983). According to Wang et al. (Wang, 1999), materials with lower CTE exhibits better hot hardness properties.
The composition or the presence of second phases also affects the hot hardness properties of the UHTCs. For instance, the effect of sintering additive on the hot hardness properties of  TiB 2 is  presented  in  Figure  7  (Raju,  2009;  Raju,  2009;  Jungling, 1993). It can also be noticed that the hardness of TiB 2 samples decreases with temperature. Basic Mechanical Property Requirements for Ultra High Temperature Structure Applications
Not only exposure to high temperatures and oxidative/corrosive environments, the ultra-high temperature applications also involve bearing of load that demands considerable structural tolerance. As can be seen from Table 1, these covalently bonded ceramics have superior room temperature hardness compared to most other ceramics (for instance hardness for Al 2 O3 ~ 19 GPa, ZrO 2 ~ 12 GPa etc.) and of course compared to the other material classes like metals, alloys and intermetallics. However, the important aspect is that the deterioration of hardness with increasing temperature should be minimal for the UHTCs so that dimensional stability can be maintained at the operating temperatures. Also, since the parts used for the concerned applications suffer from combinations of fretting (Mukhopadhyay, 2008; Basu, 2001), abrasive (Mukhopadhyay, 2011) and erosive (Babu, 2011; Manoj Kumar, 2006) wear due to oscillating parts in contact with each other, presence of abrasive/erosive media and air friction especially during re-entry into atmosphere, respectively, the materials

3.4.  Hardness of materials
The effect of varying ageing temperatures on the hardness of composites is shown in Fig. 9. The hardness of the composites enhanced due to  the  matrix  strengthening  effect  by  the  incorporation  of  ceramic materials. The load-transfer mechanism and grain strengthening are the primary reasons for the improvement of hardness of composite materials [14,18]. Initially, at 170 °C, the hardness value of the composites is increased  when  compared  to  A0.  This  was  due  to  the  addition  of ceramic  particles,  which  restrict  the  local  deformation  of  the  matrix composites  [15].  Further,  increasing  temperature  from  170  to  200 °C hardness value of all composites slightly increased. Among that, A3, A4, and  A5  composites  attained  peak  hardness  value  (136.5,  141.5,  and 149.5 HV) due to a decrease in grain size. However, the hardness value of A3, A4, A5 composites is slightly decreased at 200-230 °C. It is caused by a reduction in grain growth and dislocation density [32,33]. A1 and A2  composites  attain  high  hardness  value  (170.3  &  160.4  HV)  due  to high densification, low grain size, and lesser porosity. Finally, at 260 °C the hardness value of each composite decreased due to the weakening of  interface  bonding.  A3  composite  shows  minimum  hardness  value (121 HV) due to the high porosity level.

1. Introduction
As an important member of the high-temperature material family, ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials have moderate strength and excellent  ablation  resistance.  However,  the  ultra-high  temperature ceramic materials represented by ZrB2 or ZrC are difficult to fully meet the requirements of practical engineering applications because of their inherent brittleness and low reliability [1 -5]. To solve the above problems, the preparation process of continuous fiber toughened composites can greatly improve the damage tolerance and reliability of the materials, meet the needs of excellent ablation resistance, and has a good engineering application prospect [6 -9]. Nevertheless, due to the great differences in the physical and chemical properties of different ceramics, it  is  difficult  to  achieve uniform dispersion of ultra-high temperature multiphase ceramic powder in the matrix, which restricts the synergistic matching  of  ablation  resistance  and  high-temperature  resistance  between  multicomponent  ceramic  phases.  Meanwhile,  the  density  of ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  such  as  ZrB2,  ZrC,  and  HfC  is  high (6.1 -12.7 g/cm 3 ).
However, Polymer-derived ceramics (PDCs) can realize each component ' s  molecular dispersion and have good structural designability

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs), encompassing carbides, borides, and nitrides of transition metals from Groups IV and V, exhibit properties like high melting point, corrosion resistance, high hardness, and strength. These exceptional qualities make them ideally suited for demanding applications in extreme environments, including aerospace engines, wear-resistant components, and fuel cladding [1 -7]. However, the constant concern is that UHTCs are unable to withstand complex mechanical loads, such as impact stress and vibration, due to their low fracture  toughness  [8].  The  reinforcement  techniques  for  ceramics mainly involve the incorporation of fiber/second-phase materials and the  creation  of  solid  solutions  [9].  Classic  UHTC  composites  such  as Cf/HfC-SiC, ZrB2-SiC have been developed [10,11]. For the solid solution  strengthening  method,  the  lattice  distortion  effect  significantly improves the hardness and strength, especially in high entropy ceramics (HECs). Nevertheless, the toughness remains low (2.7 -3.4 MPa m 1/2 ) due to the lack of effective crack propagation barriers in uniform phase materials.
A potential toughening method is to control the solid solution level of ceramics to form a complex multiphase structure through sintering. The

13.5.1  Hardness
The hardness of TiB 2 -based materials can be improved with ceramic additives, such as  TiSi 2 and  MoSi 2 ,  albeit  only  up  to  an  optimal  content.  For  example,  the  reported increase  in  hardness  between  monolithic  TiB 2 (7.2  GPa)  and  TiB 2 -TiSi 2 (8.9  GPa)  at 900°C is primarily due to higher relative density. Interestingly, TiB 2 with 2.5  wt% TiSi 2 has higher hardness due to the formation of a smaller amount of soft second phase and higher  relative  density.  Even  larger  improvements  in  room-  and  elevated-temperature hardness of TiB 2 -MoSi 2 /TiSi 2 ceramics can be obtained by MS-SPS due to finer grain sizes and enhanced homogenity [70]. The reduction in Vickers hardness of TiB 2 -TiSi 2 composite  with  temperature  is  presented  in  Figure  13.11a  [24].  Comparison  of  the hardness  of  TiB 2 -based  ceramics  sintered  with  different  additives  is  presented  in Figure 13.11c. Among diboride ceramics, HfB 2 exhibits the highest room-temperature hardness of 38 GPa and retains a maximum hardness of 8.5 GPa up to 1000°C [74]. This further highlights the importance of characterization of high-temperature hardness, which might  or  might  not  vary  with  composition  in  line  with  room-temperature  hardness. question: What maximum temperature can ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials tolerate in service?
answer: Ultra-high-temperature ceramic materials can tolerate service temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C, and some can even operate above 3000 ◦ C. For instance, the melting points of these materials are generally higher than other ultra-high-temperature ceramics, even reaching 3000 ◦ C [2]. Additionally, some specific ultra-high-temperature ceramics, such as tantalum carbide (TaC) and hafnium carbide (HfC), have melting temperatures nearing 4000 ◦ C [8]. Thus, the maximum service temperature can be above 3000 ◦ C for certain UHTCs [4].

As critical materials for extreme environments, their oxidation resistance directly determines their service lifetime  and  reliability  under  high-temperature  oxidative  conditions. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics are ceramic matrix composite materials that can maintain their structural stability and function normally in  working  environments  above  2000 ◦ C.  Unlike  ordinary  hightemperature ceramics, ultra-high-temperature ceramics have a higher melting  point,  lower  density,  and  better  high-temperature  chemical stability  and ablation-resistance properties. They  were  originally developed by the US Air Force. Their superior performance is mainly used in the field of national defense, such as aerospace engines, spacecraft  head  and  wing  leading  edges,  and  high-temperature  surface coating [1 -6]. Their temperature often reaches 2000 ◦ C. Above 2000 ◦ C, special materials are needed to meet heat protection and load-bearing requirements. Ultra-high-temperature ceramics were created for these purposes.
At  present,  research  on  ultra-high-temperature  alloys  is  primarily focused on binary compounds, such as borides, nitrides, and carbides. The melting point of these kinds of ceramics is generally higher than that of  other  ultra-high-temperature  ceramics,  even  reaching  3000 ◦ C. 1. Introduction
In recent years, with the rapid development of the aerospace field, higher requirements have been put forward for the key hot-end components of aero engines, aircraft and rocket engines, which must have the ability of long-term service in extreme environments such as high temperatures, high pressures and high-temperature water-oxygen corrosion [1 -3]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) can maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C, rendering them one of the most promising potential materials for high temperature thermal protection systems [4 -6]. Zr-based UHTCs have high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), low density, excellent high temperature mechanical properties, good thermal stability, good oxidation resistance and corrosion resistance, and are therefore widely used in

1. Introduction
Ultrahigh-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) refer to thermal structural ceramics with melting points above 3000 ◦ Cthat can stably exist in high-temperature, oxygen-rich environments exceeding 2000 ◦ C [6]. These typically include carbides, borides, oxides, and nitrides of refractory metals (Figure 2) and their multicomponent composite ceramics [7]. Table 1 lists the physical and mechanical properties of several boride, carbide, and nitride-based UHTCs [5]. It is evident that UHTCs have extremely high melting points (>3000 ◦ C), high thermal conductivity, and very high strength and hardness, making them highly promising materials for ultrahigh-temperature thermal protection [8]. Figure 1f shows the historical perspective on research related to ultrahigh-temperature ceramics and composites [5]. The earliest research on UHTCs can be traced back to the 1950s, when HfB2 and ZrB2 were used as nuclear reactor materials because of their excellent high-temperature corrosion resistance [9]. Starting from the 1960s, the UHTCs were considered as thermal protection materials by the ManLabs laboratory funded by the Air Force Materials Laboratory of the USA [5,10]. At the end of the 1990s, the demonstration tests of the thermal protection structure of UHTCs carried out by Ames and Glenn Research Center together with Sandia National Laboratory and the Air Force Space Command proved their great potential in extreme environments from the viewpoint of ablation resistance, although they exposed the intrinsic brittleness [7]. Therefore, strengthening and toughening the UHTCs have become research hotspots in recent years. The fracture toughness of bulk UHTCs can be improved to a certain extent by introducing particulates, whiskers, or platelets as reinforcements. However, the intrinsic brittleness cannot be eliminated completely. In addition, due to ultrahigh processing temperatures of bulk UHTCs, it is difficult to prepare components with complex shapes on a large scale.

KEYWORDS
of ceramic materials engineered to withstand temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C. 1 An important aspect to consider is the phrase 'to withstand temperatures above 2000 ◦ C'. On the one hand, this could imply that the material remains solid, which relates to its melting point. In this context, it is relatively straightforward to compile a list of materials, as their melting points can be calculated and measured. On the other hand, we might interpret 2000 ◦ Cas the critical maximum service temperature (T max ) at which a material can be used in a load-bearing component without succumbing to oxidation, chemical changes, or excessive creep. 2 The Tmax is defined as the highest temperature at which the material can be used continuously without experiencing significant changes in its properties. An increase in temperature may lead to degradation, chemical alteration, and excessive creep. Typically, T max is determined by assessing the material's strength at various temperatures. However, this presents a challenge, as the desired material property, such as strength, depends not only on temperature but also on the surrounding environment. For instance, tantalum carbide (TaC) has a melting point close to 4000 ◦ Cand exhibits exceptional strength at elevated temperatures, surpassing that of top-quality polycrystalline graphite. 3 It is the only material known to maintain its strength between 2900 ◦ C and 3200 ◦ C in an inert atmosphere. However, the active oxidation of TaC begins at approximately 750 ◦ C. 4 It is worth noting that interest in UHTCs is driven by the aerospace industry's requirements for the development of new space engines and hypersonic vehicles. For these applications, high oxidation resistance at very high temperatures, combined with resistance to other chemical reactions, mechanical stresses, and radiation, are critical factors in selecting UHTCs.

1 INTRODUCTION
Ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are structural materials that exhibit superior strength, corrosion resistance, and oxidation resistance in high-temperature environments compared to other ceramics. 1,2 Due to such attributes, they are recognized as apposite materials for aerospace, atom energy, and other extreme applications. 3 The typical melting temperature of UHTCs is above 3000 ◦ C; and, therefore, they are widely utilized at ∼ 2000 ◦ C in air and under some aggressive aerodynamic environments. 2,4 The major compounds for UHTCs are di-borides, nitrides, and carbides of Group IVB and VB transition metals (e.g., ZrB 2 , TiB 2 , HfC, ZrC, and TiN). 5,6 These compounds contain strong covalent bonds between metal atoms and B, C, or N resulting in high hardness, strength, and melting temperature. 7
Due to the hexagonal crystal structure (the chemical structure of di-borides compounds) and covalent bonds, UHTCs have exclusive mechanical and physical properties (e.g., high thermal and electrical conductivities, good flexural strength, and great thermal shock resistance). 4,7 However, the single-component UHTCs are not favorable for structural applications because of insufficient oxidation resistance, poor damage tolerance, and deficient durability. 2,6 The multicomponent UHTC systems are adopted to overcome those limitations. Several studies 8-10 have combined ZrB2 with SiC to improve flexural strength, fracture toughness, and thermal shock resistance. More recently, literature 11-13 has shown that ternary UHTC systems demonstrate superior mechanical properties to binary systems. Moreover, some studies 14-17 have added additives (e.g., MoSi2, Si 3 N 4 , and TaSi2 ) to UHTCs for densification improvement and property enhancement.

7.3  UHTC COATINGS
Developing a UHTC coating on CC, Cf-SiC or even Cf-UHTC composites is another method of improving the high-temperature performance of materials for UHT applications. A good coating should offer very good adhesion and resist the formation of any cracks due to CTE mismatch at higher temperatures. Recent measurement of the CTE values of Cf-HfB 2 slurry impregnated composites at Loughborough University revealed that the CTE values of the composites are widely different from those of UHTC monoliths. They were also found to vary depending on the fiber orientation, which imposes additional complications for component design. Interface materials that can bridge the CTE mismatches while withstanding very high temperature need to be identified. SiC has a CTE between those of the composites and the monoliths, but  the  maximum  service  temperature  will  be  limited  by  the  performance  of  this interlayer. Coatings should also minimize diffusion of oxygen and prevent any spallation.  The  main  disadvantage  with  this  route  is  that  once  the  coating  layer  is destroyed, the base material will have little protection unless UHTC composites are involved.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are the carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metals (such as Ta, Nb, Zr, Hf, and Ti) with  melting  temperatures > 3000 ◦ C  [1,2].  UHTCs  are  the  ideal candidate  for  applications  in  the  field  of  aerospace  sectors  such  as scramjet  propulsion,  hypersonic  aerospace  vehicles,  advanced  rocket motors, nozzle throats, thrusters, and nozzle liners; nuclear fission reactors;  high  temperature  electrodes  for  metal  refining;  concentrated solar  power  and  many  others  [1].  Among  UHTCs,  tantalum  carbide (TaC) and hafnium carbide (HfC) exhibits the highest melting temperature nearing 4000 ◦ C [3]. UHTCs have gathered attention due to their excellent physical, chemical, mechanical, wear, thermal, and oxidative performance under extreme conditions ( > 2500 ◦ C) for applications such as leading edges and nozzles of re-entry vehicles. TaC starts oxidizing at 400 ◦ C and completely oxidizes at 850 ◦ C to form Ta2O5 with a melting

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-High-Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) materials, because of their high temperature resistance, are suitable as thermal protection systems for re-entry vehicles or components for space propulsion. Massive UHTC materials are characterized by poor thermal shock resistance, which may be overcome using C or SiC /uniFB01 bers in a UHTC matrix (UHTCMC).
The University of Naples ' Federico II ' has a proven experience in the /uniFB01 eld of material characterization in high-enthalpy environments. A hypersonic arc-jet facility allows performing tests in simulated atmospheric reentry conditions. The Aerospace Propulsion Laboratory is employed for testing rocket components in a representative combustion environment. Ad-hoc computational models are developed to characterize the /uniFB02 ow /uniFB01 eld in both facilities and perform thermal analysis of solid samples.
Current research programs are related to a new-class of UHTCMC materials, for rocket nozzles and thermal protection systems. The activities include design of the prototypes for the test campaign, numerical simulations and materials characterizations.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are refractory transition-metal carbides, nitrides, and borides with the highest melting temperatures known materials, making them prime candidates for applications in aerospace and hypersonic  vehicles.  Of  the  UHTCs,  tantalum  carbide  (TaC)  and  hafnium  carbide  (HfC)  feature  the  highest melting  temperatures.  We  investigated  the  binderless  consolidation  of  HfC/TaC  powder  blends  using  Field Assisted  Sintering  Technology  (FAST).  Powders  consisting  of  90/10,  50/50,  and  10/90  vol%  HfC:TaC  were sintered to high densities ( > 94 %). Bulk and nanomechanical, chemical, and microstructural characterization revealed substantially greater strength, hardness, and stiffness for ternary alloys. Mechanical properties correlated  with  physiochemical  analysis  indicated  trace  oxygen  phases,  solid-solution  strengthening,  and  nonstoichiometric carbon were the key mechanisms driving the peak property enhancement of the 50 vol% solidsolution sample, despite lower densities. This study provides insight into optimizing the compositional design of HfC-TaC alloys by balancing influences from solid solution strengthening and the thermodynamic effects of oxygen/carbon stoichiometry.

Furthermore, it has been predicted that tailoring the stoichiometry of Ta0.8Hf0.2C can lead to the formation of an UHT-stable oxide phase (Hf6Ta2O17) that may reduce oxygen diffusion rates and thus the rate of subsequent oxidation [17,19 -21].

6.  Coating treatment
Fig.  26 illustrates  the  autonomous  film  formation  mechanism  of UHTC  coatings  during  operational  exposure  [13,144,145].  The  continuous  oxidation  of  coating  constituents  promotes  the  progressive growth and lateral expansion of a glassy phase, ultimately resulting in the  formation  of  a  continuous  oxygen-blocking  layer  on  the  coating surface. However, practical applications reveal that oxidation-induced structural  imperfections,  particularly  porosity  and  crack  formation, significantly  compromise  coating  performance  and  service  life.  Furthermore, the formation of the protective glass layer through surface oxidation inherently involves the consumption of coating constituents. To address these challenges, researchers have developed an integrated approach combining preventive measures and in situ repair mechanisms. The coating densification was enhanced through pore-sealing by chemical  vapor  deposition  (CVD)  and  silicon  infiltration  techniques, which  creates dense  protective coatings and  effectively  improve thermal  oxygen-blocking  capabilities  by  establishing  defect-resistant architectures.  Additionally,  pre-oxidation  treatments  have  been  employed to establish stable oxide protective films on the coating surface, providing  preemptive  protection  against  subsequent  environmental oxidation  under  harsh  conditions.  For  unavoidable  localized  defects, micro-region repair technologies are implemented to precisely identify and  restore  damaged  areas,  thereby  maintaining  maximum  coating integrity.  Through  these  multidimensional  approaches  to  minimize oxidation-induced defects in ceramic coatings, researchers are enhancing comprehensive coating performance to extend service life in severe environments [146].

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  gained  renewed attention  in  the  last  decades  due  to  their  potential  use  as  protective coatings on metal substrates in extreme environments [1 -5]. By definition, UHTCs are ceramic materials with melting points above 3000 ◦ C [6,7].  Therefore,  they  are  suitable  candidates  for  thermal  protection structures in gas-turbine engines or chemical plants, which can withstand  application  temperatures  exceeding  ~1600 ◦ C  and  aggressive environmental conditions [1,8 -11]. In addition to their high melting point,  UHTCs  combine  improved  structural  properties  such  as  high hardness,  excellent  chemical  stability,  high  Youngs  modulus,  high refractoriness, as well as high thermal and electrical conductivity [1, 8 -10,12]. UHTCs are typically binary compounds consisting of carbides, borides or nitrides of early transition metals (e.g. ZrB2, HfN, HfC or TaC) [7,8,12]. HfC and its solid solutions with TaC such as (Hf0.2Ta0.8)C are

7.  Evaluation methods
UHTC coatings operate in extreme environments for extended periods, where their oxidation behavior is influenced by multiple factors including  severe  temperature  gradients,  variations  of  oxygen  partial pressure, water-oxygen coupling and thermomechanical shocks. These environmental  dependencies  significantly  affect  oxidation  evolution, kinetics, damage accumulation, and protective efficiency under various service  conditions  [166].  Therefore,  addressing  the  evaluation  requirements  of  coatings  under  various  operating  conditions  and  precisely analyzing the mechanism of oxidation behavior are crucial for optimizing  the  design  of  coating  structure  and  enhancing  long-term service stability.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are mainly composed of refractory transition metal carbide and boride compounds with ultrahigh melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C). They are indicated as the most potential next-generation structural thermal protection materials in aerospace field due to the high thermal conductivity (20 -100 W m GLYPH<0> 1  K GLYPH<0> 1 ), high chemical stability and high temperature strength [1 -5]. However, the intrinsic brittleness of UHTCs limits their application for structural parts [6,7]. This has aroused widespread concern about mechanical improvement of UHTCs, and three key aspects have been proposed. One is  fiber  toughening  exploiting  crack  deflection  and  fiber  pull-out mechanisms,  such  as  SiCf/SiC,  Cf/ZrB2-SiC,  Cf/HfC-SiC  [8 -10].  The second is the composite ceramics represented by ZrB2-SiC, with proved enhanced mechanical properties, through grain refinement, and oxidation resistance, thanks to the formation of a boro-silicate glass [11,12]. The  third  is  represented  by  highly  disordered  multicomponent  high entropy ceramics with cocktail effect, like (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2Ti0.2)C, exhibiting an enhanced hardness and strength due to lattice distortion [13,14]. 1    Introduction
Carbon  structural  materials,  such  as  graphite  and  C/C composite materials, are widely used in aerospace and manufacturing  industries due  to their  good  physical  and mechanical  properties  such  as  low  density,  high  specific strength and specific modulus [1-4] .  Carbon structural materials are considered as ideal ultra-high-temperature thermal structural materials due to their high melting point (>3500 ° C), unique  structural stability, excellent thermal  stability  and conductivity, low  coefficient  of  thermal  expansion,  good corrosion resistance and wear resistance [5-11] . While the carbon structural  materials  are  easily  oxidized  above  400  ° C,  which affects  the  thermal  structure  stability  under  high  temperature and limits their applications [12,13] . Therefore, with the increasing applications of carbon structural materials, improving  their  oxidation  resistance  in  a  wide  temperature range to meet the requirements is very important. Based on a large number  of experimental and theoretical simulation results,  using  oxidation  protection  coating  to  prevent  the oxidation corrosion of carbon materials is the most effective way [14-17] .

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), integrated primarily by carbides, borides and some nitrides of early transition metals, are ultrarefractory materials that exhibit exceptional thermo-chemical stability and good mechanical strength even at high temperatures, which make them highly suitable for extreme environments [1 -3]. UHTCs find practical applications in aerospace [4], nuclear power, and hypersonic vehicles [5], where their excellent thermal and mechanical properties are convenient for the production of thermal shields, furnace linings, and structural components [6,7].
ZrB2 is the most studied UHTC, with a melting point of 3250 ◦ C [1], high strength and rigidity up to 2000 ◦ C [8], high hardness, remarkable chemical stability, and outstanding electrical and thermal conductivity [9 -13]. Despite its exceptional properties, pure ZrB2 faces several limitations for its practical industrial use. Due to its high melting point, it requires sintering temperatures ranging from 2100 to 2300 ◦ C, typically resulting in coarse microstructures with some residual porosity [14]. Additionally, pure ZrB2 exhibits brittle fracture and suffers from rapid oxidation at temperatures above 1127 ◦ C [15]. These weaknesses hinder its widespread adoption in high-temperature applications.

1. Introduction
The thermal protection system and materials of hypersonic vehicles are fundamental to ensuring their safe operation in extreme environments [1 -3]. As hypersonic vehicles are designed for long service lives and potential reuse, there is a growing demand for higher reliability and durability  in  thermal  protection  materials.  Among  these  materials, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a focal point of research due to their exceptional high-temperature resistance and chemical stability [4 -6]. ZrB2  ceramics, in particular, offer advantages such as low cost, high hardness, and excellent stability, making them ideal candidates for ultra-high temperature structural components [7 -10]. However, ZrB2  ceramics are prone to oxidation at elevated temperatures, which compromises their performance and limits their practical application [11 -14].
To  enhance  the  oxidation  resistance  of  UHTCs,  researchers  have explored  the  addition  of  secondary  phases.  Studies  have  shown  that

A B S T R A C T
The first high damage resistant ultra-high temperature ceramic ScB2C2 with excellent hydrolysis resistance was successfully prepared through boron carbon thermal reduction and hot pressing sintering using Sc2O3, B4C and graphite as raw materials. The Vickers hardness, flexural strength, fracture toughness, Young ' s modulus, shear modulus, bulk modulus and Poisson ' s ratio of as-prepared ScB2C2  ceramic are 6.0 ± 0.2 GPa, 246 ± 17 MPa, 4.11 ± 0.18 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , 240 GPa, 101 GPa, 127 GPa and 0.18, respectively. In the a -b plane (B2C2  sheet) and along the c -axis direction, ScB2C2 exhibits extremely strong anisotropy of chemical bonds and elastic properties. The high residual strength, damage tolerance factor D t  and fracture toughness to strength ratio K Ic / σ f  indicates that ScB2C2 has excellent damage tolerance. In addition, ScB2C2 displayed remarkable thermal shock resistance and hydrolysis resistance. These unique features endow ScB2C2  with significant potential for future ultra-high temperature applications.

However, due to the weak chemical bonding between layers, they decompose at high temperatures (especially above 1600 ◦ C), which limits their application in ultra-high temperature fields [8,15 -17]. Therefore, developing layered ceramics with strong chemical bond anisotropy and high-temperature thermal stability is key to exploring ultra-high temperature ceramics with high damage tolerance.

question: What high-temperature exposure environment was used to test ZrB2-SiC based ceramics in Alfano et al. 2010, and how does it differ from the oxyacetylene flame environment used to ablate Ti2AlC ceramics in Song et al. 2011?
answer: The high-temperature exposure environment used to test ZrB2-SiC based ceramics in Alfano et al. 2010 is not mentioned in the provided sources. However, the oxyacetylene flame environment used to ablate Ti2AlC ceramics in Song et al. 2011 involved exposing the material to a flame temperature up to 3000 ◦ C [5]. This environment is characterized by a high-temperature flame with a temperature range of 1800-3000 ◦ C depending on the ablated materials and the oxidation products formed on the ablation surfaces [13]. The oxyacetylene flame environment is a method used to evaluate material's ablation resistance [4], and it involves a fast flowing oxyacetylene flame that can locally expose the material to a flame temperature up to 3000 ◦ C [5]. The main difference between the oxyacetylene flame environment and other high-temperature exposure environments, such as those used in arc-jet facilities or plasma ablation testing, is that the oxyacetylene flame environment is a simpler and more economical method to evaluate material's ablation resistance, although it has a restricted testing temperature compared to other methods [4].

1. Introduction
Ti2AlC is a typical example of atomically layered ternary carbides belonging to the so-called MAX phase family. 1 Recently, it has attracted scientific and commercial attention due to its excellent isothermal and thermal cycling oxidation resistance in combination with its high electrical conductivity, resistance against thermal shock and strengths at high temperatures. 2-6 The low oxidation rate is caused by the formation of a continuous protective 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer on the Ti2AlC substrate, and the superior spallation resistance of the 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer is attributed to the well-matched thermal expansion coefficients between 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer and Ti2AlC substrate. 5,6 Based on this, it is likely that Ti2AlC may withstand ultra-high temperatures for relatively short times since Ti2AlC will decompose into sub-stoichiometry Ti2Al x CoreventwinnedTiC0.5 superstructureandAlratherthan becoming a liquid phase itself as long as the temperature does not exceed the melting point ( ∼ 3250 ◦ C) of TiC x ( x =0.5-1). 7-9 Therefore, Ti2AlC is probably a promising candidate for thermal structural components exposed to ultra-high temperature ablative environments such as nose caps of missiles, rudders and nozzles of rocket engines, gas burners and heat shields in nuclear power stations and other high temperature components, where ablation is a critical material behavior aspect. Hence it is relevant to investigate the ablation properties of Ti2AlC in ultra-high temperature environments, which have not been scrutinized before. In this paper, the ultra-high temperature ablation behavior of Ti2AlC ceramics using a conventional oxyacetylene flame is presented.
∗ Corresponding author. Tel.: +31 15 2782480; fax: +31 15 2786730. E-mail address: g.song@tudelft.nl (G.M. Song).

Abstract
The linear and mass ablation rates of Ti2AlC ceramics under an oxyacetylene flame at a temperature up to 3000 ◦ Cwere examined by measuring the dimensions and weight change of the ablated samples. The linear ablation rate was decreased from 0.14 𝛍 ms -1 for the first 30 s of the ablation to 0.08 𝛍 ms -1 after 180 s. Ti2AlC ceramics gained small amounts of weight upon ablation, which is attributed to the formation of oxidation products on the ablated surface. The ablation surface exhibits a two-layer structure: an oxide outer layer, consisting mainly of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and some Al2TiO5, and a porous sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1 -x C and TiC x O y . With increasing ablation time, the content of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 in the outer layer increased, and more pores developed in the sub-surface layer. The thermal oxidation of Ti2AlC under the flame and scouring of the viscous oxidation products by high-speed flow of gas torch are the main ablation mechanisms.

Keywords: Ti2AlC; Ablation; Oxyacetylene flame; Oxidation

Table 3
Actually, the oxidising conditions generated in the bottom-up air furnace, the target temperature apart, differ a lot from those experienced by the discs exposed to the hot flame: in the former case equilibria very likely prevailed, while in the second case very fast exposure to gases imposed conditions far from thermodynamic equilibrium. In the presence of oxygen, the prevailing reactions thermodynamically favour the transformation of the (Ta,Ti)C phase to rutile-type Ti-rich dioxide and orthorhombic type Ta-rich pentoxide. Based on the calculated thermo-chemical equilibria involving only stoichiometric and pure TiC and TaC with oxygen, the release of gaseous CO2 seems to be favoured when compared to that of CO. On one side, protection against oxidation/ablation is afforded as long as solid and strongly refractory oxide products are formed. On the other hand, the in-situ formed silica glass at 1873 K, wherever is present, is stable and provides protection to the adjacent areas. An additional protective contribution might derive from specific eutectics between SiO2-Ta2O5 [10] and TiO2-Ta2O5 [17], mostly in those areas of the Cf /C-STT composite not directly facing the harsh external environment. When the testing environment becomes harsher in term of temperature, pressure and gas reactivity (this is the case of the oxyacetylene flame test), the aforementioned in-situ generated glasses have fewer (or none at all) changes to survive due to rapid volatilization, and the only solid oxidation products of (Ta,Ti)C remain as the ultimate wall capable of delaying the ongoing oxidation/ablation of the bulk composite. Traces of the Ta2O5-SiO2 eutectic, which has the appearance of a molten phase, were found in the ablated Cf /C-STT composite (see Fig. 16). The EDX spectrum of such an molten phase is compared to that of an oxide sub-layer which formed during oxyacetylene flame shot.

A B S T R A C T
Ablation-resistant performance and damage mechanism of carbon fiber reinforced ZrC based ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composite prepared via alloyed reactive melt infiltration were investigated using a laser ablation testing method under different laser power densities (LPD) and time periods. Ablation damage of the composite was obviously enhanced tested with increasing LPD and reduced with prolonging tested time. A fused ZrO2 protecting layer was in situ formed during ablation testing, significantly improving the composite's ablation-resistant performance. Ablated microstructures of the composites tested with different LPD were characterized and an ablation mechanism going through three distinct periods was proposed. In the first period, the testing temperature is low and ablation damage was thought to be mainly controlled by the oxidation of carbon, ZrC and Zr2Si phases. With the increase of testing temperatures, ablation damage became controlled by evaporating of ZrO2 and SiO2 formed in oxidizing stage. In the final period with the highest testing temperatures, ablation damage became synergistically dominated by repeatedly scouring away and new formation of the fused ZrO2 protecting layer.
and oxyacetylene flame testing were employed to test and study the ablation resistant properties of materials. For decades, a significant amount of work is done to test material's ablation resistance and behavior using these above methods. Wind-tunnel and plasma ablation testing are frequently used and can simulate the environment with strong gas flowing and high enthalpy. Nevertheless, both of them have extremely high cost [6-8]. By contrast, oxyacetylene flame ablation testing is demonstrated to be a simple and economical method to evaluate material's ablation resistance [9,10]. However, the testing temperature is restricted by oxyacetylene flame temperature (about 3000 °C). Additionally, complex combustion products (O2, CO2, etc. [11]) are included in testing environment, which may sometimes influence the reasonability of the testing results.

2. Experimental
Bulk Ti2AlC ceramic samples were prepared via an in situ solid-liquid reaction between Ti, Al and graphite powders, heated to a high temperature. Ti, Al and graphite powders with the desired atomic ratio of 2:1.1:1 were mixed by ball-milling for 20 h in an ethanol solution. The slurry was dried at 60 ◦ C and cold-pressed into blocks with 8 MPa in a graphite die, and then hot-pressed with 30 MPa at 1450 ◦ Cfor 5 h in flowing argon gas. Disk shaped samples with a size of 30 mm in diameter and 10 mm in thickness were cut from the sintered bulk Ti2AlC by using electrical discharge wire machining for ablation tests. The planar surfaces of the disks were grinded using SiC emery papers with 400 down to 1200 grit, followed by polishing with 1 𝛍 m diamond paste. The ablation tests were implemented according to the standard GJB 323A-96 with an oxyacetylene flame. 10,11 The pressure and the flow rate of acetylene were respectively, 0.095 MPa and 1.116 m 3 h -1 , and the pressure and the flow rate of oxygenwererespectively, 0.4 MPa and 1.512 m 3 h -1 . The distance between the flame gun mouth and ablation surface of the disk was fixed at 10 mm. The centres of the flame and the torch were aligned horizontally. Under such conditions the material to be tested would be locally exposed to a flame temperature up to 3000 ◦ C 10-12 leading to hot gas flow induced material removal.

3.3.1. Carbon short fiber composite oxidation
The oxidation performance of short Cf UHTCMCs has primarily been characterized  by  static  testing  in  a  high  temperature  furnace  and exposure  to  an  oxyacetylene  torch  environment.  As  is  typical,  the ZrB2 -SiC  matrix  has  been  the  subject  of  most  investigations.  When oxidized in air for an hour at 1500 ◦ C, the behavior of the Cf-reinforced composite was similar to that of the bulk ceramic, in which distinct layers of ZrO2 and SiO2 formed on the oxidized surface [160]. He et al. [177] looked closely at the effect of small amounts of fiber additions, reporting that the total oxide scale thickness decreased with increasing Cf (up  to  20  vol%).  It  was  alleged  that  the  carbon  fibers  underwent reduction in the presence of SiC and B2O3, forming phases of SiC and B4C that inhibited further oxidation. Hong et al. [171] performed a similar study but for higher contents of Cf  (20 -50 vol%), finding that static oxidation behavior was stable up to 40 vol%. When the fiber volume content was 50 %, more pores were generated from Cf  volatilization, inhibiting the formation and adhesion of a protective oxide scale. The growth  of  a  dense  and  continuous  oxide  layer  is  critical  to  protect composites  against  continued  oxidation,  which  has  motivated  researchers to enhance the scale formation by different additives and architectures. Gui et al. [174] introduced ZrSi2 to the ZrB2 matrix in place of SiC, demonstrating the growth of a dense oxide layer after exposure to M. Ammendola et al.

6. Conclusions
Fully dense hot-pressed ZrB2 containing 15%vol SiC and increasing amounts of LaB6 (10, 15, and 20 vol%) at expenses of ZrB2 were oxidized under high enthalpy supersonic air-flows using an arc-jet facility. Transient thermal analysis processed via CFD provided a good agreement between the numerical steady-state temperature and the temperature distribution obtained experimentally through a two-color pyrometer combined with an IR thermo-camera: peak temperatures up to 2300 K (11.4 MJ/kg of specific total enthalpy) were predicted at the stagnation points. During the exposure to arc-jet supersonic air flow the presence of LaB6 into the ZrB215%SiC base system did not end up in the ''in situ'' growth of an outer solid scale predominantly made of La2Zr2O7 cubic pyrochlore. XRD and SEM-EDS analyses on the oxidized specimens verified the formation of a partly protective oxide scale made of monoclinic zirconia and a (La + Zr)-containing borosilicate glass. Diffusion of oxygen through zirconia was indicated as the slowest mechanism controlling the oxidation rate of the ZrB2-SiC-LaB6 system in such special environment. The presence of LaB6 gave rise to only rare events of phase separation, which did not contribute to an effective slow-down of the diffusion rate of the oxidants through the oxide scale. The contemporary presence of SiC and LaB6 was as crucial as unfavorable in breaking up integrity and coherence of the external oxide scale, and did not bring the necessary improved oxidation resistance to ZrB2 in such extreme conditions.

3.2.1. SiC short fiber composite oxidation
Oxidation  testing  for  short  SiCf UHTC  composites  has  largely centered around high temperature exposure in a static furnace environment. A study by Silvestroni and Sciti [124] was already mentioned, in which the static oxidation weight for ZrB2 -20 vol% SiC composites was reportedly independent of how SiC is included. Another study by Sciti et al. [139] evaluated how the oxidation behavior is affected by different sintering aids, modifying the ZrB2 -SiCf composites with Si3N4, ZrSi2, and MoSi2. Out of the three, the best improvement was observed with the inclusion of MoSi2, which formed stable reaction products that limited continued oxide growth and mass gain. Silvestroni et al. [151] found that the static oxidation protection could be further improved by replacing  the  traditional  Hi-Nicalon  fibers  with  Tyranno  SA3  fibers, which  possess  superior crystallinity,  stoichiometry,  and  chemical stability.

a b s t r a c t
The performances of a ZrB2-SiC-LaB6 ultra-high temperature ceramic (UHTC) was investigated in high enthalpy supersonic air flow. The UHTC material reached and maintained steady-state radiative surface temperature of 1973 K (monitored by pyrometer) for 5 min, and survived the arc-jet plasma exposure without any optical evidence of mechanical damage. The oxide scale covering externally the sample evolved into different textures. Transient thermal analysis processed via CFD provided a good agreement between the numerical steady-state temperature and the temperature distribution obtained experimentally. The contemporary addition of LaB6 and SiC to ZrB2 had a detrimental effect on the overall oxidation resistance.

on wing leading edges and nose-cones of hypersonic vehicles, or in propulsion systems of rockets and missiles [5,6].
Massive single-phase UHTC for high temperature structural applications in severe environments are affected by poor oxidation/ablation resistance [7], as well as lacking damage tolerance [8]. A substantial research effort has been made along the past two decades to design and develop ultra-high temperature resistant composite systems. As far as transition metal diboride (MB2) - based ceramics are concerned, several additions and compositional refinements are being attempted [8], but outstanding achievements were actually reached for SiC particulate additions in the range of 10-30 vol% [7,9]. The inclusion of SiC was recognized to significantly improve thermo-mechanical stability and oxidation resistance of MB2 matrices at temperature up to 1800 K by forming ''in situ'' protective amorphous, fluid borosilicate coatings on the external surfaces directly facing the oxidizing environment at high temperature [10].

1. Introduction
ZrB2, with a hexagonal crystal structure and powerful covalent bonds, has a high melting temperature about 3245 °C, high chemical stability and strength. Hence, these features of ZrB2 make it an attractive ultrahigh-temperature ceramic to be used as high-temperature electrodes, microelectronics, cutting tools, and refractory linings [1 -7]. In addition to low density (6.119 g/cm 3 ), ZrB2 has a relatively good electrical and thermal conductivity (1.0 × 10 7 S/m and 60 W/m.K, respectively). These properties makes it suitable for speci /uniFB01 c environments associated with the atmospheric re-entry and hypersonic /uniFB02 ights [8 -13]. Among other UHTC materials, these characteristics make it one of the best appropriate materials against the thermal shock resistance [14]. Making parts by electrical discharge machining processes (EDM) is another distinguishing feature of this material [15 -17]. However, it is not su /uniFB03 cient for the ZrB2 to be used in raw form in industrial parts alone; therefore, getting it reinforced with SiC particulates is proved to be e /uniFB00 ective in intensi /uniFB01 cation of both the oxidation resistance and room temperature strength [18 -20]. Besides enjoying good ablative and oxidation resistance properties for ZrB2 -SiC UHTCs, studies have proved that presence of SiC acts as a grain growth inhibitor [21,22]. Introducing 10, 20 and 30 vol% of SiC to ZrB2 ceramics, will not only improve the oxidation behavior up to 1600 °C through formation of

A B S T R A C T

4. Conclusions
The ultra-high temperature ablation properties of Ti2AlC under a high velocity oxyacetylene flame were examined. The ablated layer formed on the Ti2AlC surface is of a two-layer structure: a porous oxide outer layer, mainly consisting of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and Al2TiO5, and a porous O-rich sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1x C and TiC x O y . With increasing ablation time, the content of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 in the outer layer increased, and more pores developed in the sub-surface layer. After ablation for 180 s, the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate dropped to low values of 0.08 𝛍 ms -1 and -180 𝛍 g s -1 , respectively. The small negative mass rate is caused by the formation of solid oxides. The thermal oxidation of Ti2AlC and the scouring of the viscous oxidation products by high-speed gas flow are the principal ablation mechanisms.

3.3. Ablation mechanisms
During the ablation of the Ti2AlC under a fast flowing oxyacetylene flame, a series of complicated physical and chemical processes take place between the ultra-high temperature oxidative gasses and Ti2AlC. First, Ti2AlC likely decompose into TiC and TiAl, analogous to the decomposition of Ti2AlC powders exposed to high velocity oxy-fuel flame 27 :

The oxidation products Al2O3, TiO2 and Al2TiO5 comprise the ablation oxide layer. The weight gain upon ablation is attributed to oxidation-induced weight increase, which can be understood via the oxidation Eqs. (4)-(9). With one molar C loss, several molars oxygen is taken up via the formation of solid oxide and oxygen incorporation into the Ti2AlC matrix to formTiC x O y or/and Ti2Al1 -x C y O z (( y + z ) ≤ 1). 3.3. Ablation mechanisms
Although the oxyacetylene flame temperature is up to about 3000 ◦ C based on the test condition, 10 the temperature of the ablated surface of a disk will be lower due to heat transfer and dynamic thermal balance existing among the flame, the sample andtheenvironment.Normally,thesurfacetemperatureiswithin the range of 1800-3000 ◦ C depending on the ablated materials and the oxidation products formed on the ablation surfaces, as confirmed by the temperature tests in the prior studies by ourselves 12,16,17 and others. 20,32 The Al2O3, TiO2 and Al2TiO5 at the ablated surface will melt, but do not totally evaporate due to their relative high melting points (2053 ◦ Cfor Al2O3, 1843 ◦ C for TiO2 and 1850 ◦ C for Al2TiO5). 7 Due to the existence of an octahedral Ti2C sub-lattice in the Ti2AlC lattice, the melting points of Ti2AlC (or Ti2Al1 -x C) and the subsequently formed TiC0.5 twins may be very close to that of TiC ( ∼ 3250 ◦ C), 7 which enables Ti2AlC to withstand the severe ablation at ultrahigh temperatures. The molten TiO2 and Al2TiO5 together with the Al2O3 layer is postulated to form a viscous layer limiting the evaporation of CO or/and CO2, which might result in a volume swelling that in turn leads to the low values of linear ablation rate measured.

Oxyacetylene torch testing showed that the C/C-B 4 C-ZrB 2 composites showed less weight loss compared to the composite with carbon block (30%). Composite tubes of C/C with ZrB 2 through slurry (40-50% solid loading) and reactive infiltration (ZrOCl 2 .8H 2 O, boric acid and phenolic resin) have been attempted (Jayaseelan et al., 2011). The infiltrated tubes were fired at 1400°C in flowing argon atmosphere showed formation of ZrB 2 , ZrC and m-ZrO 2 . Ablation testing has been performed at 2190°C for 60s. The 2D C f / SiC-ZrB 2 -TaC composites have been made by CVI and slurry paste method (Li et al., 2011). After heat treatment at 1800°C, the 2D laminate was densified with SiC by CVI. The flexural strength, inter laminar shear strength and thermal expansion of the composites are 255±15 MPa, 27.4±5.1 MPa and 3.2×10 -6 /K respectively. The ablation tests of the C f /SiC-ZrB 2 -TaC composites using oxyacetylene torch flame for 20s showed the ablation rate is reduced (0.026 mm/s) compared to C f /SiC composites (0.083 mm/s). A SiC/ZrB 2 -SiC/SiC oxidation resistance multilayer coating for C f /C composites has been produced (Yao et al., 2012). 2D C f /C composite prepared by CVI with a density of 1.7 g/cm 3 is packed with Si, C and Al 2 O3 powders at 1700-2000°C for 120 min. The slurry of ZrB 2 dispersed in PCS is applied on heat treated samples by brush.

question: How do the ablation products formed on Ti2AlC ceramics under oxyacetylene flame exposure in Song et al. 2011 compare in stability to the oxide layers that form on hot-pressed ZrB2-SiC ceramics under plasma exposure above 1550 degrees C in Alfano et al. 2010?
answer:
The ablation products formed on Ti2AlC ceramics under oxyacetylene flame exposure in Song et al. 2011 consist of a two-layer structure: an oxide outer layer, mainly consisting of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and some Al2TiO5, and a porous sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1 -x C and TiC x O y [1]. These oxides are relatively stable, as they do not fully evaporate due to their high melting points [4]. 

In contrast, the oxide layers that form on hot-pressed ZrB2-SiC ceramics under plasma exposure above 1550 degrees C in Alfano et al. 2010 are composed of silica with trace amounts of boron oxide and zirconia if the maximum temperature is lower than about 1550 ◦ C, and zirconia for higher temperatures [19]. The stability of these oxide layers is not explicitly discussed in the provided sources, but the catalytic behavior and recombination coefficients suggest that the presence of borosilicate glass layers on ZrB2-SiC materials may influence their stability. However, no direct comparison of stability between the oxide layers of Ti2AlC and ZrB2-SiC ceramics is provided in the sources.

Abstract
The linear and mass ablation rates of Ti2AlC ceramics under an oxyacetylene flame at a temperature up to 3000 ◦ Cwere examined by measuring the dimensions and weight change of the ablated samples. The linear ablation rate was decreased from 0.14 𝛍 ms -1 for the first 30 s of the ablation to 0.08 𝛍 ms -1 after 180 s. Ti2AlC ceramics gained small amounts of weight upon ablation, which is attributed to the formation of oxidation products on the ablated surface. The ablation surface exhibits a two-layer structure: an oxide outer layer, consisting mainly of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and some Al2TiO5, and a porous sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1 -x C and TiC x O y . With increasing ablation time, the content of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 in the outer layer increased, and more pores developed in the sub-surface layer. The thermal oxidation of Ti2AlC under the flame and scouring of the viscous oxidation products by high-speed flow of gas torch are the main ablation mechanisms.

3.3.2. HfB2  and ZrB2  coatings
coatings with different thicknesses under oxyacetylene flame. After ablation, three distinct ablation regions were formed on the substrate surface. The central region was the core of oxyacetylene flame with a temperature close to 3273 K, exhibiting more severe loss on the four sample surfaces, as shown in Fig. 16 (g). According to the ablation results (Fig. 16(h)), it was obvious that ZrB2  coating can improve the ablation resistance of C/SiC composites. During the ablation process, ZrB2  and SiC were oxidized to ZrO2  and SiO2, forming four layers: (i) an inner SiO2  layer, (ii) a thin discontinuous porous SiO2 layer, (iii) a dense ZrO2 -SiO2 layer, and (iv) a top ZrO2 layer. These layers served as thermal barriers, offering protective against further ablations of the inner coating, and the schematic diagram of ZrB2 coatings ablation behavior is exhibited in Fig. 16(i). Wang et al. [159] tested  the  CVD-ZrB2 coating  using  a  high-throughput  solar  beam. Compared with other ceramics, the CVD-ZrB2  coating exhibited good thermal  shock  resistance,  and  proved  particularly  effective  in  atmospheric pressure up to 1100 ◦ C.

3.1. Ablation properties
Thelinear and mass ablation rates as a function of the ablation time for the Ti2AlC samples are plotted in Fig. 2. The linear ablation rate was 0.14 𝛍 ms -1 during the initial 30 s, and decreased to 0.085 𝛍 ms -1 when the ablation time exceeded 180 s. Such a decrease in the linear ablation rate with ablation time is generally attributed to the formation of an ablation layer on the base material. Similar decreases have also been observed in other ultra-high temperature materials, such as tungsten and ZrC and TiC reinforced tungsten composites. 16,17 It is noteworthy that the mass ablation rate of Ti2AlC measured in this study was -270 𝛍 g s -1 at the onset of ablation (30 s) and -180 𝛍 g s -1 for ablating 180 s, which means that the present ablation resulted in a mass gain rather than a mass loss (Fig. 1). This mass gain behavior is contrary to ablation behavior of someultra-high temperature materials, such as carbon/carbon, 18 Cu-infiltrated tungsten and tungsten composites, 16,17 in which mass loss caused a positive mass ablation rates (i.e. Rm >0). The ablation properties of the Ti2AlC studied here are listed in Table 1 together with some modern ultra-high temperature materials tested with oxyacetylene flame according to the same standard GJB 323A-96. 10 Compared to these ultra-high temper- ature materials, Ti2AlC possesses an extreme low linear ablation rate, viz. 0.11 𝛍 ms -1 for 60 s ablation, which is close to zero. Such a low ablation rate may ensure a stable shape profile if Ti2AlC is to be used in ultra-high temperature ablative environments.
Fig. 2. Linear and mass ablation rates of Ti2AlC ceramics.

4. Conclusions
The ultra-high temperature ablation properties of Ti2AlC under a high velocity oxyacetylene flame were examined. The ablated layer formed on the Ti2AlC surface is of a two-layer structure: a porous oxide outer layer, mainly consisting of 𝛂 -Al2O3 and TiO2 and Al2TiO5, and a porous O-rich sub-surface layer containing Ti2Al1x C and TiC x O y . With increasing ablation time, the content of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 in the outer layer increased, and more pores developed in the sub-surface layer. After ablation for 180 s, the linear ablation rate and mass ablation rate dropped to low values of 0.08 𝛍 ms -1 and -180 𝛍 g s -1 , respectively. The small negative mass rate is caused by the formation of solid oxides. The thermal oxidation of Ti2AlC and the scouring of the viscous oxidation products by high-speed gas flow are the principal ablation mechanisms.

1. Introduction
Ti2AlC is a typical example of atomically layered ternary carbides belonging to the so-called MAX phase family. 1 Recently, it has attracted scientific and commercial attention due to its excellent isothermal and thermal cycling oxidation resistance in combination with its high electrical conductivity, resistance against thermal shock and strengths at high temperatures. 2-6 The low oxidation rate is caused by the formation of a continuous protective 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer on the Ti2AlC substrate, and the superior spallation resistance of the 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer is attributed to the well-matched thermal expansion coefficients between 𝛂 -Al2O3 layer and Ti2AlC substrate. 5,6 Based on this, it is likely that Ti2AlC may withstand ultra-high temperatures for relatively short times since Ti2AlC will decompose into sub-stoichiometry Ti2Al x CoreventwinnedTiC0.5 superstructureandAlratherthan becoming a liquid phase itself as long as the temperature does not exceed the melting point ( ∼ 3250 ◦ C) of TiC x ( x =0.5-1). 7-9 Therefore, Ti2AlC is probably a promising candidate for thermal structural components exposed to ultra-high temperature ablative environments such as nose caps of missiles, rudders and nozzles of rocket engines, gas burners and heat shields in nuclear power stations and other high temperature components, where ablation is a critical material behavior aspect. Hence it is relevant to investigate the ablation properties of Ti2AlC in ultra-high temperature environments, which have not been scrutinized before. In this paper, the ultra-high temperature ablation behavior of Ti2AlC ceramics using a conventional oxyacetylene flame is presented.

11. Ablation properties
Furthermore, when exposure time increased to 120 s, the oxide layer itself got spalled during cooling. Li et al. [444] studied the effect of incorporation of AlN on the ablation behaviour of ZrB2-20 vol% SiC. This composite showed improved ablation resistance, as compared to ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite, below 2000 °C for 300 s. The ablation surfaces of the samples consisted of mainly ZrO2. The Si-Al-O glassy phase could fill in the voids and micro-cracks of the oxide layer, thus preventing the diffusion of oxygen so as to improve the ablation resistance of the composite when the temperature is below 2000 °C. However, in extreme high temperature (≥2600 °C), the addition of AlN has a negative effect on the ablation behaviour due to the formation of large amounts of molten glassy phase and gas phase.

A B S T R A C T
Recent studies have reported improvements in oxidation resistance through the incorporation of TiB2  or ZrB2  into Al2O3-SiC matrices. For example, Subha et al. [31] demonstrated that Al2O3-SiC-TiB2 composites sintered at 1600 ◦ C exhibited enhanced oxidation onset temperatures (~610 ◦ C) and improved mechanical properties due to the formation of TiO2-SiO2-rich oxide scales. Similarly, Balak and Azizieh [32] showed that ZrB2-SiC composites developed a dense ZrO2-SiO2  oxide layer at 1600 ◦ C,  which  significantly  inhibited  mass  gain  during  oxidation exposure. A study by Nallusamy et al. [16,32,33] further highlighted that  the  thermal  stability  of  Al2O3-SiC-TiB2 composites  is  strongly dependent on the TiB2 content, with excessive loading leading to porous and unstable oxide scales. These findings are in line with recent studies on  TiB ₂ -based composites, which  reveal complex  microstructural changes and thermal response with increasing reinforcement content [34]Meanwhile, Hassan and Balani [35] reported that combining ZrB2 with SiC resulted in synergistic oxidation resistance due to the complementary formation of refractory ZrO2 and viscous SiO2.

4. Results and discussion
Thermodynamically-stable oxides for the ZrB2-SiC-AlN system include Al2O3, SiO2, B2O3, and ZrO2. Destabilization of the stable oxides is possible only at extremely low oxygen pressures. For instance, the destabilizing of these oxides at 2027 /C14 C (2300 K) requires an oxygen pressure below 10 /C0 10 atm. The stability of the oxides also depends on their relevant vapor species. For instance, as shown in Fig. 1b, an increase in the temperature from 1527 to 2027 /C14 C (1800 e 2300 K) results in more than two orders of magnitude increase in B2O3 vapor pressure. At the current test temperature, B2O3 is expected to evaporate signi /uniFB01 cantly, once it forms. During the experiment, a green /uniFB02 uorescence was observed at the initial stage of the plasma exposure, but diminished after a few minutes. This green /uniFB02 uorescence emission corresponds to volatilization of the boron species [18]. EDS further con /uniFB01 rmed boron is largely absent from the surface. SiO2 has a suf /uniFB01 ciently low vapor pressure that it is not likely to vaporize as gas phase SiO2. However, SiO2 is prone to volatilization during active oxidation according to the following reaction [19]:

oxidation resistance of UHTCs as it was shown for the /uniFB01 rst time by Tripp et al. [6] and to the processing by lowering sintering temperatures [7 -9]. Then, when combined with SiC, ZrB2 and HfB2-based composites exhibit indeed excellent refractoriness, high oxidation resistance, and are as such good potential candidates for the above-mentioned application. An important parameter such as the upper limit of the service temperature is strongly related to the characteristics of secondary phases. For example, above 1500 K, the oxide scale formed in air on the surface of pure MB2, with M = Zr or Hf, is unstable and non-protective due to intensive volatility of B2O3 [4 -6,10 -13] while the SiC-containing MB2 ceramics show enhanced resistance to oxidation up to 1900 K due to the formation of borosilicate based coatings. In fact for temperature higher than 1500 K, the addition of SiC promotes, on the exposed surface, the formation of a borosilicate glass that gives much more oxidation protection than B2O3 alone. Several studies have dealt with the thermal stability and physical properties of ultra-refractory MB2-based ceramics in oxidizing environments, and highlighted the role of composition and microstructure on the mechanisms governing the materials response to extreme environments [10 -12,14 -27].

a b s t r a c t
We present a design concept for developing ZrB2-SiC-AlN composites with enhanced oxidative stability at ultra-high temperatures (~2000 /C14 C) and low pressures (100 Torr). The oxidative stability of these materials arises from a protective silica based scale. However, active oxidation of SiC above 1700 /C14 C presents a challenge, which we circumvent through the in-situ growth of a zirconia layer that serves as a thermal barrier, ensuring that the effective temperature at the zirconia/Si rich subscale is less than the active oxidation temperature. The design concept is validated by a series of ultra-high temperature oxidation experiments under static as well as cyclic conditions.

3.2. Ablated microstructure
The XRD patterns recorded of the surface centre zone of the original sample and the samples after exposure to the flame for 30, 60 and 180 s are present in Fig. 5. The original material Ti2AlC contains a small amount of Al2O3 particles which was not detected by the XRD due to its low content, as shown in Fig. 1. These patterns show that when the ablation time was shorter than 30 s, the oxidation products are mainly 𝛂 -Al2O3, TiO2 and minor fractions of Al2TiO5. After ablation of longer times, more TiO2 and Al2TiO5 were formed. Furthermore, a titanium oxycarbide (TiC x O y , ( x + y ) ≤ 1) phase, which can be regarded as a TiC with partial substitution of carbon by oxygen without changing its rock-salt structure, 21 was also detected on the ablated surfaces (see the peaks at 2 θ of 41.8 and 72.5 ◦ in Fig. 5). The intensity of the peaks corresponding to TiC x O y decreased with increasing ablation time, which may imply that this oxycarbide might be less stable than the oxides of TiO2 and Al2TiO5 or even 𝛂 -Al2O3, and/or might stay beneath these oxides.

A B S T R A C T
Recent  studies  have  proposed  SiC  addition  to  improve  oxidation resistance via the formation of borosilicate layers (B2O3 -SiO2), yet this strategy still lacks robustness at ultra-high temperatures [32,43,44]. A comprehensive  review  on  boride-based  composite  oxidation  kinetics and  failure  mechanisms  also  reinforces  the  importance  of  exploring synergistic reinforcement strategies [45]. Moreover, few studies have explored the combined use of both TiB2  and ZrB2  in oxide -non-oxide hybrid ceramic matrices, despite their potential to form a synergistic multi-oxide  scale  (ZrO2-TiO2-SiO2)  that  could  offer  better  adhesion, densification, and thermal stability [35,38,46,47]. Recent developments in  multilayered  UHTC  configurations  further  validate  the  benefits  of combining diverse oxide-forming reinforcements for long-term oxidation resistance [48]. The combined use of TiB2 and ZrB2 has the potential to balance each other ' s weaknesses-TiB2  contributes to lower thermal expansion mismatch and densification, while ZrB2  supports phase stability and forms refractory oxides with better scale adherence [35,36,38, 42]. This study fills that gap by evaluating the oxidation behaviour of Al2O3-SiC composites reinforced with varying ratios of ZrB2  and TiB2, focusing  on  the  formation,  evolution,  and  thermal  resilience  of  the resulting multi-oxide protective layers. The novelty lies in identifying an optimal ZrB2-TiB2  ratio (notably, 40:20 vol. %) that delays oxidation onset, widens the resistance window, and minimises mass gain under high-temperature oxidative exposure.

4. Conclusion
