with  40  %  ZrB2 and  20  %  TiB2,  which  exhibited  the  most  notable resistance to oxidation and improved thermal stability. The observed grain growth in all specimens due to buoyancy effects was effectively mitigated by the inclusion of ZrB2, particularly up to 40 vol %, which also restricted grain growth. However, further increases in ZrB2 content led to a reduction in oxidation resistance. Moreover, the study found that ZrB2  and TiB2  reinforcements not only enhanced the thermal stability  of  the  Al2O3-SiC  composite  but  also  shifted  the  crystallisation point to higher temperatures, surpassing those of ZrB2-SiC and HfB2-SiC composites. The critical oxidation point, where char formation began, was identified, and it was noted that further temperature increases led to oxidation, forming a protective layer that contributed to the material ' s thermal stability at elevated temperatures. These findings suggest that the Al2O3-SiC-ZrB2-TiB2  composite holds significant promise for future aerospace  and  mechanical  applications,  particularly  in  environments demanding high-temperature resistance and structural integrity. This composite material could play a crucial role in addressing the stringent requirements of next-generation aeronautical and aerospace systems.

The coarsen and phase transition of the t -Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 are prevented resulting in the forming of the t -Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 nano-crystals. Such nano-crystals distribute uniformly in the SiO 2 glassy phase which forms the network structure preventing the evaporation of the SiO 2 and the viscosity of the oxide layer. Consequently, the oxide layer possesses excellent structural stability and protects the under-layer composites from further ablation damage. The Zr0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 nano-crystals with the diameter < 800 nm and the ZrO2 micron-crystals with the diameter > 1 µ mformafter adding Y2 O3. The mole fractions of the crystalline in oxide layer are illustrated as Figure 4h, which indicates the content of Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 nano-crystals increases with the rise of Y 2O3 content in composites. The Zr 0.9 Y0.1 O1.95 nano-crystals distribute uniformly in SiO 2 glassy phase. The size e/uniFB00ect of the nano-crystals increases the viscosity of oxides layer and retarding the evaporation of SiO 2 phase (Figure S6, Supporting Information), which results in increased structural stability of the oxide layer. Moreover, the mass recession rates also decrease due to the lower evaporation of SiO 2 , and as a result, the C f /ZrB2 -SiC-Y 2O3 composites are e/uniFB00ectively protected by the dense oxide layer and exhibit improved ablation resistance. Especially, the protection of the oxides layer is also associated with the oxygen permeability of oxides.

Document: Song_2011_EuropeanCeramicSociety_Ultra-hightemperature.pdf. Title: Ultra-high temperature ablation behavior of Ti2AlC ceramics under an oxyacetylene flame. The header and byline of this document reads: Available online at www.sciencedirect.com Journal of the European Ceramic Society 31 (2011) 855-862 G.M. Song a , a a ∗ , S.B. Li , C.X. Zhao a , W.G. Sloof , S. van der Zwaag b , Y.T. Pei c , J.Th.M. De Hosson c

Abstract
Microstructures were investigated for ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-HfB2-SiC ultra high temperature ceramics that were subjected to a high temperature plasma environment. Both materials were tested in the MESOX facility to determine the recombination coefficient for atomic oxygen up to 1750 ◦ C in subsonic air plasma flow. Surfaces were analyzed before and after testing to gain a deeper insight of the surface catalytic properties of these materials. Microstructural analyses highlighted oxidation induced surface modification. Oxide layers were composed of silica with trace amounts of boron oxide and zirconia if the maximum temperature was lower than about 1550 ◦ C and zirconia for higher temperatures. The differences in the oxide layer composition may account for the different catalytic behavior. In particular, the presence of a borosilicate glass layer on the surface of ZrB2-SiC materials guarantees atomic oxygen recombination coefficients that are relatively lower than the coefficients measured when only zirconia is present. The oxidation processes of ZrB2-HfB2-SiC materials, associated with catalytic tests carried out up to 1550 ◦ C, lead to the formation of hafnia as well as silica, and zirconia. The higher recombination coefficients measured in the case of ZrB2-HfB2-SiC materials can be correlated with the presence of hafnia which is probably characterized by higher catalytic activity compared to zirconia. In any case, the investigated materials demonstrate a low catalytic activity over the inspected temperature range with maximum values of recombination coefficients close to 0.1.

1. Introduction
The application of 5G/6G high-frequency communication technology  has  promoted  the  development  of  low-temperature  co-fired  ceramics technology (LTCC) towards lower sintering temperatures (lower than the melting point of the Al electrode, ≤ 660 ◦ C), namely ultra-low temperature  co-fired  ceramics  technology  (ULTCC),  to  meet  the  requirements  of  miniaturization,  low  power  consumption,  high  signal transmission rate, and high integration of electronic devices. The ULTCC substrate  materials  require  a  low  relative  permittivity  ( ε r ),  a  low dielectric loss (tan δ or high quality-factor, Q = 1/ tan δ and f is the frequency) and a near-zero resonant frequency temperature coefficient ( τ f ) to meet the high-frequency communication performance of electronic substrates [1,2]. The amorphous characteristic of glass network structure and the high covalency of glass forming chemical bonds endow glass-ceramic materials with intrinsic low ε r and low sintering temperature, making them an ideal choice for electronic substrates [3,4]. Silicate  glass-ceramics  are  the  earliest  glass  system  applied  to  LTCC substrates,  such  as  the  CaO-B2O3-SiO2 [5],  CaO-MgO-SiO2  [6]  and MgO-B2O3-SiO2 glass-ceramics [7]. These glass-ceramics have high Q × f values above 50,000 GHz. At the same time, the three-dimensional glass

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  exhibit  the  remarkable potential  for  application  in  the  aircraft  thermal  protection  systems owing  to  their  exceptionally  high  melting  points  ( > 3000 ℃ )  and excellent ablation resistance [1,2]. However, the intrinsic brittleness of ceramics results in poor thermal shock resistance, thus limiting their applications  in  structural  components  [1].  Ultra-high  temperature ceramic  matrix  composites  (UHTCMCs)  can  achieve  the  superiority integration of ultra-high temperature resistant UHTC matrix and high strength carbon fiber reinforcement. Therefore, UHTCMCs possess the excellent  ablation  resistance  and  mechanical  properties  which  are currently considered as the most promising for thermal protection systems in ultra-high temperature conditions [3,4].
As for the composition design of ceramic matrix, ZrC/ZrB2-SiC and HfC/HfB2-SiC  are  the  preferred  choices  owing  to  the  excellent  antiablation  ability  of  their  oxidation  products  [5 -11].  The  solid  oxide skeletons with high melting points (HfO2: ~2810 ℃ , ZrO2: ~2670 ℃ ) demonstrate remarkable thermal stability and ablation resistance. The formed glass phases (SiO2, borosilicate, ZrSiO4/HfSiO4, et al.) with low oxygen permeability can fill the pores of the solid ZrO2/HfO2 skeletons. The synergetic interaction between the solid oxides serving as support skeletons and the filled glass phases serving as oxygen diffusion inhibiter

1. Introduction
Development of future, high speed aerospace vehicles demands that thermal protection systems are exposed to higher thermal and structural requirements  while  in  an  oxygen  rich  environment  [1].  Such  as  the surface temperature of the front cone and wing leading edge can exceed 2500 ◦ C [2], accompanied by severe ablation. C/C composites, known for their lightweight, high strength, low thermal expansion coefficient, and enhanced mechanical properties at high temperatures, are considered ideal materials for thermal protection components [3]. However, the high susceptibility to oxidative degradation above 450 ◦ C poses a major challenge to their long service life, with a significant reduction in mechanical properties.
The introduction of ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) inside C/C composites is a promising approach to enhance their service performance  [4,5].  UHTCs  exhibit  high  physicochemical  stability  under extremely high temperature environments ( > 2000 ◦ C, plasma, atomic

1. Introduction
In recent years, with the rapid development of the aerospace field, higher requirements have been put forward for the key hot-end components of aero engines, aircraft and rocket engines, which must have the ability of long-term service in extreme environments such as high temperatures, high pressures and high-temperature water-oxygen corrosion [1 -3]. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) can maintain stable service performance under high temperature environments above 3000 ◦ C, rendering them one of the most promising potential materials for high temperature thermal protection systems [4 -6]. Zr-based UHTCs have high melting point ( ≥ 3000 ◦ C), low density, excellent high temperature mechanical properties, good thermal stability, good oxidation resistance and corrosion resistance, and are therefore widely used in

A B S T R A C T
Qualification and reusability campaigns were performed on ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMCs) made of a ZrB2-SiC matrix with short/long carbon fibre to assess their performance as thermal protection systems. Heat fluxes and stagnation pressures were set following those of reference re-entry missions. An interdisciplinary analysis followed by combining infrared measurements of the thermal response, estimation of  surface  properties  and  microstructural  characterization.  Compared  to  conventional  C/SiC  composites,  the increased erosion resistance of the new UHTCMCs was assessed in ultra-hot regime: although these experienced much higher thermal loads, they underwent minimal erosion and preserved their structural stability even after multiple exposures.
high temperature strength and capability to manage and conduct heat when the service temperature exceeds 2250 K [9 -11]. These characteristics  allow  UHTCs  to  work  in  thermo-chemically  aggressive  environments encountered in such demanding space applications [12 -15]. These  materials  include  groups  IV-V  transition  metal  diborides  and carbides (i.e. ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, TaC). Diborides in particular, despite having  lower  melting  point  than  carbides,  are  characterized  by  substantially high thermal conductivity, therefore they succeed in relieving temperature gradients and minimizing peak of temperatures nearby the stagnation region by uniformly redistributing heat into the material.

3. Results and discussion
3 that the Hertzian curves have the typical shape reported for other polycrystalline ceramics [36,41-46], with a first linear stretch attributable to the elastic deformation regime and a second nonlinear stretch associated with the quasiplastic deformation regime. The particular details of the curves depend however on the test temperature and on the metal disilicide used as sintering additive. Also, it is quite clear from the mere visual comparison of the asymptotic limit of these curves that, for a given temperature, ZrB2 + 20 vol%MoSi2 is harder than ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2 and ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, both of which have similar hardness; these observations are consistent with the trends inferred from the Vickers indentation tests at room temperature.

3.4. Multiphase UHTCs-Modified C/C Composites
Studies revealed that single-phase UHTCs modification offers limited ablation resistance improvement over unmodified C/C composites. At lower temperatures (<1000 ◦ C), the UHTCs phase is prone to oxidation and cracking, offering limited protection. At higher temperatures, there is a significant difference in thermal expansion coefficients between UHTCs and their oxides with the C/C matrix, making it easy for porous oxide layers to form and crack off during ablation, resulting in even poorer ablation resistance than unmodified C/C composites [70,76,100,106-108]. In fact, the operating temperatures of hypersonic vehicles and re-entry atmospheric flight vehicles vary continuously between 1000 ◦ C and 3000 ◦ C, requiring thermal protection materials with short processing times, low density, high-temperature resistance, high strength and toughness, good long-term oxidation resistance and ablation durability, and a long service life [109,110]. To overcome single-phase limitations and further enhance performance, researchers try to introduce the second or third phase (e.g., SiC, MoSi2, and [111]) to form multiphase UHTCs-modified C/C composites, leveraging their differing oxidation resistance properties for broader thermal protection [112-116].

1. Introduction
Hypersonic vehicles have attracted much attention in recent years because of their high speed (Mach number > 5) and super maneuverability with regard to subsonic and supersonic vehicles [1]. However, when it flying in the atmosphere (the near space: 20 -100 km away from the ground) at high velocities, high dynamic pressure, high aerodynamic heating and the dissociation of oxygen are occurred, which results in the demand for thermal protection systems (TPS). For example, the nose cone, wing leading edge and other parts of hypersonic aircraft must be able to serve for a long time in harsh environment with the temperatures exceeding 2000 ◦ C and highly dissociated oxygen, which puts forward new  requirements  for  ultrahigh  temperature  heat-resistant  materials [2 -4]. C/C composites exhibit outstanding high-temperature mechanical properties and thermal stability in inert atmosphere [5]. Nevertheless, C/C composites exhibit poor oxidation resistance, and the service temperature under oxidation conditions can-not exceed 500 ◦ C [6]. In the past decades, ultrahigh temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have been introduced into the matrix of the C/C composites to prepare C/C-ultrahigh temperature ceramics ( i.e. ,  C/C-UHTCs) by several authors, which take advantage of the refractoriness of the UHTCs and the non-brittleness of C/C composites [4,7 -13].
The widely used methods for introducing UHTCs phase into the C/C

A B S T R A C T
Borate glass-ceramics are one of the most suitable materials for ULTCC substrates. However, it is still difficult to prepare a glass-ceramic with both ultra-low sintering temperature and high quality-factor, for the complexity of crystallization behavior in borate glass network. Herein, 1.6mol% CuO doping promotes the transition of B 3 + ions into three coordination and the increase of superstructural units with low connectivity, in CuO-ZnO-B2O3 glass. The variation of short-range and medium-range structure facilitates the complete transformation of the crystalline phase into Zn3B2O6  crystal. It optimizes the chemical bond characteristics and microstructure. This method increases the Q × f values by 40 %. At a sintering temperature of 650 ◦ C, it exhibits excellent microwave dielectric properties of ε r ~6.31, Q × f ~27,375 GHz, τ f ~ GLYPH<0> 8 ppm/ ◦ C, Vickers hardness ~599 kgf/mm 2 and good chemical compatibility with Ag or Al electrodes. New possibilities have been demonstrated for the preparation of electronic substrates using glass-ceramics.
network structure composed of [SiO4] tetrahedron makes the sintering temperature  of  silicate  glass-ceramics  higher  than  900 ◦ C.  Borate glass-ceramics exhibit high adaptability in ULTCC substrates, due to its ultra-low  sintering  temperature  that  can  meet  the  temperature  requirements of advanced electronic components such as Al electrodes. This is attributed to the loose glass network of borate glass composed of two-dimensional [BO3] triangles. ZnO-B2O3  [8] and Li2O-Al2O3-B2O3 [9] glass-ceramics are the representative glass systems, which superior chemical compatibility is the main advantage. However, the Q × f value of  borate  glass-ceramic  is  between  10,000 -20,000  GHz.  Therefore, related  research  has  focused  on  improving  the Q × f value  of  borate glass-ceramics.

3. Results and discussion
Besides the excellent microwave dielectric properties, the H3BO3 ceramic has the lowest density (1.46 g/cm 3 ) among the microwave dielectric ceramics. Therefore, the H3BO3 ceramic is very attractive for the light weight of microwave circuits. The simple room-temperature preparing process of H3BO3 ceramic is also bene /uniFB01 cial for the easy integration with microwave circuits, and satis /uniFB01 es the requirement on the densi /uniFB01 cation temperature for the newly developed ultra-low temperature co/uniFB01 red ceramic (ULTCC) technology ( /C20 700 /C14 C) [86]. As shown in Table S1, the H3BO3 ceramic is distinguished among the low-permittivity ULTCC materials considering the low densi /uniFB01 cation temperature, low permittivity, high Qf value and low density. In fact, the ultra-low permittivity of H3BO3 ceramic is related to its low density. According to Equation (1), the predicted permittivity of H3BO3 is 2.36 and near to the measured value (2.84). The ultra-low permittivity of H3BO3 is partially attributed to its low molecule polarizability of 6.08 Å 3 , which bene /uniFB01 ts from the neglectable dielectric polarizability of H þ and the very low polarizability of B 3 þ (0.05 Å 3 ) [20]. Besides, the molecule volume of H3BO3 is as high as 81.64 Å 3 due to its molecule crystal structure shown in Fig. S1, which not only leads to the ultralow permittivity of H3BO3 according to Equation (1), but also results in a low theoretical density of 1.50 g/cm 3 . The results also indicate that more ultra-low-permittivity microwave dielectric materials may be explored in the molecule crystals with low molecule polarizability and large molecule volume, which will /uniFB01 ll the gap between polymers and traditional microwave dielectric ceramics that are usually ionic crystals and covalent crystals.

4. Conclusions
An optimization method for the quality factor of glass-ceramic was developed in this work. Based on the regulation of the glass network structure  in  the  short-range  and  medium-range,  the  crystallization behavior of glass-ceramic is optimized by doping with trace amounts of CuO. 1.6mol% CuO doping resulted in the main crystalline phase of glass-ceramic complete  transformation into  Zn3B2O6 crystal.  The chemical bond characteristics and microstructure of glass-ceramic have also been optimized. This increases the Q × f value of glass-ceramic by 40 %. At an ultra-low sintering temperature of 650 ◦ C, the novel CuO-ZnOB2O3 glass-ceramic exhibits satisfactory microwave dielectric properties of ε r ~6.31, Q × f ~27,375 GHz, τ f ~ GLYPH<0> 8 ppm/ ◦ C, Vickers hardness ~599 kgf/mm 2 and good chemical compatibility with Ag or Al electrodes. This dielectric behavior optimization method provides new possibilities for the application of glass-ceramic materials in ULTCC substrates.

1. Introduction
With  the  evolution  of  hypersonic  vehicles  toward  higher  flight speeds ( > 5 Ma), extended operational durations,  and  enhanced maneuverability, their thermal protection systems (TPS) are confronted with  extreme  service  environments  exceeding  3000 ℃ ,  coupled  with severe  challenges  such  as  high-enthalpy  airflow  scouring  and  hyperoxidative  ablation  [1].  Carbon/carbon  (C/C)  composites,  renowned for their low density, thermal stability at elevated temperatures (maintaining strength above 1600 ℃ ), and exceptional resistance to thermal shock, have emerged as the material of choice for high-enthalpy thermal structural  components  in  hypersonic  vehicles  [2,3].  However,  their application is critically  hindered  by  rapid oxidative ablation in oxygen-rich  high-enthalpy  environments,  which  induces  structural degradation  [4,5].  Consequently,  material  modification  strategies  to enhance ablation resistance are urgently required, with the incorporation of ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) carbides identified as a

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  modified  carbon/carbon composites  (C/C-UHTCs)  not  only  retain  the  inherent  advantages  of traditional  carbon/carbon  composites,  including  high  strength,  low thermal  expansion  coefficient,  and  exceptional  thermal  shock  resistance, but also demonstrate improved oxidation and ablation resistance due to the incorporation of UHTCs [1,2]. As a result, C/C-UHTCs have found  widespread  application  in  components  of  hypersonic  aircraft, missiles, and reusable launch vehicles [3,4].
UHTCs, including ZrC [5,6], TaC [7], HfC [8], ZrB2  [9] and HfB2 [10],  exhibit  exceptional  hardness  and  melting  points  that  exceed 3000 ◦ C. Among these materials, ZrC is particularly notable for its industrial applications due to its relatively low density and cost-effectiveness [11,12]. However, ZrC is prone to oxidation at lower temperatures, resulting in the formation of ZrO2. This oxide possesses a loose and porous structure that facilitates oxygen diffusion [13,14]. SiC

1. Introduction
With the rapid development of hypersonic vehicles, advanced aerospace facilities such as high-speed ballistic missiles, atmospheric re-entry vehicles, and hypersonic cruise aircraft have been successfully developed and tested, with top speeds reaching Mach 10 or even over Mach 20 [1]. Although the first hypersonic flight was developed around 70 years ago, there has been increasing interest from a broader audience due to the fact that modern engineering advances are poised to revolutionize suborbital travel, rapid access to space, and national defensive capabilities [2]. However, when vehicle speeds increase into the hypersonic regime, the physics of external aerodynamic flows become dominated by aerothermal heating (Figure 1a). Aerodynamic compression and friction create high-enthalpy gas dynamics that impart additional physical phenomena, including high heat fluxes (>hundreds of W/m), extreme thermal gradients (changing from -170 ◦ C to 3000 ◦ C across distances of order 1 cm), high stagnation pressures (~10 5 -10 7 Pascals), as well as destructive plasma from gas ionization. With the increase in Mach numbers, these formidable phenomena must be accommodated by materials in the principal subsystems of hypersonic vehicles, such as aeroshell/primary structure, leading edges, control surfaces, acreage thermal protection, propulsion, and guidance systems (Figure 1b-e). 1. Introduction
Carbon fiber reinforced ultra-high temperature ceramics (Cf/UHTCs) have attracted significant attention due to their exceptional properties, including  high  melting  points  (over  3000 ◦ C),  low  oxygen  diffusion coefficients(Li  et  al.,  2025),  outstanding  mechanical properties(Wang et al., 2024), and high-temperature chemical stability(Bai et al., 2023). The excellent performance of Cf/UHTCs in extremely high-temperature environments makes them ideal materials for applications in hypersonic vehicle thermal protection systems (Wyatt et al., 2023).

1. Introduction
With the progressive development of hypersonic systems, stringent requirements  are  imposed  on  materials  used  in  Thermal  Protection Systems (TPS) [1 -4]. Previously, carbon-based materials, particularly graphite  (Gr)  and  carbon  fiber  reinforced  carbon  matrix  (C/C)  composites, are considered as promising candidates for TPS owing to their low density, small thermal expansion coefficient, and outstanding mechanical properties at elevated temperatures [5 -12]. However, carbon is readily oxidized in open air at a temperature over 500 ◦ C, leading to high risk of failure of carbon-based TPS.
To  improve  the  anti-ablation  performance  against  oxidation  and mechanical erosion, ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) that have high  strength  and  excellent  high-temperature  resistance  are  incorporated  in  carbon  to  form  composites.  Under  an  oxyacetylene  flame, UHTCs can degrade to oxides with sufficient fluidity to seal micropores and heal microcracks on surfaces of TPS [13 -15]. Importantly, the oxide layer has low oxygen permeability, thereby protecting the carbon matrix from oxidation. For example, Zhong et al. [16] prepared ZrC -SiC reinforced C/C (C/C -ZrC -SiC) composites. In the oxyacetylene ablation test, ZrC -SiC  UHTCs  were  oxidized  into  a  ZrO2 -SiO2 layer,  which  can effectively protect C/C composites, giving rise to a small linear ablation

question: Guo et al. 2024 used machine learning (SVR, SHAP, and genetic algorithm symbolic regression) to design ZrB2-SiC formulations. How does this data-driven design approach compare to the future-research recommendation made by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 for high entropy ceramic development?
answer:
The data-driven design approach used by Guo et al. 2024, which incorporates machine learning techniques such as support vector regression (SVR), SHAP (Shapley-Additive-exPlanations), and genetic algorithm symbolic regression, focuses on optimizing the ZrB2-SiC system for enhanced oxidation resistance through predictive modeling and feature importance analysis [1][2][3]. This approach enables the rapid identification of optimal material formulations by leveraging historical experimental data and advanced analytical tools.

In contrast, Ujah and Olubambi 2025 recommend future research directions for high entropy ceramic development that emphasize the integration of multi-scale modeling and simulation methods, the adoption of cost-effective synthesis techniques, and the exploration of functional applications in energy storage, electronic barriers, and functional coatings [10][11]. They also highlight the importance of combining machine learning with CALPHAD and DFT to design more robust high entropy ceramics with improved structural and functional performance [10].

The comparison reveals that while Guo et al. focus on optimizing specific ceramic systems using machine learning for performance enhancement, Ujah and Olubambi advocate for broader research strategies that include multi-scale modeling, advanced synthesis methods, and expanded functional applications of high entropy ceramics. The information regarding the specific future-research recommendations made by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 for high entropy ceramic development is not covered in the provided sources.

A B S T R A C T
This study employed machine learning that leverage historical experimental data to construct predictive models capable of estimating the oxidation resistance of ultra-high-temperature diboride. The support vector machine regression (SVR) model exhibited superior prediction metrics with R 2 of 0.88. The Shapley-Additive-exPlanations and genetic algorithm symbolic regression models revealed that SiC content plays a crucial role in the antioxidant damage of UHT borides, and the results suggested that the critical addition amount of SiC in the ZrB2-SiC system is 13.8 vol%, and the optimal addition amount is 23.5 vol%. The trained SVR model promptly identified optimal formulas exhibiting exceptional oxidation resistance.
impossible to comprehensively understand the effects of all these factors [7,8]. Another approach, known as the third paradigm, involves simulation calculations to address the rapid design and optimization challenges  of  UHTCs  [9].  However,  due  to  the  complex  nature  of  high enthalpy, high heat, and intricate mass and heat transfer processes that occur during the oxidation resistance of UHTCs, the current simulation calculation strategies can only predict material properties through many approximations [10]. Moreover, large-scale models require substantial computational power, making it difficult to predict the high-temperature oxidation resistance of UHTCs with both high accuracy and efficiency. Clearly, the traditional strategies based on experimental trial-and-error and simulation calculations are time-consuming and costly, which hinders the rapid design and performance enhancement of UHTCs. Therefore, there is an urgent need to explore the antioxidant mechanisms and formulations of UHTCs  through more intelligent strategy.

4. Conclusions
This study represents an effort to utilize published data and existing knowledge to develop a model for the rapid evaluation and design of high-temperature  oxidation  resistance  in  UHTCs.  The  findings  unequivocally demonstrate  the  feasibility of  employing  data-driven methodologies to accurately model and predict the oxide thickness in UHT diborides. After Pearson correlation analysis and model evaluation of  feature  importance,  7  features  are  retained.  Among  the  evaluated models,  the  SVR  model  exhibits  superior  prediction  metrics,  demonstrating exceptional accuracy and robustness compared to other models such as LR, RFR, XGBR, KNN, and MLPR. The SVR model achieved a MAE of 31, MSE of 2489, and a R 2 value of 0.88. The SHAP analysis conducted on the SVR model reveals quantitatively that the TTE and TTI present a pronounced positive influence on the thickness of the oxide layer. Conversely, SiC demonstrates a substantial inhibitory effect on the oxide layer thickness. The mathematical explicit expression based on genetic algorithm symbolic regression also proves this conclusion. On the  other  hand,  the  AGS  displays  a  more  intricate  and  multifaceted relationship with the oxide layer thickness. Furthermore, the SVR model suggests that the critical addition amount of SiC in the ZrB2-SiC system is W. Guo et al.

Fig. 12. Oxide layer thickness of the (a~h) different formulations of ZrB2-SiC and (a1~h1) corresponding oxygen element EDS mapping.
Fig. 13. Errors between the predicted oxide thickness of SVR model and the experimental oxide thickness for (a) different formulations, (b) test time, and (c) test temperature.

3.3. Model prediction
We  utilized  the  well-trained  SVR  model  to  predict  the  hightemperature  oxidation  resistance  of  commonly  used  ZrB2-SiC  system formulations (SiC content: 0 -30 vol%, AGS: 1 -20 μ m, RD: 95%, TTI: 120 s, TTE: 1600 ◦ C). Our objective is to explore the optimal formula of ZrB2-SiC with the most exceptional antioxidant performance within the entire range of formula. The thickness of the oxide layer was found to vary with the SiC content and AGS, as depicted in Fig. 9. As the SiC content and AGS increased, the thickness of the oxidation layer in the ZrB2-SiC  system  decreased  (Fig.  9(a)).  However,  this  trend  was  not simply linear; instead, it exhibited a complex saddle shape, that is, there is  a  complex  coupling  effect  between  SiC  and  AGS,  resulting  in  the formulation  of  ZrB2-SiC  with  the  best  high-temperature  oxidation resistance (highlighted by the red extreme value line). Further analysis of the results presented in Fig. 9(b) revealed that the SiC content and grain size of the ceramic play a crucial role in achieving superior hightemperature oxidation resistance, provided they are at their ideal values.

I. INTRODUCTION
The synthesis of materials with desired properties often comes with the challenge of selecting proper chemistry as the overall property is predominantly dependent on phase formation (single-phase solid solution vs multi-phase). Thus, phase prediction in these multicomponent ceramic systems is currently a main topic for investigation, which may broaden the compositional range of high-entropy ceramics, unlocking possibilities for their utilization across diverse scientific domains. The design of these materials is often challenging due to the sheer number of unexplored compositions, and the scarcity of the literature data has posed an obstacle in generalizing any suitable design strategy. Sarker et al. discovered the entropy-forming ability (EFA) descriptor utilizing the ab initio approach, which indicates the single-phase formability of any given composition, i.e., higher the EFA value, higher the chance of singlephase formation. 32 Very recently, a new thermodynamic parameter has been introduced, namely, disordered enthalpy -entropy descriptor (DEED), which captures the balance between entropy gains and enthalpy costs on the formation of homogeneous solid solutions for any class of multicomponent ceramics. 33 First, the choice of elements in these multicomponent ceramics is found to be a critical factor. It is prudent to have prior knowledge of the combination of elements for a particular HEC to synthesize a single-phase pure multicomponent HEC successfully. Second, the choice of precursor is equally important. Using earth-abundant precursors, like oxides, will make the synthesis attractive and relatively economical for technologically important structural applications. In this connection, it is crucial to derive data-driven prediction tactics combined with an easy synthesis route. However, the accuracy comes at a cost of intense calculation time and the complexity of calculations for the assessment of the density of states. On the contrary, machine learning (ML) offers an effective alternate high-throughput design approach for designing these materials. Machine learning, being a data-driven tool, can predict the outcome without requiring any atomic structural information.

4. Conclusion
all showing a negative influence on the ablation rate. Symbolic regression further indicates that the average melting point of ceramics (AMPC), thermal conductivity of material (TCM), and thermal expansion coefficient of oxides (TECO) have an exponential negative correlation with linear ablation rate, consistent with the analysis of the SHAP model, and average melting point of ceramics (AMPC) is the most sensitive factor affecting UHTCMCs, while thermal conductivity of material (TCM), and thermal expansion coefficient of oxides (TECO) have equally important impacts on the linear ablation rate. Experimental validation shows that Cf/(TiZrHfNbTa)C high-entropy ceramic composites, by increasing the proportions of HfC and ZrC and containing appropriate amounts of TiC, NbC, and TaC, and through the use of high thermal conductive carbon fibers and ultra-high temperature heat treatment, the composites could achieve  near-zero  ablation  at  2000 ◦ C  for  300  s.  This  data-driven approach to material selection and design offers an effective means of reducing  the  time,  labor,  and  associated  costs  typically  incurred  in traditional experimental trial-and-error methods.

A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high-temperature  ceramic  coatings  are  critical  for  thermal  protection  systems  in  hypersonic  vehicles. However,  their  compositional  optimization  is  constrained  by  the  inefficiency  of  traditional  trial-and-error method. In this study, a machine learning-driven design strategy is proposed for the ZrC-TaC-SiC ternary system (denoted Z n T m S t , n , m , t = 0 -100) to enhance ablation resistance. A random forest regression model was constructed using 170 literature data to predict surface ablation temperature, mass ablation rate, and linear ablation rate across the full compositional space of the ZrC-TaC-SiC ternary system. Four optimized formulations (Z60T30S10, Z60T10S30, Z80T15S5, Z80T5S15), each exhibiting stable mass and linear ablation rates in the order of 10 GLYPH<0> 4 g/s and 10 GLYPH<0> 4 mm/s, respectively, were selected through the model and fabricated via supersonic atmospheric plasma spraying. Oxyacetylene ablation test results confirmed the reliability of the model, with prediction  errors  for  linear  ablation  rate  within  the  order  of  10 GLYPH<0> 5 mm/s.  Microstructural  characterization revealed that high-TaC content (15 -30 wt%) led to excessive formation of Zr6Ta2O17 during ablation, resulting in reduced thermal stability of the oxide scale and discontinuities in structure. Moreover, non-uniform sintering caused  an  increase  in  porosity.  Interestingly,  the  low-TaC  (5  wt%)  Z80T5S15  sample  achieved  a  dense  and continuous oxide layer by a proper Zr/Ta content ratio in oxide phase, exhibiting excellent ablation resistance. This study establishes a machine-learning -assisted strategy combined with experimental validation, which can provide a new paradigm for intelligent design and performance prediction of ultra-high-temperature ceramic coatings.

Document: Ujah_2025_NextMaterials_Evaluatingtheproperties.pdf. Title: Evaluating the properties and applications of high entropy borides, nitrides and carbides. The header and byline of this document reads: Review article Chika Oliver Ujah * , Peter Apata Olubambi Center for Nanoengineering and Advanced Materials, School of Mining, Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, University of Johannesburg, South Africa A R T I C L E  I N F O Keywords: High entropy alloys High entropy carbides High entropy ceramics

A B S T R A C T
This  study  investigates  the  unique  properties  and  prospective  uses  of  high  entropy  ceramics,  with  special emphasis on borides (HEBs), carbides (HECbs), and nitrides (HENs). Being an innovative material, these ceramics consist of multiple principal elements chemically harmonized under severe lattice distortion and large configurational entropy, which control their unique thermal stability, mechanical hardness, and wear resistance. The principal objective of the research is to analyze the intrinsic characteristics of these high entropy systems and determine their applicability in ultra-high-temperature environments. Through a comprehensive literature review, focusing on critical assessment of experimental and computational works, the research discovered the prospective performance of HEBs, HECbs, and HENs in severe environments like aerospace, defense, and nuclear applications. Nevertheless, poor oxidation resistance, low fracture toughness, and scalability of synthesis are the unresolved  challenges  affecting  HECs.  The  research  recommends  developing  industrially  viable  synthesis methods like self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS), flame spray pyrolysis, as well as combining machine learning with CALPHAD and DFT to design and develop more smart production of robust HECs with improved structural and functional performance. The study further recommends that future research should inquire not only the structural roles of HECs but also their functional applications in energy storage, electronic barriers, and functional coatings, thereby expanding the impact of high entropy ceramics in emerging technologies. The next wave of research should aim at increasing the oxidation tolerance, develop industrial viable synthesis pathways, and expand the multifunctional applications of HECs in harsh conditions.

5.2. Future outlook of research
High entropy ceramics offer great possibilities for the next generation  ultra-high-temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs).  Nevertheless,  future studies  should  address  a  few  scientific  and  technological  obstacles militating  against  the  use  of  HECs  in  extreme  environments.  One important direction is to apply a multi-scale approach in modeling and simulation methods, other than the traditional DFT route, to effectively comprehend  the  dynamics  of  defects,  lattice  distortion  and  thermal transport of disordered atomic frameworks. Techniques incorporating machine-learning of interatomic interactions, phase-field modeling, and CALPHAD-informed thermodynamics have the potential to make radical increase  in  predictive  discovery  of  sustainable  HEC  materials,  with higher accuracy.
Moreover, innovations in synthesis will play a crucial role in the large-scale  industrial  production  of  continuous,  single-phase  HECs having  predictable  microstructures.  Spark  plasma  sintering  is  a  very expensive  fabrication  method.  Hence,  cost-effective  methods  (flame spray pyrolysis, cold crucible induction melting, and self-propagating high-temperature synthesis (SHS) should be adopted. These processes are  scalable,  energy-efficient  and  suitable  for  complex  geometries required in aerospace and defense components.
Experimental  research  should  concentrate  on  the  oxidation  resistance, fracture toughness, and thermal shock stability of HECs under cyclic elevated environments. This will involve consideration of toughening mechanism  through  multilayered architectures, bioinspired lamella and ductile second phase strengthening. In addition, real-time in-situ characterization (e.g. synchrotron XRD, neutron diffraction and environmental TEM) may yield more insightful information on phase evolution and failure mechanisms.

A B S T R A C T
With the advancement of hypersonic vehicles, extreme high temperature environments have imposed increasingly stringent requirements on the performance of thermal protection systems. Consequently, the development of high-performance thermal protection materials capable of withstanding extreme conditions has become a primary focus of current research. Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) and their composites, known for their excellent oxidation resistance and ablation performance, are regarded as highly promising non-ablative thermal protection materials. This paper provides a systematic review of recent research progress on UHTC composites in several key areas, including innovations and optimizations in fabrication processes, exploration of toughening strategies and mechanisms, in-depth studies on oxidation and ablation resistance mechanisms, and the  development  and  potential  applications  of  high-entropy  ceramics.  Furthermore,  the  paper  discusses  the practical application prospects of UHTCs and their composites in extreme environments, analyzes the current technical challenges, and proposes future research directions and priorities.

1. Introduction
The advent of high entropy alloys (HEAs) has considerably improved the advancement of materials science and engineering, especially in the enhancement of sustainable structural and functional materials. HEAs are class of alloys discovered in the 2000s by Cantor and Yeh which consist of five (5) or more principal elements combined in equi-atomic or near equi-atomic proportions [1,2]. High Entropy Ceramics (HECs), on the other hand, are a class of composite material consisting of HEA matrix and ceramic reinforcement dispersed as the secondary phase on the  matrix  [3].  Contrary  to  conventional  alloys  and  ceramics  that contain a single principal element, HEAs and HECs must contain at least five principal elements. The multiple principal elements stimulate high entropy  effect  in  the  composite  system,  promoting  the  evolution  of stable single phase solid solution microstructure, and creating unique chemical, mechanical, thermal and tribological properties [4 -7].
HECs, especially high entropy borides (HEBs), high entropy nitrides

6. Conclusion and recommendations
This research has critically evaluated the structural, thermal, mechanical, electrical, and functional characteristics of high entropy borides (HEBs), carbides (HECbs), and nitrides (HENs) and their future applications. The results prove that the synergistic role of configurational entropy, slow diffusion, distortion of the lattice, and cocktail effects  are  the  basis  of  the  excellent  hardness,  thermal  stability,  and oxidation resistance of these ceramics. HECbs have the best hardness and  better  tunability  via  compositional  and  stoichiometric  control, making them useful in cutting and machining tools; HENs have the best fracture toughness and stiffness enabling applications in cyclic loading and strain-resistant applications, like turbine blades, spacecraft actuators and nuclear reactor casing. The HEBs, in their turn, are characterized by their high resistance to oxidation, thermal conductivity, and the possibility to operate in an environment with ultra-high temperatures, making  it  useful  in  aerospace  and  harsh  electronics/photovoltaic applications.
Notwithstanding the prospective advantages of the studied HECs, we identified some issues militating against their optimal performance and their universal adoption as the next generation advanced materials. The limitations include: (i) the low fracture toughness of most compositions, (ii) incomplete oxidation resistance above 1500 ◦ C, (iii) difficulties in scaling up the synthesis process and (iv) a lack of data on long-term service performance with cyclic thermal, oxidative and irradiation environments. However, their new use in aerospace, hypersonic planes, nuclear technology, cutting tools, and electronic equipment highlight their  transformational  capacity  in  very  harsh  conditions.  When  optimized, high entropy ceramics have the potential to traverse the chasm between discovery-based material science research and high-scale industrial application.
The research, therefore, recommends the following:

Additionally, their unique oxygen-insensitive property further enhances their utility in demanding environments. However, a critical challenge persists: resistivity drift caused by anion sublattice disorder in pyrochlore-structured  ceramics  remains  a  key  bottleneck,  impeding their widespread practical adoption [9,21].
In recent years, the high-entropy strategy has attracted significant attention in the field of advanced ceramics due to its ability to enhance material  performance  through  structural  complexity  and  disorder [22 -24]. High-entropy ceramics (HECs), which arise from this strategy, are typically single-phase solid solutions composed of five or more elements. These materials are characterized by the occupation of one or more  Wyckoff  sites  by  multiple  principal  elements  in  equiatomic  or near-equiatomic  ratios  [25,26].  It  is  noteworthy  that  the  anion  sublattice in HECs undergoes significant lattice distortions to accommodate differences in cation size and bonding, thereby endowing the HECs with exceptionally high lattice energy. Additionally, the high configurational entropy introduced by multi-element co-doping reduces the Gibbs free energy change associated with the ordered-to-disordered transition of the  anion  sublattice.  This  effect  suppresses  the  disordered  transition through the formation of a unique ' entropy-stabilized ' structure. The synergy  between  these  two  thermodynamic  mechanisms  offers  an innovative solution for reducing resistance drift in pyrochlore-structured ceramics caused by sublattice disorder at elevated temperatures. Hence, this study employed a multi-principal rare earth element co-doping strategy to synthesize three novel high-entropy rare-earth zirconates, (La0.2Nd0.2Sm0.2Eu0.2A0.2)2Zr2O7 (A = Dy, Ho, or Er), via  a  high-temperature  solid-state  reaction  method,  and conducted a systematic  analysis  of  their  electrical  properties  and  aging  stability under extreme high-temperature conditions.

5. Summary and outlook

Presently, the range of potential applications for these materials is broadening to include the fields of energy (nuclear fission and fusion, energy harvesting, concentrated solar power), materials processing (high temperature electrodes, high  speed  machining  tools,  molten  metal  containment),  microelectronics  (conductors,  barrier  layers,  lattice  matched  substrates),  and others. The lure of revolutionary advances in performance and the need to withstand increasingly extreme operating conditions will continue to motivate research on UHTCs. Research efforts should evolve from the present focus on synthesis, processing, densification, structure-property relationships  and  oxidation  to  tackle  challenges  associated  with  performance in extreme environments, fiber-reinforced composites, computational studies, and new materials. By about 2020, the second wave of UHTC researchwill have been active for nearly 30 years, which is significant because this is the typical time required for a new class of materials  to  find  its  initial  commercial  applications.  Recently,  the concept of high-entropy alloys was expanded to the field of ceramics, leading to the fast development of high-entropy ceramics. Among them, the  high-entropy  diborides  and  carbides  whose  related  individual components are UHTCs such as ZrB2, HfB2, ZrC, HfC, and TaC are also called high-entropy UHTCs. Up to now, the main works are focused on the crystal structure, phase formation ability and the related theoretical model, powder synthesis, densification, mechanical properties, thermal conductivity, and oxidation resistance of high-entropy UHTCs. Besides, up to now there is no report to point out that the developed high-entropy UHTCs  show  any  entropy  stabilized  reversible  phase  transformation phenomenon. As UHTCs reach this milestone, potential funding sources will likely expand from the predominance of government funded efforts focused on basic research to include increasingly applied research and development  efforts  funded  by  companies  and  industrial  consortia. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  including  borides  and carbides have been the focus of research due to their melting temperatures of more than 3000 ◦ C [1], thermal conductivities that can exceed 100 W/(m.K) [1]) and hardness values greater than 15 GPa [1]. High entropy carbide (HEC) and high entropy boride (HEB) ceramics are solid solutions of 4 or more transition metals in either a cubic (for HEC) or hexagonal (for HEB) crystal structure. Both HEC and HEB ceramics have attracted interest due to improved hardness and better oxidation resistance than the constituent  components  [2 -7]. The  most  recent advancement is directed towards the development of dual phase high entropy ceramics, which contain both carbide and boride phase, due to their potential for improved properties compared to the individual HEBs and HECs. The present work describes development of dual phase high entropy ceramic from commercial powders. Further, SiC is incorporated to  investigate  its  effect  on  microstructure,  mechanical  and  thermal properties of the dual phase ceramic.
A limited number of recent studies have been reported on dual phase high entropy ultra-high temperature ceramics because of their recent

Evaluating the properties and applications of high entropy borides, nitrides and carbides
Chika Oliver Ujah * , Peter Apata Olubambi
Center for Nanoengineering and Advanced Materials, School of Mining, Metallurgy and Chemical Engineering, University of Johannesburg, South Africa

4. Summary and Outlook
- (4) Clarification of challenges and future directions: It explicitly identifies the core challenges hindering the practical applications of C/C-UHTCs composites (e.g., precise control of preform pore structure, fabrication of large/complex-shaped components, thorough understanding of ablation mechanisms in multicomponent/high-entropy systems, cost reduction, bridging ground-space simulation gaps). Building on current knowledge, it outlines critical future research priorities, encompassing advanced structural design (e.g., graded structures and nanowire reinforcement), development and application of novel high-entropy/multicomponent ceramics, integration of multiscale modeling with real-service environment testing, and breakthroughs in low-cost, scalable manufacturing technologies.
This review not only serves as a comprehensive knowledge base for researchers to understand the materials science fundamentals and performance optimization strategies of C/C-UHTCs, but more significantly, it establishes a solid theoretical foundation for designing next-generation high-performance, long-lifetime thermal protection materials for extreme environments (e.g., hypersonic vehicle leading edges and engine hot-section components). It also clearly delineates the critical research pathways essential for advancing the field. Future breakthroughs will heavily rely on the deep integration of material design, processing technology, characterization methods, and theoretical modeling to ultimately realize the scalable and highly reliable application of C/C-UHTC composites in the aerospace sector.

question: How does the smooth, low-damage ablation surface of the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating reported by Li et al. 2025 relate to the description of high thermal stability and oxidation resistance of high entropy borides (HEBs) discussed by Ujah and Olubambi 2025?
answer: The smooth, low-damage ablation surface of the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating, as reported by Li et al. 2025, is related to the high thermal stability and oxidation resistance of high entropy borides (HEBs) discussed by Ujah and Olubambi 2025 in that both involve materials with enhanced thermal and oxidation resistance due to structural and compositional characteristics. Specifically, the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating exhibits a smooth surface with minimal damage and low surface roughness (16.2 μm) after ablation, which suggests a high degree of structural integrity and resistance to ablation [1]. This is consistent with the properties of HEBs, which are noted for their high thermal stability and oxidation resistance, attributed to their hexagonal AlB₂-like structure, strong covalent bonding, and high configurational entropy [5]. However, the direct relationship between the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating and HEBs is not explicitly discussed in the provided sources. The information on how the smooth, low-damage ablation surface of the Nb10-doped (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating relates to the high thermal stability and oxidation resistance of HEBs is not covered in the provided sources.
