3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
Based on the above analysis, considering the similarity and structural integrity effect of Nb and Ta addition, the next composition optimization on the sprayed (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating system can be based on (Hf-ZrNb)C system. Fig. 6 exhibits the optical photos and surface morphology of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings after 120 s ablation under the heat flux  of  2.4 MW/m 2 .  Significant  ablation  damage  occurred  in  Nb50 coating,  and  the  oxide  layer  showed  a  splashing  characteristic  with maximum ablation crater depth of 216.5 μ m and surface roughness of 47.5 μ m. Compared to Nb50 coating, both Nb20 and Nb10 coatings exhibited a complete oxide layer structure without any peeling bulging and splashing. Especially, Nb20 coating presented a few micro craters resulting from structural instability with surface roughness of 31.0 μ m, while the Nb10 coating still exhibited a smooth surface with no apparent cracks or pores with surface roughness of only 16.2 μ m. The XRD patterns of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings were given in Fig. S4, where more (Hf, Zr)O2  phase was observed with the content decrease of Nb element. Hence, the decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-TaNb)C coating systems was found effective to improve ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
Fig. 11a presents the average linear ablation rate and the average mass  ablation  rate  of  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and  Zr20Nb10  coatings  after 300 s ablation. Zr20Nb10 coating exhibited significant thinning (0.30 ± 0.15 μ m/s) and weight loss (0.61 mg/s), suggesting severe peeling off under ablation conditions. In contrast, the Zr70Nb10 and Nb10 coatings maintained a thickening trend ( GLYPH<0> 0.09 ± 0.02 μ m/s and GLYPH<0> 0.01 ± 0.07 μ m/s, respectively). Zr70Nb10 coating exhibited weight loss (0.18 mg/ s), while the mass of Nb10 coating continued to increase ( GLYPH<0> 0.04 mg/s), suggesting the best ablation property of Nb10 coating. The comparison of ablation property among different protective coatings reported in the literature [15 -17,19,26,34,52 -55] is displayed in Fig. 11b. The ablation property of reported HfC-ZrC-TaC coating [19] and (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating in  this  work  was  also  presented  in  Table  S5,  both  of  which  showed optimal ablation performance at a Ta/Nb content of around 10 mol%. In particular, the ablation rates of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coating was significantly lower than that of HfC-ZrC-TaC coating, despite of longer ablation time. In this work, the as-optimized Nb10 coating (named by (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C  protective  system)  shows  nearly  zero  linear  and mass ablation rate after 300 s ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C, as well as excellent cyclic ablation protection performance.

3.3. Performance optimization based on (Hf-Zr-Nb)C protective system
To visually compare the effect of varying Nb element content on the surface temperature distribution of coatings, Fig. 7a presents the surface temperature curve and surface temperature field distribution of Nb50, Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings  during  120 s  ablation,  where  the  surface temperatures increased with more Nb content. Fig. 7b shows the average linear ablation rate and average mass ablation rate of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10  coatings  after  120 s  ablation.  The  Nb20  and  Nb10  coatings exhibited thickness increase and weight gain and Nb10 showed smaller change in mass and thickness ( GLYPH<0> 0.65 ± 0.04 μ m/s, GLYPH<0> 0.47 mg/s) change, while Nb50 coating showed significant thickness decline. As reported by Li et al. [19], the service-derived (Hf, Zr)O2 layer of (Hf0.5Zr0.5)C coating under 2.4 MW/m 2 oxyacetylene flame was porous and loose after 120 s ablation, with an average linear ablation rate of GLYPH<0> 0.88 μ m/s, which is also higher than that of Nb10 coating in this work.
Figs. 7c1-c3 present the surface BSE images of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10
Fig. 6. The optical photos (a1-a3) and surface morphology (b1-b3) of Nb50, Nb20 and Nb10 coatings after ablation.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
To verify the above optimized results ( N = 0.4 -0.5) of appropriate Hf/Zr molar ratio in (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system, sprayed Zr70Nb10 ( N = 0.78), Nb10 ( N = 0.5) and Zr20Nb10 ( N = 0.22) coatings were evaluated by oxyacetylene ablation lasting for 300 s at the heat flux of 2.4 MW/m 2 . Figs.  9a1-a3 show  the  optical  photos  of  the  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation, respectively. It can be observed that large cracks and peeling off in the central region appeared in the oxide layers of Zr70Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings, along with severe delamination in the border region. By contrast, the oxide layer of Nb10 coating only showed minor damage in the central region.
From the surface morphology of ablated coatings in Fig. 9b1-b3, the central region of the Zr70Nb10 coating (roughness: 63.8 μ m) and the Zr20Nb10  coating  (roughness:  73.0 μ m)  also  displayed  more  severe peeling off compared to Nb10 coating (roughness: 25.9 μ m). The crater depth of the Zr20Nb10 coating in the central region reached up to 160.8 μ m, indicating almost complete coating delamination, while the coating delamination of Zr70Nb10 coating occurred in some local areas.
Surface  temperature  curves  of  Zr70Nb10,  Nb10  and  Zr20Nb10 coatings during 300 s ablation are shown in Fig. 10a. During the first two  ablation  cycles,  the  surface  temperatures  of  all  three  coatings gradually  increased  with  each  cycle,  with  the  Zr20Nb10  coating consistently exhibiting the highest surface temperatures. However, from the third ablation cycle, the surface temperature of Zr20Nb10 and Nb10

2.1. Crystal structure and phase stability of HEBs
High entropy borides (HEBs) are a subset of the ultra-high temperature  ceramics  that  consist  of  a  hexagonal  AlB ₂ -like  structure  which boron  atoms  crystalize  in  hexagonal  layers  whereas  metallic  atoms reside in interstitial positions. In an approximate equiatomic form, the metal sublattice can contain multiple transition metal elements like Ti, Zr, Hf, Nb and Ta [9,28]. The high configurational entropy stabilizes a multicomponent structure causing the formation of a single-phase solid solution  over  multi-phase  segregation  at  elevated  temperatures  [29]. Several factors help to stabilize HEBs, among which include mismatch of atomic  size,  difference  in  atomic  electronegativity,  valence  electron concentration,  and  mixing  enthalpy  [30].  These  factors  inhibit  the crystallization  of  intermetallic  phases  and  stabilize  the  single-phase thermodynamics of HEBs. Another stabilizing factor is the strong covalent bonding in the boron sublattice which promotes unique thermal and mechanical properties of HEBs at elevated temperatures of over C.O. Ujah and P.A. Olubambi
Fig. 1. Ionic Bonding in HECs vs. Metallic Bonding in HEAs.
2000 ◦ C [31]. Fig. 2 shows a hexagonal lattice of borides. In Fig. 2, boron atoms are organized in flat, hexagonal AlB ₂ motif. Meanwhile, in this study, the term 'HEBs ' refers specifically to AlB ₂ -type hexagonal diborides  (MB ₂ ),  whereas monoborides (MB) are discussed separately as a related  high-entropy  boride  subclass  due  to  their  different  crystal structure (orthorhombic or B27-type) and bonding characteristics.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
Fig. 12a1-a3  show the surface BSE images of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation. It can be observed that the oxide layers of the Zr70Nb10 and Nb10 coatings still exhibited equiaxed grain structures. As the Zr content decreased, the weakening trend of the (Hf, Zr)O2  grain  boundaries  increased.  The  Zr70Nb10  coating  contained numerous small-sized pores ( < 1 μ m) and intergranular cracks, while the oxide layer of the Nb10 coating was relatively dense. In contrast, the Zr20Nb10 coating developed a substantial number of small lath structures along the grain boundaries, forming large-sized pores (5 -15 μ m). This indicates that the Zr content in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coatings affects the pore defects and phase composition in the oxide layer. The average grain sizes of the oxide layers of the Zr70Nb10 and Nb10 coatings were 6.0 ± 3.2 μ m and 3.6 ± 1.9 μ m, respectively, which significantly smaller than those of the Zr20Nb10 coating (9.1 ± 2.1 μ m).

Table 3 Spot EDS analytical results of the A-Nb-O layer in Fig. 12 (A = Hf, Zr).

2.  Fundamental aspects of high entropy contributions in HEUHTCs
Regardless of the debate on the 'role of entropy', the topic of HEUHTC is preceding demand demonstrating exceptional mechanical and physical properties, high-temperature thermal stability, and good oxidation  resistance.  It  is  worth  mentioning  that  there  have  been  investigations of a variety of borides, carbides, and nitrides, which exhibit superior  hardness,  wear  and  oxidation  resistance  compared  to  individual constituents [11,13,14,24,25,28,30,32,34,35,44,46,49,52, 58,59,68-70].  However,  a  majority  of  these  while  exhibiting  exceptional properties are far from being addressed for entropic evaluation or stabilization.

3.2. Application of HEBs, HECbs and HENs in nuclear and energy systems
High  entropy  borides  have  shown  unique  stability  under  severe pressure (over 220 GPa) and temperatures (over 2000 ◦ C), preserving nanocrystalline  phase  with  high  bulk  modulus  (  over  407 GPa) [123 -125]. The above properties are prevalent in most HEBs, and such make them useful in plasma-engulfed components and structures like the nuclear reactors which are prone to high radiation fields and severe heat stress (see Fig. 13). Research is under way to explore the use of HEBs for neutron and gamma ray encapsulation, as report has it that C.O. Ujah and P.A. Olubambi W ₂ B ₅ can diminish neutron flux by 10 times in tokamak simulations (plasma dynamics) [126].
Fig. 12. Aerospace Components that HEBs, HENs and HECbs are Useful.
High entropy carbides like have excellent hardness (over 30 GPa), unique fracture toughness (about 5.9 MPa ⋅ m¹ ᐟ ²) when reinforced with ZrO ₂ [7,87,127]. With their ultra-high melting temperatures and severe lattice disorder, they have high resistance to irradiation, less prone to swelling and amorphization, properties which are highly needed for the next generation nuclear reactors. They can equally perform creditably as fuel particle coatings because of their excellent heat resistance and radiation resilience/tolerance, which can outperform traditional ZrC.
High entropy  nitrides  have  profound  resistance  to  corrosion,  and therefore useful in coatings in lead bismuth eutectic (LBE) cooled reactors [128]. Magnetron-sputtered HEN films on T91 steel demonstrated dense structural resistant to LBE corrosion over 1200 h at 800 ◦ C [129]. Their  outstanding  stiffness  and  chemical  stability  under  irradiation, prominent in heat and nuclear reactors, qualify their suitability for heat exchanger surfaces and corrosion protectors.

2.7. Comparative properties of HEBs, HENs and HECbs
High  entropy  carbides  typically  have  the  most  superior  hardness among  the  three  HECs,  around  30 GPa  at  optimal  valence  electron concentration  (VEC)  of  8.4,  being  far  much  higher  than  the  rule-ofmixtures  values  by  as  much  as  30 -50 %.  Exceedingly  large  also  is their  elastic  moduli  which  is  attributed  to  the  strong  Metal -carbide bonding  and  severe lattice distortion. HECbs,  moreover,  exhibit adjustable ductility and toughness by manipulating the carbon-to-metal ratios [117].
On the other hand, high entropy nitrides exhibit an average hardness of 20 -25 GPa, with excellent elastic moduli that is 17 % above the rule- C.O. Ujah and P.A. Olubambi of-mixtures, and hardness that is higher by 22 %. HENs are noted for their high stiffness and improved ductility making them useful in applications requiring resilience under strain like hypersonic leading edges and propulsion components in aerospace [77].
Moreover,  high  entropy  borides  have  a  balance  of  hardness  and elevated thermal conductivity of > 20 GPa and 75 -105 W/mK, respectively.  Their  resistance  to  oxidation  is  10  times  greater  than  binary diborides. They exhibit low fracture toughness but high thermal stability which are attributed to  their  hexagonal  configuration.  By  and  large, HECbs  are  good  at  highest  hardness  and  strength,  HENs  have  high stiffness and toughness, while HEBs have excellent oxidation resistance and elevated temperature stability [118]. Table 7 shows the comparative properties of HEBs, HENs and HECbs [104].

3.4. Application of HEBs, HECbs and HENs in electronic devices
High-entropy borides are presently categorized as smart material for electrocatalysis  and  photothermal  synthesis,  making  use  of  their adjustable electronic properties and unique thermal resilience for photobased absorption and energy conversion systems. Take for example, HEB films  deposited on stainless steel have exhibited over 90 % solar absorptivity  and  less  than  9 %  emissivity,  being  stable  at  400 -500 ◦ C which is a requisite for photovoltaic panels or passive heat modulation in spacecrafts [132]. The electrical conductivity of metal diborides situates HEBs as prospective materials for elevated temperature electrodes, electrical connectors, or heat spreaders in harsh electronics (spacecraft electronics, hypersonic missile guidance systems, oil & gas, and downhole  tools)  (see  Fig.  15).  Their  excellent  performance  at  these  harsh electronic environments is attributed to their high melting temperatures and high resistance to oxidation [9,49].
Despite the fact that HECbs are principally renowned for their mechanical  stability,  they  are  equally  useful  in  some  special  electronic devices. Their inherent configurational disorder prevalent in some of them like (W-Ta-Nb-Zr-Ti)C generates high thermal and electrical conductivity, situating them as adequate substrates or diffusion shield in power electronics [133] (solar inverters, UPS inverters, switched-mode power  supplies)  and  high-frequency  devices  (radio  transmitters  and receivers  (3 -30 MHz),  microwave  systems  (1 -100 GHz)),  with  their subsequent thermal stress. The ability to maintain high phase stability with their inherent sluggish diffusion enables HECbs to be useful in heat sink coatings and high thermal structural layers [7].
Fig. 15. Image of Prospective Applications of HEBs.
C.O. Ujah and P.A. Olubambi

6. Conclusion and recommendations
This research has critically evaluated the structural, thermal, mechanical, electrical, and functional characteristics of high entropy borides (HEBs), carbides (HECbs), and nitrides (HENs) and their future applications. The results prove that the synergistic role of configurational entropy, slow diffusion, distortion of the lattice, and cocktail effects  are  the  basis  of  the  excellent  hardness,  thermal  stability,  and oxidation resistance of these ceramics. HECbs have the best hardness and  better  tunability  via  compositional  and  stoichiometric  control, making them useful in cutting and machining tools; HENs have the best fracture toughness and stiffness enabling applications in cyclic loading and strain-resistant applications, like turbine blades, spacecraft actuators and nuclear reactor casing. The HEBs, in their turn, are characterized by their high resistance to oxidation, thermal conductivity, and the possibility to operate in an environment with ultra-high temperatures, making  it  useful  in  aerospace  and  harsh  electronics/photovoltaic applications.
Notwithstanding the prospective advantages of the studied HECs, we identified some issues militating against their optimal performance and their universal adoption as the next generation advanced materials. The limitations include: (i) the low fracture toughness of most compositions, (ii) incomplete oxidation resistance above 1500 ◦ C, (iii) difficulties in scaling up the synthesis process and (iv) a lack of data on long-term service performance with cyclic thermal, oxidative and irradiation environments. However, their new use in aerospace, hypersonic planes, nuclear technology, cutting tools, and electronic equipment highlight their  transformational  capacity  in  very  harsh  conditions.  When  optimized, high entropy ceramics have the potential to traverse the chasm between discovery-based material science research and high-scale industrial application.
The research, therefore, recommends the following:

3.4. Ablation behavior
Oxidation and ablation resistance is a key criterion for evaluating thermal protection materials. To assess the oxidation and ablation performance of the fabricated Cf/ZrB2-SiC-C composite, an oxyacetylene ablation  test  was  conducted.  During  the  test,  surface  morphology changes were observed through a neutral density filter, as shown in Fig. 8a. At 100 s, the exposed carbon fibers on the sample surface had been completely ablated, leaving behind visible grooves. However, no significant  changes  occurred  on  the  surface  during  the  subsequent ablation  process  up  to  the  end  of  the  test.  The  surface  oxide  layer exhibited no signs of cracking or delamination, indicating its long-term thermal stability at high temperatures. Fig. 8b shows the temperature evolution on the sample surface during the test, as well as the surface appearance before and after the test. It can be observed that the surface temperature remained above 2300 ◦ C for more than 300 s, with a peak temperature reaching 2358 ◦ C. After the test, the originally gray-black surface was completely covered by a white oxide layer. Based on calculations, the linear ablation rate was GLYPH<0> 2.75 × 10 GLYPH<0> 4 mm/s, and the mass ablation rate was as low as 4.14 × 10 GLYPH<0> 6 g/(mm 2 ⋅ s). The phase composition of the sample surface before and after the oxyacetylene ablation test  was  analyzed  using  X-ray  diffraction  (XRD),  and  the  results  are shown in Fig. 8c. As illustrated, before the test, the surface phases were composed of  hexagonal  ZrB2  (h-ZrB2)  and β -SiC.  However,  after

3.3. Ablation resistance of HfC-TiC ceramics
As shown in Fig. 4d, the surface oxidation layer of HfC-TiC presented three distinct areas. The ablation center exhibited a white and relatively rough surface. The  ablation  transitional  region  appeared  grey,  while ablation  edge  region  appeared  white.  Experimental  data  reveal  a nonlinear relationship between Cf content and the ablation resistance of the  material.  At  5 vol%  Cf  content,  catastrophic  fracture  occurred  in sample,  accompanied  by  severe  oxidation  on  fracture  cross-sections, resulting in the highest mass ablation rate. For sample with 10 vol% Cf, no fracture was observed. However, significant macro-cracks formed in the matrix, which accelerated oxidation and increased the ablation rate. In Samples 2 and 3, insufficient Cf  content failed to establish an effective stress-buffering or crack-inhibiting network, rendering them unsuitable for application under extreme high-temperature conditions. When Cf content reached 15 -20 vol%, the matrix maintained structural integrity with excellent thermal shock resistance, ensuring stability in high-temperature environments. Given that the oxidation of carbon fibers generates gaseous products (e.g., CO/CO ₂ ), leading to pore formation  and  accelerated  oxygen  diffusion  pathways,  the  carbon  fiber content was optimized by balancing two critical factors: 1) preserving adequate thermal shock resistance, and 2) minimizing the fiber content to mitigate oxidation-induced structural degradation. Accordingly, the HfC-TiC  with  15 vol%  carbon  fibers  was  selected  for  systematic investigation in this work. Compared to the others reported in literature (Table 6), HfC-35mol%TiC still exhibited excellent ablation resistance after being ablated at a higher temperature (3000 K) for a longer time (300 s),  with  a Rm value  of GLYPH<0> 1.11 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 g ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 and  a Rl value  of GLYPH<0> 1.40 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3  mm ⋅ s GLYPH<0> 1 .

11. Ablation properties
Arc jet testing is often used to replicate the atmospheric re-entry conditions and to assess material behaviour in hypersonic applications. Zhang et al. [359] reported the ablation behaviour of hot pressed ZrB2-20 vol% SiC in ground simulated atmospheric reentry conditions using arc-jet testing with heat fluxes of 1.7 MW/m 2 and 5.4 MW/m 2 . There was little mass or configuration change after ablation at the low heat flux condition, where the surface temperature reached up to 1700 °C. Nevertheless, at higher heat flux, the ZrB2-SiC composite underwent severe ablation (see Fig. 32) and whose surface temperatures exceeded 2300 °C. The authors compared the performance of ZrB2-SiC composite with those of C/SiC composite in the simulated re-entry environment and reported that the ZrB2-based composite possess better thermal-oxidative and configurational stability. ZrB2 containing 20 vol% SiC showed superior ablation resistance properties compared to those containing 10 and 30 vol% SiC due to its good thermal properties and presence of Zr-oxide and Si-oxide in adequate amounts to form ZrSiO4 [369]. This ZrSiO4 further decomposes to form ZrO2 and liquid at high temperature, which further suppress oxidation. In another work, Zhang et al. [437] also reported the effect of SiC content (15, 30 vol%) on the ablation behaviour of ZrB2 at 3000 °C for 100 s. Under those conditions, it was observed that ZrB2-15 vol% SiC suffered from lesser oxidation and mass loss, as compared to ZrB2-30 vol% SiC. The ablation resistance was found to decrease upon considerable mass loss and void formation, which, in turn, is caused by fast evaporation of SiO2. The surface appearance of ablation tested samples is shown in Fig. 33. In the case of ZrB2-15 vol% SiC, the surface appeared to be a continuous ablation layer with a speckle pattern in the outer region and a splashing spot in the central ablation region.

3.4. Ablation resistance
both composites initially increased and subsequently stabilized, eventually reaching thermal equilibrium. Among them, in the initial stage (0 s -32 s), 2.5D MP-ZS rapidly transferred heat owing to its high thermal conductivity,  thereby  effectively  reducing  the  thermal  gradient  and surface temperature. In the high-temperature steady state (32 s -60 s), although  the  thermal  conductivity  of  2.5D  MP-ZS  was  still  slightly higher than that of 2.5D PAN-ZS, the difference in the surface temperatures between the two composites gradually narrowed and eventually converged, which was mainly attributed to the synergistic influence of several factors. Firstly, as the thermal conduction process approached a steady state, the internal temperature gradient within the composites was significantly reduced, and the heat flux density tended to be stabilized. Secondly, surface oxidation reactions resulted in the formation of oxide layers predominantly composed of ZrO2 on both composites. With the gradual convergence of surface composition, the initial temperature differences  caused  by  differences  in  the  thermal  conductivity  were further  alleviated.  Finally,  the  thermal  stresses  generated  during  the ablation process induced the formation of microcracks and pores on the surface. These microstructural defects increased thermal resistance and inhibited outward heat diffusion, leading to a more consistent surface temperature evolution.

5. Conclusions
ZrC-based and ZrB2-based coatings were fabricated by VPS method and  their  ablation  behaviors  were  studied  and  compared,  and  the following conclusions were obtained:
- (1)  ZrB2-based  exhibited  better  ablation/oxidation  resistance  than ZrC-based, which is characterized with lower surface temperature, thinner thickness of total oxide layer and SiC-deleted layer under the same ablation condition.
- (2)  The low surface temperature of ZrB2-based coating was mainly due  to  good  thermal  conductivity  of  pristine  coating  at  the
X. Xu et al.
Fig. 15. Volatility diagrams for ZCS-H at 2000 ℃ (a), ZBS-H at 1700 ℃ (b), and ZZS-H at 2000 ℃ (c).
- beginning.  Subsequently,  a  thin  oxide  scale  with  low  thermal resistance was synergistic in maintaining low surface temperatures. In addition, the volatilization of gas products would further play the role of heat dissipation.
- (3)  The thin oxide and SiC-depleted layers of the ZrB2-based coating was attributed to the low surface temperature and formation of a continuous  dense  glassy  layer,  which  drastically  declined  the internal oxygen partial pressure.

4. Conclusions
Relationships  between  element  types,  composition  and  ablation property in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems have been discussed step by  step  via  SAPS  coating  preparation  and  performance  tests  under oxyacetylene ablation (2.4 MW/m 2 ). The synergistic effects of Hf, Zr, Ta and  Nb  elements  were  studied  through  different  procedures:  data collection, mechanism analysis, composition optimization and experiment  verification,  where  an  optimized  coating  system  for  long-term ablation protection above 2000 ◦ C was obtained. Main conclusions are as follows:
- (1)  Non-equimolar design based on (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating systems was found to be related to intrinsic porosity and self-derived low-
Fig. 12. Surface BSE images (a) and cross-sectional BSE images (b-c) of Zr70Nb10, Nb10 and Zr20Nb10 coatings after 300 s ablation.
melting-point phases. When the content ratio of high-meltingpoint  (Hf/Zr-based  oxides)  and  low-melting-point  phases  (Ta/ Nb-based oxides) is close to 1:1 in the as-prepared coatings systems (~15 % porosity), they are difficult to provide long-term effective protection ( < 120 s) above 2000 ◦ C.
- (2)  The decline of Ta or Nb relative content in the (Hf-Zr-Ta-Nb)C coating  systems  was  found  effective  in  improving  ablation property, attributed to the supporting effect of more retained (Hf, Zr)O2 skeleton phases on the coating structure.
- (3)  The optimized (Hf0.45Zr0.45Nb0.1)C coating system exhibited the best  long-term  ablation  property  (-0.01 μ m/s,  300 s),  corresponding to the Zr/Hf molar ratio around 1:1 in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system via first-principles calculation screening and experimental verification.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
As shown in Table 3, as the Zr content decreases, the Nb content in the outermost layer of the oxide layer gradually increased (Spot 15, Nb/ (Hf + Zr) = 0.16;  Spot  16,  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.29;  Spot  17,  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.35),  which  is  consistent  with  the  EDS  analysis  results  in  surface oxide layer. Additionally, as the distance from the surface increased, the Nb content in the oxide layer initially increased  and then decreased (Spot 16, Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.29; Spot 18, Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.44; Spot 19, Nb/ (Hf + Zr) = 0.32). This trend aligns with the distribution characteristics of Ta in the Zr-Ta-O oxide layer under oxyacetylene ablation conditions as reported by Hu et al. [59], which is primarily due to the outward diffusion and surface consumption of Nb element.
Overall, the Nb10 coating in the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C system exhibited the most outstanding ablation property ( > 300 s).  When  the  Nb  content remained constant (10 mol%), the ablation property of the (Hf-Zr-Nb)C coatings  initially  increased  and  then  decreased  as  the  Zr  content decreased. This trend is primarily due to the optimal amount of Hf, Zr and Nb elements which enhances the densification and integrity of the oxide layer, thereby improving its ablation property.

3.5. Verification on ablation properties of (Hf-Zr-Nb)C systems
From the  spot  EDS  analysis  results  presented  in  Table  3,  the  Nb content  in  the  Zr70Nb10  (Spot  9:  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.11;  Spot  10:  Nb/ (Hf + Zr) = 0.07) and Nb10 coatings (Spot 11: Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.15; Spot 12:  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.14)  was  consistently  below  0.25,  which  corresponds with the absence of lath-like A6B2O17 phase precipitation [34]. In the Zr20Nb10 coating, the Nb content within the lath structures (Spot 13:  Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.35) exceeded 0.25, while the Nb content in the equiaxed grain structures (Spot 14: Nb/(Hf + Zr) = 0.24) was slightly below 0.25, which indicates a higher local Nb element enrichment in the oxide scales in the Zr20Nb10 coating.

2.4.1. Thermal, Mechanical, and Electrical Properties of HEBs
A) Thermal properties: HEBs exhibit excellent thermal stability as well as oxidation resistance at high temperatures. A sufficient configurational entropy is achieved through the incorporation of multiple principal elements in near equimolar ratios and stabilizes the single-phase solid solution while enhancing thermal properties. For example, (Hf, Mo, Nb, Ta, Ti)B ₂ was found to retain structural integrity and resist oxidation up to 1200 ◦ C [45].  Furthermore,  the  coexistence  of  strong  covalent  B -B  bonds  and metallic  M -B  interactions  in  the  relatively  open  AlB ₂ -type  hexagonal structure are the contributing  factors to  high thermal  conductivity as  well  as low thermal expansion coefficients, ultimately resulting in high thermal shock  resistance.  Long  et  al.  [46] worked  on  thermal  properties  of (V0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2W0.2)B2  HEB. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was used to analyze the thermal characteristics of the HEB. It was observed that  as  the  temperature  increased,  DSC  slope  increased  and  the  mass  volume increased too, showing that partial oxidation of the material was taking place. However, the oxidation of the material from 500 to 1200 ◦ C was characterized  by  surface  oxidation  of  boron  with  the  evolution  of  the transition-metal oxides (TMOs). Boron oxide (B2O3) and TMOs produced passive oxidation layers which shielded oxygen from getting into the material to cause further oxidation. Meanwhile, after 1200 ◦ C, a swift increase in the mass curve of the material was observed which was attributed to the complete oxidation of the boron oxide. So, the protection of (V0.2Nb0.2Ta0.2Cr0.2W0.2)B2 against high temperature oxidation was facilitated by the formation of B2O3  and TMOs which isolated oxygen from penetrating  the  HEB.  It  is  noteworthy  that  liquid  B ₂ O ₃ formed

question: In Zamharir 2015, what relative density was achieved for ZrB2-25vol% SiC at the optimal hot pressing conditions of 1850°C, 90 min holding time, 16 MPa pressure, and 200 nm SiC particle size?
answer: In Zamharir 2015, a relative density of 100% was achieved for ZrB2-25vol% SiC at the optimal hot pressing conditions of 1850°C, 90 min holding time, 16 MPa pressure, and 200 nm SiC particle size [1].

Abstract
ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC ceramic composites were prepared by hot pressing in order to investigate the effects of processing parameters and SiC particle size (20 nm, 200 nm and 5 m m) on the densi /uniFB01 cation behavior. At this study, an experimental design technique (Taguchi method) was used to specify the signi /uniFB01 cance of four factors and to obtain the optimal conditions. The statistical analysis distinguished the applied pressure as the most effective parameter on the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites. A relative density of /C24 100% was obtained at the optimal processing conditions: temperature of 1850 1 C, holding time of 90 min, pressure of 16 MPa and SiC particle size of 200 nm. The con /uniFB01 rmation test, performed under optimum conditions, indicated that the experimental outcomes were equal with the expected values from the Taguchi prediction. The mechanisms of enhanced densi /uniFB01 cation of hot pressed ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites were discussed. & 2015 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

for 15 -25 min under a uniaxial pressure of 45 MPa. It was found that the addition of ultra /uniFB01 ne SiC to ZrB2 matrix had improved its sinterability. Moreover, the addition of micronsized SiC particles to ZrB2 matrix, which dispread intergranularly along ZrB2/ZrB2 interfaces inhibited the grain growth of the ZrB2 grains [21]. A ZrB2 ( /C24 3 μ m) ceramic composite containing 10 vol% /uniFB01 ne α -SiC (0.8 m m), was fully densi /uniFB01 ed by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 20 min under 40 -50 MPa. It was indicated that, introducing ultra /uniFB01 ne α -SiC particles was a key factor in enabling both the control of the diboride grain growth and the attainment of full density [17]. In another study, the in /uniFB02 uence of SiC particle size on the density of ZrB2 -SiC composites was investigated. ZrB2 -30 vol% SiC ceramic composites, using four different α -SiC powders with mean particle sizes ranging from 0.45 to 10 m m, were prepared by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 45 min under 32 MPa. It was indicated that decreasing the average size of the starting SiC particles, from 10 to 0.45 m m, had improved the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites from 97.4 to 99.8% [22].

1. Introduction
Although finding an appropriate composition is one of the most critical challenges for achieving the best relative density, hardness, and flexural strength, selecting a proper sintering process and its parameters is another essential issue. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC has been studied as a base composition with a ZrB2: SiC volume ratio of 4:1 and a ZrC: Cf volume ratio of 2:1. Optimum densification and mechanical characteristics, 99.6% relative density, 18.1 GPa hardness and 564.5 MPa flexural strength, have been reported for ZrB2-18.5 vol% SiC-5.0 vol% ZrC-2.5 vol% Cf composite [26]. Among several sintering processes such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [27-33], pressureless sintering (PS) [34], hot pressing (HP) [35-37], and reactive hot pressing (RHP) [38,39], samples produced by the SPS process were found to have relatively higher mechanical characteristics [40-45]. Because of strong bonding in carbides and borides, it is difficult to achieve superb sintering characteristics by conventional sintering processes [46-50]. According to ANOVA, the optimal process conditions are the temperature of 1850 1 C, the holding time of 90 min, the applied pressure of 16 MPa and the SiC particle size of 200 nm. Table 7 indicates the expected content of the relative density for the optimal processing conditions and results of the con /uniFB01 rmation test applied at the optimal quali /uniFB01 cation. As shown in Table 7, the relative density at the optimal processing conditions is 100.459, which indicates that ZrB2 -SiC composite has reached full density. The con /uniFB01 rmation test is a critical and de /uniFB01 nite step in establishing the outcomes attained from the Taguchi method. Indeed, accomplishment of the con /uniFB01 rmation test is necessary to obtain the optimal conditions via comparing the measured results with the expected consequences. The approximation is superlatively associated with the actual relative density of the samples densi /uniFB01 ed at optimal conditions. In other words, the experimental consequences accord the initial design and analysis for optimizing the processing factors.

2.2. Hot-pressing
ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC composite was fabricated by hot pressing under vacuum. ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC with 1 wt% B4C powder was mixed in a polythene bottle with alumina balls for 24 h and then hot-pressed in graphite dies at 2000 /C14 C with a heating rate of 15 /C14 C/min and a uniaxial pressure of 25 MPa for 1 h. The bulk density, theoretical density and grain size of the composite (ZrB2-20 vol.% SiC-1 wt.% B4C) designated ZS are 5.43 g cm /C0 3 , 5.44 g cm /C0 3 and 4.02 m m, respectively.

5. Conclusions
The aim of the present study was to explore the effects of four processing factors; the temperature, the applied pressure, the holding time and the particle size of SiC on the density of ZrB 2 -based ceramic composites using the Taguchi method. This work was performed to /uniFB01 nd the optimum processing conditions which collaborated to maximize the relative density of ZrB2-based ceramic composites. Based on the observations, the applied pressure and the temperature were speci /uniFB01 ed as the main factors which controlled the densi /uniFB01 cation of hot pressed ZrB2-based ceramic composites. The relative density at the optimal conditions of 1850 1 C, 90 min, 16 MPa and 200 nm, reached /C24 100%. The predicted and measured values of the relative density for the optimal processing conditions were wholly well correlated. Because of the higher activity of nano-sized SiC particles than micron-sized, chemical reactions between materials were well conducted, hence, the densi /uniFB01 cation was promoted ef /uniFB01 ciently. In constant volume of SiC (25 vol%), with decreasing the particle size of SiC, the number of SiC particles that inhibited the grain growth of ZrB2 increased, hence, the densi /uniFB01 cation process improved signi /uniFB01 cantly. Densi /uniFB01 cation mechanisms of ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites at different temperatures varied as at the low temperature of 1700 1 C, the dominant mechanisms were mechanical interweaving, particles fragmentation and rearrangement, but at higher temperatures, other densi /uniFB01 cation mechanisms such as plastic deformation and grain boundary diffusion were activated.

question: Why did Zamharir 2015 add ultrafine SiC particles to the ZrB2 matrix for the densification of this ceramic composite?
answer: Zamharir 2015 added ultrafine SiC particles to the ZrB2 matrix to enhance sinterability, inhibit grain growth, and improve mechanical properties [4]. The addition of ultrafine SiC particles also contributes to achieving higher relative density in ZrB2-SiC composites [8].

3. Results and discussion
It is well known that the use of fine powders has beneficial effects on the densification of ZrB2 -SiC, thereby a fine ZrB2 powder (2 𝛍 m) was also chosen as starting material in this study. Fig. 8 shows SEM micrograph from a polished surface of the hotpressed ZrB2-SiCW composite using fine ZrB2 powder: it reveals a nearly full-dense ZrB2-SiCW composite and that the majority of SiC whiskers were retained after hot pressing. Moreover, the SiC whiskers were homogenously distributed in ZrB2 matrix as can be seen in Fig. 8. The measured fracture toughness of ZrB2-SiCW composite was 6.0 ± 0.30MPam 1/2 , which is ∼ 71% higher than that of the ZrB2 ceramic without SiC whisker and is also higher than SiC particle reinforced ZrB2 composite. Fig. 9 shows the path of a crack produced by Vickers indentation on the polished surface of the hot-pressed ZrB2-SiCW composite. Whisker bridging and crack deflection were observed for the composite. Fracture surface examined by SEM shows some whiskers were pulled out and a predominantly transgranular matrix fracture mode (Fig. 10). How- ever, the majority of SiC whiskers were fractured directly and the whiskerpullout is very limited. High interfacial bonding strength of ZrB2-SiCW interface and the decreased strength of SiC whisker are probably responsible for the limited whisker pullouts. The increase in fracture toughness of the ZrB2-SiCW composite was mainly due to SiC whisker bridging and crack deflection. The control of whisker/matrix interfaces and the densification without degradation of SiC whisker for this material will be made in future work. Other densification methods should be taken into account in the future for fabrication this composite with high performance, such as spark plasma sintering due to rapid densification resulting in reduced damage of SiC whisker.

Signi /uniFB01 cance of hot pressing parameters and reinforcement size on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC UHTCs

2000 1 C such as aerospace applications and conventional industries (e.g. electrode elements and refractory crucibles) [5,12]. Hence, it is necessary to fabricate dense body sections of ZrB2-based composites. Generally densi /uniFB01 cation of a single-phase ZrB2 is dif /uniFB01 cult and usually needs high temperatures because of its covalent bonding and low bulk and grain boundary diffusion rates [8,13]. Its poor sinterability prevents this material from being used in a wide range of applications; therefore sinterability of ZrB2 must be improved before the potential applications of ZrB 2 can be fully realized.
The introduction of secondary phases or sintering aids has been an appropriate strategy for improving the sinterability of monolithic ZrB2 ceramics [4,14 -17]. With this aim in mind, sintering aids such as boron and carbon, metallic materials (e.g. Ni and Mo), metal silicides or Si3N4 were used to enhance the densi /uniFB01 cation and also reduce the sintering temperature [18 -21].
ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites with different amounts of SiC (5, 10, 15 and 20 vol%), which mean particle size of ZrB2 and SiC were 4 -6 m m and 0.8 m m, respectively, were densi /uniFB01 ed to the near theoretical density by vacuum hot pressing at 1900 1 C

A B S T R A C T
This  study  presents  new  findings  on  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  composites,  where  silicon  carbide  and graphene were incorporated into a zirconium diboride matrix. The research focused on the densification process, and the mechanical and electrical properties of the produced materials. A novel aspect of this work was the investigation of the influence of graphene alignment on composite properties. The results indicated that despite SPS curves suggested full consolidation, the final relative density was lower than expected, and SEM observations revealed the presence of pores in the microstructure. This suggests that additional sintering activators may be necessary to achieve full densification of ZrB2-based materials. Furthermore, high consolidation temperature (2000 ◦ C)  caused  partial  decomposition  of  graphene,  with  only  some  flakes  preserving  their  structure.  Agglomerates of silicon carbide particles were also observed in SiC composites. Regarding mechanical properties, all composites showed a significant increase in hardness compared to pure ZrB2. The highest hardness (1750 HV10) and fracture toughness (3.5 MPa m 1 / 2 ) were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% SiC. For electrical properties, all composites showed enhanced conductivity than the reference ZrB2 sample. Notably, the graphene composites exhibited a clear anisotropy. Specimens tested parallel to the graphene alignment showed a conductivity increase of a few orders of magnitude. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that SPS is an effective method for consolidating ZrB2-based composites without sintering aids, leading to significant improvements in hardness and electrical conductivity. However, achieving full densification may require the use of activators. The observed anisotropy of electrical properties in graphene composites highlights their potential for applications where directional conductivity is desired. This work opens new paths for better understanding ultra-high temperature ceramic-based composites.

Signi /uniFB01 cance of hot pressing parameters and reinforcement size on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC UHTCs
Recent works have shown that the introduction of nanosized SiC particles causes an increase in the sinterability and densi /uniFB01 cation of ZrB2-based ceramic composites at moderate temperatures (1800 -1900 1 C) [23,24]. It was suggested that using nano-sized SiC particles can result in superior density of ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composites. ZrB2 -SiC composites, with different amounts of 30 nm β -SiC (10, 20 and 30 vol%), were manufactured by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 60 min under a 30 MPa uniaxed pressure, as a relative density of 97.1% was obtained for ZrB2 -10 vol% SiC [23]. A ZrB2-based ceramic composite containing 20 vol% β -SiC particles, which mean particle size of ZrB2 and SiC were 2 μ m and 30 nm, respectively, was fabricated at 1900 1 C for 30 min under a uniaxial pressure of 30 MPa by hot pressing. It was indicated that the grain growth of ZrB2 matrix had impressively graveled by submicron-sized SiC particles, where it was located along the grain boundaries [24].
Generally, the addition of ultra /uniFB01 ne SiC to the ZrB2 matrix is performed to enhance sinterability, inhibit the grain coarsening, improve mechanical properties, and enhance oxidation/reduction resistance [21,22,25 -28]. Hence, introducing SiC particles into ZrB2 yields a ZrB2 -SiC ceramic composite that is more sinterable than the monolithic ZrB2.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have garnering attentions in the last decades because of the development of hypersonic aerospace vehicles [1-6]. From this category of advanced materials, ZrB2 has a series of excellent characteristics such as chemical stability, high melting point, thermal conductivity, hardness, and low theoretical density. These properties make ZrB2-based ceramics as suitable choices in aerospace and other industrial applications such as rocket propulsion systems, hypersonic flight and cutting tools, crucibles for molten metals and high-temperature electrodes [7-11].
Similar to other non-oxide refractory ceramics, ZrB2 generally oxidizes under air exposure at high temperatures. Therefore, the oxidation behavior studies of ZrB2 has been one of the interesting research topics since the 60 s [12-16]. Introducing SiC particles not only provides remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance of zirconium diboride by supporting the formation of a protective layer of borosilicate glassy phase [17-19], but also restrains the growth of zirconium diboride grains which improves final mechanical behavior such as flexural strength, hardness, and fracture toughness [20-23]. Noteworthy that the densification of ZrB2-based composites is very difficult owing to the covalent character of the bonding, low volume self-diffusion coefficient and presence of oxide films on the particles' surface [24-29]. Therefore, different densification routes are utilized for

3.1. Microstructure and composition
It is also obvious that with ZrB2 increase in the composites, the volume percentage of carbon fiber, V f , decreases from 37.1% (without ZrB2 introduction) to 18.9% (with 24.6% ZrB2), and V f decrease will decrease mechanical properties of the composites. The reason for V f decrease comes from the thicker matrix layer between two pieces of carbon fiber cloth due to higher content of ZrB2 in the slurry, and at the same time, V SiC remains almost at the same level (33%) in all C/SiC-ZrB2 samples, except 48.7% for C/SiC (ZB-0) sample.
The density of the composites also logically increases with ZrB2 increase, and reaches 2.59 g/cm 3 when ZrB2 content in the slurry (ZrB-60) is 60%, compared with 1.87 g/cm 3 of C/SiC (ZB-0) composites. The difference would be much larger if C/ SiC-ZrB2 and C/SiC samples had the same porosity because the density of ZrB2 (5.8 g/cm 3 ) is much higher than that of SiC (3.2 g/cm 3 ) as matrix. The porosity of C/SiC-ZrB2 composites ( /C24 20%), is significantly higher than that of C/SiC (14.2%), indicating that ZrB2 introduction is not beneficial for densification. And also trend is observed that higher content of ZrB2 will cause higher porosity in the composites, which would be ascribed as the agglomerates of ZrB2 particles and hindrance of PCS infiltration.

6.6  MB 2 WITH SiC
Figure 6.8 shows the resulting microstructures of two ZrB 2 -SiC composites, ZS05 and ZS20, where it can be clearly seen that, although both were processed at the same peak temperature of 1900°C, coarsening of the ZrB 2 grains was slower as a direct consequence of a larger concentration of SiC particulates at grain boundaries. SiC particles hinder grain-boundary migration during the final stage of sintering and restrain coarsening of the  ZrB 2 matrix,  increasing  the  driving  force  available  for  further  densification. The proof is that, during the isothermal stage at 1900°C and 40 MPa of applied external pressure, a longer hold, 25 min, was required for the ZS05 composition to achieve full density compared to 15 min for ZS20. These results emphasize the double role of SiC as densification activator and grain growth controller for ZrB 2 .  On the other hand, a milled ZrB 2 powder sample, that is, Z0, containing no SiC particulates, exhibited a sharp drop in densification rate above 1850°C and reached a final relative density of only 87%
because most of the available driving force was consumed by coarsening of the matrix during ramping up to 1850°C.

Signi /uniFB01 cance of hot pressing parameters and reinforcement size on densi /uniFB01 cation behavior of ZrB2 -25 vol% SiC UHTCs
for 15 -25 min under a uniaxial pressure of 45 MPa. It was found that the addition of ultra /uniFB01 ne SiC to ZrB2 matrix had improved its sinterability. Moreover, the addition of micronsized SiC particles to ZrB2 matrix, which dispread intergranularly along ZrB2/ZrB2 interfaces inhibited the grain growth of the ZrB2 grains [21]. A ZrB2 ( /C24 3 μ m) ceramic composite containing 10 vol% /uniFB01 ne α -SiC (0.8 m m), was fully densi /uniFB01 ed by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 20 min under 40 -50 MPa. It was indicated that, introducing ultra /uniFB01 ne α -SiC particles was a key factor in enabling both the control of the diboride grain growth and the attainment of full density [17]. In another study, the in /uniFB02 uence of SiC particle size on the density of ZrB2 -SiC composites was investigated. ZrB2 -30 vol% SiC ceramic composites, using four different α -SiC powders with mean particle sizes ranging from 0.45 to 10 m m, were prepared by hot pressing at 1900 1 C for 45 min under 32 MPa. It was indicated that decreasing the average size of the starting SiC particles, from 10 to 0.45 m m, had improved the relative density of ZrB2 -SiC composites from 97.4 to 99.8% [22].

5.2. ZrB2 ceramics
Among the carbides, SiC has been extensively used and investigated as a sinter-additive since the SiC addition enhances sinterability, inhibits grain growth and improves mechanical, oxidation and other properties [239-252]. Use of SiC as sinter-additive to ZrB2 has been studied in different ways by varying SiC content (up to ~30 vol%), use of different sizes of SiC starting powder (viz., micron, sub-micron and nano-sized) [239], different forms of SiC (particulate, whisker, fiber) and combining other additives along with SiC. It has been reported that the densification and densification rate of ZrB2 ceramics gets enhanced with increasing SiC-content due to liquid phase formation and control of grain growth [240]. Monteverde reported that with the use of 10 vol% SiC (ultrafine, 0.8 µm), near-theoretical sinter-density could be achieved for ZrB2 after hot press sintering at 1900 °C for 20 min in vacuum [241]. Similarly, in another work, ZrB2 could be fully densified at still lower sintering temperature (HP at 1650 °C, 120 min) by using ~22.4 vol% nano-sized SiC powders [10]. In both cases, liquid phase sintering was reported as main densification mechanism. Liu et al. [242] developed ZrB2-SiC-ZrC system and observed linear relationship between the mechanical properties (Young's modulus and hardness) and the composition. In another work, Khoeini et al. [243] further used carbon as sinter-additive to improve the densification of ZrB2-SiC via pressureless sintering. Removal of ZrO2, B2O3 and SiO2 surface oxides on the starting powder particles aided the densification in that case.

question: In the Chen 2025 study, how does nitrogen doping affect the thermodynamic stability of (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution, and what does this mean for synthesis processing temperatures?
answer: In the Chen 2025 study, nitrogen doping in the (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution enhances its thermodynamic stability [1]. This is evidenced by the decrease in mixing enthalpy (ΔHmix) and mixing Gibbs free energy (ΔGmix) of the system [1]. For instance, the mixing enthalpy of the (Zr1−xTix)C solid solution is 11.025 kJ/mol at a Ti atomic concentration of 25 at%, and increases to 15.443 kJ/mol at Ti atomic concentration of 50 at% [2]. However, with nitrogen doping, the mixing enthalpy decreases significantly, from 11.025 kJ/mol (0 at% N) to 0.59 kJ/mol (50 at% N) [5]. A lower ΔHmix implies reduced heat absorption during solid solution formation, which means that the addition of nitrogen reduces the difficulty in preparing the (Zr1−xTix)C,N solid solution [4]. Thus, nitrogen doping lowers the required synthesis temperatures for forming stable solid solutions [1].

A B S T R A C T
In this study, special quasirandom structure (SQS) models of (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N were developed to explore ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs). The structural stability, mechanical properties, thermodynamic and electronic structures of these solid solutions were systematically investigated. Results revealed that the addition of Ti atoms into ZrC reduces the lattice constants and volume of the solid solution as well as its thermodynamic stability. Mechanical and thermal properties, including melting point, Young ' s modulus, hardness, and wear resistance, exhibited a general decline, while the fracture toughness and thermal conductivity increased. The (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C system exhibited mixed covalent -ionic Zr/Ti-C bonds. Conversely, N doping in the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C system enhanced its  thermodynamic  stability.  Meanwhile,  the  mixing  enthalpy  and  mixing  Gibbs  free  energy  of  the  system decreased. The melting point, thermal conductivity, Young ' s modulus, hardness, and wear resistance initially increased and then decreased with increasing N content, peaking at a N concentration of 0.19. Compared to the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution, N doping in the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution facilitated the formation of strong covalent bonds between Zr and N atoms while enhancing the strength of Ti-C bonds. These findings provide a theoretical foundation for designing ZrC-based UHTCs.

3.1. Structure and property analysis of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution
atomic doping concentration on the structural stability of solid solutions (Fig. 2(a)). As revealed by Fig. 2(a), the formation energy increases with higher Ti content, indicating that Ti atomic incorporation reduces the structural stability of ZrC. This phenomenon can be explained by the gradual increase in the lattice distortion and elevated microscopic internal  stress,  which  collectively  increase  the  total  energy.  The  bond lengths  and  angles  changed  when  ZrC  lattice  incorporates  titanium atoms, thereby causing lattice distortion. Moreover, since the atomic radius of titanium atoms is smaller than that of zirconium atoms, the lattice around the zirconium atoms will contract when titanium atoms occupy the sites of zirconium atoms, and tensile stress will be generated within the crystal. Additional energy is required for solid solution to reconstruct the local structure, thus resulting in an increase in formation energy.  Meanwhile,  the  cohesive  energy  of  the  solid  solution  also gradually decreases, which is primarily because the strength of the Ti-C bonds formed is weaker than that of the Zr-C bonds. Notably, although Ti doping destabilizes the structure, the consistently negative formation energy values confirm the thermodynamic feasibility of the solutions formation. Fig. 2(b) shows the mixing enthalpy of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution, revealing that it is 11.025 kJ/mol at a Ti atomic concentration of 25 at%, and increases to 15.443 kJ/mol at Ti atomic concentration of 50  at%.  A  positive  mixing  enthalpy  indicates  that  the  solid  solution formation from single-component carbides is an endothermic process, requiring a higher synthesis temperature. Furthermore, the increase in Y. Chen et al.

The pseudogap (energy gap between sharp DOS peaks near EF) can be employed to indicate the strength of the overall covalent bonding within the crystal. As shown in Fig. S5, the magnitude of the Y. Chen et al.
pseudogap in the DOS exhibits an overall upward trend as the N content is increased. The maximum value is attained at a N content of 50 at%, indicating that the incorporation of N atoms improves the overall covalent bonding strength in the solid solution.
In summary, nitrogen doping significantly alleviates the degradation of structural stability and mechanical properties induced by TiC incorporation in (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution ceramics. Moreover, (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N formed  the  similar  ZrO2/TiO2 oxide  layer  as  (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C  after  high temperature oxidation enables the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N coating exhibit equally excellent oxidation resistance [47,48].

3.2. Effects of N doping on the structure and properties of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution
implies reduced heat absorption during solid solution formation. Additionally, nonmetallic sublattices in the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution also possess configurational entropy. The mixed entropy of the system exhibits a gradual increase, as shown in Fig. 7(c). The decreasing Δ Hmix  and increasing Δ Smix  jointly drive the critical temperature (where Δ Gmix = 0) downward. Thus, the addition of N reduces the difficulty in preparing the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution, as shown in Fig. 7(d).
Fig. 8(a) shows the elastic constants of (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solutions with varying compositions calculated using the first-principles method.
All solid solutions satisfy the mechanical stability criteria: C11 GLYPH<0> C12 > 0, C11 > 0, C44 > 0, and C11 + 2C12 > 0. The incorporation of N atoms into the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution significantly modifies its elastic constants. Specifically,  the  elastic  constants  C11 (uniaxial  longitudinal  stiffness) and C44  (shear stiffness)  initially  increase  with  N  content,  indicating enhanced  resistance  to  uniaxial  compression  and  shear  deformation. However, as the N concentration surpasses critical thresholds (19 and 25 at%), C11 and C44 exhibit a subsequent decrease. By contrast, uniaxial transverse  stiffness  (C12)  first  decreases  and  then  increases  as  the  N concentration increases. At N contents of 0.19 and 0.25, C11  and C44 reach  maximum values of 505.54 and 164.29 GPa, respectively.  C44 shows the lowest value of 109.18 GPa, observed at a N content of 19 at %,  reflecting  weakened  longitudinal  compressive  resistance  at  this point. Notably, C12 shows minimal overall variation.

3.2. Effects of N doping on the structure and properties of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution
The formation and cohesive energies of carbide solid solutions at different compositions were calculated; the results are shown in Fig. 7 (a). As revealed by Fig. 7(a), as the N content increases, the formation energy of the solid solution gradually decreases, signifying enhanced energetic stability of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution. The incorporation of N reduces internal microstress within the lattice, thus reducing the total energy of the system. Conversely, cohesion energy increases with N content. Cohesion energy reflects the strength of interatomic bonding in the  unit  cell,  and  its  increase  may  be  attributed  to  the  formation  of weaker chemical bonds (Zr-N and Ti-N) compared to the original Zr-C and  Ti-C  bonds.  To  verify  this,  we  calculated  the  populations  and lengths  of  different  chemical  bonds  in  the  solid  solution  (Table  S1). Compared to Zr-C (length 2.326 Å, population 0.189) and Ti-C (length 2.267 Å, population 0.143) bonds, Zr-N (length 2.298 Å, population 0.154) and Ti-N (length 2.343 Å, population 0.095) bonds exhibited longer bond lengths and smaller populations, confirming their weaker bonding strength. Notably, the negative values of formation and cohesive energies confirm the thermodynamic feasibility of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution formation. Fig. 7(b) depicts the mixing enthalpy under different N contents, revealing that Δ Hmix  decreases significantly from 11.025 kJ/mol (0 at% N) to 0.59 kJ/mol (50 at% N) as the N content increases. A lower Δ Hmix

3. Results and discussion
Furthermore, the two kinds of spacings are smaller than those of pure HfC and HfN, respectively, demonstrating that the transformation of HfO2 into HfC/HfN occurs before the solid solution doping to form HfC x N1x .
Fig. 9. TEM images of the A-1700 and N-1700 samples. (a) Elemental mapping of A-1700; (b-d) TEM, HRTEM, and SAED images of A-1700; (e) elemental mapping of N-1700; (f-h) TEM, HRTEM, and SAED images of N-1700.
Fig. 7 shows the evolution mechanism for the pyrolyzed precursor at high temperatures. The precursor has gone through an organic-inorganic conversion to become amorphous products constituted of C-N bonds, C-C bonds, and HfO2 before 1100 ° C. In an argon atmosphere, the carbothermal/nitrogen thermal reduction of HfO2 , N, and C can occur at temperatures exceeding 1100 ° C, facilitated by the catalytic effect of nitrogen. During this process, HfC x N1x begins to nucleate at the edges of HfO2 grain boundaries and continues to grow as the temperature increases, forming a single-phase HfC x N1x ceramic with good crystallinity. However, under a nitrogen atmosphere, HfO2 directly transforms Hf7 O8 N4 due to the N2 -rich atmosphere in the nitrogen-thermal reduction reaction, which enhances the formation of this compound. The formation of Hf7 O8 N4 commences directly at the grain boundary, subsequently transforming into HfC and HfN, as the temperature increases. Finally, HfC and HfN form HfC x N1x solid solution ceramics.

A B S T R A C T
composites exhibited good ablation resistance at elevated temperatures from 2000 to 3000 ◦ C. Superior oxidation  and  ablation  properties  upon  3000 ◦ C  were  also  found  in nitrogen-doped HfC ceramics due to the unprecedented melting point and the elevated oxidation energy barrier. In particular, nitrogen doping led to the enhancement of mechanical properties, e.g ., the hardness and fracture toughness increased from 19.2 to 26.4 GPa and from 3.11 to 5.02 MPa.m 1/2 , respectively, which are desirable for the applications of UHTC materials in extremely demanding thermo-mechanical environments [25].

3.2. Effects of N doping on the structure and properties of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C solid solution
Fig.  8(b)  illustrates  the  B,  G,  and  G/B  for  the  (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solutions as a function of different N contents. As observed in Fig. 8(b), B shows  a  slight  improvement  with  increasing  N  content,  indicating enhanced resistance to volumetric compression. Further, G initially rises but later declines, peaking at N atomic fraction of 19 at%, corresponding to the maximum shear resistance. G/B, which reflects the brittleness of the material, follows an identical trend, reaching its highest value at a N content of 19 at%. This result indicates that the solid solution has the weakest metallic properties at this point. Fig. 8(c) displays the melting points derived from elastic constants, revealing that the melting temperature first increases and then decreases, subsequently rising again with N content, attaining a maximum of 3530 K at a N content of 19 at%. This  trend  demonstrates  that  N  doping  mitigates  the  melting  point reduction induced by Ti substitution in ZrC-based UHTCs, surpassing the theoretical melting point of pristine ZrC (3400 K) calculated at 0 K and 0 Pa. Fig. 8(d) and Fig. S3 (phonon velocity data) reveal that both the Debye temperature ( Θ D) and thermal conductivity ( κ ) peak at a N content of 19 at%, with values of 744 K and 1.510 W/m ⋅ K, respectively, which  are  higher  than  that  of  pure  ZrC  (693  K  and  1.376  W/m ⋅ K, respectively). These results indicate stronger bonding strength between atoms in the solid solution.

3.1. Nanoscale

[95] demonstrated that nitrogen doping reduces the sintering temperature of high-entropy carbonitride ceramics (HECN0.95) by 200 ◦ C (from 2100 ◦ C to 1900 ◦ C) compared to high-entropy carbide ceramics (HECC0.95), via increased configurational entropy and lattice distortion. This compositional  modification  also  improves  densification  (relative  density  increases from 97.25 % to 98.73 %) and enhances mechanical properties, including a 29 % increase in flexural strength and a 30 % improvement in fracture toughness.

Ferroelastic domain switching has also emerged as a novel toughening mechanism in ceramics such as yttria-stabilized zirconia (YSZ) [162], zirconia [163 -165], YTaO4  [166 -168], and La2Zr2O7  [169]. In these  materials,  reversible  domain  reorientation  dissipates  crack-tip energy and mitigates stress concentrations, thereby enhancing fracture toughness. Li et al. [170] used indentation and scanning transmission electron microscopy to show that compressive loading of YSZ films induces  ferroelastic  deformation  and  domain  switching  at  the  surface, imparting increased ductility and toughness. Composition tuning offers control  over  this  mechanism.  Luo  et  al.  [166] demonstrated  that  in YTaO4, domain-boundary migration and transformation absorb deformation  energy  and  arrest  cracks.  Moderate  segregation  of  Zr 4 + at domain walls further suppresses crack initiation, whereas excessive Zr 4 + leads to t-ZrO2 formation and reduced toughness. Cao et al. [171] achieved a substantial toughness increase in La2Zr2O7  by adding 24 vol% BaTiO3, where ferroelastic switching in the second phase absorbs fracture energy.

The mixing entropy increases with higher Ti atomic ratios, reflecting greater configurational disorder in the system, as shown in Fig. 2(c). Fig. 2(d) illustrates the Δ Gmix  of different solid solutions.  The  temperature  of Δ Gmix = 0  rises  progressively  with increasing  Ti  atomic  concentration,  indicating  that  higher  synthesis temperatures are required to obtain a stable solid solution.

4. Conclusions
This study predicted the structure and properties of ZrC-based solid solutions  by  combining  first-principles  calculations  and  SQS  models. The following conclusions are obtained: The addition of Ti to ZrC reduces the structural stability, melting point, Young ' s modulus, hardness and abrasion resistance. The toughness of the solid solution increases, but  it  remains  brittle.  Furthermore,  the  addition  of  N  atoms  to  the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C ternary solid solution improves structural stability. With the increase in nitrogen content, the Young ' s modulus and hardness of the solid solution first increase and then decrease, reaching their maximum values of 422.7 and 24.71 GPa, respectively, at a nitrogen content of 19 at%.  Similarly,  the  melting  point,  Debye  temperature,  and  thermal conductivity reach their  maxima at a nitrogen content of 19 at%. N enhances the electron density between Ti and C atoms, strengthening TiC covalent bonds. Moreover, the pseudogap of the (Zr1 GLYPH<0> xTix)C,N solid solution exhibits an overall increasing trend, indicating that the addition of nitrogen atoms augments the strength of the covalent bonds in the solid solution.

3.2.1. Conversion of Zr and ZrO2  precursors to ZrC by CH4
By contrast to the conversion of Zr, the reduction of ZrO2 necessitates higher processing temperatures for spontaneous conversion (~685 ◦ C). During SLRS processing, this thermodynamic threshold was achieved through photothermal irradiation despite the low levels of optical absorption for ZrO2 at 445 nm (3.3 × 10 GLYPH<0> 3 a.u.). Overall laser processing of ZrO2  in  CH4  produced  low  carbide  yields  of  approximately  3.5  wt% ZrCyO1-y  using the 3 W processing conditions. During SLRS synthesis, the  majority  of  the  unreacted  oxide  phase  was  determined  to  be monoclinic-ZrO2  (95.6  wt%)  with  approximately  0.9  wt%  remaining with a tetragonal crystal structure. Undoped monoclinic-ZrO2 undergoes a temperature-induced martensitic transformation from monoclinic to tetragonal polymorphs at approximately 1170 ◦ C and is stable up to 2370 ◦ C at which point cubic zirconia is formed [48,70]. XRD results suggest that both the spontaneous reaction temperature for ZrC formation  (685 ◦ C)  and  the  martensitic  transformation  temperature  were reached  during  SLRS  where  peak  reaction  temperatures  occurred (somewhere between 1170 ◦ C and 2370 ◦ C). This relatively low-temperature processing may have produced inefficient reaction kinetics for rapid conversion even if thermodynamic reaction thresholds were exceeded.

Synthesis
Starting powders were screened through a brass screen and working fractions were collected (<40 /c109 m for ZrB2, <50 /c109 m for silicides). Silicon carbide was not previously screened. Then wet powder mixing was performed in a laboratory planetary mill with balls and milling vessel of silicon nitride for 24 h with a drum rotation frequency of 150 rpm. Then the charge was extracted from milling vessel, dried and rubbed through a steel screen with a cell size of 200 /c109 m. All operations were performed in air. The mixture prepared in this way was placed in an HP unit of original construction with a force up to 10 tons. Compaction and simultaneous sintering was carried out in graphite molds, lubricated with boron nitride. Compaction pressure was about 20 MPa, maximum temperature 1800 - 1900°C, and holding at the maximum temperature for 30 min.
Specimens Nos. 1 and 2 were prepared from ZrB 2 powder produced by OOO KTM (ZrO2 < 3 wt.%), and the rest of the specimens were prepared from ZrB 2 produced by OOO Ékos-Ural (ZrO 2 ~ 7 wt.%). In specimen No. 1 SiC w (cubic /c98 -SiC) fibers were used [7]. Specimens Nos. 3, 4, 5a, and 6a were prepared from SiC of the Zaporozh'e Abrasive Combine, and specimens Nos. 5b and 6b were prepared from SiC from the Volga Abrasive Plant (see Table 1). Specimen 6a was not dense and synthesis was established with achievement of a temperature of about 1700°C with holding.

2.1. Materials and synthesis
Precursor powders - ZrC, TaC, TiC, ZrB2  and TiB2  were weighed in equiatomic proportions and mixed using a planetary ball mill (Fritsch, Model  No.  SYJ-150,  MTI  Corporation)  with  tungsten  carbide-lined stainless-steel jars at 280 RPM in dry condition for 5 hours. WC balls were used as milling media with a ball-to-powder weight ratio of 5:2. The process followed a 10-min milling cycle with 5-min intermittent cooling. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) micrographs of the ballmilled  powders  are  shown  in  Fig.  1a,  b,  c.  Spark  plasma  sintering (SPS) using a Dr. Sinter 515S system (Kanagawa, Japan) was employed to sinter ball-milled powders at 1850 ◦ C with a heating rate of 100 ◦ C/ min.  A  multi-stage  holding  process  was  utilized,  consisting  of  2-min holds at 1450 ◦ C and 1650 ◦ C (Appendix Fig. A2 and Fig. A3) for the surface oxide removal, followed by 30 min hold at 1850 ◦ C for the reaction and uniform densification. Sintering temperatures above 1850 ◦ C were avoided, as they may cause abnormal grain growth and deteriorate the mechanical properties, particularly strength. Previous studies have also reported successful densification of UHTCs at similar or lower SPS temperatures (~1850 ◦ C) with varied holding times, which supports the present approach [9,31 -33].
Sintering was carried out with a uniaxial pressure of 40 MPa under vacuum conditions ( < 6 Pa). The final sintered pellets measured ~15 mm in diameter and ~5 mm in thickness. These pellets were coarsely ground to remove the graphite surface layer, followed by sequential polishing  with  abrasive  papers  (grit  size  80 -2500)  and  final  cloth K.P. Singh et al.

2.1. Materials
The raw materials used in the present study were ZrO2 and HfO2 powders (99.9%, 1 𝛍 m, China New Metal Materials Technology Co., Ltd, Beijing, China) and carbon black (99%, -100 mesh, Mudanjiang Qianjin Reagent Co., Ltd, Mudanjiang, China). According to Eqs. (6) and (7), two kinds of reactant powders with the molar ratios of ZrO2/C = 1/3 and HfO2/C = 1/3 were ball-milled in absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 h using polyurethane jar and zirconia grinding balls. After ball-milling, the slurries were dried in an oven until the alcohol evaporated completely. Then the powders were sieved through a 120-mesh screen to obtain homogeneous reactants. In order to determine the synthesis temperature of ZrC and HfC and control the gas releasing process, the reactant powders were heated to 1750 ◦ C in a thermal analyzer (NETZSCH STA 409 CD/7/403/5/G, Germany) in flowing argon at a heating rate of 5 ◦ C/min. Meanwhile, shrinkage behavior was monitored using a dilatometer (NETZSCH DIL 402 E, Germany) through heating the columnar shaped green bodies (7 mm in diameter and 15 mm in height) up to 1850 ◦ C in flowing argon at a heating rate of 5 ◦ C/min to investigate the shrinkage behavior during reaction and sintering.
Porous ZrC and HfC were prepared through gas-released insitu reactions followed by high-temperature sintering, as shown in Fig. 1(c). The reactant powders were uniaxial pressed into columnar shaped green bodies of 30 mm in diameter. According to the thermal analysis results, formation temperatures of ZrC and HfC are selected as 1470 ◦ C and 1580 ◦ C, respectively, by heating at a rate of 5 ◦ C/min and the partial sintering temperature is fixed at 1850 ◦ C.

question: In Licheri et al. (2019), how did the surface roughness of porous graded ZrB2 ceramics correlate with the holding temperature used during SPS processing?
answer: In Licheri et al. (2019), the surface roughness of the resulting porous graded ZrB2 ceramics increased as the holding temperature was reduced. This information is provided in the abstract of the study [1].

A B S T R A C T
Porous graded materials (PGMs) consisting of ZrB2 are obtained by Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) taking advantage of an asymmetric die con /uniFB01 guration with changing cross section. Due to the temperature gradient established along the longitudinal direction, porosity changes up to about 26 vol.% were generated across the volume. The surface roughness of the resulting products increased as the holding temperature was reduced. Superior solar absorbance ( α ) and lower spectral selectivity ( α / ε ) properties were correspondingly obtained. PGMs displayed higher α and lower α / ε values with respect to highly dense samples produced using a constant cross section die. More important, these products exhibit similar or improved optical properties with respect to the reference SiC material. These /uniFB01 ndings are important in view of the development of a novel class of ceramics for solar energy applications, able to operate under higher temperature conditions while simultaneously satisfying optical, microstructural and mechanical needs.
porous graded structures, mainly addressed to the biomedical /uniFB01 eld [5]. These include the Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) technique, an e /uniFB00 ective hot pressing method, where powders to be consolidated are rapidly heated by an electric current /uniFB02 owing through a conductive die, while a mechanical load is simultaneously applied along the axial direction [6]. Speci /uniFB01 cally, Cu-10 wt%Sn samples with graded porosity distribution were obtained by SPS using a graphite mold with changing cross-sectional area, aimed to determine a temperature variation along the longitudinal direction [7]. A similar approach was also recently adopted with success to produce functionally graded 3Y-PSZ/316L materials in one-step by SPS [8].

Journal of the European Ceramic Society
A positive contribution to make the processing conditions relatively milder was recently provided by the use of ZrB2 powders prepared by Self-propagating High-temperature Synthesis (SHS) and subsequently consolidated by SPS [18]. Indeed, 96% or denser monolithic materials were correspondingly obtained at temperatures of 1850 ± 50 °C [18]. The bene /uniFB01 cial e /uniFB00 ect produced by the latter two-steps processing route is then exploited in this work for the fabrication of porous graded ZrB2 specimens. To establish the required temperature gradient across the sample, a suitably shaped graphite novel die is here proposed and tested under di /uniFB00 erent SPS conditions. The microstructural, topological and optical properties of the resulting ceramics will be /uniFB01 nally compared.

3. Results and discussions
The mechanical properties of the spark plasma sintered ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics, along with those of similar materials processed via conventional processing techniques [14], are shown in Table 1. With the sintering temperature increasing from 1600 1 C to 1750 1 C during SPS process, both /uniFB02 exural strength and fracture toughness were increased. However, the mechanical properties of the spark plasma sintered ZrB2 -rO2f ceramics were decreased at the temperature of 1800 1 C. The ceramics sintered at 1750 1 C had the highest /uniFB02 exural strength and fracture toughness (667 7 48 MPa, 6.2 7 0.6 MPa m 1/2 ), which were higher than that of the similar ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics obtained by hot pressing (633 7 63 MPa, 5.6 7 0.7 MPa m 1/2 ) [14]. Fig. 3 shows the microstructures of fracture surfaces of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics (SPS; 1750 1 C for 10 min). The observed fracture mode was a mixed mode, comprising both intergranular and transgranular fracture.
The typical /uniFB02 aw structures, such as pores and microcracks, have an obvious effect on the mechanical properties in ceramics. The relationship between porosity and strength of the ceramics can be expressed as [19]:
Fig. 2. SEM micrographs of the polished surface of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrO2f sintered by SPS with different sintering temperature: (a) 1600 1 C, (b) 1650 1 C, (c) 1700 1 C, (d) 1750 1 C, (e) 1800 1 C; (f) SEM micrographs of the polished surface of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrO2f sintered by HP.
