where a is the residual porosity, σ and σ 0 denote the material strength and the strength without any defect, respectively. This equation reveals that strength is increased with decreasing porosity.

3. Results and discussions
The relative densities of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrO2f ceramics sintered by SPS, compared with literature reports of densi /uniFB01 cation achieved via conventional processing techniques [14], are shown in Table 1. The results revealed that the sintering temperature had remarkable in /uniFB02 uence on the relative density. The activation of the powder particle surface due to spark and possible plasma generation, along with superior Joules heating (for conducting materials) would result in enhanced mass transport and hence faster densi /uniFB01 cation kinetics during SPS [17,18]. With sintering temperature rising from 1600 1 C to 1750 1 C, the relative density of the bulk compact was increased. The maximum densi /uniFB01 cation of /C24 99.2% ρ th was obtained at 1750 1 C by SPS. However, the sintered density of ZrB2 -ZrO2f was lowered from /C24 99.2% ρ th to /C24 96.6% ρ th, as the sintering temperature increasing from 1750 1 C to 1800 1 C during SPS process. In our earlier research [15], /C24 98.1% ρ th can be obtained by hot pressing at 1950 1 C for 2 h for the same starting powder. However, much less sintered density of 78.7% ρ th was obtained by pressureless sintering at 1950 1 C for 10 h. Such observations clearly indicated the advantage offered by SPS, which was a comparatively novel sintering process that allowed the compaction of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics to be sintered at lower temperatures, with shorter processing time.

journal homepage: www.elsevier.com/locate/jeurceramsoc reference SiC absorbers [9,11,12]. Nonetheless, the wide di /uniFB00 usion of such class of materials is hampered by the highly refractory character of the related powders, that makes the fabrication of massive ZrB2 bodies a di /uniFB03 cult target to accomplish. Indeed, temperature levels exceeding 2000 °C are typically required with conventional sintering methods to reach high densi /uniFB01 cation levels, unless introducing appropriate secondary phases [13,14]. In this regard, the presence of oxygen and other contaminants on the surface of ZrB2 particles is known to play a negative role during powder consolidation, so that this aspect has been investigated in the literature and various possible solutions to remove such impurities have been recently proposed [15 -17]. For instance, a nitride boronizing process was developed to produce h-BN coated nanosized ZrB2 powders with improved sinterability [15]. Bulk ZrB2-BN composites were also obtained under rather low sintering conditions starting from ZrN and B powders [16]. More recently, Ma et al. [17] evidenced that oxygen, boron and the other light elements present on the surface and the lattice of ZrB2 powders subjected to ball milling could not be totally eliminated after a vacuum treatment at 1500 °C. This holds also true when Si3N4 was incorporated in the powders to react with impurities, thus producing h -BN during the heat treatment.
Fig. 1. Standard [18] and modi /uniFB01 ed (asymmetric) graphite assembly used for the fabrication of standard and porous graded ZrB2 samples, respectively, by SPS.
The drawback mentioned above concerning the di /uniFB03 culties in the consolidation of ZrB2 powders has to be overcome also when considering porous graded structures, since low porosity levels have to be still obtained in the material bulk to assure adequate mechanical properties.

A B S T R A C T
sintering techniques that has been tried to improve the densification of ZrB2  [21 -24]. In general, the sintering temperature achieved in single step  SPS,  which  causes  to  develop  temperature  gradients  across  the cross-section of sample and deteriorates the mechanical properties [25, 26]. To control these problems multi step (MS)-SPS is suggested for the densification, in which sample is held at intermediate temperatures for certain duration of time before achieving the final sintering temperature.  The  MS-SPS  processed  samples  shows  uniform  microstructure, homogeneous densification without exaggerated grain growth, which leads to improvement in the mechanical properties [21,22,27,28]. In view of the high temperature applications of ZrB2, understanding its oxidation bevaviour is very important [1]. In particular, additives such as SiC and other silicon based ceramics are added to ZrB2 or other high temperature  borides  with  a  view  to  improve  its  oxidation  resistance [29 -43].
In  this  work,  tantalum  metallic  element  was  added  with  varying amounts from 2.5 to 10 wt% to ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composite. The samples were densified using MS-SPS at 1900 ◦ C for holding time of 3 min. The hardness and elastic modulus of ZrB2  composites were evaluated using  nano-indentation  test  using  Oliver  Pharr  method  [44].  The oxidation resistance of the composites was evaluated at high temperature of 1600 ◦ C for 10 h in stagnant air. The oxidation resistance of the

a b s t r a c t
The compositional effects in ZrB2-SiC-ZrC ultra high temperature composites with four different compositions were investigated via Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) at a maximum temperature of 1800 ◦ C. Density, Rockwell hardness, and thermal conductivity were measured, along with structural X-ray diffraction (XRD) and microstructural characterization. The relative amounts of SiC and ZrC had an influence on the composites' density, mechanical and thermal properties.

sintering aids, improve the oxidation behavior at high temperature. The rapid heating and short holding times at maximum temperature employed in Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) have been shown to contribute to restricted grain growth and therefore, superior mechanical properties. In addition, SPS has the advantage of eliminating the need for sintering aids, leading to improved material properties at high temperatures. SPS is a consolidation technique that uses a combination of pulsed electrical field application with high heating rates to densify, while preserving an ultra-fine grain size in a large variety of powder materials that have proven to be difficult to sinter by other methods [6-16].

the other hand, SPS is a favorite process because of removing the particle oxide surfaces due to spark discharge phenomenon [51-58]. Thus, enhancements in grain boundary diffusion, densification, and mechanical properties can be achieved [59-64]. Shahedi Asl [20] observed that 1850 °C temperature, 30 MPa punch pressure, and 6 min dwelling time are the most effective parameters for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-10 vol% Cf composite using the SPS process.
In this research, the authors intended to improve the mechanical properties of these groups of UHTCMCs by designing, producing, and investigating a quadruplet ZrB2-SiC-Cf-ZrC composite. The purpose and innovation of this research is to achieve improvements in relative density, hardness, and flexural strength for the mentioned composites.

3. Results and discussion
Regarding the mechanical properties, the Vickers hardness and toughness measured at room temperature were 15.4 ± 0.9 GPa and 2.0 ± 0.5 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol% MoSi2, 10.3 ± 0.6 GPa and 2.9 ± 0.5 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2, and 11 ± 1 GPa and 2.1 ± 0.3 MPa m 0.5 for ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, respectively. The hardness of ZrB2 + 20 vol%MoSi2 is well within the typical range reported for ZrB2-MoSi2 ceramics [25,26], whereas the lower hardnesses of ZrB2 + 20 vol%TaSi2 and ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2 are attributable to the greater ductility of their second phases. This would also explain the toughness trends, which do not correlate directly with the grain size. Fig. 3 shows the Hertz indentation stress-strain curves of the three ZrB2 + 20 vol%MeSi2 (Me = Mo, Ta, or Zr) UHTCs processed in this study. The maximum testing temperature was 800 ◦ C, above which creep already occurred at the high contact pressures used in the Hertzian indention. In the case of ZrB2 + 20 vol%ZrSi2, however, the tests were interrupted at 600 ◦ C because, as shown in Fig. 4, at 800 ◦ C in air this UHTC already underwent severe peptization (confirmed by X-ray energy dispersive spectrometry in the SEM; not shown) that impeded its mechanical characterization. Therefore, the ZrB2 ceramic sintered here with ZrSi2 additive does not seem to fulfil the criterion of tolerance to the high temperatures in oxidizing environments that is demanded of UHTCs, an aspect that requires further investigation in future studies. 3.2. Experimental results
Extreme values of surface roughness and consolidations were disregarded from future consideration. Fig. 4 shows the experimental surface roughness and HAZ dimension as function of the process parameter. The results strengthened the observation that for this material combination: the electron beam power has more influence over the quality as compared  to  the  electron  beam  scanning  speed.  Higher  line  energy density  has  resulted  in  the  formation  of  a  network  of  the  solidified material in the track. Fig. 5 compares the SEM images of the un-heated and ZrB2-30% ZrSi2 under the power of 500 W and a scanning speed of 750 mm/s. As seen from the figures, after the EBM process, the ZrSi2 spreads on the surface of the ZrB2. The SEM of the network structure reveals that ZrSi2 wetted the ZrB2 powder particles, which remain in the un-molten state. The ZrSi2  has formed a network that joins the entire track. The time duration for heat transfer at any instance is higher than required for a line energy density between 1.5 and 2.5, and hence it results in the formation of consolidations. The surface roughness of the process  track  is  lower  than  the  predetermined  threshold  and  the consolidation is considerably lower than the cut-off value. For all the process  combinations,  this  case  has  the  lowest  value  of  surface roughness and consolidation. Also, the HAZ width matches that predicted by the FE model. Fig. 6 shows the SEM images of the ZrB2-30% ZrSi2 under different process parameters. By using power 1000 W and a scanning speed of 750 mm/s, well treated surface can also be obtained. 3.2. Experimental results
The experimented single tracks on the pucks were then viewed under a  Confocal  Laser  microscope  and  a  Scanning  Electron  Microscope  to better understand the effects of EBM conditions on the processing of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrSi2. The HAZ Width, the surface roughness of the track and  the  dimensions  of  the  consolidation  were  determined  using  the Laser Microscopy. Since the starting condition was the prepared pucks with an absence of powder raking action, the surface roughness can be an outcome of the interaction of the powder and the electron beam only. The surface roughness measurement of the HAZ became the first criterion to select the process parameter. The surface roughness of around 125 μ m was decided to be the selection factor where the starting pucks had the surface roughness of 77 μ m. Computational tools also helped in determining the dimensions of the consolidation. Consolidation with a linear dimension greater than 125 μ m, which is a quarter of the beam spot size, was considered as a downsizing factor. The presence of bigger consolidations on the surface was a factor for rejecting the combination of process parameter for the fabrication of ZrB2-30 vol% ZrSi2.

Spark plasma sintering of ZrO2 /uniFB01 ber toughened ZrB2-based ultra-high temperature ceramics
ZrO2f ceramics are obtained at 1950 1 C by hot pressing for 2 h; the /uniFB02 exural strength and fracture toughness are 633 7 63 MPa and 5.6 7 0.7 MPa m 1/2 , respectively. However, this high hotpressing temperature ( 4 1900 1 C) is harmful to the performance of ZrO2 /uniFB01 bers. Here, we investigate an alternative route for improving the properties of ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics. The densi /uniFB01 -cation of the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics with rapid heating and short holding times at maximum temperature employed in spark plasma sintering is studied. Because the temperature is the key in /uniFB02 uencing factor, it is necessary to understand the effect of different holding temperatures on the microstructure and mechanical properties of the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics. The purpose of this work is to /uniFB01 nd the most suitable SPS condition for the ZrB2 -ZrO2f.

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering was applied for the densi /uniFB01 cation of the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics. This method enabled the fabrication of /uniFB01 negrained ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics by providing much more rapid heating and lower temperatures than conventional sintering techniques. The high /uniFB02 exural strength of 667 7 48 MPa and fracture toughness of 6.2 7 0.6 MPa m 1/2 were achieved for the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics sintered by SPS at 1750 1 C for 10 min, with a holding pressure of 25 MPa and heating rate of 150 1 C min /C0 1 . High density and small grain size were the main factor to improve the mechanical properties of the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics sintered by SPS process. Due to the good mechanical properties, the ZrB2 -ZrO2f ceramics prepared by SPS technique was a wonderful candidate for high temperature structural applications, and in order to identify the toughness mechanisms of the /uniFB01 bers, the future investigation on fracture behavior should be put forward.

3. Results
The introduction of ceramics to C/C or graphite would cause a decrease in surface temperature as heat flux decreased. For instance, thermal protection materials including both ZrB 2 and SiC displayed the lowest surface temperature under condition 4, and over 400 ° C lower than those without ceramic additives or with SiC alone. The largest surface temperature difference is up to 864 ° C among these thermal protection materials under this test condition. In contrast to C/SiC, the addition of ZrB 2 , even at a small amount, to graphite-SiC significantly decreased its surface temperature and increased oxidation and ablation resistance within 200 s testing. It should be mentioned that GSZ tested for 200 s under condition 4 was further exposed to this heating environment for a long-time of 10 0 0 s. The surface temperature increased from 1641 ° C for the first cycle to 2388 ° C for the second cycle as seen in Fig. 3 . The surface temperature rising rate of pre-tested GSZ is higher than that without pre-treatment in the initial heating process. However, the rising rate of surface temperature for the former is higher than latter with extended periods. Meanwhile, a much higher surface temperature of initial and final steady states was achieved under the second cycle testing. In comparison, ZrB 2 -SiC was first oxidized at 1400 ° C for 1 h under atmosphere and then tested under the condition 4. As shown in Fig. 4 , both surface temperature and temperature rising rate of pre-oxidized ZS material are higher than those of untreated material. The reasons for these phenomena will be discussed in the next section.

question: In Jayaseelan et al. 2015, at what sintering temperature was ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3 (ZSLO) densified, and what was the thickness of the outer oxidized layer formed after oxidation at 1600°C for 1 hour?
answer: ZS20/10 wt.% La2O3 (ZSLO) was densified at a sintering temperature of 1950°C [6]. The thickness of the outer oxidized layer formed after oxidation at 1600°C for 1 hour was ∼ 250 𝛍m [1].

chunks.text:
3.2.2. Oxidation of ZS20/10 wt.% LaB6 (ZSLB)
ZSLB had similar oxidation layer thickness ( ∼ 250 𝛍 m) as observed in ZSLO after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦ C[13]. Microstructure and SIMS analyses from different regions in the cross-section of the oxidised surface are shown in Fig. 7. The oxidised surface is heterogeneous and contains a variety of phase morphologies. In someregions, it appears to be compact with grain boundary phases presumablyderivedfromliquid.Twolayerscanbeseenatlowmagnification in Fig. 7(a). The outer layer is ∼ 120 𝛍 mthick and dense whiletheintermediatelayeris ∼ 50 𝛍 mthickandporous(Fig.7(b)). Theporosity present in the intermediate layer may reduce strength degradationafteroxidation[7].Ourpreviouswork[13]verifiedthat the continuous phase in the outer layer contained mainly Zr, La, and O while the isolated dark regions were mostly silica suggesting that they derive from liquid since silica would melt at this temperature. Positive ion SIMS data was collected from different regions in the cross-section front of the oxidised surface and is shown in Fig. 7(c). Region 1 is La, Zr and their respective oxides (Zr O and La O) indicating that the top surface layer must be either La2 Zr2 O7 or mixture of La2O3 and ZrO2 or both. The presence of Si in the SIMS spectra could be due to the overlap of phases present as the layers are ragged. SIMS of region 2 reveals more B and Si along with Zr, La, Zr O and La O. Boron was not observed on the top exposed surface owing to the rapid evaporation of boria from the BS above 1500 ◦ C [39].

3.2.1. Oxidation of ZS20/10 wt.% La2 O3 (ZSLO)
Fig. 2(a) is an electron image (EI) of cross-section surface microstructures of oxidised ZSLO at 1600 ◦ C taken in FIB station. Fig. 2(a) reveals three oxidised layers. The outer >250 𝛍 m thick layer (1) is predominantly a continuous bright phase with isolated (5-30 𝛍 m diameter) dark regions present. The intermediate layers above the unaffected bulk material are about 50 𝛍 mthick. Two intermediatelayerscanbedistinctlyseeninthisFIBelectronimage. SIMS data collected (not shown here) on the dark contrast phase (layer 2) in Fig. 2 (b) consists mainly of Si and O whereas the bright phase (layer 3) is predominantly Zr and O. SIMS analysis was carried out at the interface between the top surface layer and the top intermediatelayerinFig.2(a). The crater formed after SIMS analysis is shown in Fig. 2(b) revealing clearly the presence of two different phases. The contrast of the ion signal can be different from the SE contrast (channelling, voltage contrast) and hence ion imaging can give additional information. SIMS analysis (Fig. 2(c)) revealed the presence of Si, Zr, La, B and their oxides in the oxidised top surface. Interestingly, boron was also detected indicating that in ZSLO, even after oxidation for 1 h at 1600 ◦ C, boron was not completely removed. Reports on the detection of boron in the oxidised layers using EDS in the SEM are sparse with limited ability to detect boron.
Fig. 2. (a) FIB-EI of cross-section microstructure of ZSLO oxidised at 1600 ◦ C for 1 h showing ∼ 250 𝛍 mthick oxidised layers, (b) crater formed at the interface of layers 1 and 2 and (a) and (c) positive mass spectra taken on rectangular crater of (b) showing the presence of B, Si, Zr, La and their respective oxides.

3.2.3. Oxidation of ZS20/10 wt.% Gd2O3 (ZSGO)

Such phase separation has also been observed in the oxidised layer of ZSLB in the present study and previously by other researchers [38,39]. The observation of phase separation is important since it is known to increase glass viscosity leading to slower oxidation in such systems, In all three samples (ZSLO (Fig. 2(a)), ZSLB (Fig. 7(a)) and ZSGO (Fig. 11(a))) after oxidation for 1h at 1600 ◦ C d, the common features observed after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C from their crosssection microstructures are a protective top crystalline oxide layer, intermediate layer(s) with BS containing immiscible liquid and unoxidised bulk material. These observations have been used to design a candidate oxidation and thermal protection system using UHTCs in which reduced strength degradation after oxidation is expected [40]. Although, ZrB2-SiC oxidised at 1600 ◦ C had much thinner and more compact coatings [13], due to the fact it is a glassy phase, at higher velocity for a long time it becomes soft and will be blown off subsequently. Furthermore, in the presence of water vapour, silica is not an ideal candidate material as it readily reacts with water to form Si(OH)4 which will result in the loss of material [41]. When comparing the cross-section micrographs of ZSLO [13], ZSLB and ZSGO after oxidation at 1600 ◦ C, they have similar thickness of oxidation layer. However, the notable difference between them when comparing the chemical composition of the top and intermediate layers is that the presence of boron, which is higher in ZSLB than other two compositions. It can be derived that ZSLO and ZSGO will have edge over ZSLB in terms of protection around 1600 ◦ C.

3.3.1. ZS20
Oxidation of ZS20 is explained in terms of an oxidation power law with exponent n from 1 to 2 in the temperature range 1400-1600 ◦ C. In addition, mass gain and oxide scale thickness of the BS layer is larger at 1400 ◦ Cthan at 1500 ◦ Cfor the same oxidation time as seen in Figs. 2 and 4 respectively, which is explained in terms of lower boria content, which vaporizes from the BS layer more rapidly at 1500 ◦ C than at 1400 ◦ C. In fact, oxygen penetrates deeper in ZS20 at 1400 ◦ Cthan at 1500 ◦ Cas the oxygen diffusion coefficient is greatly reduced by boria evaporation, as seen in Fig. 5 which reveals a much thicker scale at 1400 than 1500 ◦ C. According to Opeka et al. 3 , B2O3 content in the BS melt decreases with temperature and approximately stabilizes in the range 1200-1400 ◦ C to a level of 10 wt.% B2O3, which corresponds to a 90 wt.% ( ∼ 89 mol%) SiO2 BS melt. However, there are no data on the boria content after oxidation at 1500 ◦ CwhenpresumablyB2O3 contentintheBSmeltshould have been reduced. Considering a SiO2 concentration increase of 5 mol% as a consequence of B2O3 evaporation, which is reasonable, the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the BS melt is reduced ∼ 5 times at 1550 ◦ C. 22 Moreover, considering an activation energy of ∼ 125 kJ/mol for oxidation, it is calculated that D O21500 ◦ C ∼ = 2 D O21400 ◦ C. Therefore, a layer of BS melt with
Fig. 4. Oxide scale thickness of ZS20 from different layers (total oxide scale, BSmeltandporous ZrO2 + SiC-depleted layers) at: (a) 1400 ◦ Cand(b) 1500 ◦ C.

3.3.2. ZS20La
ZS20La showed pure parabolic behavior at 1400 ◦ C ( n ∼ 2) and its oxidation kinetic exponent increases with temperature to
∼ 2.5 at 1500 ◦ C. In addition, values of n for mass gain and oxide scale thickness at 1600 ◦ C are different by almost four times, 2.1 for mass gain and 7.5 for oxide scale, which indicates that an oxygen adsorption mechanism may be active because large changes in mass gain lead to small changes in oxide scale. Oxide scale thickness of ZS20La is smaller than oxide scale thickness of ZS20 at 1400 ◦ C while the converse applies for 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C as seen in Fig. 2, presumably due to the higher volatilization of B2O3 present in the BS layer of ZS20 at these temperatures. Therefore, ZS20La is more oxidation resistant at 1400 ◦ C but not at 1500 ◦ C and 1600 ◦ C. The main difference between the oxide layers of ZS20 and ZS20La is the mixing of the BS glassy melt with the ZrO2 scale which is more viscous in ZS20La and does not ascend directly to the top surface, producing a more homogeneous mixing between glassy and oxide phase as seen if Fig. 7 is compared to the ZS20 oxide layer in Fig. 5a or b. Homogeneous mixing of BS melt with ZrO2 could be detrimental for the system as diffusion through ZrO2 is much quicker than through a BS melt even if it has higher viscosity due to the presence of La2O3. La2O3 proximity to SiC promotes the inclusion of La2O3 into the BS phase, increasing viscosity and thermal stability as well as lowering the oxygen diffusion coefficient through the glassy phase. In fact, La2O3 is used as an additive to increase creep resistance of MoSi2, 45 which contains glassy SiO2 at grain boundaries and triple junctions. 2. Experimental details
ZrB2 (>99%, /SUB =6.085g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK) and SiC ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, /SUB =3.217g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) were used as starting materials to form base line ZrB2/20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed ZS20) UHTC. Rare earth (RE) additions in the form of La2O3 (>99%, /SUB =6.51g/cm 3 , Fluka, USA), LaB6 (>99%, /SUB =4.72g/cm 3 , Fluka, USA) and Gd2O3 (>99%, /SUB =7.41g/cm 3 , Fluka, USA) were added to the starting materials. UHTCsnamely,ZS/10wt.%La2O3 (ZSLO),ZS/10wt.%LaB6 (ZSLB)and ZS/10wt.%Gd2O3 (ZSGO)weredensifiedinasparkplasmasintering (SPS) furnace (FCT Systems, Germany) using the sintering parameters given in Table 1 [13]. Oxidation studies were performed for the sintered (99% dense) samples in a bottom loaded open hearth MoSi2 furnace by heating samples to 1600 ◦ C at a rate of 10 ◦ C/min, holding for 1 h and finally air-quenching to room temperature by lowering the specimen chamber.

2.1. Sintering procedure
ZrB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 2.5 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.085 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), HfB2 powder (>99%, d 50 ∼ 5.0 𝛍 m, ρ = 10.5 g/cm 3 , Sigma Aldrich, Gillingham, UK), SiC powder ( 𝛂 -SiC, 99%, d 50 ∼ 0.7 𝛍 m, ρ = 3.217 g/cm 3 , Good Fellow Chemicals, Huntingdon, UK) and La2O3 (>99%, d 50 ∼ 10 𝛍 m, ρ = 6.51 g/cm 3 , Fluka chemicals supplied through Sigma Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany) were used to form different UHTC compositions. ZrB2, HfB2 and 𝛂 -SiC (6H) have a hexagonal structure with lattice parameters of a = 3.17 ˚ A, c = 3.53 ˚ A, a = 3.139 ˚ A, c = 3.473 ˚ A and a = 3.08 ˚ A, c = 15.12 ˚ A, respectively. Powders with the following compositions were prepared, ZrB2, ZrB2+ 20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed ZS20), ZrB2+ 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (hereafter termed ZS20La), HfB2, HfB2+ 20 vol.% SiC (hereafter termed HS20), HfB2+ 20 vol.% SiC + 2 wt.% La2O3 (hereafter termed HS20La). They were wet ball milled in plastic containers using ZrO2 balls in ethanol for nearly 24 h and dried using a rotary evaporator under vacuum. Then, they were sintered in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace (Type HP D 25, FCT systeme GmbH, Rauenstein, Germany). A 40 mm graphite die was used and covered with graphite felt to reduce heat loss, the temperature was monitored and controlled by an optical pyrometer.

3.3.2. ZS20La
Reaction was complete or SiO2 was completely eliminated at a La2O3/SiO2 wt.% ratio of 3.2. 45 Two different polymorphs of La2Si2O7 were observed with a wt.% ratio of 6.4, which corresponds to a 4 wt.% La2O3/MoSi2 composites ( ∼ 5 vol.%). Therefore, in the present study La2O3 is completely dissolved in the La2O3-doped UHTCs BS melt for <5 vol.% La2O3content andprotective Me2La2O7 is not formed unless La2O3 concentration is at least 10 vol.%. 28 However, precipitates of La2O3-SiO2 phase were found on top of the oxide layer and identified with EDS in samples oxidized at 1500 ◦ C as observed in Fig. 7a indicated by the arrow, which confirms higher B2O3 volatilization at 1500 ◦ C than at 1400 ◦ C, facilitating the precipitation of La2O3-SiO2 particles. Furthermore, a completely new phenomena was observed on samples oxidized at 1600 ◦ C.
Fig. 6. SEM micrographs of oxide scale of ZS20 at 1600 ◦ C for a holding time of: (a) 1 h and (b) 2 h.
Fig. 7. SEM micrographs of oxide scale of ZS20La at 1500 ◦ C for a holding time of: (a) 8 h and (b) 16 h. Arrow indicates precipitation of La2O3-SiO2 particles.

2.1. Sintering procedure
A sintering temperature of 2000 ◦ C was used for monolithic ZrB2, HS20 and HS20La, 1950 ◦ C for ZS20 and ZS20La and 2100 ◦ C
for HfB2, a heating rate of 100 ◦ C/min was maintained with an applied load of 50 MPa for all compositions and holding times between 3 and 6 min. In addition, a load rate of 50 MPa/min was applied at 1300 ◦ C during SPS.

2.2 Optimization of oxidation resistance
Additionally, the oxidation resistance behavior has been shown to be further enhanced by the addition of silicides [51, 61]. Silvestronid et al . [64] studied ZrB2 composites doped with 15% (in volume) silicides (ZrSi2, MoSi2, TaSi2, WSi2) sintered (Fig. 2). Above 1500 ℃, ZrSi2/Wsi2 formed low-melting Zr-Si phases or WO3-ZrO2 eutectic, causing porous columnar ZrO2 scales with significant thickness of 120 μm for ZrB2- ZrSi2 and 135 μm for ZrB2-Wsi2 at 1650 ℃. TaSi2 generated TaZr2.75O8, which cracked due  to  TaB2  volatilization,  forming  a  500  μm  thick  oxide  scale  at  1650  ℃.MoSi2 outperformed  others via MoB  formation  and  viscous  SiO2  glass,  limiting  oxide thickness to 40 μm at 1800 ℃. MoSi2 stabilizes subsurface layers by suppressing gas escape and oxygen diffusion, while other silicides degrade via liquid phases or volatiles. This highlights MoSi2 as optimal for oxidation-resistant ZrB2 composites in extreme environments.  The  addition  of  rare  earth  materials  is  also  an  effective  method  to enhance the oxidation resistance of the ZrB2-SiC system. Jayaseelan et al . [65] added 10% (in mass) rare earth additives (LaB6, La2O3 and Gd2O3) to ZrB2-20% (in volume) SiC  composites  and  observed  the  formation  of  a  dense  layer  of  ZrO2  and  REzirconates(where RE = La and Gd in this study) after oxidation at 1600 ℃ for 1 h. Their study showed that the ZrO2 and RE-zirconates materials remained solid throughout the oxidation process, which helped to maintain the low oxygen permeability of liquid SiO2 and thus inhibited the coating oxidation process.

question: How does the electrical conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites compare to graphene-free ZrB2 when tested at room temperature?
answer: The electrical conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites, when tested at room temperature, is significantly higher than that of graphene-free ZrB2. Specifically, the conductivity of ZrB2-5 vol% graphene composites is a few orders of magnitude higher than the reference ZrB2 sample [14]. The exact value for ZrB2 at room temperature is 10.3x10^6 S/m [8], while for graphene composites, the electrical conductivity was observed to be in the range of 3.37*10^4 to 9.12*10^4 μS/m when tested parallel to the graphene alignment [3]. This represents a significant increase compared to the ZrB2 reference.

chunks.text:
4. Summary and conclusions
This  work investigates the  fabrication  of  ZrB2-SiC/graphene composites  without  sintering  aids  using  Spark  Plasma  Sintering  (SPS), focusing  on  densification,  mechanical  properties,  and  the  resulting anisotropy from graphene alignment.
Despite sintering curves suggesting full consolidation, relative density  measurements  and  SEM  observations  revealed  the  presence  of porosity,  indicating  that  sintering  activators  might  be  necessary  for ZrB2. Composites with SiC had agglomerates, while those with graphene showed partial decomposition of the graphene due to the high sintering temperature (2000 ◦ C).
Regarding  mechanical  properties,  all  composites  demonstrated higher hardness than the pure ZrB2  reference. The highest values for hardness  (1750  HV10)  and  fracture  toughness  (3.5  MPa  m 1 / 2 )  were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% of SiC. In graphene composites, hardness measured 1400 HV10 perpendicular and 1600 HV10 parallel to  alignment,  while  fracture  toughness  remained  low  (2.1 -2.3  MPa m 1 / 2 ).
All  composites  exhibited  superior  electrical  conductivity  than  the ZrB2 reference.  In  graphene  composites,  a  clear  anisotropy  was observed: electrical conductivity was significantly higher in the direction parallel to the graphene alignment (3.37*10 GLYPH<0> 4 to 9.12*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m), exceeding the reference sample by several orders of magnitude.

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
To identify the underlying mechanisms of temperature dependence and interfacial thermal conductance of graphene/ZrB2 composites, we inspected diffuse interface models with or without graphene at different temperatures in Figure 1. Previous theoretical calculations had illustrated that the Zr-terminated (0001) and (1010) surfaces were thermodynamically more favorable than other Zrterminated counterparts in ZrB 2 . 30 At high temperatures (below the melting point of the buck ZrB 2 ), disordered grain boundaries enhance phonon scattering and then generate an increased thermal resistance (Figure 1A). Graphene layers prepared by the exfoliated method are almost free of defects and grain boundaries. Extensive literature reported that exfoliated graphene layers with atomically thin tend to adhere to the ceramic grain boundary via vdWs force. 19 Given the fact that phonons are the main heat carriers of graphene and ceramic materials, the electronic thermal conductivity can be negligible in graphene and ZrB2 materials. 31 The rough edges of 2D materials, such as graphene, and its interlayers are typi- cally much less sensitive to phonon scattering than temperature fluctuation. 32 The graphene heterostructure with or without defects has almost the same temperature jump ∆ T at the heterogeneous interface. 17 That is, graphene layers with or without intrinsic defects (or crystal boundaries or rough edges) expand nearly the same amount with the same temperature fluctuation. So we dislodge phonon scattering from defect scattering and rough edges of graphene layers. The vdWs force between graphene and ZrB2 ceramic particles produces perturbation of the atomic bonding in the graphene near the ZrB2 /graphene/ZrB2 heterogeneous interface. This short-range vdWs interaction only affects the graphene layers (1 # , 3 # ) adjacent to the ZrB2 grain boundaries, which is consistent with the reported literature.

3. Results and discussion
Results of electrical conductivity for produced composites are presented in Fig. 9. Due to the low value (6.38*10 GLYPH<0> 7 μ S/m) and readability of the figure (Fig. 9), the bar for the reference sample is not shown. For all  composites,  measured  values  are  higher  than  for  pure  zirconium diboride specimen, but vary one to another depending on the series. Sinters  with  SiC  presented  just  a  slight  increase,  to  9.86*10 GLYPH<0> 6 -3.19

In  the  case  of  graphene,  for  both  series  the  measured  electrical  conductivity  was  higher  than  for  SiC  composites,  and  clear  differences depending on the direction were also observed. Examination performed perpendicular to alignment revealed values in the range of 6.44*10 GLYPH<0> 5 to 1.22*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m (for 2 vol% addition). On the other hand, the highest electrical conductivity was measured for the series with Gn parallel to alignment, with values in the range of 3.37*10 GLYPH<0> 4 to 9.12*10 GLYPH<0> 4 μ S/m (once again for 2 vol% addition). 3. Results and discussion
Fig. 8. Influence of SiC/Gn addition on fracture toughness of produced ZrB2 composites.
Fig.  9. Influence  of  SiC/Gn  addition  on  electrical  conductivity  of  produced ZrB2  composites.
it is a few order of magnitude increase when compared to the reference specimen. The results show a clear anisotropy of electrical conductivity, which  is  a  direct  consequence  of  the  alignment  of  graphene  flakes perpendicular to the pressing direction in the SPS furnace. During the SPS process, the uniaxial pressing causes the graphene flakes, which have  a  high  aspect  ratio,  to  orient  themselves  perpendicular  to  the pressing direction. This creates a texture, that facilitates the easy flow of electrons parallel to the flakes, resulting in higher electrical conductivity. At the same time, in the direction perpendicular to the flakes, current flow is hindered due to the higher resistance of the ceramic matrix, leading to a distinctly lower conductivity. This microstructural alignment is therefore the key mechanism responsible for the anisotropy of electrical properties in the tested composites. This shows that graphene, with its exceptional electrical resistivity, is an ideal material for use in composites where it is important to improve electrical properties and maintain high mechanical properties.
Fig. 10 presents XRD patterns for composites with 30 vol% SiC and 2 vol% Gn. A thoroughly analysis of the phase composition, showed only the presence of zirconium diboride and silicon carbide. No new phases were detected during the performed examination. It should be noted, however, that for the adopted X-ray diffraction device, the detection threshold is roughly 2 %.

KEYWORDS
bonding configurations, ceramic composites, heat transfer, ordered graphene, thermal converger
dynamic flow of the high-speed aircraft surface, generates a vast chasm between rapid energy-dissipation requirement and ultrahigh thermal conductance. Graphene has an ultrahigh in-plane thermal conductivity of more than 5 × 10 3 W ∙ m -1 ∙ K -1 , which is higher than that of metal and graphite. 2 The concurrent superiority of scalability, large surface-to-volume ratio, and high-temperature stability enables graphene to be the best heat reinforcement of UHTCs. 3 The excellent features of dispersion and assembly further make it easy to be incorporated into the UHTCs matrix. These exotic properties ignite strong interest in accelerating the heat transfer of ZrB 2 composites by constructing external heat-dissipation channels with graphene at ZrB2 grain boundaries. Yet graphene layers were either exfoliated from imperfect graphite powder or grown by chemical vapor deposition. 4 The in-plane thermal conductivity of embedded graphene layers, owing to the phonon Umklapp scattering of a heterogenous interface, decreasing from ∼ 10 2 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at room temperature to ∼ 10 1 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at high temperature. 5 Many worksofliterature have reported that the universal phonon scattering of heterogenous interface and Kapitza thermal resistance of graphene layers cause a reversed correlation between thermal conductance and temperature. 6,7 Interestingly, few researchers have found that the thermal conductivity of ZrB 2 composites with or without graphene reduces the diverse amount with the same temperature fluctuation. 5,8 Therefore, the underlying mechanisms behind the temperature-dependent heat transfer deterioration as the highly anisotropic phonon transport of embedded graphene layers remain elusive.

2. Thermal properties of graphene/ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites
Graphene is a promising conductive material with a carrier mobility of up to 1.5 × 10 4 cm 2 /(V s) and thermal conductivity of up to 5.3 × 10 3 W/(m K) at room temperature, making it an ideal thermoelectric material. Graphene is used to enhance the thermal conductivity of the matrix, accelerate heat loss in the local superheated area, and improve its service life.
Simonenko et al. incorporated 2% graphene into HfB2-30% (volume ratio) SiC, and its thermal conductivity increased from 113 to 164 W/(m K), which is an increase of 45%. 53 Cheng et al. found that in a ZrB2-SiC/graphene composite system, the thermal diffusion coefficient, thermal conductivity, and specific heat coefficient significantly improved after the addition of graphene. 54 Excessive addition not only causes the thermal conductivity of the material to decrease, mainly because the graphene itself will aggregate, resulting in a decrease in thermal conductivity, but also causes the grain size of the ceramic matrix to decrease and the porosity to increase.
Graphene is an excellent thermal conductivity interface that can form a good thermal conductivity interface in the ceramic matrix and build an efficient 3D heat transfer channel, thereby improving thermal conductivity. In addition, the introduction of graphene provides a more efficient method for phonon transport, thus improving thermal conductivity.

A B S T R A C T
As the total thermal conduction in diborides comprises both electronic and phononic component,   electrical conductivity ( σ e) at room temperature  has  also  been  assessed.  The  electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck coefficient (S) of the HfB2-ZrB2-based composites was measured in the He atmosphere at room temperature using ZEM-3M10 apparatus (ULVAC-RICO Inc.). The figure of  merit  ( ZT = S 2 σ eT κ )  was  calculated, Table  2,  from  thermoelectric  parameters  (electrical  conductivity  and Seebeck  coefficient).  Seebeck  coefficient  is  observed  to  be  negative, suggesting n-type semiconductor behavior, with electrons to be dominant charge carriers in all the composites, and following similar trend of electrical conductivity at room temperature with maximum of -3.57 V/K observed for HZ20S6C. The power factor is ~6 × 10 -5 Wm -1 K 2 at room temperature for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C composites (Table 2) except for H20S (where the presence of HfB2  phase dominates). On the other hand, ZT value for Z20S is ~43 times to that of H20S. After incorporation of CNT, all thermoelectric parameters in HZ20S6C are observed to increase ~4 -6% as compared to that of HZ20S. Low ZT value in ~1 × 10 -4 Wm -1 K -1 range (due to high κ ) for these composites is not sufficient for thermoelectric applications at room temperature.

3.2 The bonding configurations of ZrB2/graphene/ZrB2 heterostructure
Research results have shown that interfacial thermal conductance is strongly influenced by interface bonding, and the existence of a saturated bonding rate can make the interface thermal conductance reach the predicted value in the acoustic mismatch model. 47 Therefore, the interface thermal conductance can be controlled by adjusting the Zr-C interfacial bonding rate below the saturated interfacial bonding ratio. Only six interfacial configurations with different concentrations of Zr-C interfacial bonding (1-6 vol.%) are constructed for each interface. A critical consideration is the graphene layers' thermal expansion coefficient (TEC), should match that of the ZrB 2 ceramic that it contacts. The in-plane thermal expansion coefficient of graphene (ranges from negative values to 1.2 × 10 -5 K -1 ) is ∼ 2 times that of ZrB 2 (5.9 × 10 -6 K -1 ) at room temperature. 48,49 Andthermal expansion coefficient depends on the interfacial interactions with heterogeneous materials. Interlayer phonon scattering of graphene reduces the in-plane thermal conductivity to approach the value of graphite when the thickness is increased to only about four layers. 50 So three-layer graphene was embedded into the ZrB 2 matrix to investigate the correlation between interlayer phonon scattering and interfacial thermal resistance. Owing to the outer 1 # and 3 # layers being covalent bonding while the inner 2 # layer is held by vdWs force, the expansion of each graphene layer is different. The interfacial thermal resistance of ZrB 2 /graphene plays a leading role in hindering thermal transport at low volume concentrations of graphene, while graphene-graphene (1 # /2 # or 2 # /3 # ) contact resistance becomes dominant in hindering the thermal transport at high content.

A B S T R A C T
The  experimental  electrical  conductivity  at  room  temperature  is maximum for Z20S (~5.69 × 10 6 Sm -1 ), which is ~ 2.85 times (~1.99 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) to that of H20S owing to inherently high electrical conductivity of  pure ZrB2 (~1.0 × 10 7 Sm -1 [8,9]) than HfB2 (~9.1 × 10 6 Sm -1 [9]). However, SiC is the least electrically conducting phase (~10 2 Sm -1 [9]) in all the four compositions, thus, acting as electronic scattering centers. The σ e is comparable (~5.6 -5.7 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) for Z20S, HZ20S and HZ20S6C (~0.01 × 10 6 Sm -1 ). To obtain complex electron interaction in HZ20S and HZ20S6C, the effects of porosity and grain size are normalized where on the other hand the formation of solid solution is taken into consideration which restricts the electron motion due to the hindrance by intermixed electrons of Hf/ZrB2  [31] . Thus, a slight increase in electrical conductivity (~5%) of HZ20S is a combined effect of solid solution formation and presence of porosity ( < 4%). The electrical conductivity further increases ~4% (~5.72 × 10 6 Sm -1 ) on addition of CNT ( σ e~10 4 Sm -1 ) to HZ20S.

A few studies have been carried out to estimate the thermal conductivity of ZrB2 at room temperature and at elevated temperatures (till 2000 ◦ C), yet no clear trend of increasing or decreasing thermal conductivity with temperature is observed. Samsonov et al. [167] reported thermal conductivity of 60 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 for ZrB2 at 1000 ◦ C, which steadily increased to 130 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 2000 ◦ C while as Branscomb and Hunter estimated a value of 105 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 200 ◦ C which decreased to 91 Wm GLYPH<0> 1 K GLYPH<0> 1 at 1000 ◦ C [168]. This discrepancy in thermal conductivity is probably due to the dependence of thermal diffusivity, which is the most important parameter in calculating thermal conductivity indirectly, on factors like composition, grain size, contact resistance between grains, porosity and purity etc. [61] Thus, a consensus on the intrinsic thermal conductivity value of ZrB2 is actually missing.

99.5 ZrB2/SiCp, 99 SiCw/Cg
Thompson et al . [20] and Andrievskii et al . [11] also investigated ZrB 2 with carbon additions. The k values increased with increasing temperature, indicating that the diboride was  the  primary  influence  on k .  For  Andrievskii et  al .,  26  vol%  carbon  was  added  as graphite, which reduced the conductivity approximately 10  W/m-K at all temperatures [11]. Thompson et al . added carbon in the form of phenolic resin, resulting in 1 and 3  wt% amorphous carbon after pyrolysis. After hot pressing, graphitic carbon was identified at the grain boundaries. ZrB 2 containing 1  wt% C had k values lower than pure ZrB 2 . They hypothesized that graphite was aligned, with its basal plane parallel to the grain boundaries, thus disrupting phonon transport between ZrB 2 grains. However, k did not continue to decrease when C content was increased to 3  wt% C due to the segregation of W into ZrC after testing up to 2000°C. The ZrC phase, produced due to carbon additions, served as a sink for W, reducing the W content of the ZrB 2 grains and boosting the overall k of the composite [20].
The addition of ZrC to ZrB 2 typically reduces k . Andrievskii et al . evaluated both 5 and 50 vol% ZrC from 100 to 900°C, while Fridlender et al . added 20, 40, and 45 vol% ZrC and tested up to 2150 or 2200°C. The higher ZrC additions resulted in k values between 40 and 55  W/m-K, while the lower additions resulted in k values between 64 and 80  W/m-K, despite the different testing temperatures and compositions. The differences, and the decrease in k with increasing ZrC content, were probably due to the variability in k values for ZrC (20-45  W/m-K at room temperature and increasing to 30-50  W/m-K at 2000°C), which are well below the values for pure ZrB 2 [70].

A B S T R A C T
This  study  presents  new  findings  on  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  composites,  where  silicon  carbide  and graphene were incorporated into a zirconium diboride matrix. The research focused on the densification process, and the mechanical and electrical properties of the produced materials. A novel aspect of this work was the investigation of the influence of graphene alignment on composite properties. The results indicated that despite SPS curves suggested full consolidation, the final relative density was lower than expected, and SEM observations revealed the presence of pores in the microstructure. This suggests that additional sintering activators may be necessary to achieve full densification of ZrB2-based materials. Furthermore, high consolidation temperature (2000 ◦ C)  caused  partial  decomposition  of  graphene,  with  only  some  flakes  preserving  their  structure.  Agglomerates of silicon carbide particles were also observed in SiC composites. Regarding mechanical properties, all composites showed a significant increase in hardness compared to pure ZrB2. The highest hardness (1750 HV10) and fracture toughness (3.5 MPa m 1 / 2 ) were achieved for the composite with 40 vol% SiC. For electrical properties, all composites showed enhanced conductivity than the reference ZrB2 sample. Notably, the graphene composites exhibited a clear anisotropy. Specimens tested parallel to the graphene alignment showed a conductivity increase of a few orders of magnitude. In conclusion, this study demonstrates that SPS is an effective method for consolidating ZrB2-based composites without sintering aids, leading to significant improvements in hardness and electrical conductivity. However, achieving full densification may require the use of activators. The observed anisotropy of electrical properties in graphene composites highlights their potential for applications where directional conductivity is desired. This work opens new paths for better understanding ultra-high temperature ceramic-based composites.

9.1. Thermal expansion, thermal conductivity and electrical conductivity
In a different work, the electrical conductivity of ZrB2 composite decreased with increasing SiC, since SiC exhibits higher electrical resistivity compared to ZrB2 [287]. Furthermore, SiC (up to 20 vol%) containing ZrB2 exhibits still better electrical conductivity. On a slightly different perspective, He et al. [371] studied the effect of ZrB2-20SiC content on the electrical and mechanical properties of MoSi2. Their work indicated that the electrical resistivity of MoSi2 decreased with increase in the ZrB2-20SiC content; which again highlights the conductivity aspect of ZrB2-SiC ceramic composites. Overall, it has been realized that incorporation of optimized contents of disilicides and carbides to transition metal di-borides helps improve the conductivity of transition metal borides [137,147,372].

3.2 The bonding configurations of ZrB2/graphene/ZrB2 heterostructure
The thermal conductance of ZrB2 ceramics can be improved compared to that of the two ZrB 2 grain particles by sandwiching an intermediate graphene layer between them with a pre-designed array. The heat transfer of embedded graphene layers is limited by the phonon transmission from the top and bottom ZrB2 slabs, which is also unavoidably constrained by the edge scattering effect. To further investigate the effect of Zr-C interfacial bonding on the interfacial thermal conductance, the PDOS of ZrB2 , graphene in sandwich-like structure with vdWs force and 6% Zr-C interfacial bonding at 700 K are exhibited in Figure 6 and Figures S5 and S6. The PDOS of pure ZrB 2 and graphene are extremely different, while the sandwichlike structure has intermediate PDOS, which serves to bridge the gap between ZrB2 and graphene. Since the atomic ratio of bonding configurations involved in Zr up C 1# and Zr down -C 3# models is 1: 1, the only difference being the positions of Zr-C interfacial bonding. There are two influencing factors: (1) contact thermal conductance at the graphene 1# /graphene 2# or graphene 2# /graphene 3# interface which reduces transmission via vdWs forces and (2) overlapping PDOS which increases transmission at the ZrB2 /graphene 1# and graphene 3# /ZrB 2 interface via Zr-C interfacial bonding.

A B S T R A C T
In this study, functionally graded ZrB2 -B4C -SiC -LaB6 composite materials (FGMs) with potential applications in hypersonic aircraft thermal protection systems were fabricated using spark plasma sintering. A systematic study of the thermal conductivity of the FGM, the conductivity of respective FGM layers, and the equivalent nongraded composites, was performed from room temperature up to 450 ◦ C. The results suggest that the thermal conductivity of the FGMs (in the through-thickness direction) and their equivalent non-graded composites ranged between 25 and 34.9 W/mK, which was ~60 % less than ZrB2. While the overall thermal conductivity of the FGM and equivalent non-graded composites were similar, in the FGM, the topmost layer with high ZrB2content displayed up to 245 % higher thermal conductivity than the bottom layer with high B4C content. A systematic comparison between experimentally determined conductivity and relevant thermal conductivity models was conducted.

question: What densification results are achieved for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composites sintered at 1800°C using spark plasma sintering?
answer: The densification results for ZrB2-20 vol% SiC composites sintered at 1800°C using spark plasma sintering (SPS) achieve a relative density of 99.6% [3].

chunks.text:
4.4. Spark Plasma Sintering
In the case of spark plasma sintering (SPS) of ZrB2 ceramics, the effect of processing parameters (sintering time and sintering temperature) and powder particle size (nano- or micron- sized) on the densification has been shown in Fig. 7b [120]. It can be noted that the densification rate is extremely high for the smaller particle size (≤25 nm) and near-theoretical densification of ZrB2 is attained even at very low sintering temperatures (below 1700 °C) and shorter sintering times of 16 min or lesser. The spark plasma sintered di-borides are usually observed to possess fine microstructure and good combination of properties. In order to address some issues related to non-uniform densification (and properties) primarily due to fast heating rate and lesser holding times, recently we had explored the application of multi-stage spark plasma sintering (MSS-SPS) schedule, which involves holding at one or more intermediate temperatures for few minutes before reaching the final sintering temperatures [98,173-179]. Keeping the total holding time constant (i.e. lesser holding time at the final sintering temperature), more uniform densification and finer microstructures were achieved for ZrB2-SiC-TiSi2 [175] and TiB2-TiSi2 composites [98], resulting in still improved mechanical properties (as compared to similar compositions sintered via the usual single-step SPS route).

a b s t r a c t
In this paper spark plasma sintering (SPS) of silicon carbide and multi-walled carbon nanotube reinforced zirconium diboride ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites is reported. Systematic investigations on the effect of reinforcement type (SiC and CNTs) and content (10-40 vol.% SiC and 2-6 vol.% CNTs) on densification behavior, microstructure development, and mechanical properties (microhardness, biaxial flexural strength, and indentation fracture toughness) are presented. With the similar SPS processing parameters (1900 ◦ C, 70 MPa pressure, and 15 min soaking time), near-full densification (>99% relative density) was achieved with 10-40% SiC (in ZrB2-SiC) and 4-6% CNT (in ZrB2-CNT) reinforced composites. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particlematrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

1. Introduction
Spark plasma sintering (SPS) route was employed for preparation of quadruplet ZrB2 -SiC -ZrC -Cf ultrahigh temperature ceramic matrix composites (UHTCMC). Zirconium diboride and silicon carbide powders with a constant ZrB2:SiC volume ratio of 4:1 were selected as the baseline. Mixtures of ZrB2 -SiC were co-reinforced with zirconium carbide (ZrC: 0 -10 vol%) and carbon /uniFB01 ber (Cf: 0 -5 vol%), taking into account a constant ratio of 2:1 for ZrC:Cf components. The sintered composite samples, processed at 1800 °C for 5 min and 30 MPa punch press under vacuumed atmosphere, were characterized by densitometry, /uniFB01 eld emission scanning electron microscopy, energy dispersive spectroscopy, X-ray di /uniFB00 ractometry as well as mechanical tests such as hardness and /uniFB02 exural strength measurements. The results veri /uniFB01 ed that the composite co-reinforced with 5 vol% ZrC and 2.5 vol% Cf had the optimal characteristics, i.e., it reached a relative density of 99.6%, a hardness of 18 GPa and a /uniFB02 exural strength of 565 MPa.
less-volatile borosilicate glasses, but will also promote the mechanical properties [23,24].
It is established that adding 20 vol% SiC to ZrB2 will result in achievement of optimal high-temperature thermo-mechanical application for ZrB2 -SiC composites [25]. Overally, excellent densi /uniFB01 cation behavior, oxidation and thermal shock resistance, because of the smaller coe /uniFB03 cient of thermal expansion compared to ZrB2 and ZrC, make silicon carbide one of the most important materials for making e /uniFB03 cient composites with ZrB2 [26,27].

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering processing was used to produce dense short /uniFB01 bre-reinforced ceramic composites based on ZrB2 with a carbon /uniFB01 bres content of 45 vol%. The relative density of 96.7%, achieved for samples sintered at 1900 °C, is signi /uniFB01 cantly higher than analogous specimens produced by conventional hot pressing, which achieved only 85% of the theoretical density. Careful control of the sintering parameters, such as the heating rate, maximum temperature and holding time resulted in materials with near full density and no appreciable damage of the carbon /uniFB01 bres.
- It has also been assessed that above a critical temperature (> 1900 °C), set by the eutectic melt met in the ZrB2-SiC-C system, extreme /uniFB01 bre degradation occurs, with associated brittle behaviour of the composite. It has also been observed that slight deviations in the density level have a strong impact on the fracture behaviour.
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at http://dx. doi.org/10.1016/j.matdes.2017.05.029.

4. Conclusions
Spark plasma sintering was successfully used to sinter fine-grained ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composites to near-full densification (>99% relative density). Detailed analysis of punch displacement during SPS sintering indicated that the densification is favored for the ZrB2-SiC and ZrB2-CNT composite samples in early stage I (particle re-distribution stage) and final stage III (densification starting at earlier times and at lower temperatures). While the fine grains (<2 𝛍 m) were retained in ZrB2-SiC composites, some grain growth with grain size in the range 5-10 𝛍 m was observed for ZrB2-CNT composites. Raman spectroscopy analysis and high magnification SEM micrographs indicated that the multi-walled CNTs were retained in ZrB2-CNT composite samples sintered at high sintering temperature of 1900 ◦ C. The SiC and CNT reinforcement further improved the indentation fracture toughness of the composites through a range of toughening mechanisms, including particle shearing, crack deflection at the particle-matrix interface, and grain pull-outs for ZrB2-SiC composites, and CNT pull-outs and crack deflection in ZrB2-CNT composites.

1. Introduction
Although finding an appropriate composition is one of the most critical challenges for achieving the best relative density, hardness, and flexural strength, selecting a proper sintering process and its parameters is another essential issue. ZrB2-20 vol% SiC has been studied as a base composition with a ZrB2: SiC volume ratio of 4:1 and a ZrC: Cf volume ratio of 2:1. Optimum densification and mechanical characteristics, 99.6% relative density, 18.1 GPa hardness and 564.5 MPa flexural strength, have been reported for ZrB2-18.5 vol% SiC-5.0 vol% ZrC-2.5 vol% Cf composite [26]. Among several sintering processes such as spark plasma sintering (SPS) [27-33], pressureless sintering (PS) [34], hot pressing (HP) [35-37], and reactive hot pressing (RHP) [38,39], samples produced by the SPS process were found to have relatively higher mechanical characteristics [40-45]. Because of strong bonding in carbides and borides, it is difficult to achieve superb sintering characteristics by conventional sintering processes [46-50]. 4.2.2. Mechanical behavior
Incorporation  of  5  wt  %  C3N4 in  ZrB2-25  vol  %  SiC  composite resulted in an enhanced densification (from 95% to fully dense) during spark plasma sintering [145]. The removal of oxide impurities by C3N4 not only improved densification, but also refined the microstructure as well, yielding better mechanical properties while forming in situ BN and ZrC [145]. Vickers hardness increased from ~17 GPa to ~21 GPa and flexural strength also enhanced from ~460 MPa to ~580 MPa. Apart from achieving an enhanced densification, in situ formation of BN and ZrC yielded higher fracture toughness due to crack deflection and crack bridging  mechanisms  (6.1  MPam 1/2 as  compared  to  that  of  4.5 MPam 1/2 )  [145].  10  vol  %  CNT  and  20  vol  %  SiC  reinforcement increased the flexural strength of ZrB2  (i.e., from 322.8 MPa) to 347.6 MPa and 378.7 MPa, respectively. question: How does the thermal conductivity of TaB2-SiC-graphene composites change over a temperature range of 300-1500°C?
answer: The provided sources do not contain specific information regarding the thermal conductivity of TaB2-SiC-graphene composites over the temperature range of 300-1500°C. However, based on the available information about similar composites, thermal conductivity generally decreases with increasing temperature due to increased phonon scattering [3]. For instance, the thermal conductivity of ZrB2-SiC composites was observed to decrease with increasing temperature [3], and similar behavior is expected for TaB2-SiC-graphene composites. Additionally, the addition of graphene can enhance thermal conductivity, but excessive addition may lead to a decrease due to graphene aggregation [1]. The exact change in thermal conductivity for TaB2-SiC-graphene composites over the specified temperature range is not covered in the provided sources.

2. Thermal properties of graphene/ultra-high temperature ceramic matrix composites
Graphene is a promising conductive material with a carrier mobility of up to 1.5 × 10 4 cm 2 /(V s) and thermal conductivity of up to 5.3 × 10 3 W/(m K) at room temperature, making it an ideal thermoelectric material. Graphene is used to enhance the thermal conductivity of the matrix, accelerate heat loss in the local superheated area, and improve its service life.
Simonenko et al. incorporated 2% graphene into HfB2-30% (volume ratio) SiC, and its thermal conductivity increased from 113 to 164 W/(m K), which is an increase of 45%. 53 Cheng et al. found that in a ZrB2-SiC/graphene composite system, the thermal diffusion coefficient, thermal conductivity, and specific heat coefficient significantly improved after the addition of graphene. 54 Excessive addition not only causes the thermal conductivity of the material to decrease, mainly because the graphene itself will aggregate, resulting in a decrease in thermal conductivity, but also causes the grain size of the ceramic matrix to decrease and the porosity to increase.
Graphene is an excellent thermal conductivity interface that can form a good thermal conductivity interface in the ceramic matrix and build an efficient 3D heat transfer channel, thereby improving thermal conductivity. In addition, the introduction of graphene provides a more efficient method for phonon transport, thus improving thermal conductivity.

7.2.1.  Thermogravimetric analysis and wide-temperature-range oxidation

[98] investigated the effect of volume ratios between HfB2 and TaB2 on the oxidation resistance of HfB2-MoSi2-TaB2 coatings. Over a temperature range of 300-1700 °C, the optimized coating containing 15 vol% TaB2 extended the initial oxidation temperature from 300 °C to 500 °C, reducing dynamic oxidation loss by 75.85% compared to the 60HfB2-40MoSi2 coating. Zhu et al. [188] densified a porous SiC-ZrSi2 pre-coating  using  in-situ  generated  SiO2 glass,  producing  a  gradient composite coating with a dense outer layer and a microporous intermediate layer. During dynamic oxidation testing from 573 K to 1773 K, the gradient coating demonstrated enhanced mass retention, improving from 62.9% (pure SiC coating) to 97.94%.
Fig.  36. Oxidation  curve  of  SiC/glaze  precursor-coated  samples  in  air  from room temperature to 1600 °C. Reproduced with permission from Ref. [184], © Elsevier 2017.
The  existing  thermogravimetric  and  wide  temperature  range  oxidation tests have verified the nonlinear relationship between the selfhealing ability of coating oxide films under temperature gradients and temperature. However, the linear temperature change mode is difficult to  reproduce  the  transient  competition  mechanism  of  thermal  shock and oxidation coupling during space reentry, which limits the accuracy of  predicting  the  oxygen  diffusion  path  of  coatings  under  extreme thermal gradients.

3.2. Thermal and electrical properties
Fig. 7(b) shows that the overall thermal conductivity of all composites decreased with increasing temperature. Previous literature on ZrB2-based composites has also reported similar temperature dependence of thermal conductivity [5,6,29,30,43]. Due to the presence of strong covalent and ionic bonding, the thermal conductivity of B4C and SiC is mainly controlled by the interaction of phonons with other phonons, defects, and grain boundaries. As temperature increases, thermal conductivity decreases due to increased phonon scattering [5,6]. For ZrB2, the thermal and electrical conductivity are mainly governed by electronic conduction [44]. As temperature increases, conductivity decreases due to the scattering of conducting electrons by lattice vibrations [45].
Fig. 7(c) presents the variation of specific heat capacity with temperature for all compositions. The results suggest a consistent trend of increasing specific heat capacity with B4C volume fraction, aligning well with findings in the published literature [6,30]. The results reveal that the specific heat capacity of the bottom-most layer was the highest, which has practical implications for in-service conditions. When heat is transferred from the upper layers to this bottom layer, its temperature rise remains comparatively low, thereby protecting the metallic structure directly underneath the FGM. Although the overall specific heat capacity values of the FGMs were almost similar to their corresponding equivalent non-graded compositions, the FGM design has the advantage of allowing a layerwise variation in the specific heat capacity, not achievable in non-graded composites.

A B S T R A C T
The most crucial goal is the improvement of Ultra High Temperature Ceramic (UHTC) fracture toughness without negatively impacting the ceramic high-temperature properties. Carbon nanostructures in UHTCs appear to be one of the most promising ways forward. The influence of reduced graphene oxide vs. carbon black on microstructure and properties of TiB2-SiC ceramics is studied. The composite ceramic samples with additive contents up to 7.4 wt% were manufactured via the reactive hot pressing of B4C-TiC-Si-reduced graphene oxide/carbon black precursors at 1850 ◦ С and 30 MPa for 4 min. The sintered materials composition and structure were analysed  with  Raman  spectroscopy,  XRD,  SEM,  and  TEM.  Reduced  graphene  oxide  (rGO)  survives  the  hotpressing procedure and, at lower concentrations, distributes inside the TiB2-SiC matrix in the form of separate sheets. However, at 7.4 wt%, the rGO was shown to agglomerate. The rGO improved the hardness, fracture toughness, and thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC by up to 16%, 20%, and 53%, respectively. The composite characteristics reached the values of HV = 26.1 GPa, K1 C = 7.4 MPa · m 1/2 , and k = 69 W/m · K. Carbon black additive formed roughly equiaxial two micrometre inclusions and was not observed to impact the investigated properties.
the TiB2 matrix resulted in a material hardness of 19.4 GPa, toughness of 6.9 MPa ⋅ m 1/2 , and flexural strength of 797 MPa. Zhou et al. [10] showed an enhancement of SiC high-temperature tribological properties with an addition  of  20%  of  TiB2.  Sintering  to  high  density  is  still  very  challenging. We previously showed that reactive hot pressing produces high composite density at lowered temperatures and shorter processing times (see references below).

3.3. Characterization of sintered materials
There  are  a  number  of  papers  presenting  research  on  graphene ' s influence on TiB2-SiC [31,33,38,39]. Koval ˇ cíkov ´ a et al. [33] presented the mechanical properties of TiB2-SiC-based materials containing 1, 2, 5, and 10 wt% of graphene. TiB2-SiC matrix in [33] had slightly higher hardness and lower toughness. The toughness dependence on graphene content dependence presented in Fig. 10 for comparison is similar to that for rGO in the current work. In contradiction, the material hardness in [33] showed a monotonical decrease resembling carbon black-containing composites.
Fig. 7. SEM micrographs of the fracture surfaces of TiB2-SiC-based composites: a) reference (TS00); b,d) 3.8 wt% rGO (TSG03); c,e) 3.8 wt% carbon black (TSC03). The marked red areas in b) and c) are shown in d) and e), respectively. Carbon sheets are indicated with red arrows.
Fig. 8. SEM micrograph of the fracture surface of the 7.4 wt% rGO composite (TSG04), showing rGO agglomerates.
The carbon black presence seems to have minimal impact on the thermal conductivity of the TiB2-SiC matrix (see Fig. 11). The rGO additive, however, leads up to a 50% thermal conductivity coefficient rise at  3.8 wt%  as  a  peak  value.  At  higher  rGO  concentrations,  thermal conductivity slumps. The latter, again, could be attributed to the rGO sheet agglomeration at higher carbon contents.

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
We examine the PDOS mismatch of ZrB2/graphene 1# ( J + direction) and graphene 3# /ZrB 2 interface ( J -direction) in Figure 3 and Figure S4. PDOS of C atoms in graphene 1# and graphene 3# depict that the PDOS of C atoms in graphene are extremely close to that of reported work, 7 suggesting the negligible contributions from vdWs force at ZrB 2 /graphene 1# heterogeneous interface. The mean free path of phonon scattering in the basal plane of graphene depends on ω and T according to l i ∼ T -1 ⋅ ω -2 . 39 The low-frequency acoustic phonons of graphene make larger contributions to the thermal conductivity than high-frequency phonons at 700 K. The PDOS of the ZrB2 and graphene 1# domains match well at low frequency ( < 25 THz) in Figure 3A-C. Additionally, the PDOS are slightly staggered in the J -direction from graphene 3# to ZrB 2 , which hinders the transmission of lattice vibrational energy across the graphene 3# /ZrB 2 interface in Figure S4. We further calculate PDOS overlap ( S ) to quantify the matching of the phonon bands. The overlap area S + at graphene 1# /ZrB 2 ( J + direction) and S -at ZrB2 /graphene 3# interface ( J -direction) are 41.7 and 41.3 at 300 K, respectively. These values interpret that the amount of overlap between the phonon spectra of the two sides of the ZrB 2 /graphene/ZrB 2 heterostructure changes with temperature. It has been commonly used to elucidate that a larger PDOS overlap corresponds to a higher interfacial thermal conductance across heterogeneous interfaces.

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
The regions (2 unit cell long) close to both source and sink Langevin reservoirs are eliminated to avoid the nonphysical effect. Multi-layer graphene has strong CC bonding and weak vdWs coupling between planes. 2 These high-quality graphene layers adhere to the ZrB2 grain boundary with minimum energy dissipation to completely relax and accommodate the strain. The mismatch effect of the thermal expansion coefficient is retarded after high-temperature reduction. The heterostructure model treated the ZrB 2 /graphene coupling as a weak vdWs-type interaction in Figure 1C.
The temperature profiles in the ZrB 2 grain boundary and ZrB2 /graphene/ZrB 2 heterostructure at 300 K are shown in Figure 1B,D. A rapid and discontinuous drop in temperature occurred across the ZrB 2 grain boundary or ZrB 2 /graphene/ZrB 2 heterostructure. The temperature distributions at the non-equilibrium steady state are timedependent. The time-dependent heat flux density and the total heat flow are the same within these sections. The low-temperature gradient in ZrB 2 originates from its high thermal conductivity. Due to the large difference in the lattice parameters in grain boundary or heterogeneous interface, an apparent temperature jump ∆ T indicates interfacial thermal resistance. Their interfacial thermal conductance ( λ ) and contact thermal conductance ( R c ) can be expressed by Gλ,Rc = J /∆ T , where J is defined as the amount of thermal transfer per unit area per unit time across grain boundary or heterogeneous interface. And J can be expressed by 35 :

3.1 Diffuse interface model with or without graphene
15512916, 2025, 3, Downloaded from https://ceramics.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/jace.20253 by UNIVERSITY OF CINCINNATI, Wiley Online Library on [06/11/2025]. See the Terms and Conditions (https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/terms-and-conditions) on Wiley Online Library for rules of use; OA articles are governe In order to investigate the physical mechanisms behind temperature-dependent thermal rectification, we created a schematic diagram of the ZrB2 /graphene/ZrB 2 heterostructure divided into three segments of pure ZrB2 materials (I and III) and heterogeneous interface (II). This thermal rectification in Figure 2C shows that heat preferentially flows from the ZrB2 domain to the graphene domain. The physical mechanisms of the thermal rectification in buck materials (ZrB 2 ) and graphene are the dependence of the thermal conductivity on temperature and location. When the length of graphene is smaller than the phonon mean free path (0.1-100 µ m), the local energies at the heat source and sink are dependent on each other, and then the thermal rectification appears. The net heat current due to the temperature bias ∆ T is calculated as /u1D43D = (/u1D715/u1D438 Hot ∕/u1D715/u1D447 +/u1D715/u1D438 c/u1D45C/u1D459 /u1D451 ∕/u1D715/u1D447)∕2 , where E Hot and E Cold are the total energy in the heat source and sink, respectively. It illustrates that thermal rectification can be initiated at high ∆ T . The pink and blue dots in Figure 2D. depict the interfacial thermal conductance in both ZrB 2 /graphene 1# (the J + direction) and graphene 3# /ZrB 2 (the J -direction) interface, respectively.

3.2 The bonding configurations of ZrB2/graphene/ZrB2 heterostructure
Research results have shown that interfacial thermal conductance is strongly influenced by interface bonding, and the existence of a saturated bonding rate can make the interface thermal conductance reach the predicted value in the acoustic mismatch model. 47 Therefore, the interface thermal conductance can be controlled by adjusting the Zr-C interfacial bonding rate below the saturated interfacial bonding ratio. Only six interfacial configurations with different concentrations of Zr-C interfacial bonding (1-6 vol.%) are constructed for each interface. A critical consideration is the graphene layers' thermal expansion coefficient (TEC), should match that of the ZrB 2 ceramic that it contacts. The in-plane thermal expansion coefficient of graphene (ranges from negative values to 1.2 × 10 -5 K -1 ) is ∼ 2 times that of ZrB 2 (5.9 × 10 -6 K -1 ) at room temperature. 48,49 Andthermal expansion coefficient depends on the interfacial interactions with heterogeneous materials. Interlayer phonon scattering of graphene reduces the in-plane thermal conductivity to approach the value of graphite when the thickness is increased to only about four layers. 50 So three-layer graphene was embedded into the ZrB 2 matrix to investigate the correlation between interlayer phonon scattering and interfacial thermal resistance. Owing to the outer 1 # and 3 # layers being covalent bonding while the inner 2 # layer is held by vdWs force, the expansion of each graphene layer is different. The interfacial thermal resistance of ZrB 2 /graphene plays a leading role in hindering thermal transport at low volume concentrations of graphene, while graphene-graphene (1 # /2 # or 2 # /3 # ) contact resistance becomes dominant in hindering the thermal transport at high content.

The anomalous nature of intrinsic thermal conductance in low-dimension materials, particularly for graphene with dimensional crossover, is termed a breakdown of Fourier's law. 6,9 Quantum mechanical calculations of the threephonon scattering processes revealed that the thermal conductance of suspended graphene layers contributes to the flexural acoustic (ZA) phonons. 10 Their high inplane thermal conductivity at the microscale was related to the logarithmic divergence of the 2D intrinsic thermal conductivity. 9 They also found that graphene layers with rough edges remain on a downswing by orders of magnitude compared with those with perfect edges. 11 Their assembly procedure with ZrB2 ceramic particles and high-temperature reduction inevitably left defects, and rough edges, which generated strong phonon scattering or leaking in graphene as well as at heterogenous interfaces. 12 This intrinsic edge-induced downswing trend was confirmed by the observed thermal rectification of few-layer graphene. 13 A large amount of reported works has also clarified that the interfacial thermal transfer of ZrB 2 /graphene heterostructure dominated by phonon transport decreases rapidly with increasing temperature. 5 This illustrates that heat transfer deterioration induced by the graphene/ZrB2 interface at high temperatures is another reason for aggravated damage of UHTCs. Hence, decreasing the local interfacial thermal resistance of graphene/ZrB2 heterostructure and restraining the edgeinduced downswing trend (or edge scattering effect) of embedded graphene layers are both key steps to reduce the thermal stress and prolong the life of next-generation spacecraft.

dynamic flow of the high-speed aircraft surface, generates a vast chasm between rapid energy-dissipation requirement and ultrahigh thermal conductance. Graphene has an ultrahigh in-plane thermal conductivity of more than 5 × 10 3 W ∙ m -1 ∙ K -1 , which is higher than that of metal and graphite. 2 The concurrent superiority of scalability, large surface-to-volume ratio, and high-temperature stability enables graphene to be the best heat reinforcement of UHTCs. 3 The excellent features of dispersion and assembly further make it easy to be incorporated into the UHTCs matrix. These exotic properties ignite strong interest in accelerating the heat transfer of ZrB 2 composites by constructing external heat-dissipation channels with graphene at ZrB2 grain boundaries. Yet graphene layers were either exfoliated from imperfect graphite powder or grown by chemical vapor deposition. 4 The in-plane thermal conductivity of embedded graphene layers, owing to the phonon Umklapp scattering of a heterogenous interface, decreasing from ∼ 10 2 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at room temperature to ∼ 10 1 W ⋅ m -1 ⋅ K -1 at high temperature. 5 Many worksofliterature have reported that the universal phonon scattering of heterogenous interface and Kapitza thermal resistance of graphene layers cause a reversed correlation between thermal conductance and temperature. 6,7 Interestingly, few researchers have found that the thermal conductivity of ZrB 2 composites with or without graphene reduces the diverse amount with the same temperature fluctuation. 5,8 Therefore, the underlying mechanisms behind the temperature-dependent heat transfer deterioration as the highly anisotropic phonon transport of embedded graphene layers remain elusive.

Despite high thermal conductivity of SiC at room temperature its thermal conductivity drops at high temperatures due to phonon transport. Enhanced phonon scattering occurs due to the lattice vibrations at high temperatures  and  presence  of  impurities,  quickly  dropping  off  the  thermal conductivity  values.
