question: What is the erosion resistance of C/C-ZrC-SiC-Graphite composites when exposed to 120 seconds of ablation in a solid rocket motor environment?
answer: The erosion resistance of C/C-ZrC-SiC-Graphite composites when exposed to 120 seconds of ablation in a solid rocket motor environment is not directly covered in the provided sources. However, the sources do mention the ablation behavior of similar composites. For example, the ablation behavior of C/C-ZrC-SiC composites was studied under various conditions. In one study, the linear ablation rate (LAR) of C/C-ZrC-SiC composites was 0.415 µm/s and the mass ablation rate (MAR) was 0.500 mg/s/cm² when exposed to an oxygen-acetylene flame for 120 seconds [7]. Additionally, another study reported that C/C-ZrC-SiC composites had a LAR of -0.028 µm/s and a MAR of 0.545 mg/s/cm² when tested for 300 seconds [8]. These results suggest that C/C-ZrC-SiC composites have good erosion resistance, but specific data for C/C-ZrC-SiC-Graphite composites in a solid rocket motor environment is not available in the provided sources.

2.3.2. Boride UHTCMCs of Cf/C e SiC-UHTC type
The ablation behavior of Cf/C e SiC e ZrB2 composites with a leading edge shape was also investigated in the combustion environment of a simulated solid rocket motor (SRM) [159]. The results demonstrated that the introduction of SiC e ZrB2 ceramic phases into the matrix resulted in a 26% reduction in linear ablation rate and a 45% reduction in volume ablation rate, respectively. Therefore, the resistance to chemical ablation in SRM could be signi /uniFB01 -cantly enhanced due to the low reactivity of ceramic phases. However, cracks were observed on the surface of the Cf/C e SiC e ZrB2 composites due to the mismatch in the CTE of different components. Furthermore, the active oxidation of SiC and the formation of a porous ZrO2 layer were unable to resist the exposure of chemical ablation at ultra-high temperatures. The synergistic process of thermochemical ablation and mechanical erosion in a high-speed, two-phase /uniFB02 ow with a high concentration of particles in the SRM plume environment resulted in a signi /uniFB01 cant amount of leading edge entrainment [159].
Composites of Cf/C e SiC-UHTC type based on HfB2 are also intensively being investigated [162,164,165]. Ablation behavior and thermal retardant ability of Cf/C e HfB2 e SiC composites were studied using oxyacetylene torch with a heat /uniFB02 ux of 2.38 MW/m 2 for 90 s [164]. The increase in ablation resistance was mainly attributed to the formation of a mixed layer of hafnium dioxide and borosilicate glass, which could act as an effective barrier against /uniFB02 ame exposure. The main possible reactions during ablation were (2), (9 e 18), (21), (26), (27), (38) and (39) [162,164,165]:
<!-- formula-not-decoded -->

a b s t r a c t
Conventional ultra-high-temperature ceramic-modified carbon/carbon composites (C/C-UHTCs) face limitations such as excessive erosion in the gas environment of solid rocket motors (SRMs). In contrast, metalreinforced C/C-UHTCs exhibit enhanced mechanical properties, thermophysical characteristics, and resistance to high-temperature erosion. These advantages indicate significant potential for metal-reinforced C/C-UHTCs to augment the thrust capabilities of SRMs. This study demonstrated the preparation of (Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu cermet modified C/C composites using slurry infiltration and reactive melt infiltration at 1400 ° C. The microstructure, formation mechanisms, mechanical properties, and thermophysical properties of C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite materials, as well as the ablation behavior of the composite materials under the SRM environment were compared. The results showed that the linear and mass ablation rates of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite material decreased by 112.1 % and 160.4 % (0.074 mm s -1 , 0.086 g s -1 ), respectively, compared with those of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C composite material. This could be attributed to the formation of a dense, high-strength W-skeleton composite (Hf, Zr, Ti)O2 oxide layer during the ablation of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)B2 -W-Cu composite, which effectively resisted erosion from high-temperature, high-speed and high-particleconcentration two-phase gas flow.

3.1. C/C-Hf(Zr, Ta)C composites
TaC is another common modifier used to improve the ablation resistance of C/C composites as SRM materials due to its high melting point of 3880 °C and good mechanical properties at high temperatures. Xiong et al. [28] fabricated C/C-TaC composites by PyC and TaC alternately deposited using TaCl5-C3H6-H2-Ar mixtures as raw materials by a CVI process. The composites with a 14 vol.% TaC content were obtained and exhibited the better ablation resistance than C/C for 120 s at over 2000 K under an oxyacetylene torch [92] . In addition, HfC-TaC co-deposited by CVD on graphite fiber composites for rocket thruster applications have been reported. The thruster with a weight ratio HfC/TaC of 7:3 was measured at NASA Lewis, survived two 5-s tests without failure and maintained an internal pressure of approximately 0.5 MPa [6] . These results highlighted the excellent high temperature oxidation and ablation resistance of the HfC/TaC system by the CVI/D and its suitability as a coating for carbon fiber composites for advanced rocket thrusters because of the difficulty of the infiltration of Hf, Zr and Ta-containing radicals in the technique. However, in our works, the addition of TaC was proven to severely deteriorate the ablation properties of C/C composites in oxyacetylene and liquid rocket engine environments because of the strongly mechanical denudation of the liquid Ta2O5 formed under the combustion gas impact [91] . The inconsistency of the ablation results was possibly caused by the different ablation conditions, particularly by temperatures and oxygen partial pressure.

Fig. 3. SEM images of a C/C-ZrC composite nozzle-throat after SRM ablation.

4.2. Oxidation and ablation of thermal protection materials
C/C composites are attractive materials for use in applications such as reentry vehicle nose tips, rocket nozzles, and hypersonic wing leading edges. In an inert atmosphere or in vacuum, C/C composites retain their strength, modulus, and other mechanical properties to temperatures higher than those tolerated by other materials [2] . However, the most important high temperature structural applications for C/C composites occurred in oxidizing environments, and the oxidation resistance is a major issue for this kind of the materials. Ceramics (e.g., SiC, ZrC, HfC, ZrB 2 and HfB 2 , etc.) were conventionally introduced into C/C composites to improve their oxidation and ablation resistance. C/C and graphite have almost the same thermal response and their oxidation and ablation rates strongly depend on the relative density irrespective of molecular structure, implying that the testing and optimization of C/C and C/C-based thermal protection materials can be based on graphite and graphite-based thermal protection materials from the viewpoints of thermal response, oxidation and ablation, respectively, since the latter with any composition can be easily prepared at low cost and with a little time. This could provide a powerful strategy to obtain the optimized composition of C/C-based materials quickly for a given service environment at low cost without the need to prepare and test C/C-based materials themselves, instead relying on graphite-based materials. Generally, these materials used as thermal structures should withstand mechanical load beside high temperature oxidation and ablation, and thereby C/C-based materials might be excellent candidates compared to graphite-based materials in a variety of high temperature applications.

Fig. 29. (a) SEM micrographs of GSZ in the center, and (b) edge regions after test under condition 2 where the outside oxide scale was flaked off during the cooling process.

2.2.1. Carbide UHTCMCs of Cf/C e UHTC type
Li et al. [108] examined the in /uniFB02 uence of /uniFB01 re test parameters on the ablation resistance of Cf/C e ZrC composites. When the heat /uniFB02 ux was 2.38 MW/m 2 , the composites exhibited excellent ablation resistance. A ZrO2 layer formed on the ablated surface partially isolated the heat /uniFB02 ux, thereby retarding the penetration of oxygen into the inner material. As the heat /uniFB02 ux increased to 4.18 MW/m 2 , the ablation resistance of Cf/C e ZrC composites decreased, primarily due to the spalling of the ZrO2 layer as the temperature reached 2500 /C14 C. This resulted in the inability of the ZrO2 layer to provide an effective protection to the substrate [108].
The presence of ZrC improves the ablation resistance of Cf/C composites in an oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame [56,107 e 110,114 e 117,119,120], under laser ablation conditions [97,110,112], in a solid rocket motor environment [111], in a plasma torch environment [113,118,121]. Thermochemical ablation (chemical erosion) is the main determinant of the ablation properties of the composites in the oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame [107,119], while mechanical breakage (mechanical denudation) is an important ablation mechanism for the composites under the solid rocket motor environment [111]. The ablation of Cf/C e ZrC composites in a nitrogen plasma torch is primarily determined by chemical erosion, mechanical denudation, and carbon sublimation. However, carbon sublimation plays a relatively minor role in the ablation of the composites [121].

Figure 16 illustrates the ablation process of C f /ZrB 2 -SiC composites. During ablation, ZrB 2 reacts with O 2 to generate ZrO 2 andgaseousB2O3,withZrO2 particles stacking on the surface. Simultaneously, SiC undergoes rapid oxidation to form molten glassy SiO 2 , while partial SiC sublimates into gaseous SiO, CO, or CO 2 . The highly fluid SiO 2 integrates with ZrO 2 particles, spreading across the sample surface. The dispersed ZrO 2 particles enhance the composite's erosion resistance under high-speed gas flow. When the surface temperature exceeds 2200 ◦ C, the viscosity of SiO2 decreases significantly, promoting detachment from the ablation center under gas shear. Approaching the boiling point of SiO 2 , extensive volatilization creates abundant pores and cracks in the ZrO 2 layer, providing channels for

a b s t r a c t
This work presents a novel approach to enhancing the ablation resistance of C/C-UHTCs and holds significant promise for the development of structural materials in SRM environments.
© 2026 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
mal stress that can easily lead to its failure. Consequently, the ablation performance and mechanical properties of the nozzle throat are critical indicators for assessing engine performance [ 3 ].
Currently, the materials primarily used for nozzle throats include W-Cu composites, high-purity graphite, and C/C composite materials [ 4-6 ]. W-Cu composites have been employed as nozzle throats for SRM; however, their relatively high density (16.84 g cm -3 ) and challenging processing requirements hinder their ability to meet the engine's lightweight specifications. Conversely, highpurity graphite has a high melting point, stable chemical properties, and excellent erosion resistance [ 7 ]. High-purity graphite is typically used for the nozzle throats, but it exhibits low strength and poor thermal shock resistance, thereby limiting its application [ 8 ]. C/C composite materials offer several advantages, including their low graphite density, minimal thermal expansion, stable chemical properties, and excellent thermal shock resistance. Additionally, they exhibit increased strength with increasing temperature when used in an inert atmosphere [ 9 , 10 ]. C/C composite materials have been successfully used in the nozzle throats of an Ariane
5 rocket engine. However, they demonstrate higher ablation rates in SRM, which pose challenges for meeting their long-term operational requirements. Consequently, enhancing the ablation performance of the C/C nozzle throat in SRM environments remains a critical problem to solve.

a b s t r a c t
The erosion process of the nozzle throat during operation can be categorized into two primary mechanisms: (1) Thermochemical erosion: This occurs because of reactions between the nozzle throat materials and oxidizing components (such as H2 O and CO2 ) in high-temperature gas, and (2) Two-phase flow erosion: This involves a high-temperature and high-pressure ablation environment where condensed-phase particles (e.g., Al 2 O3 ) and gases (H2 O, H2 , CO2 , etc.) coexist. The primary cause of mechanical erosion under these conditions is the impact of condensed-phase Al 2 O3 particles on the nozzle throat surface during ablation [ 11 ]. To enhance the thermochemical ablation performance, ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are frequently employed to improve the ablation resistance of C/C composites, owing to their high melting point (exceeding 30 0 0 ° C), stable chemical properties, and excellent antioxidation characteristics [ 12 ]. Shen et al. [ 13 ] prepared C/C-ZrC composite materials by incorporating ZrC ceramics into C/C and investigated their ablation behavior in an SRM environment. The preferential oxidation of ZrC reduced the consumption of C/C, resulting in a 20.6 % increase in the linear ablation rate of C/C-ZrC compared with that of C/C. However, the amount of ZrC added was relatively small (5 wt.%), and the enhancement of the ablation performance using single-component ceramics was limited. Medium-entropy carbide UHTCs have garnered considerable attention owing to their excellent oxidation resistance [ 14-16 ]. Zhang et al. [ 17 ] introduced (Zr, Hf, Ti)C ceramics into C/C-SiC using a precursor impregnation cracking method and examined their ablation properties.

a b s t r a c t
The mechanical erosion of the C/C nozzle throat results primarily from the peeling of the carbon matrix under shear forces and damage to the carbon fibers (Cfs) due to particle erosion. In addition, C/C composites oxidize in aerobic environments at temperatures exceeding 400 ° C, leading to a gradual decline in their high-strength and toughness properties. The incorporation of high-strength and high-toughness metal phases with stable performance can significantly enhance the strength and fracture toughness of the C/C composites, thereby improving their erosion resistance in SRM environments. Tungsten (W) has a high melting point (3410 ° C), exceptional hardness, and considerable strength among metals, and is regarded as the only material that is not corroded by aluminum-containing engine gases [ 21 ]. Sun et al. [ 22 ] prepared zirconium carbide/tungsten (ZrC/W) composites with vary- ing W content using a reaction-sintering method and investigated the impact of W content variations on the mechanical properties of ZrC/W. The results showed that owing to the interface strengthening effect, increasing the W content from 65 vol.% to 80 vol.% considerably enhanced the strength (from 402 MPa to 705 MPa) and fracture toughness (from 8.3 MPa m 1/2 to 10.7 MPa m 1/2 ) of ZrC/W. Additionally, reducing the surface temperature of the sample by incorporating dopants that preferentially evaporate at high temperatures proved to be an effective method for enhancing the resistance of the material to erosion [ 23 ]. Cu is frequently used as a coolant in materials owing to its high latent heat of melting and evaporation, which are 208.67 kJ kg -1 and 4730 kJ kg -1 , respectively [ 24 ]. Wu et al. [ 25 ] introduced Cu into C/C-SiC composites using the reactive infiltration method (RMI) and investigated their long-term ablation performance.

3.4. Ablation behavior of C/C-ZrC-SiC composites

(7) (8) (9) (10) (11) (12) (13) (14) composite reached 193.2 MPa, which is 58.78 % higher than that of the composite  prepared  using  PCZ  and  PCS  precursors.  The  composites exhibited superior ablation resistance, with a mass ablation rate of 0.10 mg/s and a linear ablation rate of 0.03 μ m/s. The compact structure and uniform dispersion of fine ceramic particles contributed to the enhanced mechanical properties and ablation resistance. Specifically, the fine ZrC and SiC particles facilitated the formation of a protective oxide scale consisting of a dense ZrO2 outer layer and a continuous SiO2 inner layer. The  dense  ZrO2  layer  effectively  inhibited  oxygen  diffusion  into  the composites, providing excellent resistance to mechanical erosion, while the continuous SiO2 layer further minimized the penetration of oxidizing atmosphere,  thereby  enhancing  the  ablation  resistance.  This  study demonstrates  that  C/C-ZrC-SiC  composites  can  be  fabricated  via  an inorganic salt strategy at a lower cost, offering improved properties and a new approach for fabricating C/C-UHTCs composites.

3.3. Ablation performances of the HZTBW composites in an SRM environment
Fig. 12 shows the microstructure of the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C composite material before and after SRM ablation. Prior to the ablation of C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C, the ceramic phase was firmly bonded to the Cf beams, and no cracks were evident within the material. Owing to the high preparation temperature, some micropores were evident around the Cf, resulting in a composite material with a porosity of 9.6 % ( Fig. 12 (b)). Fig. 12 (a) shows the internal ablation surface structure of C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C after ablation, with the flame oriented from left to right. It is evident that in the nearflame zone, the carbon matrix underwent considerable erosion. The ceramic phase between the Cf beams was nearly detached, leaving behind a substantial number of pores. Further characterization of the Cf revealed that it was oxidized into a bamboo shoot structure ( Fig. 12 (c)). The Al 2 O3 particles adhered to the surface of the Cf, and there was a fracture zone caused by the impact of the high-temperature, high-speed Al 2 O3 particles. The proportion of the ceramic oxide layer increased away from the flame zone, and molten spherical (Hf, Zr, Ti)O 2 particles were present. This phenomenon can be attributed to the flame temperature of the SRM combustion chamber (3043 ° C) exceeding the melting point of (Hf, Zr, Ti)O 2 . During the cooling process, (Hf, Zr, Ti)O 2 coalesced into liquid droplets that adhered to the surface of the Cf. Further analysis of the ceramic oxide layer is presented in Fig. 12 (d). The oxide layer consisted of micrometer-sized (Hf, Zr, Ti)O 2 particles, with Al 2 O3 filling the spaces between them (Fig. S5).

3.1. C/C-Hf(Zr, Ta)C composites
Although the melting point of ZrC (3540 °C) is close to the sublimation temperature of carbon (3550 °C), it has a much lower density of 6.6 g/cm 3 than TaC (14.5 g/cm 3 ) and HfC (12.7 g/cm 3 ), and is cheaper. Besides, its oxidation-resistant property and high strength and stiffness can also effectively improve the operating performance of C/C in SRM. Shen et al. [68] prepared C/C-ZrC composites by an in-situ reaction process as mentioned above, using ZrOCl2 · 8H2O as raw material, and investigated their ablation behaviors in a small SRM hot-fire testing system. The as-prepared composites were exposed to the combustion gases for 3-4 s at a temperature of 3361-3386 K and a chamber pressure of 10.5-15.8 MPa, as well as to the oxyacetylene flame (about 3273 K for 60 s) for a comparison [98] . During the ablation of the composites, the preferential oxidation of ZrC reduced the carbon consumption due to the lower Gibbs free energy for the heterogeneous reactions between ZrC and oxygen-containing species. The melting of solid ZrO2 and the evaporation of liquid ZrO2 absorbed heat from the combustion gases, which relieved the erosive attack on the carbon. Consequently, the presence of ZrC improved the ablation resistance of C/C composites. However, the linear ablation rate in the SRM with highpressure and high-velocity combustion gases was much higher than that in the oxyacetylene flame. Lots of pits and ZrO2 particles, and cone shaped fibers or irregularly shaped carbon fragments, appeared on the ablation surface of C/C-ZrC composites in the SRM (Fig. 3). Different ablation rates and ablation appearance features were attributed to the different ablation mechanisms.

3.3. Ablation performances of the HZTBW composites in an SRM environment
Fig. 9 (a) shows the pressure and time curves in the combustion chamber of the HZTBW and traditional C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C nozzle throat subjected to gas erosion in SRM. For the HZTBW composite materials, the maximum pressure within the combustion chamber reached 6.23 MPa within 1 s. Subsequently, owing to the erosion of the HZTBW nozzle throat and the retreat of the inner surface, the combustion chamber pressure decreased linearly. After 6 s, the fuel had completely burned, resulting in a final pressure of 5.62 MPa. By contrast, the pressure change process for the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C nozzle throat was similar to that for the HZTBW. The maximum pressure in the combustion chamber was 6.11 MPa; however, the pressure began to decrease rapidly after 3 s, reaching a final pressure of 4.8 MPa. The pressure drop rates of the HZTBW and C/C-(Hf-Zr-Ti)C composite-nozzle throat combustion chambers were 9.79 % and 21.4 %, respectively. Fig. 9 (b) shows the macroscopic morphology of the HZTBW and C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C composite samples before and after ablation. The surface of the HZTBW sample exhibited a notable metallic luster prior to ablation, whereas the inner surface experienced minimal and uniform retreat following SRM ablation. By contrast, the C/C-(Hf, Zr, Ti)C nozzle throat suffered from catastrophic erosion and considerable retreat after ablation ( Fig. 9 (b)). Additionally, the ablation surface exhibited an uneven texture characterized by a major retreat in a single direction, resembling the shape of water droplets.

3. Results
All materials were tested under the condition 2, but the ablation time was different with the range of 100 s to 600 s which was determined based on the oxidation and ablation resistance of these materials. UHTCs were tested for 600 s and other materials were ablated for 200 s except for GS (100 s) due to the thermal shock damage during the testing. Fig. 6 shows the morphologies of all materials after testing. UHTCs experienced remarkable oxida- tion under this condition but the final shape structures are significantly different from those tested under condition 1. The formed ZrO 2 outside scale was stable for Z and ZS which acted as an effective "thermal barrier coating" preventing these materials from rapid oxidation, whereas part of the formed ZrO 2 was removed by gas flow leaving a trumpet-shaped feature for ZSG, resulting in severe oxidation as shown in Fig. 6 (c). C/SiC exhibited the highest ablation rate and almost consumed after 200 s testing as shown in Fig. 6 (d). Oxidation and ablation behavior of C/C and graphite under condition 2 are analogous to those tested under condition 1 and the surface recession rates of both materials are slightly lower than those tested under condition 1. GS has a very low thermal shock resistance in comparison CS and catastrophic damage along radial direction occurred during test. Interestingly, the shape of GSZ was significantly changed and a very flat surface was obtained after testing. This feature is similar to those for UHTC tested under condition 1.

4. Conclusion
Ablation resistant performance of C/C-ZrC composite was comparatively investigated via an economical laser ablation testing method. LAR of the composite was obviously enhanced tested with increasing LPD and reduced with prolonging testing time periods. The composite exhibits greatly improved ablation resistant performance than PDC-C/ SiC composite. LAR are merely 28.5 and 43.7 μm/s tested with LPD of
1000 and 1500 W/cm 2 . The improved ablation resistant performance was resulted from the melting ZrO2 layer produced on the ablated surface. Damage mechanism with three periods was proposed to illustrate ablation process of C/C-ZrC composite.

question: What is the oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of multi-component UHTC systems incorporating tungsten carbide (WC) components when exposed to plasma jet at temperatures above 2800°C?
answer: The oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTC) systems incorporating tungsten carbide (WC) components when exposed to plasma jet at temperatures above 2800°C is not explicitly detailed in the provided sources. However, based on the general understanding from the literature, the addition of WC in UHTC systems can contribute to enhanced oxidation resistance through preferential oxidation mechanisms. For instance, the preferential oxidation of certain elements can lead to the formation of protective oxide layers, which can significantly improve the oxidation resistance of the material. This is evident in the formation of a layered multi-oxide scale that strongly retards inward oxygen diffusion and blocks heat flow [17,23-25]. Moreover, the synergy of hierarchical preferential oxidation can induce inter-phase migration of preferentially oxidized components, facilitating the development of oxide frameworks that offer effective protection against high-speed hot gas scouring and enhance ablation performance [12]. However, specific data regarding the oxidation resistance and preferential oxidation mechanism of MC-UHTC systems with WC components at temperatures above 2800°C is not provided in the sources.

chunks.text:
A B S T R A C T
Multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) has garnered attention due to their improved thermo-mechanical and oxidation properties. MC-UHTC was progressively formed by adding a UHTC component at  each  step,  producing  binary,  ternary  and  quaternary  UHTC  systems,  processed  by  spark  plasma  sintering (SPS). We investigate the role of multi-elements and thermochemical stability of the surface oxide layer formed during plasma arc jet exposure ( T > 2500 ◦ C) of MC-UHTCs using core-level X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) correlated with the structural investigation. The results indicate that adding a UHTC component improves oxidation resistance due to the assemblage of refractory phases forming complex mixed oxides via preferential oxidation. XPS spectra of Ta4f, Nb3d, Ti1p, and Hf4f showed that the nature of the chemical bonds switched from metal carbides to metal oxides (and metal oxy-carbides) in the binary system with retained metal carbides in ternary and quaternary UHTCs. The absence of a metal carbide peak in the high-resolution C1s scan of the binary system also supports this finding. The principal complex mixed oxide phases emanated in the exiguous oxidation of MC-UHTCs are quantified using Rietveld analysis. Overall, this study suggests that MC-UHTC is a promising material for possible application in thermal protection systems (TPS).
sharp leading edges in re-entry vehicles. These materials have gathered quick attention because of their remarkable properties, which include enhanced mechanical properties [9 -12], improved fracture toughness [11,13,14],  tribological  performance  [15 -17],  creep  resistance  [18], and low thermal conductivity [11,19 -21].

4. Summary
The MC-UHTCs showed remarkable improvement in the oxidation resistance during plasma exposure testing compared to binary UHTCs. This has been attributed to the preferential oxidation and a reduction in low melting temperature unary oxide content in the surface oxide scale from > 82% in binary UHTC to < 13% in MC-UHTC. A thermally stable complex protective oxide layer of Hf6Ta2O17 (a primary phase) in MCUHTCs has further suppressed the oxide formation. As a result of the relative thermal stability of transition metals and components in the MCUHTCs, preferential oxidation occurs in MC-UHTCs, i.e., Hf in ternary and Ti in quaternary UHTC system, while both are forming the protective mixed complex oxide layer to remarkably improve ( > 40 times) the oxidation resistance over binary and unary UHTC systems. Both XRD and XPS analysis showed retention of metal carbide phase only in MCUHTCs. The study suggests that MC-UHTCs designed to form a mixed complex protective oxide layer of group IV element under service conditions elicit extended potential in extreme space vehicle applications.

5. Conclusion
In this work, a multi-phase multicomponent UHTC, Hf0.61Zr0.26Ti0.13C- (Hf0.30Zr0.32Ti0.38)5Si3, was designed and fabricated. The oxidation mechanism of the multiphase multicomponent UHTC was investigated through experiments in conjunction with thermodynamic analysis. It is found that, during oxidation, the intraphase preferential oxidation of components (i.e., Hf and Zr) and the inter-phase preferential oxidation toward thermodynamically favored phase (i.e., silicide) would occur synchronously. In this process, the intraphase preferential oxidation led to the formation of a nanoscale self-healing oxide skeleton in  carbide  phase.  More  importantly,  the  synergy  of  the  hierarchical preferential oxidation induced an inter-phase migration of preferentially oxidized components (i.e., Hf and Zr) from carbide phase to the preferentially oxidized silicide phase, which facilitated the development of a micrometer-sized  Hf/Zr-rich  oxide  framework.  This  framework  provided additional support for the nano-sized self-healing oxide skeleton, moreover, the as-formed multi-scale hierarchical structured oxide scale offered an effective protection for the material and improved the ablation performance. The ablation test shows that multi-phase multicomponent UHTC with merely 4.4 vol% of silicide demonstrates a superior ablation resistance compared to the single-phase counterpart (a reduction of 64.7% and 62.5% in MAR and LAR, respectively). More broadly, the results obtained in this work could provide a preliminary basis for the design and optimization of the multiphase multicomponent UHTCs.

4.  Potential pathways to address challenges involved with HEUHTCs
Based on the limited available data in the literature [13,30,46,58], a comparison  of  the  mechanical  and  oxidation  resistance  of  mono, binary,  ternary,  and  multi-component  UHTC  carbides  (referred  to  as HE-carbide) is summarized and plotted in Fig. 7. The improvement in the hardness (see Fig. 7) has mainly been attributed to the solid-solutioning in the binary, ternary, and HE-UHTCs. The onset temperature at which the oxide starts forming has remarkably improved from 900 °C in monolith UHTCs to > 1100 °C for HE-UHTCs (Fig. 7 ) . This is possibly due to the formation of the complex, layered structure as well as the composition of the oxide scale in a multi-component system. The innermost oxide layer near the unoxidized sample usually has a dense structure with lesser pores (all filled with molten particles) followed by a porous oxide structure with lesser pores (see Fig. 5). Also, the complex composition of the mixed oxides due to multi-constituents restricts the inward  diffusion  of  O2 providing  oxidation  resistance,  discussed  in section 3.2. However, whether all components in a solid-solution of the HE-UHTC  system  react  with  oxygen  simultaneously  or  preferential oxidation, still needs to be looked upon in both isothermal and highspeed  plasma  jet  conditions.  Also,  this  brings  up  a  question  of  how many  constituents  (four,  five,  six,  or  more)  in  a  multi-component system would be sufficient for enhancement in the properties in the absence of entropy quantification. To get an insight into it, a study on gradually raising the constituents in UHTCs from mono, binary and so on is required along with the delineation of the entropy contributions to the properties.
Fig.  7. Comparison  of  the  hardness  and  onset  of  oxidation  temperature  in various carbides.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs), a class of ceramic materials operating above 2000 ◦ C, primarily include borides, carbides, and nitrides of transition metals[1]. As critical materials for extreme environments, their oxidation resistance directly determines their service lifetime  and  reliability  under  high-temperature  oxidative  conditions. HfB ₂ -SiC is a representative UHTC system, widely applied in hypersonic vehicle nose cones, rocket engine nozzles, and other thermal protection components due to its ultra-high melting point ( > 3000 ◦ C) and exceptional oxidation/ablation resistance[2]. 1. Introduction
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are a class of ceramic materials that possess a melting point generally greater than 3000 ℃ , have the capability to operate at temperatures around 2000 ℃ (as experienced in hypersonic applications), and are typically composed of carbides, nitrides, and borides of early transition metals [1 -3]. Due to their high-temperature stability and ablation resistance, UHTCs have been widely used in thermal protective systems of aerospace and aviation in the past decades [4,5]. As these developing technologies become more advanced and demanding, UHTCs are under increasing pressure to perform under harsher operating conditions.
Recently, it has been found that multi-component ultra-high temperature ceramics (MC-UHTCs) have better oxidation properties than single-component UHTCs [6,7]. MC-UHTC can form a mixed composite oxide protective layer of Group IV elements under oxidation and ablation environment, offering greater potential for extreme space vehicle applications [8 -10]. Zeng et al. [11] proposed a Zr0.8Ti0.2C0.74B0.26 ceramic coating on C/C composites, which realized the ablation resistance of the materials at 3000 ℃ . Kurbatkina et al. A B S T R A C T
Ultra-high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) have shown aspiration to overcome challenges in the thermal protection system (TPS) by designing new materials referred to as multi-component UHTCs (MC-UHTCs) in the compositional space. MC-UHTCs have shown remarkable improvement in oxidation resistance due to the formation of the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure during plasma exposure. Herein, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure is synthesized via a solid-state reaction between HfO2 and Ta2O5 powder mixtures during spark plasma sintering (SPS). The compositions chosen are 50 vol% of HfO2 -50 vol% of Ta2O5 (50HO-50TO) and 70 vol% of HfO2 -30 vol% of Ta2O5 (70HO-30TO). The phase quantification via Rietveld analysis showed Hf6Ta2O17 as a principal phase with some residual Ta2O5  phase in both the samples. The high-temperature thermal stability of the samples was evaluated using high-velocity plasma jet exposure for up to 3 min. 50HO-50TO was able to withstand the intense plasma condition, which is attributed to the higher content of the Hf6Ta2O17 phase (~84%) and lower strain in the Ta2O5 phase. The augmentation in the Hf6Ta2O17 phase to 94.7% (in 50HO-50TO) post plasma exposure has been attributed to the invariant transformation from a liquid state to Hf6Ta2O17 at temperatures > 2500 ◦ C during testing. The mechanical integrity is elucidated from the insignificant change in the hardness ~13.3 GPa before and 11.2 GPa after plasma exposure of the 50HO-50TO sample. As a result, the Hf6Ta2O17  superstructure ' s thermo-mechanical stability suggests developing novel oxidation-resistant MC-UHTCs in compositional space for reusable space vehicle applications.

A B S T R A C T
Generally, the superior oxidation resistance remains a core objective pursued by the research of multicomponent UHTCs, while it is indicated that the enhanced oxidation resistance is largely attributed to the formation of the layered multi-oxides scale which strongly retarded the inward oxygen-diffusion and block heat flow [17,23 -25]. Recent studies have demonstrated that thermodynamic favorability of elements plays a vital  role  in  the  assemblage  of  oxidation  product,  meanwhile  the oxidation behavior of multicomponent system exhibits highly composition dependent [5,26 -28]. 1. Introduction
Ultra high temperature ceramics (UHTCs) are candidate materials for a variety of aerospace applications owing to their unique combination of properties, including high melting temperature ( > 3273 K), high strength, and high elastic modulus [1 e 5]. However, their oxidation resistance (especially for additive free materials) is a major issue in the development of transition metal boride based UHTCs for high temperature applications as the oxidation product B2O3 evaporates resulting in a porous and non-protective columnar ZrO2 or HfO2 grain structure [6 e 9].

A B S T R A C T
Designing  ablation-resistant  multi-phase  multicomponent  ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  remains challenging due to the absence of the understanding toward their oxidation mechanism. Herein, through the investigations on the ablation behaviors of a multiphase multicomponent UHTC, the mechanism of hierarchical preferential oxidation was elucidated. We found that, during ablation, the intraphase preferential oxidation of elements  and  the  inter-phase  preferential  oxidation  of  thermodynamically  favored  phase  would  occur  synchronously. In this hierarchical preferential oxidation process, the intraphase preferential oxidation led to the formation of nanoscale self-healing oxide skeletons in carbide phase. More importantly, the synergy of the hierarchical preferential oxidation induced an inter-phase migration of preferentially oxidized elements (i.e., Hf/ Zr) to the preferentially oxidized phase (i.e., silicide), which facilitated the development of micrometer-sized Hf/ Zr-rich oxide frameworks. These frameworks and nano-sized skeletons together constituted a multi-scale hierarchical oxide layer, offering an effective protection against scouring of the high-speed hot gas and enhancing the ablation performance.
researches are mainly focus on single-phase multicomponent system, however, multi-phase multicomponent UHTCs are rarely studied so far. Given  the  fact  that  the  multi-phase  approach  has  been  successfully adopted to improve densification [18], mechanical properties [19,20] and oxidation resistance [21,22] of single-phase UHTCs, the multiphase multicomponent  UHTCs  might  achieve  better  and  more  tunable properties.

physical and chemical stabilities, ultra-high-temperature ceramics (UHTCs) demonstrate significant potential as candidate materials for TPS operating in extreme thermal environments exceeding 2000 ◦ C. 4,5 However, the operational requirements in extreme thermal-oxidative environments impose critical constraints on UHTCs. Beyond ultra-high melting points, these materials must simultaneously demonstrate exceptional retained mechanical strength along with high oxidation and ablation resistance. Current UHTCs undergo accelerated oxidation degradation between 400 ◦ C and 1200 ◦ C, forming nonprotective porous oxide films, which fail to protect the material from further disintegration. 6 Consequently, their application in extremely high-temperature environments is significantly restricted.

A B S T R A C T
On the other hand, a low oxidation rate is a required property in materials envisaged for hot structural applications. Enhanced oxidation behavior have been related to partial consumption of the incoming oxygen to form an adherent oxide layer composed of either a dense outermost scale and/or a compact and stable sub-scale. In the ceramic at hand, SiC forms an outermost SiO2 glassy coating and WC increases the glass stability and forms solid compounds in the subsurface at low oxygen partial pressure [22]. First studies on the oxidation behavior of this system mostly dealt with static furnaces, up to 1650 °C. The oxidation mechanism has been disclosed upon thorough investigations of the microstructure evolution by scanning and transmission electron microscopy, SEM and TEM [6]. Those analyses /uniFB01 rst pointed out the unique oxidation mechanism of the (Zr,W)B2 solid solution into ZrO2 grains encasing nano-beads of metallic W. In addition, a recent study on the ablation behavior upon exposure to an oxyacetylene /uniFB02 ame at 2670 K [14] assessed the e /uniFB00 ect of W-compounds in suppressing the active oxidation of SiC through their preferential oxidation. As a matter of fact, the combination of excellent high temperature mechanical and thermal properties of ZrB2-SiC-WC makes it a robust candidate as an ablation resistant material for components of hypersonic vehicles.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  have  gained  renewed attention  in  the  last  decades  due  to  their  potential  use  as  protective coatings on metal substrates in extreme environments [1 -5]. By definition, UHTCs are ceramic materials with melting points above 3000 ◦ C [6,7].  Therefore,  they  are  suitable  candidates  for  thermal  protection structures in gas-turbine engines or chemical plants, which can withstand  application  temperatures  exceeding  ~1600 ◦ C  and  aggressive environmental conditions [1,8 -11]. In addition to their high melting point,  UHTCs  combine  improved  structural  properties  such  as  high hardness,  excellent  chemical  stability,  high  Youngs  modulus,  high refractoriness, as well as high thermal and electrical conductivity [1, 8 -10,12]. UHTCs are typically binary compounds consisting of carbides, borides or nitrides of early transition metals (e.g. ZrB2, HfN, HfC or TaC) [7,8,12]. utilized SiC particles and ZrO2 fibers to reinforce ZrB2. Pre-oxidation at 1200 ◦ C produced SiO2 and ZrO2, which repaired the crack defects in the raw material. Wen et al. [14] passivated SiC/(Hf,Ta)C at 1400 ◦ C, which greatly  improved  their  oxidation  resistance  at  lower  temperatures. Therefore, for some multicomponent UHTC that struggle to form a dense protective  oxide  scale  at  low  temperatures,  and  have  poor  oxidation resistance, a high/ultra-high temperature passivation (or pre-oxidation) process  can  be  used  to  activate  the  specific  components  and  form  a robust  oxide  scale  in  advance.  This  proactive  approach  endows  the UHTC  composites with enhanced oxidation resistance at lower temperatures.
UHTCs preparation methods [25 -27] include solid state synthesis, carbo-thermal reduction, molten salt, sol-gel and polymer-derived ceramics (PDCs), etc. Compared to other methods, the single-source-precursor (SSP) method offers significant advantages in terms of adjustable ceramic composition and structure, as well as the ability  to  obtain  materials  with  uniform  composition  and  nanocomposite  structures  [28 -31].  In  this  work,  SiC/(Hf0.2Ta0.8)C  nanocomposites  with  good  oxidation  resistance  at  1200 -1600 ◦ C  were fabricated based on the idea of multi-elements and multicomponent, and taking full advantage of the SSP method. Combined with passivation, this strategy allows the composition and structure of the oxide scale on the UHTCs to be pre-modified. The ' anomalous ' oxidation behavior of the  composites  was  adjusted  to  provide  a  well-balanced  oxidation resistance across the medium to high temperature region.

1. Introduction
Ultra-high  temperature  ceramics  (UHTCs)  are  potential  candidate materials  for  thermal  protection  systems  in  extreme  environments. Preparation of UHTC coatings on fiber reinforced composites is a logical choice  for  improving  the  oxidation  and  ablation  resistance  in  high temperature  and  oxygen  contained  environments  [1 -3].  Among  all UHTCs, ZrC is very attractive for the unique combination of low density (6.73 g/cm 3 ),  high  melting  point  (3540 ◦ C) and good thermal shock resistance [4 -6]. In the past few years, ZrC based coating has attracted lots of attention and SiC has been proved to be a suitable additive to improve the ablation resistance of ZrC coating [7 -9].
Vacuum  plasma  spray  is  particularly  suitable  for  UHTC  coating fabrication and the microstructure of as-sprayed coating has important effect  on  its  oxidation/ablation  behavior.  In  our  previous  work,  we fabricated  ZrB2-20  vol%MoSi2 composite  coatings  by  atmospheric plasma spray (APS) and vacuum plasma spray (VPS), then investigated the effect of fabrication method on the oxidation behavior of the coatings. 4. Conclusions
Oxidation behaviour of spark plasma sintered TaC-SiC-CNT composites was evaluated through plasma arc jet exposure under heat flux of 2.5 MW/m 2 . Oxidation behaviour is characterised in terms of oxide layer formation, its thickness and residual stresses. After exposure to harsh aerothermal conditions, the structural integrity of these UHTC composites were measured in terms of critical energy release rate ( G IC ) which increases up to 2.5 times ( ∼ 58 to 139 Jm -2 ) with synergistic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in TaC. The protective SiO2 formation with SiC reinforcement in TaC suggested an extension of stability domain which resulted in an improved oxidation resistance. It is elicited that CNT addition improves oxidation resistance of TaC by grain sealing mechanism. Raman spectroscopic analysis elicited the presence of carbonaceous material even after plasma exposure, further supplemented by TEM analysis of T15S15C. In addition, enhanced thermal conductivity with temperature for all composites provides an indication of improved oxidation resistance via heat dissipation. Thermal studies up to 1500 ◦ C have confirmed that the temperature of oxide formation shifts towards higher temperature (from 735 ◦ C to 901 ◦ C) and decrease in the enthalpy of oxide formation (from 4.5 to 3.5 kJ/g) with SiC and CNT reinforcement. Such an observation insinuates synergetic reinforcement of SiC and CNT in enhancing the thermal stability and oxidation resistance of TaC-based UHTC composites.

4. Conclusions
A  270 μ m thick  ultra-high  temperature  ceramic  (UHTC)  coating, based on ZrB2 with additions of WC and SiC, was successfully deposited by shroud plasma spray on a ceramic matrix composite (CMC) upon proper tuning of powder feedstock compactness and granule size.
Compositional changes from the powder feedstock to the coating were  compatible  to  what  observed  on  sintered  bulks  with  the  same composition, including reaction of WC with boron oxide, yielding WB, and  negligible  ZrB2 oxidation,  which  retained  a  mean  grain  size  of around 1 -3 μ m and was surrounded by a specific (Zr,W)B2  solid solution, while SiC remained as discrete particles distributed at the triple junctions.
The  thermal  erosion  behavior  of  the  coating  was  tested  under extreme cyclic thermal-shock conditions using a combustion chamber and  different  oxygen  contents,  1  and  33 wt%  to  simulate  different phases of flights, which achieved 1500 -1600 ◦ C. Microstructural evolution upon testing highlighted minimal material recession, in the order of 100 -140 µ m and, particularly, the UHTC coating survived 10 heatingcooling cycles from 1500 ◦ C to 700 ◦ C in an air-fuel mixture with 1 wt% oxygen.  Presence  of  higher  oxygen  content  in  the  chamber  enabled longer coating duration thanks to the boosting of self-healing mechanisms. In all cases, no signs of coating delamination or crater formation were observed, indicating good adhesion strength of the coating to the Cf/C-SiC substrate. On the contrary, the uncoated same CMC reference was destroyed after 100 s.
A continuous layer of unoxidized UHTC remained well anchored to the  underlying  CMC,  thus  assessing  the  good  prospectives  of  these coatings for their implementation in next-generation thermal protection systems (TPS).
